topics.title: Random Variables
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GCMAT401 - MATHEMATICS FOR PHYSICAL SCIENCE AND LIFE SCIENCE ŌĆō 4
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Random Variables

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Random Variable (RV)** is a real-valued function defined on a sample space of a random experiment. Formally, if $\Omega$ is the sample space, then a random variable $X$ is a function

$$X : \Omega \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$$

that assigns a unique real number $X(\omega)$ to every outcome $\omega \in \Omega$. The range of $X$ ŌĆö the set of all possible real values the variable can take ŌĆö is called the **range space** or **image** of $X$, denoted $R_X$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Clarification (Common KTU Mistake):** A random variable is *NOT* a variable in the algebraic sense (like $x$ in $f(x) = x^2$). It is a **function** that maps outcomes to numbers. This distinction is heavily tested in KTU Part A questions.

### 1.2 Classification of Random Variables

Random variables are broadly classified into two categories based on the nature of their range:

| Type | Symbol Convention | Nature of Range | Typical Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Discrete Random Variable** | $X$ (uppercase), $x_i$ (values) | Countable (finite or countably infinite) | Number of defective items, number of phone calls per hour, dice roll outcome |
| **Continuous Random Variable** | $X$ (uppercase), $x$ (values) | Uncountable (any value in an interval) | Height of students, time to failure, voltage fluctuation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mixed Random Variables** (those with both discrete and continuous components) exist but are outside the scope of the KTU 2024 GCMAT401 syllabus.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **coffee vending machine**. Every time you press the button, *something* happens ŌĆö you either get a cup of coffee, no cup, or even a malfunction. The **set of all possible outcomes** (the sample space) is hard to express numerically. Now, suppose we define a rule:

- "If the machine dispenses coffee, assign the number **+1**"
- "If the machine fails, assign the number **ŌĆō1**"
- "If the machine gives change only, assign the number **0**"

That rule ŌĆö converting messy outcomes into clean numbers ŌĆö *is* the random variable. The **probability of each numerical outcome** is what we later study using PMF, PDF, and CDF.

> [!TIP]
> Think of a random variable as a **translator** between the world of outcomes (often non-numerical) and the world of real numbers (where calculus and probability rules apply smoothly).

### 1.4 Why Random Variables Matter in Engineering

- **Signal Processing:** Noise in a communication channel is modeled as a continuous random variable.
- **Quality Control:** The number of defective products in a batch is a discrete random variable governed by the Binomial distribution.
- **Reliability Engineering:** Time-to-failure of components is typically modeled as an exponential or Weibull random variable.
- **Biostatistics / Life Science:** Heart rate variability, blood pressure measurements, and enzyme kinetics are all continuous random variables.

---

### 1.5 Probability Mass Function (PMF) ŌĆö Discrete Case

For a discrete random variable $X$ that takes values $x_1, x_2, x_3, \ldots$, the **Probability Mass Function** $P_X(x)$ (often written $p(x)$) is defined as

$$P_X(x_i) = P(X = x_i)$$

**Properties of a Valid PMF:**

$$P_X(x) \geq 0 \quad \forall x \in R_X$$

$$\sum_{x \in R_X} P_X(x) = 1$$

### 1.6 Probability Density Function (PDF) ŌĆö Continuous Case

For a continuous random variable $X$, the **Probability Density Function** $f_X(x)$ is a non-negative function such that, for any interval $[a, b]$,

$$P(a \leq X \leq b) = \int_{a}^{b} f_X(x) \, dx$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crucial Distinction (Frequently Tested in KTU):** For continuous RVs, $P(X = x) = 0$ for any single point. We only assign probabilities to *intervals*. The PDF itself is **not** a probability ŌĆö only the integral over an interval is.

**Properties of a Valid PDF:**

$$f_X(x) \geq 0 \quad \forall x \in \mathbb{R}$$

$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_X(x) \, dx = 1$$

### 1.7 Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF) ŌĆö Universal Definition

The **Cumulative Distribution Function** $F_X(x)$ is the most general tool, applicable to both discrete and continuous random variables. It is defined as

$$F_X(x) = P(X \leq x)$$

This single function unifies the treatment of both types of random variables.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic of Random Variables ŌĆö Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Step 1 ŌĆö Define the Sample Space:** Identify all possible outcomes of the random experiment (denoted $\Omega$).
2. **Step 2 ŌĆö Define the Mapping Rule:** Construct the function $X: \Omega \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$. This rule must be unambiguous: every outcome gets exactly one real number.
3. **Step 3 ŌĆö Identify the Range Space $R_X$:** Determine the set of all possible values $X$ can take. This range decides whether $X$ is discrete or continuous.
4. **Step 4 ŌĆö Assign the Probability Structure:** Either write a PMF table (discrete) or define a PDF (continuous), ensuring all probability axioms are satisfied.
5. **Step 5 ŌĆö Verify Validity:** Check that all probabilities are non-negative and that the total probability (sum for discrete, integral for continuous) equals **1**.
6. **Step 6 ŌĆö Compute Required Probabilities:** Use the PMF, PDF, or CDF to answer questions about $P(X \leq a)$, $P(a \leq X \leq b)$, etc.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a Random Variable is Measurable:** For a random variable to be mathematically valid, the set $\{\omega \in \Omega : X(\omega) \leq x\}$ must be an event in the probability space. This technicality is what allows us to write $P(X \leq x)$ meaningfully.

### 2.2 Discrete vs. Continuous ŌĆö Comparative Analysis

| Feature | Discrete RV | Continuous RV |
|---|---|---|
| **Range** | Countable | Uncountable (interval) |
| **Function** | PMF: $P_X(x_i)$ | PDF: $f_X(x)$ |
| **Probability at a point** | $P(X = x_i) > 0$ possible | $P(X = x) = 0$ always |
| **Validity Condition 1** | $P_X(x_i) \geq 0$ | $f_X(x) \geq 0$ |
| **Validity Condition 2** | $\sum P_X(x_i) = 1$ | $\int f_X(x) \, dx = 1$ |
| **CDF** | Right-continuous, jump function | Continuous, smooth |
| **Graph of CDF** | Step function with jumps | Smooth, non-decreasing curve |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section ŌĆö these are the high-yield formulas examiners expect you to reproduce under exam pressure.**

**I. Fundamental Definitions**

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Random Variable | $X: \Omega \to \mathbb{R}$ | Function on sample space |
| 2 | PMF (Discrete) | $P_X(x) = P(X = x)$ | Sum to 1 over range |
| 3 | PDF (Continuous) | $P(a \le X \le b) = \int_a^b f_X(x)\, dx$ | Integral to 1 over $\mathbb{R}$ |
| 4 | CDF (Universal) | $F_X(x) = P(X \le x)$ | Same for both types |
| 5 | $F_X(\infty)$ | $= 1$ | Total probability |
| 6 | $F_X(-\infty)$ | $= 0$ | Null probability |

**II. Relationships Between PMF, PDF, and CDF**

| # | Relationship | Formula | Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| 7 | CDF from PMF | $F_X(x) = \sum_{x_i \le x} P_X(x_i)$ | Discrete |
| 8 | CDF from PDF | $F_X(x) = \int_{-\infty}^{x} f_X(t)\, dt$ | Continuous |
| 9 | PMF from CDF | $P_X(x_i) = F_X(x_i) - F_X(x_i^{-})$ | Discrete (jump) |
| 10 | PDF from CDF | $f_X(x) = \dfrac{d}{dx} F_X(x)$ | Continuous (where derivable) |
| 11 | $P(a < X \le b)$ | $= F_X(b) - F_X(a)$ | Universal |

**III. Key Properties of CDF $F_X(x)$**

| # | Property | Statement |
|---|---|---|
| 12 | Non-decreasing | $x_1 < x_2 \Rightarrow F_X(x_1) \le F_X(x_2)$ |
| 13 | Right-continuous | $\lim_{x \to a^+} F_X(x) = F_X(a)$ |
| 14 | Bounded | $0 \le F_X(x) \le 1$ |
| 15 | Limits | $F_X(-\infty) = 0$, $F_X(+\infty) = 1$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Use `\vert` or `\mid` (NOT `\|`)** in your answer sheet for absolute value and conditional probability expressions. KTU examiners often deduct marks for ambiguous notation.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Random Variable Modeled | Distribution |
|---|---|---|
| **Communication Systems** | Thermal noise voltage | Gaussian (Normal) |
| **Queueing / Networks** | Inter-arrival time of packets | Exponential |
| **Quality Control** | Number of defectives per lot | Binomial / Poisson |
| **Reliability Engineering** | Component lifetime | Exponential / Weibull |
| **Biostatistics** | Patient recovery time | Lognormal |
| **Civil Engineering (loads)** | Wind speed, snow load | Extreme value (Gumbel) |
| **Finance & Insurance** | Stock returns, claim sizes | Normal / Lognormal |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: $P(a \leq X \leq b) = F_X(b) - F_X(a)$

This is a foundational identity that KTU examiners test every semester. We derive it directly from the definition of the CDF.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Write the definition of $F_X(b)$:**

$$F_X(b) = P(X \le b)$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Partition the event $\{X \le b\}$ into two disjoint events:**

$$P(X \le b) = P(X < a) + P(a \le X \le b)$$

(assuming the boundary point $X = a$ has zero measure, which holds in the continuous case and is treated as a minor adjustment in the discrete case).

**Step 3 ŌĆö Recognize that $P(X < a) = F_X(a^{-})$ (left-hand limit of the CDF at $a$). For continuous $X$, this equals $F_X(a)$:**

$$F_X(b) = F_X(a) + P(a \le X \le b)$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Solve for the desired probability:**

$$P(a \le X \le b) = F_X(b) - F_X(a) \qquad \blacksquare$$

> [!TIP]
> Memorize this identity. It is used as a building block in nearly every KTU problem involving either PDF or PMF questions.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: $f_X(x) = \dfrac{d}{dx} F_X(x)$ (Continuous Case)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Start with the definition of the CDF as an integral:**

$$F_X(x) = \int_{-\infty}^{x} f_X(t) \, dt$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus (Leibniz rule):**

By the FTC, if $F_X(x) = \int_{-\infty}^{x} f_X(t)\, dt$, then

$$\frac{d}{dx} F_X(x) = f_X(x) \qquad \blacksquare$$

> [!NOTE]
> The derivative exists at all points where $f_X$ is continuous. At jump points (discrete), the derivative does not exist in the classical sense, and we use the Dirac delta representation instead.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 1: Constructing a PMF from Raw Data

> **Problem:** A fair die is rolled. Let $X$ be the number that appears on the top face. Define $X$ as a random variable, find its PMF, and compute $P(X \leq 3)$ and $P(2 \leq X \leq 5)$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Sample Space:** $\Omega = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6\}$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Mapping:** $X(\omega) = \omega$. So $X$ takes values $1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6$.

**Step 3 ŌĆö PMF:** Since the die is fair, each outcome has probability $\frac{1}{6}$:

$$P_X(x) = \begin{cases} \frac{1}{6}, & x = 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Validity Check:**

$$\sum_{x=1}^{6} P_X(x) = 6 \times \frac{1}{6} = 1 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Compute $P(X \leq 3)$:**

$$P(X \le 3) = \sum_{x=1}^{3} P_X(x) = 3 \times \frac{1}{6} = \frac{1}{2}$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Compute $P(2 \leq X \leq 5)$:**

$$P(2 \le X \le 5) = \sum_{x=2}^{5} P_X(x) = 4 \times \frac{1}{6} = \frac{2}{3}$$

---

### 3.4 Worked Example 2: Building a CDF from a PDF

> **Problem:** A continuous random variable $X$ has PDF

$$f_X(x) = \begin{cases} kx, & 0 \le x \le 2 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

> Find (i) the value of $k$, (ii) the CDF $F_X(x)$, (iii) $P(1 \leq X \leq 1.5)$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Use the normalization condition $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_X(x)\, dx = 1$ to find $k$:**

$$\int_{0}^{2} kx \, dx = 1$$

$$k \left[ \frac{x^2}{2} \right]_{0}^{2} = 1$$

$$k \cdot \frac{4}{2} = 1$$

$$k \cdot 2 = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad k = \frac{1}{2}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Build the CDF piecewise.** For $x < 0$:

$$F_X(x) = 0$$

For $0 \leq x \leq 2$:

$$F_X(x) = \int_{0}^{x} \frac{1}{2} t \, dt = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{x^2}{2} = \frac{x^2}{4}$$

For $x > 2$:

$$F_X(x) = \int_{0}^{2} \frac{1}{2} t \, dt = 1$$

So,

$$F_X(x) = \begin{cases} 0, & x < 0 \\ \dfrac{x^2}{4}, & 0 \le x \le 2 \\ 1, & x > 2 \end{cases}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Verify CDF properties:** $F_X$ is non-decreasing, right-continuous, and satisfies $F_X(0) = 0$, $F_X(2) = 1$. Ō£ō

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compute $P(1 \leq X \leq 1.5)$:**

$$P(1 \le X \le 1.5) = F_X(1.5) - F_X(1) = \frac{(1.5)^2}{4} - \frac{(1)^2}{4} = \frac{2.25 - 1}{4} = \frac{1.25}{4} = 0.3125$$

---

### 3.5 Worked Example 3: Recovering PDF from CDF

> **Problem:** The CDF of a continuous RV is

$$F_X(x) = \begin{cases} 0, & x < 0 \\ \frac{x^2}{9}, & 0 \le x \le 3 \\ 1, & x > 3 \end{cases}$$

> Find the PDF, and verify normalization.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Differentiate the CDF in each region where it is differentiable:**

For $x < 0$: $F_X(x) = 0 \Rightarrow f_X(x) = 0$.

For $0 \leq x \leq 3$: $F_X(x) = \frac{x^2}{9} \Rightarrow f_X(x) = \frac{2x}{9}$.

For $x > 3$: $F_X(x) = 1 \Rightarrow f_X(x) = 0$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Combine into a piecewise PDF:**

$$f_X(x) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{2x}{9}, & 0 \le x \le 3 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Verify normalization:**

$$\int_{0}^{3} \frac{2x}{9} \, dx = \frac{2}{9} \left[ \frac{x^2}{2} \right]_{0}^{3} = \frac{2}{9} \cdot \frac{9}{2} = 1 \quad \checkmark$$

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Method Selection Guide

| Question Type | Best Approach | Key Formula |
|---|---|---|
| Given a PDF, find $P(a \le X \le b)$ | Direct integration | $\int_a^b f_X(x)\, dx$ |
| Given a PMF, find $P(a \le X \le b)$ | Summation | $\sum_{x_i \in [a,b]} P_X(x_i)$ |
| Given a PDF, find CDF | Integrate from $-\infty$ to $x$ | $F_X(x) = \int_{-\infty}^x f_X(t)\, dt$ |
| Given a CDF, find PDF | Differentiate | $f_X(x) = \frac{d}{dx} F_X(x)$ |
| Given any CDF, find $P(a \le X \le b)$ | Difference of CDF values | $F_X(b) - F_X(a)$ |
| Find $k$ (unknown constant) | Normalization | $\sum P = 1$ or $\int f = 1$ |
| Find $P(X = c)$ for continuous RV | Trivially zero | $P(X=c) = 0$ |
| Find $P(X = c)$ for discrete RV | Direct lookup | $P_X(c)$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Flow ŌĆö Random Variable Ecosystem

This block diagram maps how a random experiment flows through the various probability constructs. Study this carefully ŌĆö it is the mental model examiners expect you to internalize.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Random Experiment] --> B[Sample Space omega]
    B --> C[Random Variable X mapping]
    C --> D{Discrete or Continuous}
    D -- Discrete --> E[PMF P sub X of x]
    D -- Continuous --> F[PDF f sub X of x]
    E --> G[CDF F sub X of x]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Probabilities and Moments]
    E --> H
    F --> H
    H --> I[Engineering Applications]
```

### 4.2 PMF vs. PDF ŌĆö Visual Architecture

The following block diagram highlights the structural differences in how probability is encoded for discrete and continuous random variables.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DISCRETE
        P1[PMF P of x] --> P2[Probability assigned to each value]
        P2 --> P3[Sum equals 1]
        P2 --> P4[CDF is a step function]
    end
    subgraph CONTINUOUS
        Q1[PDF f of x] --> Q2[Probability assigned to intervals]
        Q2 --> Q3[Integral equals 1]
        Q2 --> Q4[CDF is a smooth curve]
    end
```

### 4.3 CDF Construction Workflow

This diagram captures the procedure to construct a CDF from any starting definition (PMF or PDF), which is the single most-tested skill in KTU 2024 GCMAT401.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start] --> S2{Source given}
    S2 -- PMF --> S3[Sort values in ascending order]
    S3 --> S4[Cumulative sum at each value]
    S4 --> S5[Add step jumps of size PMF value]
    S5 --> S6[CDF F sub X of x]
    S2 -- PDF --> S7[Integrate from minus infinity to x]
    S7 --> S8[Build piecewise expression]
    S8 --> S6
    S6 --> S9[Check F minus infinity equals 0 and F infinity equals 1]
    S9 --> S10[End]
```

### 4.4 Hierarchy of Probability Functions

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Cumulative Distribution Function F of x] --> B[Single unified definition]
    A --> C[Non decreasing]
    A --> D[Right continuous]
    A --> E[Bounds between 0 and 1]
    B --> F[Discrete RV yields step function]
    B --> G[Continuous RV yields smooth curve]
    F --> H[PMF recovered by differences]
    G --> I[PDF recovered by differentiation]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]**
**Define a random variable. Distinguish between discrete and continuous random variables with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

A *random variable* $X$ is a real-valued function defined on the sample space $\Omega$ of a random experiment, i.e., $X : \Omega \to \mathbb{R}$, which assigns a unique real number to each outcome.

A *discrete random variable* takes only a countable set of values, with probabilities given by a Probability Mass Function (PMF). Example: The number of heads in 3 coin tosses ($X \in \{0, 1, 2, 3\}$).

A *continuous random variable* takes values in one or more intervals of real numbers, with probabilities given by a Probability Density Function (PDF) via integration. Example: The lifetime of an electronic component (e.g., $X \in [0, \infty)$).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]**
**State any four properties of the Cumulative Distribution Function $F_X(x)$.**

**Model Answer:**

1. $0 \leq F_X(x) \leq 1$ for all $x \in \mathbb{R}$.
2. $F_X(x)$ is a non-decreasing function of $x$ (i.e., if $x_1 < x_2$, then $F_X(x_1) \leq F_X(x_2)$).
3. $F_X(x)$ is right-continuous: $\lim_{x \to a^+} F_X(x) = F_X(a)$.
4. $F_X(-\infty) = 0$ and $F_X(+\infty) = 1$.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024 Style]**

**(a) (7 marks)** A discrete random variable $X$ has the following probability distribution:

| $x$ | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $P(X = x)$ | $k$ | $2k$ | $3k$ | $4k$ | $5k$ |

Find (i) the value of $k$, (ii) $P(X \leq 2)$, (iii) $P(1 \leq X \leq 3)$, and (iv) the CDF $F_X(x)$.

**(b) (7 marks)** A continuous random variable $X$ has the PDF

$$f_X(x) = \begin{cases} kx^2, & 0 \leq x \leq 3 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

Find (i) the value of $k$, (ii) the CDF $F_X(x)$, and (iii) $P(1 \leq X \leq 2)$.

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) Solution:**

**(i) Finding $k$:** Use the normalization condition $\sum P_X(x) = 1$:

$$k + 2k + 3k + 4k + 5k = 1$$

$$15k = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad k = \frac{1}{15}$$

**(ii) Computing $P(X \leq 2)$:**

$$P(X \le 2) = P(0) + P(1) + P(2) = \frac{1}{15} + \frac{2}{15} + \frac{3}{15} = \frac{6}{15} = \frac{2}{5}$$

**(iii) Computing $P(1 \leq X \leq 3)$:**

$$P(1 \le X \le 3) = P(1) + P(2) + P(3) = \frac{2}{15} + \frac{3}{15} + \frac{4}{15} = \frac{9}{15} = \frac{3}{5}$$

**(iv) Constructing the CDF $F_X(x)$:**

$$F_X(x) = P(X \le x) = \begin{cases} 0, & x < 0 \\ \frac{1}{15}, & 0 \le x < 1 \\ \frac{3}{15} = \frac{1}{5}, & 1 \le x < 2 \\ \frac{6}{15} = \frac{2}{5}, & 2 \le x < 3 \\ \frac{10}{15} = \frac{2}{3}, & 3 \le x < 4 \\ \frac{15}{15} = 1, & x \ge 4 \end{cases}$$

---

**Part (b) Solution:**

**(i) Finding $k$:** Use the normalization condition $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_X(x)\, dx = 1$:

$$\int_0^3 kx^2 \, dx = 1$$

$$k \left[ \frac{x^3}{3} \right]_0^3 = 1$$

$$k \cdot \frac{27}{3} = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 9k = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad k = \frac{1}{9}$$

**(ii) Constructing the CDF $F_X(x)$:**

For $x < 0$:

$$F_X(x) = 0$$

For $0 \leq x \leq 3$:

$$F_X(x) = \int_0^x \frac{t^2}{9} \, dt = \frac{1}{9} \cdot \frac{x^3}{3} = \frac{x^3}{27}$$

For $x > 3$:

$$F_X(x) = 1$$

So,

$$F_X(x) = \begin{cases} 0, & x < 0 \\ \dfrac{x^3}{27}, & 0 \le x \le 3 \\ 1, & x > 3 \end{cases}$$

**(iii) Computing $P(1 \leq X \leq 2)$:**

$$P(1 \le X \le 2) = F_X(2) - F_X(1) = \frac{2^3}{27} - \frac{1^3}{27} = \frac{8 - 1}{27} = \frac{7}{27} \approx 0.2593$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023 Style]**

**(a) (7 marks)** The cumulative distribution function of a random variable $X$ is given by

$$F_X(x) = \begin{cases} 0, & x < 0 \\ \frac{x}{4}, & 0 \le x < 4 \\ 1, & x \ge 4 \end{cases}$$

Find (i) $P(X > 2)$, (ii) $P(1 \leq X \leq 3)$, and (iii) the PDF $f_X(x)$.

**(b) (7 marks)** A random variable $X$ has the PDF

$$f_X(x) = \begin{cases} \frac{x}{8}, & 0 \le x \le 4 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

Find (i) the CDF $F_X(x)$, (ii) $P(X \leq 2)$, and (iii) $P(X \geq 3)$.

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) Solution:**

**(i) Computing $P(X > 2)$:**

Using the complement rule and the CDF:

$$P(X > 2) = 1 - P(X \le 2) = 1 - F_X(2) = 1 - \frac{2}{4} = 1 - \frac{1}{2} = \frac{1}{2}$$

**(ii) Computing $P(1 \leq X \leq 3)$:**

$$P(1 \le X \le 3) = F_X(3) - F_X(1) = \frac{3}{4} - \frac{1}{4} = \frac{1}{2}$$

**(iii) Finding the PDF $f_X(x)$:**

Differentiating $F_X(x)$ in the relevant region:

For $x < 0$: $f_X(x) = 0$.

For $0 \leq x < 4$: $f_X(x) = \frac{d}{dx}\left(\frac{x}{4}\right) = \frac{1}{4}$.

For $x \geq 4$: $f_X(x) = 0$.

$$f_X(x) = \begin{cases} \frac{1}{4}, & 0 \le x < 4 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **uniform distribution** on $[0, 4]$ ŌĆö a frequently tested KTU model.

---

**Part (b) Solution:**

**(i) Constructing the CDF $F_X(x)$:**

For $x < 0$:

$$F_X(x) = 0$$

For $0 \leq x \leq 4$:

$$F_X(x) = \int_0^x \frac{t}{8} \, dt = \frac{1}{8} \cdot \frac{x^2}{2} = \frac{x^2}{16}$$

For $x > 4$:

$$F_X(x) = 1$$

So,

$$F_X(x) = \begin{cases} 0, & x < 0 \\ \dfrac{x^2}{16}, & 0 \le x \le 4 \\ 1, & x > 4 \end{cases}$$

**(ii) Computing $P(X \leq 2)$:**

$$P(X \le 2) = F_X(2) = \frac{2^2}{16} = \frac{4}{16} = \frac{1}{4}$$

**(iii) Computing $P(X \geq 3)$:**

$$P(X \ge 3) = 1 - P(X < 3) = 1 - F_X(3^{-}) = 1 - \frac{3^2}{16} = 1 - \frac{9}{16} = \frac{7}{16}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Boundary Inclusion Errors:** For continuous RVs, $P(X \leq a) = P(X < a)$ because $P(X = a) = 0$. For discrete RVs, however, $P(X \leq a) = P(X < a) + P(X = a)$. Many students lose **1ŌĆō2 marks** by writing $P(X \leq a) = P(X < a)$ in discrete problems.
> 2. **Forgetting to Use the Complement:** When asked to find $P(X > a)$ or $P(X \geq a)$, always use $1 - P(X \leq a)$ or $1 - P(X < a)$ first, especially when the upper limit is $\infty$.
> 3. **Missing Constants:** In every "find $k$" question, **always state the normalization condition explicitly** before solving. KTU examiners specifically test this step.
> 4. **Confusing PDF with Probability:** The PDF $f_X(x)$ for a continuous RV is **NOT** a probability. Writing $P(X = x) = f_X(x)$ loses marks.
> 5. **CDF Direction:** The CDF is defined as $P(X \leq x)$, not $P(X < x)$. Right-continuity at jump points is a key property ŌĆö draw step diagrams for discrete CDFs to avoid missing the jump at the boundary.
> 6. **No Empty Set in PMF Table:** Each row of a PMF table must list a value of $x$ *and* its probability. Empty cells invite mark deductions.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute revision checklist ŌĆö read this 30 minutes before entering the exam hall.**

- [x] A **random variable** is a *function* $X : \Omega \to \mathbb{R}$, not a variable in the algebraic sense.
- [x] **Discrete RV** $\Rightarrow$ **PMF**; probabilities sum to 1.
- [x] **Continuous RV** $\Rightarrow$ **PDF**; integral equals 1; $P(X = x) = 0$.
- [x] **CDF** $F_X(x) = P(X \leq x)$ ŌĆö universal for both types.
- [x] CDF is **non-decreasing, right-continuous**, bounded in $[0, 1]$, with $F_X(-\infty) = 0$ and $F_X(+\infty) = 1$.
- [x] $P(a \leq X \leq b) = F_X(b) - F_X(a)$ ŌĆö the workhorse identity.
- [x] $f_X(x) = \dfrac{d}{dx} F_X(x)$ ŌĆö recover PDF by differentiation.
- [x] $F_X(x) = \int_{-\infty}^{x} f_X(t)\, dt$ ŌĆö build CDF by integration.
- [x] $P_X(x_i) = F_X(x_i) - F_X(x_i^{-})$ ŌĆö recover PMF from CDF using jump heights.
- [x] Always verify the **normalization condition** before using any PMF/PDF.
- [x] Use `$\vert$` or `$\mid$` for absolute values; avoid `$\|$` in tables.
- [x] The **step-function shape** of the CDF is the visual signature of a discrete RV.
- [x] The **smooth, non-decreasing curve** shape of the CDF is the visual signature of a continuous RV.
- [x] Common engineering models: **Binomial** (discrete, defect counts), **Poisson** (discrete, rare events), **Uniform** (continuous, equal likelihood), **Exponential** (continuous, waiting time), **Normal** (continuous, measurement error).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Discrete Random Variables and Their Probability Distributions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GCMAT401 - MATHEMATICS FOR PHYSICAL SCIENCE AND LIFE SCIENCE ŌĆō 4
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Discrete Random Variables and Their Probability Distributions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Random Variable

A **Random Variable (RV)** is a real-valued function defined on the sample space $S$ of a random experiment. It assigns a unique real number to every possible outcome of the experiment. Mathematically,

$$X : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$$

so that for every elementary outcome $s \in S$, the corresponding numerical value is $X(s) = x$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A random variable is a function from the sample space of a random experiment into the set of real numbers. The function itself is deterministic ŌĆö only its input (the outcome) is random.

## 1.2 Classification of Random Variables

Random variables are broadly classified into two families relevant to Module 1:

| Type | Domain | Function Type | Probability Descriptor |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Discrete Random Variable** | Countable (finite or countably infinite) | Step function | Probability Mass Function (PMF) |
| **Continuous Random Variable** | Uncountable (interval of $\mathbb{R}$) | Continuous | Probability Density Function (PDF) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This module deals strictly with **discrete random variables**, i.e., those that take a finite or countably infinite set of values such as $\{0, 1, 2, \dots\}$ or $\{1, 2, \dots, 6\}$.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy ŌĆö The "Numbered Chits in a Box"

Imagine a sealed box containing **50 numbered chits** drawn randomly one at a time. Each chit has a single integer written on it. The "number that appears" on the chit you draw is your **discrete random variable** $X$.

- The *experiment* = drawing a chit.
- The *sample space* $S$ = the set of all 50 chits.
- The *random variable* $X$ = the number printed on the chit.
- The *probability distribution* of $X$ = a rule telling us how likely each number is to appear.

Even though the act of picking is "random," once the rule of assignment (which number corresponds to which chit) is fixed, $X$ behaves as a perfectly well-defined mathematical function.

## 1.4 Probability Distribution ŌĆö The Big Picture

A **probability distribution** of a discrete random variable $X$ is a complete description that specifies, for every admissible value $x_i$ of $X$, the corresponding probability $P(X = x_i)$. Two equivalent representations are used in the KTU syllabus:

1. **Probability Mass Function (PMF):** $p(x) = P(X = x)$.
2. **Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF):** $F(x) = P(X \leq x)$.

> [!TIP]
> In KTU board exams, you will almost always be asked to *first construct the PMF table*, then derive the CDF, mean, variance, and MGF from it. Always start with the PMF.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Probability Mass Function (PMF)

Let $X$ be a discrete random variable taking values $x_1, x_2, x_3, \dots$ with respective probabilities $p_1, p_2, p_3, \dots$. The function

$$p(x_i) = P(X = x_i)$$

is called the **Probability Mass Function** of $X$, provided it satisfies the following two **Kolmogorov axioms for the discrete case**:

$$p(x_i) \geq 0 \quad \text{for every } i$$

$$\sum_{i} p(x_i) = 1$$

The summation is over *all* admissible values of $X$ (finite or countably infinite).

> [!NOTE]
> A PMF is the discrete analogue of a density. Since the values are *point masses*, no probability is "spread" continuously ŌĆö every probability is concentrated at a single point $x_i$.

## 2.2 Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF)

The CDF of a discrete random variable $X$ is defined for any real number $x$ as

$$F(x) = P(X \leq x) = \sum_{x_i \leq x} p(x_i)$$

For a discrete RV, $F(x)$ is a **right-continuous step function** with jumps of size $p(x_i)$ at each point $x_i$.

**Key properties of $F(x)$:**

- $0 \leq F(x) \leq 1$ for all $x \in \mathbb{R}$.
- $F(x)$ is non-decreasing: $x_1 < x_2 \Rightarrow F(x_1) \leq F(x_2)$.
- $F(-\infty) = 0$ and $F(+\infty) = 1$.
- $P(a < X \leq b) = F(b) - F(a)$.

## 2.3 Expectation (Mean) and Moments

The **expected value** or **mathematical expectation** of a discrete random variable $X$ is

$$E(X) = \mu = \sum_{i} x_i \, p(x_i)$$

provided the series converges absolutely. Expectation is a *weighted average* where the weights are the probabilities themselves.

### 2.3.1 Expectation of a Function of $X$

For any real-valued function $g(X)$,

$$E[g(X)] = \sum_{i} g(x_i) \, p(x_i)$$

This is the **Law of the Unconscious Statistician (LOTUS)** ŌĆö we never need to find the PMF of $Y = g(X)$ to compute $E(Y)$.

### 2.3.2 Moments about the Origin

The $r$-th moment of $X$ about the origin is

$$\mu_r^{\prime} = E(X^r) = \sum_{i} x_i^{\,r} \, p(x_i)$$

- $r = 1$: $\mu_1^{\prime} = \mu = E(X)$ ŌĆö the **mean**.
- $r = 2$: $\mu_2^{\prime} = E(X^2)$ ŌĆö the **second raw moment**.

### 2.3.3 Moments about the Mean (Central Moments)

The $r$-th central moment is

$$\mu_r = E\!\left[(X - \mu)^r\right] = \sum_{i} (x_i - \mu)^r \, p(x_i)$$

### 2.3.4 Variance and Standard Deviation

$$\text{Var}(X) = \sigma^2 = E\!\left[(X - \mu)^2\right] = E(X^2) - [E(X)]^2$$

$$\sigma = \sqrt{\text{Var}(X)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU exam board **always** gives full credit for the computational identity $\text{Var}(X) = E(X^2) - \mu^2$. Memorise it.

## 2.4 Moment Generating Function (MGF)

The **Moment Generating Function** of a discrete RV $X$ is defined as

$$M_X(t) = E(e^{tX}) = \sum_{i} e^{t x_i} \, p(x_i), \quad t \in \mathbb{R}$$

assuming the series converges in a neighbourhood of $t = 0$.

**Key properties:**

- $M_X(0) = 1$ (always, since probabilities sum to 1).
- $E(X^r) = M_X^{(r)}(0)$, i.e., the $r$-th derivative of $M_X(t)$ evaluated at $t = 0$ gives the $r$-th raw moment.
- $E(X) = M_X^{\prime}(0)$ and $E(X^2) = M_X^{\prime\prime}(0)$.

## 2.5 Skewness and Kurtosis (Brief Mention)

- **Skewness** $\gamma_1 = \dfrac{E[(X - \mu)^3]}{\sigma^3}$.
- **Kurtosis** $\gamma_2 = \dfrac{E[(X - \mu)^4]}{\sigma^4}$.

These are higher-order descriptors rarely asked in KTU Module 1, but their definitions form the basis for later topics.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Conditions / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | PMF | $p(x_i) = P(X = x_i)$ | $p(x_i) \geq 0$, $\sum p(x_i) = 1$ |
| 2 | CDF | $F(x) = \sum_{x_i \leq x} p(x_i)$ | Right-continuous step function |
| 3 | Probability in interval | $P(a < X \leq b) = F(b) - F(a)$ | Use when $a,b$ are real numbers |
| 4 | Mean | $\mu = E(X) = \sum x_i \, p(x_i)$ | Convergence required |
| 5 | $E[g(X)]$ (LOTUS) | $\sum g(x_i) \, p(x_i)$ | Do not derive PMF of $g(X)$ |
| 6 | $r$-th raw moment | $\mu_r^{\prime} = E(X^r) = \sum x_i^{\,r} \, p(x_i)$ | $r \in \mathbb{N}$ |
| 7 | Variance | $\sigma^2 = E(X^2) - \mu^2$ | Always use this in KTU exams |
| 8 | Standard deviation | $\sigma = \sqrt{\sigma^2}$ | Non-negative by definition |
| 9 | MGF | $M_X(t) = \sum e^{t x_i} p(x_i)$ | $M_X(0) = 1$ |
| 10 | Moments via MGF | $E(X^r) = M_X^{(r)}(0)$ | Differentiate $r$ times, set $t=0$ |
| 11 | Skewness | $\gamma_1 = E[(X-\mu)^3] / \sigma^3$ | Symmetric if $\gamma_1 = 0$ |
| 12 | Kurtosis | $\gamma_2 = E[(X-\mu)^4] / \sigma^4$ | Mesokurtic if $\gamma_2 = 3$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering utility:** Discrete distributions model *counted* phenomena ŌĆö number of defective items in a batch (binomial), number of arrivals per unit time (Poisson), number of trials until the first success (geometric), number of successes in $n$ trials (binomial), and so on. These appear directly in **quality control, reliability engineering, queueing theory, and signal processing**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Computational Identity for Variance

We start from the definition $\sigma^2 = E\!\left[(X - \mu)^2\right]$ and expand the square inside the expectation.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Expand the quadratic.**

$$(X - \mu)^2 = X^2 - 2\mu X + \mu^2$$

This is the standard binomial expansion of a perfect square, with $\mu$ treated as a constant with respect to the outer expectation.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply the expectation operator term-by-term.**

$$\sigma^2 = E\!\left[X^2 - 2\mu X + \mu^2\right] = E(X^2) - 2\mu \, E(X) + E(\mu^2)$$

Linearity of expectation is used here. Recall that $E(aX + b) = aE(X) + b$ for any constants $a,b$.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Simplify using $E(X) = \mu$.**

Since $E(X) = \mu$ and $\mu$ is a constant, $E(\mu^2) = \mu^2$. Substituting:

$$\sigma^2 = E(X^2) - 2\mu \cdot \mu + \mu^2 = E(X^2) - 2\mu^2 + \mu^2$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Final simplification.**

$$\sigma^2 = E(X^2) - \mu^2$$

This completes the derivation. $\blacksquare$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This identity is the single most-used formula in KTU Module 1 problems. The derivation is routinely asked as a 3-mark sub-question.

## 3.2 Derivation of Moments from the MGF

**Step 1 ŌĆö Write the MGF definition.**

$$M_X(t) = \sum_{i} e^{t x_i} \, p(x_i)$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Differentiate once with respect to $t$.**

$$M_X^{\prime}(t) = \sum_{i} x_i \, e^{t x_i} \, p(x_i)$$

Differentiation can pass through the sum because each $x_i$ and $p(x_i)$ are constants with respect to $t$, and $\frac{d}{dt}(e^{t x_i}) = x_i e^{t x_i}$.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Evaluate at $t = 0$.**

$$M_X^{\prime}(0) = \sum_{i} x_i \, e^{0} \, p(x_i) = \sum_{i} x_i \, p(x_i) = E(X)$$

So $E(X) = M_X^{\prime}(0)$.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Differentiate again to obtain the second moment.**

$$M_X^{\prime\prime}(t) = \sum_{i} x_i^{\,2} \, e^{t x_i} \, p(x_i)$$

$$M_X^{\prime\prime}(0) = \sum_{i} x_i^{\,2} \, p(x_i) = E(X^2)$$

By induction, $E(X^r) = M_X^{(r)}(0)$. $\blacksquare$

## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example ŌĆö A Custom PMF

**Problem.** A discrete random variable $X$ takes the values $-1, 0, 1, 2$ with probabilities $P(X = -1) = 0.2$, $P(X = 0) = 0.1$, $P(X = 1) = 0.3$, $P(X = 2) = 0.4$. Find the mean, variance, MGF, and CDF.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Verify the PMF sums to 1.**

$$\sum p(x_i) = 0.2 + 0.1 + 0.3 + 0.4 = 1.0 \;\checkmark$$

Both the non-negativity and the unit-sum axioms are satisfied.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute the mean $E(X)$.**

$$E(X) = \sum x_i \, p(x_i) = (-1)(0.2) + (0)(0.1) + (1)(0.3) + (2)(0.4)$$

$$E(X) = -0.2 + 0 + 0.3 + 0.8 = 0.9$$

The mean of the distribution is $\mu = 0.9$.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute $E(X^2)$.**

$$E(X^2) = \sum x_i^{\,2} \, p(x_i) = (-1)^2 (0.2) + (0)^2 (0.1) + (1)^2 (0.3) + (2)^2 (0.4)$$

$$E(X^2) = 0.2 + 0 + 0.3 + 1.6 = 2.1$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compute the variance.**

$$\text{Var}(X) = E(X^2) - [E(X)]^2 = 2.1 - (0.9)^2 = 2.1 - 0.81 = 1.29$$

$$\sigma = \sqrt{1.29} \approx 1.1358$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Construct the MGF.**

$$M_X(t) = \sum e^{t x_i} p(x_i) = 0.2 \, e^{-t} + 0.1 \, e^{0} + 0.3 \, e^{t} + 0.4 \, e^{2t}$$

$$M_X(t) = 0.2 e^{-t} + 0.1 + 0.3 e^{t} + 0.4 e^{2t}$$

**Verification of moments via MGF:**

$$M_X^{\prime}(t) = -0.2 e^{-t} + 0.3 e^{t} + 0.8 e^{2t}$$

$$M_X^{\prime}(0) = -0.2 + 0.3 + 0.8 = 0.9 = E(X) \;\checkmark$$

$$M_X^{\prime\prime}(t) = 0.2 e^{-t} + 0.3 e^{t} + 1.6 e^{2t}$$

$$M_X^{\prime\prime}(0) = 0.2 + 0.3 + 1.6 = 2.1 = E(X^2) \;\checkmark$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Construct the CDF as a step function.**

For a discrete RV, the CDF is a right-continuous staircase:

$$F(x) = \begin{cases} 0, & x < -1 \\ 0.2, & -1 \leq x < 0 \\ 0.3, & 0 \leq x < 1 \\ 0.6, & 1 \leq x < 2 \\ 1.0, & x \geq 2 \end{cases}$$

The jumps at $x = -1, 0, 1, 2$ are $0.2, 0.1, 0.3, 0.4$ respectively ŌĆö exactly the PMF values.

**Step 7 ŌĆö Verification using the CDF identity $P(a < X \leq b) = F(b) - F(a)$.**

For example, $P(0 < X \leq 2) = F(2) - F(0) = 1.0 - 0.3 = 0.7$. This matches the direct sum $P(X=1) + P(X=2) = 0.3 + 0.4 = 0.7$. $\checkmark$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Flow: From Experiment to Numerical Descriptors

The following Mermaid block diagrams the **complete pipeline** by which a random experiment is transformed into numerical descriptors of a discrete random variable. This is the architecture that KTU expects students to internalise for Module 1.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Random Experiment"]:::node0 --> B["Sample Space S - all outcomes"]:::node1
    B --> C["Define Random Variable X as a function from S to R"]:::node2
    C --> D["List admissible values x1 x2 ... xn"]:::node3
    D --> E["Assign probabilities p1 p2 ... pn"]:::node4
    E --> F{"Check PMF axioms p_i >= 0 and sum p_i = 1"}:::node5
    F -->|Valid| G["Construct PMF table p of x"]:::node6
    F -->|Invalid| H["Revise probability assignment"]:::node7
    G --> I["Derive CDF F of x as right-continuous step function"]:::node8
    G --> J["Compute mean mu equals E of X"]:::node9
    G --> K["Compute E of X squared"]:::node10
    K --> L["Variance sigma squared equals E of X squared minus mu squared"]:::node11
    G --> M["Construct MGF M of X of t equals sum of e to the t x_i times p_i"]:::node12
    M --> N["Differentiate r times and evaluate at t = 0 to get raw moments"]:::node13

    classDef node0 fill:#E8F0FE,stroke:#1A73E8,color:#1A1A1A
    classDef node1 fill:#FFF4E5,stroke:#F39C12,color:#1A1A1A
    classDef node2 fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#34A853,color:#1A1A1A
    classDef node3 fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#D81B60,color:#1A1A1A
    classDef node4 fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#00838F,color:#1A1A1A
    classDef node5 fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,color:#1A1A1A
    classDef node6 fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#8E24AA,color:#1A1A1A
    classDef node7 fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#C62828,color:#1A1A1A
    classDef node8 fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#6A1B9A,color:#1A1A1A
    classDef node9 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#1A1A1A
    classDef node10 fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#1565C0,color:#1A1A1A
    classDef node11 fill:#DCEDC8,stroke:#558B2F,color:#1A1A1A
    classDef node12 fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,color:#1A1A1A
    classDef node13 fill:#B2DFDB,stroke:#00695C,color:#1A1A1A
```

## 4.2 Block Architecture: Relationship Between Descriptors

The Mermaid block diagram below shows how the **PMF, CDF, MGF, Mean, and Variance** interlock as a system. Each descriptor can be computed from the PMF, and the MGF acts as a "master key" that unlocks all moments.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PMF["PMF p of x"]:::pmf --> CDF["CDF F of x"]:::cdf
    PMF --> MEAN["Mean mu"]:::mean
    PMF --> EX2["E of X squared"]:::ex2
    PMF --> MGF["MGF M of X of t"]:::mgf
    EX2 --> VAR["Variance sigma squared"]:::var
    MEAN --> VAR
    MGF --> MEAN
    MGF --> EX2
    CDF --> CHECK["Verify P of a X b equals F of b minus F of a"]:::check

    classDef pmf fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#1A1A1A
    classDef cdf fill:#F1F8E9,stroke:#33691E,color:#1A1A1A
    classDef mean fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,color:#1A1A1A
    classDef ex2 fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#AD1457,color:#1A1A1A
    classDef mgf fill:#E0F2F1,stroke:#004D40,color:#1A1A1A
    classDef var fill:#EDE7F6,stroke:#4527A0,color:#1A1A1A
    classDef check fill:#FFFDE7,stroke:#F57F17,color:#1A1A1A
```

## 4.3 Schematic: The CDF as a Step Function

The staircase nature of $F(x)$ for a discrete RV is best understood visually. The diagram below shows the working for the example in Section 3.3.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["x less than minus 1: F = 0"]:::s0 --> B["minus 1 less than or equal x less than 0: F = 0.2"]:::s1
    B --> C["0 less than or equal x less than 1: F = 0.3"]:::s2
    C --> D["1 less than or equal x less than 2: F = 0.6"]:::s3
    D --> E["x greater than or equal 2: F = 1.0"]:::s4

    classDef s0 fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#1A1A1A
    classDef s1 fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,color:#1A1A1A
    classDef s2 fill:#FFFDE7,stroke:#F57F17,color:#1A1A1A
    classDef s3 fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#1A1A1A
    classDef s4 fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#1A1A1A
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2023, Module 1]`

**Define a discrete random variable. State the two Kolmogorov axioms that its probability mass function must satisfy.**

**Model Answer:**

A **discrete random variable** $X$ is a real-valued function defined on a countable sample space $S$ of a random experiment, i.e., $X : S \to \mathbb{R}$, such that the set of values taken by $X$ is finite or countably infinite.

The probability mass function $p(x_i) = P(X = x_i)$ must satisfy:

$$p(x_i) \geq 0 \quad \text{for all } i$$

$$\sum_{i=1}^{\infty} p(x_i) = 1$$

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2022, Module 1]`

**For a discrete random variable $X$, write the definition of the moment generating function and explain how the first two raw moments are obtained from it.**

**Model Answer:**

The moment generating function (MGF) of a discrete random variable $X$ is

$$M_X(t) = E(e^{tX}) = \sum_{i} e^{t x_i} \, p(x_i), \quad t \in \mathbb{R}$$

The first raw moment is obtained by differentiating once and evaluating at $t = 0$:

$$E(X) = M_X^{\prime}(0)$$

The second raw moment is obtained by differentiating twice and evaluating at $t = 0$:

$$E(X^2) = M_X^{\prime\prime}(0)$$

In general, $E(X^r) = M_X^{(r)}(0)$, the $r$-th derivative of $M_X(t)$ evaluated at $t = 0$.

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Choice-Based)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme regulations, Part B questions carry **14 marks** with internal choice. A typical split is **7 marks + 7 marks** across two sub-parts, with the choice operating at the *complete question level* (i.e., the student answers either **Question A** in full or **Question B** in full).

### Question A `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Module 1]`

A discrete random variable $X$ has the following probability distribution:

| $x_i$ | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $p(x_i)$ | $k$ | $2k$ | $3k$ | $2k$ | $k$ |

**(a)** Find the value of $k$. Compute the mean $\mu$ and variance $\sigma^2$ of $X$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Find the cumulative distribution function $F(x)$ of $X$. Hence find $P(1 < X \leq 4)$ and $P(X \geq 2)$. Also find the MGF $M_X(t)$ of $X$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) ŌĆö Finding $k$, mean and variance.**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Apply the unit-sum axiom of the PMF.**

The sum of all probabilities must equal 1:

$$k + 2k + 3k + 2k + k = 1$$

$$9k = 1 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad k = \tfrac{1}{9}$$

So the PMF is $P(X=0) = 1/9$, $P(X=1) = 2/9$, $P(X=2) = 3/9$, $P(X=3) = 2/9$, $P(X=4) = 1/9$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute the mean $\mu = E(X)$.**

$$\mu = \sum_{i=0}^{4} x_i \, p(x_i) = 0 \cdot \tfrac{1}{9} + 1 \cdot \tfrac{2}{9} + 2 \cdot \tfrac{3}{9} + 3 \cdot \tfrac{2}{9} + 4 \cdot \tfrac{1}{9}$$

$$\mu = \tfrac{1}{9}(0 + 2 + 6 + 6 + 4) = \tfrac{18}{9} = 2$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute $E(X^2)$.**

$$E(X^2) = \sum_{i=0}^{4} x_i^{\,2} \, p(x_i) = 0 + 1 \cdot \tfrac{2}{9} + 4 \cdot \tfrac{3}{9} + 9 \cdot \tfrac{2}{9} + 16 \cdot \tfrac{1}{9}$$

$$E(X^2) = \tfrac{1}{9}(0 + 2 + 12 + 18 + 16) = \tfrac{48}{9} = \tfrac{16}{3}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compute the variance.**

$$\sigma^2 = E(X^2) - \mu^2 = \tfrac{16}{3} - 4 = \tfrac{16 - 12}{3} = \tfrac{4}{3}$$

$$\sigma = \sqrt{\tfrac{4}{3}} = \tfrac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 1.1547$$

**Part (a) Results:** $k = 1/9$, $\mu = 2$, $\sigma^2 = 4/3$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Part (b) ŌĆö CDF, probabilities, and MGF.**

**Step 5 ŌĆö Construct the CDF $F(x) = P(X \leq x)$.**

For $x < 0$, no mass is included: $F(x) = 0$.

For $0 \leq x < 1$: only $X = 0$ contributes, $F(x) = 1/9$.

For $1 \leq x < 2$: $X \in \{0, 1\}$, $F(x) = 1/9 + 2/9 = 3/9 = 1/3$.

For $2 \leq x < 3$: $X \in \{0, 1, 2\}$, $F(x) = 1/9 + 2/9 + 3/9 = 6/9 = 2/3$.

For $3 \leq x < 4$: $X \in \{0, 1, 2, 3\}$, $F(x) = 1/9 + 2/9 + 3/9 + 2/9 = 8/9$.

For $x \geq 4$: $F(x) = 1$.

$$F(x) = \begin{cases} 0, & x < 0 \\ \tfrac{1}{9}, & 0 \leq x < 1 \\ \tfrac{1}{3}, & 1 \leq x < 2 \\ \tfrac{2}{3}, & 2 \leq x < 3 \\ \tfrac{8}{9}, & 3 \leq x < 4 \\ 1, & x \geq 4 \end{cases}$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Compute $P(1 < X \leq 4)$.**

Using the CDF identity $P(a < X \leq b) = F(b) - F(a)$:

$$P(1 < X \leq 4) = F(4) - F(1) = 1 - \tfrac{1}{3} = \tfrac{2}{3}$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Compute $P(X \geq 2)$.**

Using the complement rule, $P(X \geq 2) = 1 - P(X < 2) = 1 - P(X \leq 1) = 1 - F(1)$:

$$P(X \geq 2) = 1 - \tfrac{1}{3} = \tfrac{2}{3}$$

Equivalently, summing the PMF: $P(X \geq 2) = 3/9 + 2/9 + 1/9 = 6/9 = 2/3$. $\checkmark$

**Step 8 ŌĆö Construct the MGF.**

$$M_X(t) = \sum_{i=0}^{4} e^{t x_i} \, p(x_i) = \tfrac{1}{9} + \tfrac{2}{9} e^{t} + \tfrac{3}{9} e^{2t} + \tfrac{2}{9} e^{3t} + \tfrac{1}{9} e^{4t}$$

$$M_X(t) = \tfrac{1}{9}\left(1 + 2 e^{t} + 3 e^{2t} + 2 e^{3t} + e^{4t}\right)$$

**Verification using the MGF:** $M_X^{\prime}(t) = \tfrac{1}{9}(2 e^{t} + 6 e^{2t} + 6 e^{3t} + 4 e^{4t})$, so

$$M_X^{\prime}(0) = \tfrac{1}{9}(2 + 6 + 6 + 4) = \tfrac{18}{9} = 2 = \mu \;\checkmark$$

**Part (b) Results:** CDF as above, $P(1 < X \leq 4) = 2/3$, $P(X \geq 2) = 2/3$, $M_X(t) = \tfrac{1}{9}(1 + 2 e^{t} + 3 e^{2t} + 2 e^{3t} + e^{4t})$. **(7 marks)**

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024, Module 1]`

A box contains 5 red balls and 3 white balls. Three balls are drawn **at random without replacement**. Let $X$ denote the number of red balls drawn.

**(a)** Construct the probability mass function of $X$. Find the mean $E(X)$ and the variance $\text{Var}(X)$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Find the moment generating function $M_X(t)$. Use it to verify $E(X)$ and $E(X^2)$. Also find the cumulative distribution function $F(x)$ and use it to find $P(1 \leq X \leq 2)$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) ŌĆö PMF, Mean, Variance.**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Identify the distribution.**

This is a classic **hypergeometric** setup, but for Module 1 we compute the PMF directly by combinatorial counting. Total ways to choose 3 balls from 8:

$$\binom{8}{3} = 56$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute $P(X = x)$ for $x = 0, 1, 2, 3$.**

- $P(X = 0) = \dfrac{\binom{5}{0}\binom{3}{3}}{\binom{8}{3}} = \dfrac{1 \cdot 1}{56} = \dfrac{1}{56}$
- $P(X = 1) = \dfrac{\binom{5}{1}\binom{3}{2}}{\binom{8}{3}} = \dfrac{5 \cdot 3}{56} = \dfrac{15}{56}$
- $P(X = 2) = \dfrac{\binom{5}{2}\binom{3}{1}}{\binom{8}{3}} = \dfrac{10 \cdot 3}{56} = \dfrac{30}{56}$
- $P(X = 3) = \dfrac{\binom{5}{3}\binom{3}{0}}{\binom{8}{3}} = \dfrac{10 \cdot 1}{56} = \dfrac{10}{56}$

**Verification:** $\frac{1 + 15 + 30 + 10}{56} = \frac{56}{56} = 1 \;\checkmark$

**PMF Table:**

| $x_i$ | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $p(x_i)$ | $1/56$ | $15/56$ | $30/56$ | $10/56$ |

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute the mean.**

$$E(X) = 0 \cdot \tfrac{1}{56} + 1 \cdot \tfrac{15}{56} + 2 \cdot \tfrac{30}{56} + 3 \cdot \tfrac{10}{56}$$

$$E(X) = \tfrac{1}{56}(0 + 15 + 60 + 30) = \tfrac{105}{56} = \tfrac{15}{8} = 1.875$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compute $E(X^2)$.**

$$E(X^2) = 0 + 1 \cdot \tfrac{15}{56} + 4 \cdot \tfrac{30}{56} + 9 \cdot \tfrac{10}{56}$$

$$E(X^2) = \tfrac{1}{56}(0 + 15 + 120 + 90) = \tfrac{225}{56}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Compute the variance.**

$$\text{Var}(X) = E(X^2) - \mu^2 = \tfrac{225}{56} - \left(\tfrac{15}{8}\right)^2 = \tfrac{225}{56} - \tfrac{225}{64}$$

Common denominator 448:

$$\text{Var}(X) = \tfrac{225 \cdot 8}{448} - \tfrac{225 \cdot 7}{448} = \tfrac{225}{448} \approx 0.5022$$

**Part (a) Results:** $\mu = 15/8$, $\sigma^2 = 225/448$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Part (b) ŌĆö MGF, verification, CDF, and probability.**

**Step 6 ŌĆö Construct the MGF.**

$$M_X(t) = \sum_{x=0}^{3} e^{tx} \, p(x) = \tfrac{1}{56} + \tfrac{15}{56} e^{t} + \tfrac{30}{56} e^{2t} + \tfrac{10}{56} e^{3t}$$

$$M_X(t) = \tfrac{1}{56}\left(1 + 15 e^{t} + 30 e^{2t} + 10 e^{3t}\right)$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Verify $E(X)$ via MGF.**

$$M_X^{\prime}(t) = \tfrac{1}{56}\left(15 e^{t} + 60 e^{2t} + 30 e^{3t}\right)$$

$$M_X^{\prime}(0) = \tfrac{1}{56}(15 + 60 + 30) = \tfrac{105}{56} = \tfrac{15}{8} = E(X) \;\checkmark$$

**Step 8 ŌĆö Verify $E(X^2)$ via MGF.**

$$M_X^{\prime\prime}(t) = \tfrac{1}{56}\left(15 e^{t} + 120 e^{2t} + 90 e^{3t}\right)$$

$$M_X^{\prime\prime}(0) = \tfrac{1}{56}(15 + 120 + 90) = \tfrac{225}{56} = E(X^2) \;\checkmark$$

**Step 9 ŌĆö Construct the CDF as a step function.**

$$F(x) = \begin{cases} 0, & x < 0 \\ \tfrac{1}{56}, & 0 \leq x < 1 \\ \tfrac{16}{56} = \tfrac{2}{7}, & 1 \leq x < 2 \\ \tfrac{46}{56} = \tfrac{23}{28}, & 2 \leq x < 3 \\ 1, & x \geq 3 \end{cases}$$

**Step 10 ŌĆö Compute $P(1 \leq X \leq 2)$.**

By the CDF identity, $P(1 \leq X \leq 2) = F(2) - F(0) + P(X = 1) = F(2) - F(0) + p(1)$.

Equivalently, $P(1 \leq X \leq 2) = P(X = 1) + P(X = 2) = \tfrac{15}{56} + \tfrac{30}{56} = \tfrac{45}{56}$.

Cross-check via CDF: $P(1 \leq X \leq 2) = P(X \leq 2) - P(X \leq 0) = F(2) - F(0)$ would exclude the jump at $x = 1$. We must be careful:

$P(1 \leq X \leq 2) = P(X < 1) + P(X = 1) + P(X = 2) - P(X < 1) - P(X = 1) = P(X = 1) + P(X = 2) = \tfrac{45}{56}$.

Or more cleanly: $P(1 \leq X \leq 2) = P(X \leq 2) - P(X \leq 0) = F(2) - F(0) = \tfrac{46}{56} - \tfrac{1}{56} = \tfrac{45}{56}$.

**Part (b) Results:** $M_X(t) = \tfrac{1}{56}(1 + 15 e^{t} + 30 e^{2t} + 10 e^{3t})$, $E(X) = 15/8$, $E(X^2) = 225/56$, CDF as above, $P(1 \leq X \leq 2) = 45/56$. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls ŌĆö Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the unit-sum check:** Always verify $\sum p(x_i) = 1$ *before* computing the mean. If probabilities are given in symbolic form, find the unknown constant first.
> 2. **Confusing $P(a < X \leq b)$ with $P(a \leq X \leq b)$:** The CDF identity $F(b) - F(a)$ gives $P(a < X \leq b)$. For inclusive boundaries, add the boundary mass explicitly.
> 3. **Variance formula sign errors:** Write $\sigma^2 = E(X^2) - \mu^2$ and *never* $\sigma^2 = \mu^2 - E(X^2)$. Variance is non-negative; this serves as a built-in check.
> 4. **MGF domain:** KTU exam answers require you to state that $M_X(t)$ is defined for all $t \in \mathbb{R}$ (or in some neighbourhood of $0$) for the series to converge. Lose a mark if you omit this.
> 5. **CDF continuity:** The CDF of a discrete RV is *right-continuous*. Use $\leq$ on the left, $<$ on the right of each interval. Writing $0 \leq x < 1$ versus $0 < x \leq 1$ changes the boundary behaviour.
> 6. **LOTUS misuse:** Do **not** attempt to find the PMF of $Y = g(X)$ when asked for $E[g(X)]$. Apply LOTUS directly on $X$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Random Variable:** A real-valued function on the sample space; deterministic map, random input.
- **Discrete vs Continuous:** Discrete RVs take countable values; described by PMF. Continuous RVs take uncountable values; described by PDF.
- **PMF axioms:** $p(x_i) \geq 0$ and $\sum p(x_i) = 1$ ŌĆö *always* check both.
- **CDF properties:** $0 \leq F(x) \leq 1$, non-decreasing, right-continuous, $F(-\infty) = 0$, $F(+\infty) = 1$.
- **Probability from CDF:** $P(a < X \leq b) = F(b) - F(a)$.
- **Mean:** $\mu = E(X) = \sum x_i \, p(x_i)$ ŌĆö a probability-weighted average.
- **LOTUS:** $E[g(X)] = \sum g(x_i) \, p(x_i)$ ŌĆö no need to derive the PMF of $Y = g(X)$.
- **Variance identity:** $\sigma^2 = E(X^2) - \mu^2$ ŌĆö the most-used formula in Module 1.
- **Standard deviation:** $\sigma = \sqrt{\sigma^2} \geq 0$.
- **MGF:** $M_X(t) = \sum e^{t x_i} p(x_i)$ with $M_X(0) = 1$ and $E(X^r) = M_X^{(r)}(0)$.
- **Higher moments:** Skewness $\gamma_1 = E[(X-\mu)^3] / \sigma^3$, Kurtosis $\gamma_2 = E[(X-\mu)^4] / \sigma^4$.
- **Symmetry check:** A discrete distribution is symmetric about $\mu$ if and only if $p(\mu - a) = p(\mu + a)$ for all admissible $a$.
- **Engineering relevance:** Discrete distributions underpin quality control (binomial), reliability (geometric), queueing (Poisson), and sampling (hypergeometric) ŌĆö all foundational for the engineering sciences.
- **Exam tactic:** In every KTU Module 1 problem, follow the *pipeline* ŌĆö PMF ŌåÆ CDF ŌåÆ Mean ŌåÆ Variance ŌåÆ MGF ŌåÆ Higher moments. The order earns you method marks even if a later computation is slightly off.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cumulative Distribution Function
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GCMAT401 - MATHEMATICS FOR PHYSICAL SCIENCE AND LIFE SCIENCE ŌĆō 4
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Cumulative Distribution Function

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

Let $(\Omega, \mathcal{F}, P)$ be a probability space and let $X : \Omega \to \mathbb{R}$ be a real-valued random variable. The **Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF)** of $X$, denoted by $F_X(x)$, is defined for every real number $x \in \mathbb{R}$ as:

$$F_X(x) \;=\; P(X \le x) \;=\; P\bigl(\{\omega \in \Omega : X(\omega) \le x\}\bigr)$$

The CDF is the **unique** function that completely characterises the probability law of a real random variable. Knowing $F_X(x)$ for all $x \in \mathbb{R}$ is mathematically equivalent to knowing the entire probability distribution of $X$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why CDF is "cumulative"**: The word *cumulative* means *running total up to x*. The CDF accumulates all probability mass lying in the interval $(-\infty, x]$. As $x$ increases, the CDF never decreases ŌĆö it either stays flat or rises.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English)

Imagine a water tank of total capacity **1 litre** sitting on a tilted table. The water always settles at the bottom (left side). As you move a vertical scanning line from the far **left** of the tank towards the far **right**, the scanning line "captures" more and more water. The amount of water captured to the *left* of the line at any horizontal position $x$ is exactly the value $F_X(x)$.

| Position of the scanning line | Water captured (CDF value) | Intuition |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Far left ($x \to -\infty$) | $0$ litres | Nothing captured yet |
| Somewhere in the middle | A fraction between 0 and 1 | Partial capture |
| Far right ($x \to +\infty$) | $1$ litre (the full tank) | All water captured |

- For a **discrete** random variable: the tank has *step-like ledges* where water pools ŌĆö each ledge corresponds to a point mass.
- For a **continuous** random variable: the tank has a *smoothly rising slope* ŌĆö the density $f(x)$ is the slope of the tank at that point.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight**: The CDF is the *most fundamental* object in probability theory. All other descriptors ŌĆö the PMF for discrete variables, the PDF for continuous variables, the probability of any interval ŌĆö can be derived from the CDF.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Boundary Values

For every well-defined CDF, the following two boundary values are **mandatory** and **must be remembered verbatim** for the KTU board exam:

$$\lim_{x \to -\infty} F_X(x) \;=\; 0$$

$$\lim_{x \to +\infty} F_X(x) \;=\; 1$$

These represent the **total probability axiom** ŌĆö the sum of all probability across the real line equals **1** (i.e., 100% of outcomes are accounted for).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Cardinal Properties of a CDF (Theorem)

A function $F : \mathbb{R} \to [0, 1]$ is a valid cumulative distribution function **if and only if** it satisfies all four properties below. This is a classic KTU board question and is often asked for **3 marks** as a short answer.

**Property P1 ŌĆö Non-decreasing (Monotone Non-decreasing):**
For every $x_1 < x_2$, we must have $F(x_1) \le F(x_2)$.

*Why this is true:* Since $\{X \le x_1\} \subseteq \{X \le x_2\}$, by monotonicity of probability measures $P(A) \le P(B)$ whenever $A \subseteq B$.

**Property P2 ŌĆö Right-Continuity:**
$F$ is continuous from the right at every point $x$, i.e.,
$$\lim_{h \downarrow 0} F(x + h) \;=\; F(x) \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad F(x^+) = F(x).$$

*Why this is true:* Probability measures are continuous from above on decreasing sequences of sets. Taking $\{X \le x + 1/n\} \downarrow \{X \le x\}$ yields right-continuity.

> [!WARNING]
> A common student mistake is to confuse *right-continuous* with *left-continuous*. For CDFs, it is **right-continuity** that is required, not left-continuity. The limit from the **left** $F(x^-)$ may or may not equal $F(x)$ ŌĆö it equals $F(x)$ if and only if $X$ is continuous at $x$.

**Property P3 ŌĆö Limit at Negative Infinity:**
$$\lim_{x \to -\infty} F(x) \;=\; 0$$

**Property P4 ŌĆö Limit at Positive Infinity:**
$$\lim_{x \to +\infty} F(x) \;=\; 1$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Pearl**: If any function $F$ is given and the examiner asks "is $F$ a valid CDF?", the student must verify *all four* properties. Omitting even one (typically P2) costs full marks.

## 2.2 Probability of an Interval via CDF

One of the most useful applications of the CDF is computing probabilities of arbitrary intervals:

| Interval of interest | Formula in terms of $F$ |
| :--- | :--- |
| $P(a < X \le b)$ | $F(b) - F(a)$ |
| $P(a \le X \le b)$ | $F(b) - F(a^-)$ |
| $P(a \le X < b)$ | $F(b^-) - F(a^-)$ |
| $P(a < X < b)$ | $F(b^-) - F(a)$ |
| $P(X > a)$ | $1 - F(a)$ |
| $P(X \ge a)$ | $1 - F(a^-)$ |
| $P(X = a)$ | $F(a) - F(a^-)$ |

> [!NOTE]
> For **continuous** random variables, $F(a) = F(a^-)$, so the distinctions above collapse and the simple formula $P(a < X \le b) = F(b) - F(a)$ works for all interval types.

## 2.3 CDF for Discrete Random Variables

If $X$ is discrete with probability mass function (PMF) $p_X(k) = P(X = k)$ taking values in a countable set $\{x_1, x_2, x_3, \ldots\}$ (where $x_1 < x_2 < x_3 < \cdots$), the CDF is a **right-continuous step function**:

$$F_X(x) \;=\; P(X \le x) \;=\; \sum_{k :\, x_k \le x} p_X(k)$$

The CDF jumps at each value $x_k$ by an amount equal to $p_X(x_k)$, i.e., the size of the jump is:

$$F(x_k) - F(x_k^-) \;=\; p_X(x_k) \;=\; P(X = x_k)$$

Between jumps, the CDF remains **constant** (flat).

## 2.4 CDF for Continuous Random Variables

If $X$ is absolutely continuous with probability density function (PDF) $f_X(t)$, the CDF is given by the integral from $-\infty$ up to $x$:

$$F_X(x) \;=\; \int_{-\infty}^{x} f_X(t)\, dt$$

By the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, at every point $x$ where $f_X$ is continuous:

$$\frac{d}{dx} F_X(x) \;=\; f_X(x)$$

The CDF of a continuous random variable is a **continuous, non-decreasing** function that is differentiable almost everywhere.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **complete high-yield formula sheet** for the topic *Cumulative Distribution Function* in KTU 2024 scheme. Memorise this table ŌĆö every line is a potential exam question.

| Concept | Mathematical Expression | Notes / Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Definition of CDF | $F_X(x) = P(X \le x)$ | Range of $F$ is $[0, 1]$; no units |
| Non-decreasing | $x_1 < x_2 \Rightarrow F(x_1) \le F(x_2)$ | Monotonicity property |
| Right-continuity | $F(x^+) = F(x)$ | Always true for any CDF |
| Limit at $-\infty$ | $F(-\infty) = 0$ | Total probability below $-\infty$ is zero |
| Limit at $+\infty$ | $F(+\infty) = 1$ | Total probability equals 1 (certainty) |
| Interval probability | $P(a < X \le b) = F(b) - F(a)$ | Most-used formula in board problems |
| Tail probability | $P(X > a) = 1 - F(a)$ | Complement rule applied to CDF |
| Point mass | $P(X = a) = F(a) - F(a^-)$ | Jump size at $x = a$ |
| Discrete CDF | $F(x) = \sum_{k : x_k \le x} p_X(k)$ | Step function; right-continuous |
| Continuous CDF | $F(x) = \int_{-\infty}^{x} f(t)\, dt$ | Smooth (differentiable) function |
| PDF from CDF | $f(x) = \dfrac{d}{dx} F(x)$ | At points of continuity of $f$ |
| CDF of $-X$ | $F_{-X}(x) = 1 - F_X(-x^-)$ | Symmetry / transformation rule |
| CDF of $aX + b$ | $F_{aX+b}(x) = F_X\!\left(\dfrac{x - b}{a}\right)$ for $a > 0$ | Sign of $a$ matters |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Reliability Engineering & Survival Analysis**: The CDF gives the *failure probability* up to time $t$, while $1 - F(t)$ (the *survival function*) gives the probability of survival beyond $t$. Critical in warranty analysis, MTBF calculations, and biomedical device reliability.
- **Signal Processing & Communication**: The CDF of a noise distribution is used to compute *bit error rates* (BER) in digital communication. The Gaussian CDF (Q-function) is fundamental in this context.
- **Quality Control & Six Sigma**: Process capability indices and control charts use CDF values to determine defect rates.
- **Risk Analysis in Finance**: Value-at-Risk (VaR) calculations directly use the inverse CDF (quantile function) of the return distribution.
- **Physics & Life Sciences**: The CDF of a particle's energy distribution, reaction time distribution, or species population distribution are all standard usage points.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exhaustive-Detail Mandate**: Every algebraic step, every inequality, and every logical transition is shown below. No step is skipped or hand-waved.

## 3.1 Derivation: Probability of an Open Interval in Terms of the CDF

**Goal:** Show that for any $a < b$,
$$P(a < X \le b) \;=\; F(b) - F(a).$$

**Step 1 (Decompose the event).** The event $\{X \le b\}$ can be partitioned into two disjoint sub-events:
$$\{X \le b\} \;=\; \{X \le a\} \cup \{a < X \le b\}.$$
The events $\{X \le a\}$ and $\{a < X \le b\}$ are **mutually exclusive** (disjoint), and their union is the event $\{X \le b\}$.

**Step 2 (Apply additivity of probability).** Since the two events are disjoint, finite additivity gives:
$$P(X \le b) \;=\; P(X \le a) + P(a < X \le b).$$

**Step 3 (Recognise the CDFs).** By the definition of the CDF:
$$P(X \le b) = F(b) \quad \text{and} \quad P(X \le a) = F(a).$$

**Step 4 (Solve for the target probability).** Substituting into Step 2:
$$F(b) \;=\; F(a) + P(a < X \le b).$$

Rearranging:
$$P(a < X \le b) \;=\; F(b) - F(a). \qquad \blacksquare$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the *single most important identity* in the entire topic. The KTU board examiner expects students to reproduce this derivation cleanly for full marks in any 7 or 14 mark question.

## 3.2 Derivation: Monotonicity (Property P1)

**Goal:** Show that $x_1 < x_2 \Rightarrow F(x_1) \le F(x_2).$

**Step 1.** Consider the events $A = \{X \le x_1\}$ and $B = \{X \le x_2\}$. Since $x_1 < x_2$, every outcome satisfying $X \le x_1$ also satisfies $X \le x_2$. Therefore:
$$A \;=\; \{\omega : X(\omega) \le x_1\} \;\subseteq\; \{\omega : X(\omega) \le x_2\} \;=\; B.$$

**Step 2.** Apply the **monotonicity property of probability measures**: if $A \subseteq B$ then $P(A) \le P(B)$. Hence:
$$F(x_1) = P(A) \;\le\; P(B) = F(x_2). \qquad \blacksquare$$

## 3.3 Derivation: CDF of a Discrete Random Variable (Full Worked Example)

**Problem:** A discrete random variable $X$ has PMF:
$$P(X = -1) = 0.2, \quad P(X = 0) = 0.5, \quad P(X = 2) = 0.3.$$
Find the CDF $F_X(x)$ and verify the four properties.

**Step 1 (Sort the support points in ascending order).** The support is $\{-1, 0, 2\}$ with $-1 < 0 < 2$.

**Step 2 (Build the CDF piecewise).** For $x < -1$, no probability mass lies at or below $x$, so:
$$F_X(x) = 0 \quad \text{for } x < -1.$$

For $-1 \le x < 0$, only the point mass at $-1$ has been accumulated:
$$F_X(x) = 0.2 \quad \text{for } -1 \le x < 0.$$

For $0 \le x < 2$, the point masses at $-1$ and $0$ have been accumulated:
$$F_X(x) = 0.2 + 0.5 = 0.7 \quad \text{for } 0 \le x < 2.$$

For $x \ge 2$, all three point masses have been accumulated:
$$F_X(x) = 0.2 + 0.5 + 0.3 = 1.0 \quad \text{for } x \ge 2.$$

**Step 3 (Write the final piecewise definition).**
$$F_X(x) = \begin{cases} 0, & x < -1, \\ 0.2, & -1 \le x < 0, \\ 0.7, & 0 \le x < 2, \\ 1.0, & x \ge 2. \end{cases}$$

**Step 4 (Verify the four properties).**
- **P1 (Non-decreasing):** The values $0 \le 0.2 \le 0.7 \le 1.0$ are non-decreasing. Ō£ō
- **P2 (Right-continuity):** At $x = -1$, $F(-1) = 0.2 = F(-1^+)$ ŌĆö closed from above. At $x = 0$, $F(0) = 0.7 = F(0^+)$. At $x = 2$, $F(2) = 1.0 = F(2^+)$. Ō£ō
- **P3 (Limit at $-\infty$):** $F(-\infty) = 0$. Ō£ō
- **P4 (Limit at $+\infty$):** $F(+\infty) = 1.0$. Ō£ō

**Step 5 (Compute a probability).** As a sanity check, compute $P(-1 < X \le 2)$ using the formula:
$$P(-1 < X \le 2) = F(2) - F(-1) = 1.0 - 0.2 = 0.8.$$
Direct verification: $P(X = 0) + P(X = 2) = 0.5 + 0.3 = 0.8$. Ō£ō

## 3.4 Derivation: Recovering the PDF from the CDF (Continuous Case)

**Problem:** Suppose the CDF of a continuous random variable $X$ is given by
$$F(x) = \begin{cases} 0, & x < 0, \\ \dfrac{x^2}{4}, & 0 \le x \le 2, \\ 1, & x > 2. \end{cases}$$
Find the PDF $f(x)$ and verify that $f$ is a valid density.

**Step 1 (Differentiate the CDF on each interior piece).** The relationship $f(x) = F'(x)$ holds wherever $F$ is differentiable, which on the interior $(0, 2)$ is:
$$f(x) = \frac{d}{dx}\!\left(\frac{x^2}{4}\right) = \frac{2x}{4} = \frac{x}{2}, \quad \text{for } 0 < x < 2.$$

**Step 2 (Set $f = 0$ outside the support).** Outside the interval $[0, 2]$, the CDF is constant, so the PDF is zero:
$$f(x) = 0, \quad \text{for } x < 0 \text{ or } x > 2.$$

**Step 3 (Define $f$ at the boundary points).** A single point contributes zero measure, so $f(0)$ and $f(2)$ can be defined as anything (conventionally $0$).

**Step 4 (Final piecewise form).**
$$f(x) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{x}{2}, & 0 \le x \le 2, \\ 0, & \text{otherwise}. \end{cases}$$

**Step 5 (Verify it is a valid density).**
- **Non-negative:** $f(x) = x/2 \ge 0$ for $x \in [0, 2]$. Ō£ō
- **Integrates to 1:**
$$\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} f(x)\, dx \;=\; \int_{0}^{2} \frac{x}{2}\, dx \;=\; \left[\frac{x^2}{4}\right]_0^2 \;=\; \frac{4}{4} - 0 \;=\; 1. \; \checkmark$$

**Step 6 (Cross-verify using CDF reconstruction).** Reconstruct $F(1)$ via the integral:
$$F(1) = \int_{0}^{1} \frac{x}{2}\, dx = \left[\frac{x^2}{4}\right]_0^1 = \frac{1}{4} = 0.25.$$
Directly from the given CDF: $F(1) = 1^2 / 4 = 0.25$. Ō£ō

## 3.5 Derivation: $P(X > a) = 1 - F(a)$ (Tail Probability)

**Step 1.** The sample space partitions into two disjoint events:
$$\Omega \;=\; \{X \le a\} \cup \{X > a\}.$$
These are disjoint and cover the entire sample space.

**Step 2.** Therefore:
$$P(\Omega) \;=\; P(X \le a) + P(X > a).$$

**Step 3.** Since $P(\Omega) = 1$ and $P(X \le a) = F(a)$:
$$1 \;=\; F(a) + P(X > a).$$

**Step 4.** Solve:
$$P(X > a) \;=\; 1 - F(a). \qquad \blacksquare$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Careful**: The formula $P(X \ge a) = 1 - F(a^-)$ is *not* the same as $1 - F(a)$. For continuous random variables the two coincide; for discrete variables the difference equals $P(X = a)$.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Worked Problem Bank

The table below lists *canonical* problem types on CDF that appear in KTU examinations, each with the appropriate formula and a one-line solution strategy.

| # | Problem Type | Key Formula | Solution Strategy |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Given PMF, find CDF | $F(x) = \sum_{k \le x} p_X(k)$ | Sort support, accumulate masses |
| 2 | Given CDF, find PMF | $p_X(k) = F(k) - F(k^-)$ | Compute the jumps |
| 3 | Given PDF, find CDF | $F(x) = \int_{-\infty}^{x} f(t)\, dt$ | Integrate the density |
| 4 | Given CDF, find PDF | $f(x) = dF/dx$ | Differentiate (piecewise) |
| 5 | $P(a < X \le b)$ | $F(b) - F(a)$ | Two CDF evaluations and subtract |
| 6 | $P(X > a)$ | $1 - F(a)$ | Complement via $F$ |
| 7 | $P(\vert X \vert \le c)$ | $F(c) - F(-c^-)$ | Symmetric interval probability |
| 8 | Verify $F$ is valid CDF | All 4 properties | Check monotonicity, right-continuity, limits |
| 9 | $F_{aX+b}(x)$ | $F_X\!\left(\dfrac{x-b}{a}\right)$ for $a > 0$ | Linear transformation rule |
| 10 | $F_{X^2}(x)$ for $x \ge 0$ | $F_X(\sqrt{x}) - F_X(-\sqrt{x})$ | Monotone transformation of $X$ |

The diagram below illustrates the *logical pipeline* connecting a random experiment to its cumulative distribution function, and from the CDF to the other probability descriptors.

    B --> C["Random Variable X mapping Omega to R"]
    C --> D["Probability Law of X"]
    D --> E["Cumulative Distribution Function F of x"]
    E --> F1["PMF for discrete case jumps"]
    E --> F2["PDF derivative of F for continuous case"]
    E --> F3["Interval probabilities via F"]
    E --> F4["Tail probabilities 1 minus F of a"]
    E --> F5["Quantile function inverse of F"]
    F1 --> G1["Step function right continuous"]
    F2 --> G2["Smooth non negative density"]
    F3 --> G3["Event probabilities"]
    F4 --> G4["Survival function"]
    F5 --> G5["Median quartiles VaR"]
    E --> H["Complete characterisation of X"]
```

    end

```
