<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE] These are direct short-answer conceptual/definition questions as per the KTU 2024 Part A format.

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Question, 3 Marks]**
**Define the joint probability mass function of two discrete random variables. State its two essential properties.**

**Model Answer:**

The joint PMF of two discrete random variables $X$ and $Y$ is defined as:
$$p_{X,Y}(x, y) = P(X = x \text{ and } Y = y)$$

It assigns a non-negative probability to every pair of values $(x, y)$.

**Two essential properties:**
1. **Non-negativity:** $p_{X,Y}(x, y) \geq 0$ for all $(x, y) \in \mathbb{R}^2$.
2. **Unity of total probability:** $\displaystyle \sum_{x} \sum_{y} p_{X,Y}(x, y) = 1$.

**(Note: A complete answer should also include the third property ŌĆö probability of a region ŌĆö for a 3-mark full score.)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Question, 3 Marks]**
**How is the marginal PMF of $X$ obtained from the joint PMF $p_{X,Y}(x, y)$? Write the formula and explain the underlying principle.**

**Model Answer:**

The marginal PMF of $X$ is obtained by **summing the joint PMF over all possible values of $Y$**:

$$p_X(x) = \sum_{y} p_{X,Y}(x, y)$$

**Underlying Principle:** This follows from the **Law of Total Probability**. The event $\{X = x\}$ is the union of mutually exclusive events $\{X = x, Y = y_j\}$ for all values $y_j$ in the support of $Y$. Hence, we add up the joint probabilities to obtain the marginal probability.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Each 14-mark question has sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying 7 marks. Students choose ONE of TWO alternatives.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Question, 14 Marks]**

The joint PMF of two discrete random variables $X$ and $Y$ is given by:
$$p_{X,Y}(x, y) = \frac{(x + 2y)}{k} \quad \text{for } x = 1, 2 \text{ and } y = 1, 2, 3$$
and is zero elsewhere.

**(a) [7 Marks]** Find the value of $k$ and the marginal PMFs of $X$ and $Y$. Also compute $P(X = Y)$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Find the conditional PMF $p_{X \mid Y}(x \mid 2)$ and determine whether $X$ and $Y$ are independent.

---

#### **Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a) ŌĆö Finding $k$, Marginals, and $P(X = Y)$**

**Step 1: Apply the total probability condition.**

The sum of the joint PMF over the support must equal 1:

$$\sum_{x=1}^{2} \sum_{y=1}^{3} \frac{(x + 2y)}{k} = 1$$

$$\frac{1}{k} \sum_{x=1}^{2} \sum_{y=1}^{3} (x + 2y) = 1$$

**Step 2: Compute the double sum.**

Inner sum over $y$ (for fixed $x$):
$$\sum_{y=1}^{3} (x + 2y) = 3x + 2(1 + 2 + 3) = 3x + 12$$

Outer sum over $x$:
$$\sum_{x=1}^{2} (3x + 12) = (3 + 12) + (6 + 12) = 15 + 18 = 33$$

So:
$$\frac{33}{k} = 1 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \boxed{k = 33}$$

**Step 3: Construct the joint PMF table.**

| $X \backslash Y$ | $y = 1$ | $y = 2$ | $y = 3$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| $x = 1$ | $\frac{3}{33}$ | $\frac{5}{33}$ | $\frac{7}{33}$ |
| $x = 2$ | $\frac{4}{33}$ | $\frac{6}{33}$ | $\frac{8}{33}$ |

**Step 4: Find marginals by row/column sums.**

$$p_X(1) = \frac{3 + 5 + 7}{33} = \frac{15}{33} = \frac{5}{11}$$

$$p_X(2) = \frac{4 + 6 + 8}{33} = \frac{18}{33} = \frac{6}{11}$$

$$p_Y(1) = \frac{3 + 4}{33} = \frac{7}{33}$$

$$p_Y(2) = \frac{5 + 6}{33} = \frac{11}{33} = \frac{1}{3}$$

$$p_Y(3) = \frac{7 + 8}{33} = \frac{15}{33} = \frac{5}{11}$$

**Step 5: Compute $P(X = Y)$.**

From the support, the only pairs with $x = y$ are: $x = 1, y = 1$ and $x = 2, y = 2$ (since $x \in \{1, 2\}$ and $y \in \{1, 2, 3\}$).

$$P(X = Y) = p_{X,Y}(1, 1) + p_{X,Y}(2, 2) = \frac{3}{33} + \frac{6}{33} = \frac{9}{33} = \boxed{\frac{3}{11}}$$

---

**Part (b) ŌĆö Conditional PMF and Independence Test**

**Step 1: Compute $p_{X \mid Y}(x \mid 2)$ using $p_Y(2) = 11/33$.**

$$p_{X \mid Y}(1 \mid 2) = \frac{p_{X,Y}(1, 2)}{p_Y(2)} = \frac{5/33}{11/33} = \frac{5}{11}$$

$$p_{X \mid Y}(2 \mid 2) = \frac{p_{X,Y}(2, 2)}{p_Y(2)} = \frac{6/33}{11/33} = \frac{6}{11}$$

**Verification:** $\frac{5}{11} + \frac{6}{11} = \frac{11}{11} = 1$ Ō£ō

**Step 2: Test for Independence.**

We need to check if $p_{X,Y}(x, y) = p_X(x) \cdot p_Y(y)$ for all $(x, y)$. Test the pair $(1, 1)$:

$$p_{X,Y}(1, 1) = \frac{3}{33} = \frac{1}{11}$$

$$p_X(1) \cdot p_Y(1) = \frac{5}{11} \cdot \frac{7}{33} = \frac{35}{363}$$

Convert $1/11$ to a common denominator:
$$\frac{1}{11} = \frac{33}{363}$$

Since $\frac{33}{363} \neq \frac{35}{363}$:

$$\boxed{p_{X,Y}(1, 1) \neq p_X(1) \cdot p_Y(1) \quad \Longrightarrow \quad X \text{ and } Y \text{ are NOT independent}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks ŌĆö Alternative Choice)**

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Question, 14 Marks]**

The joint PMF of two discrete random variables $X$ and $Y$ is given by:
$$p_{X,Y}(x, y) = \frac{xy}{C} \quad \text{for } x = 1, 2, 3 \text{ and } y = 1, 2, 3$$
and is zero otherwise.

**(a) [7 Marks]** Find the value of $C$. Hence find $P(X + Y \leq 4)$ and $P(X \cdot Y \geq 4)$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Find the marginal PMFs of $X$ and $Y$ and check whether $X$ and $Y$ are independent.

---

#### **Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a) ŌĆö Finding $C$ and Probabilities**

**Step 1: Apply $\sum \sum p = 1$.**

$$\sum_{x=1}^{3} \sum_{y=1}^{3} \frac{xy}{C} = 1 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \frac{1}{C} \sum_{x=1}^{3} x \sum_{y=1}^{3} y = 1$$

Compute the sums:
$$\sum_{x=1}^{3} x = 1 + 2 + 3 = 6, \quad \sum_{y=1}^{3} y = 1 + 2 + 3 = 6$$

Therefore:
$$\frac{1}{C} \cdot 6 \cdot 6 = \frac{36}{C} = 1 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \boxed{C = 36}$$

**Step 2: Enumerate the support for $P(X + Y \leq 4)$.**

Pairs $(x, y)$ with $x, y \in \{1, 2, 3\}$ and $x + y \leq 4$:

| Pair $(x, y)$ | $x + y$ | $xy$ | Probability $\frac{xy}{36}$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| $(1, 1)$ | 2 | 1 | $\frac{1}{36}$ |
| $(1, 2)$ | 3 | 2 | $\frac{2}{36}$ |
| $(1, 3)$ | 4 | 3 | $\frac{3}{36}$ |
| $(2, 1)$ | 3 | 2 | $\frac{2}{36}$ |
| $(2, 2)$ | 4 | 4 | $\frac{4}{36}$ |
| $(3, 1)$ | 4 | 3 | $\frac{3}{36}$ |

$$P(X + Y \leq 4) = \frac{1 + 2 + 3 + 2 + 4 + 3}{36} = \frac{15}{36} = \boxed{\frac{5}{12}}$$

**Step 3: Enumerate the support for $P(X \cdot Y \geq 4)$.**

Pairs $(x, y)$ with $x, y \in \{1, 2, 3\}$ and $xy \geq 4$:

| Pair $(x, y)$ | $xy$ | Probability |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| $(2, 2)$ | 4 | $\frac{4}{36}$ |
| $(2, 3)$ | 6 | $\frac{6}{36}$ |
| $(3, 2)$ | 6 | $\frac{6}{36}$ |
| $(3, 3)$ | 9 | $\frac{9}{36}$ |

$$P(X \cdot Y \geq 4) = \frac{4 + 6 + 6 + 9}{36} = \frac{25}{36}$$

---

**Part (b) ŌĆö Marginals and Independence**

**Step 1: Marginal of $X$.**

$$p_X(x) = \sum_{y=1}^{3} \frac{xy}{36} = \frac{x}{36} \sum_{y=1}^{3} y = \frac{x \cdot 6}{36} = \frac{x}{6}$$

So:
$$p_X(1) = \frac{1}{6}, \quad p_X(2) = \frac{2}{6} = \frac{1}{3}, \quad p_X(3) = \frac{3}{6} = \frac{1}{2}$$

**Verification:** $\frac{1}{6} + \frac{2}{6} + \frac{3}{6} = \frac{6}{6} = 1$ Ō£ō

**Step 2: Marginal of $Y$.** By symmetry of the joint PMF in $x$ and $y$:

$$p_Y(y) = \frac{y}{6}$$

So:
$$p_Y(1) = \frac{1}{6}, \quad p_Y(2) = \frac{1}{3}, \quad p_Y(3) = \frac{1}{2}$$

**Step 3: Independence Test.**

For any $(x, y)$:
$$p_X(x) \cdot p_Y(y) = \frac{x}{6} \cdot \frac{y}{6} = \frac{xy}{36} = p_{X,Y}(x, y) \checkmark$$

Since the product of marginals equals the joint PMF for **every pair** $(x, y)$:

$$\boxed{X \text{ and } Y \text{ are INDEPENDENT}}$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams (Lose 2ŌĆō3 Marks per Mistake):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the constant $k$** ŌĆö Many students compute the double sum and forget to invert it. Always end with $k = \dfrac{1}{\text{double sum}}$.
> 2. **Arithmetic errors in summation** ŌĆö When $p(x, y) = k(x + y)$ or $k(xy)$, students often make errors in expanding. **Always write out each term explicitly** as shown in Section 3.1.
> 3. **Independence test misuse** ŌĆö To prove **dependence**, ONE counter-example is enough. To prove **independence**, you must check **ALL** pairs in the support. Many students incorrectly claim dependence after checking only one pair successfully.
> 4. **Marginal sum verification skipped** ŌĆö KTU examiners reward students who verify that $\sum p_X(x) = 1$ and $\sum p_Y(y) = 1$ at the end. This is worth 1 mark as a sanity check.
> 5. **Confusing conditional with marginal** ŌĆö $p_{X \mid Y}(x \mid y)$ is **NOT** the same as $p_X(x)$. Conditional PMF depends on the *given* value of $Y$.
> 6. **Wrong domain** ŌĆö When computing $P(X > Y)$, list out each qualifying pair explicitly. Do not just "estimate" or "skip" pairs.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE] **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist ŌĆö Pin This Before the Exam!**

- Ō£ģ **Joint PMF Definition:** $p_{X,Y}(x, y) = P(X = x, Y = y)$.
- Ō£ģ **Two Cardinal Properties:** $p_{X,Y}(x, y) \geq 0$ AND $\sum_x \sum_y p_{X,Y}(x, y) = 1$.
- Ō£ģ **Marginal of $X$:** $p_X(x) = \sum_y p_{X,Y}(x, y)$ (sum across rows in the joint table).
- Ō£ģ **Marginal of $Y$:** $p_Y(y) = \sum_x p_{X,Y}(x, y)$ (sum down columns in the joint table).
- Ō£ģ **Marginal Verification:** Always check that each marginal sums to 1.
- Ō£ģ **Conditional PMF:** $p_{X \mid Y}(x \mid y) = \dfrac{p_{X,Y}(x, y)}{p_Y(y)}$, valid only if $p_Y(y) > 0$.
- Ō£ģ **Multiplication Rule:** $p_{X,Y}(x, y) = p_{X \mid Y}(x \mid y) \cdot p_Y(y)$.
- Ō£ģ **Independence Criterion:** $X \perp Y \iff p_{X,Y}(x, y) = p_X(x) \cdot p_Y(y) \,\, \forall (x, y)$.
- Ō£ģ **Dependence Proof:** One counter-example pair is sufficient.
- Ō£ģ **Region Probability:** $P((X, Y) \in A) = \sum_{(x,y) \in A} p_{X,Y}(x, y)$.
- Ō£ģ **Joint CDF:** $F_{X,Y}(x, y) = \sum_{x_i \leq x} \sum_{y_j \leq y} p_{X,Y}(x_i, y_j)$.
- Ō£ģ **Joint PMF from Joint CDF:** $p_{X,Y}(x, y) = \Delta_{x} \Delta_{y} F_{X,Y}(x, y)$ (mixed difference).
- Ō£ģ **Step-by-Step Method for KTU Problems:**
   1. Find $k$ from $\sum\sum p = 1$.
   2. Build the joint PMF table.
   3. Compute marginals by row/column sums.
   4. Compute region probabilities by summing qualifying cells.
   5. Compute conditional PMFs using $p_{Y}(y)$ in denominator.
   6. Test independence by checking the product formula.
- Ō£ģ **Final Verification Trick:** All marginals and conditionals **must sum to 1**. If not, an arithmetic error exists.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Marginal PMF
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** GCMAT401 - MATHEMATICS FOR PHYSICAL SCIENCE AND LIFE SCIENCE ŌĆō 4 > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Marginal PMF # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition: Marginal Probability Mass Function (Marginal PMF) Consider two discrete random variables **X** and **Y** defined on a common probability space, with **Joint PMF** denoted by $p_{X,Y}(x,y)$ over the sample space $\{(x,y) : x \in S_X,\ y \in S_Y\}$. > [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Formal Definition (Marginal PMF)** > > The **Marginal PMF** of a discrete random variable $X$, obtained from the joint PMF $p_{X,Y}(x,y)$, is defined as the sum of the joint probabilities over all admissible values of the *other* variable $Y$: > > $$p_X(x) = \sum_{y \in S_Y} p_{X,Y}(x, y), \quad \text{for all } x \in S_X$$ > > Similarly, the marginal PMF of $Y$ is: > > $$p_Y(y) = \sum_{x \in S_X} p_{X,Y}(x, y), \quad \text{for all } y \in S_Y$$ The terminology **"marginal"** originates from the practice of writing joint probability values inside a two-way table (contingency table) and then summing each row and column ŌĆö the resulting totals are physically placed in the **margins** (borders) of the table. --- ## 1.2 Pre-Requisite: Joint PMF Recap For discrete random variables $X$ and $Y$: $$p_{X,Y}(x,y) = P(X = x,\ Y = y)$$ with two mandatory properties: 1. $0 \le p_{X,Y}(x,y) \le 1$ for every $(x,y)$ 2. $\sum_{x \in S_X} \sum_{y \in S_Y} p_{X,Y}(x,y) = 1$ The joint PMF gives a **complete probabilistic description** of the pair $(X,Y)$, while the marginal PMF gives a *partial* description of $X$ or $Y$ alone. --- ## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy ŌĆö The "Birthday-Party Survey" > [!NOTE] **Real-World Analogy: Eye Colour vs. Hair Colour Survey** > > Imagine surveying **500 students** for two attributes ŌĆö *Eye Colour* (Blue, Brown) and *Hair Colour* (Black, Brown, Blonde). You build a 2├Ś3 contingency table where each cell holds the *number* (or probability) of students having that specific combination. > > Now, suppose a researcher asks: *"What is the probability that a randomly picked student has **Blue eyes**, regardless of hair colour?"* > > You simply **add up the entire Blue-eye column**, ignoring the hair-colour split. That sum is exactly the **marginal PMF of Eye Colour**. The hair-colour information is "marginalised out." > > The marginal PMF is, therefore, the **shadow** that the joint distribution casts when projected onto a single axis ŌĆö losing one dimension of detail but retaining all probabilistic mass. --- ## 1.4 Engineering Significance Marginal PMFs are foundational in: - **Signal Processing:** Obtaining the amplitude distribution of a multidimensional discrete signal. - **Digital Communications:** Computing bit-error probabilities after marginalising noise dimensions. - **Reliability Engineering:** Component-failure probability by marginalising over stress levels. - **Machine Learning:** Computing class priors $P(Y)$ by marginalising joint $P(X,Y)$. - **Quality Control:** Yield analysis by marginalising defect types. --- ## 1.5 Margins of Generalisation The concept extends naturally to $n$ discrete random variables $X_1, X_2, \dots, X_n$: $$p_{X_1}(x_1) = \sum_{x_2, \dots, x_n} p_{X_1, X_2, \dots, X_n}(x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)$$ # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Logic ŌĆö The "Why" Behind Marginalisation The derivation rests on the **Law of Total Probability**, applied to the discrete case. For any fixed value $x = x_0$: $$P(X = x_0) = P(X = x_0, Y \in S_Y) = \sum_{y \in S_Y} P(X = x_0, Y = y)$$ Since $S_Y$ is the **entire** sample space of $Y$, the events $\{Y = y\}$ for $y \in S_Y$ are mutually exclusive and exhaustive. Hence summing the joint probabilities over $y$ recovers $P(X = x_0)$ without any information loss ŌĆö only a *reduction in dimensionality*. --- ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Algorithmic Logic 1. **Start with** the joint PMF $p_{X,Y}(x,y)$ defined on a discrete grid $S_X \times S_Y$. 2. **Fix** the variable of interest (say $X = x$). 3. **Sum** the joint PMF over every admissible value of the *other* variable $Y$: $$p_X(x) = \sum_{y \in S_Y} p_{X,Y}(x, y)$$ 4. **Repeat** for each $x \in S_X$ to build the full marginal PMF table. 5. **Verify** that $\sum_x p_X(x) = 1$. For the marginal of $Y$, simply transpose the role and sum **across rows** instead of columns. --- ## 2.3 Two Essential Properties of a Marginal PMF Let $p_X(\cdot)$ be the marginal PMF of a discrete random variable $X$. | # | Property | Mathematical Statement | Physical / Engineering Meaning | |---|----------|------------------------|-------------------------------| | 1 | **Non-negativity** | $p_X(x) \ge 0$ for all $x \in S_X$ | Probability values are never negative. | | 2 | **Unity** | $\sum_{x \in S_X} p_X(x) = 1$ | Total probability mass across all values of $X$ is conserved (equals 1). | | 3 | **Marginalisation consistency** | $\sum_y p_{X,Y}(x,y) = p_X(x)$ | Sum of joint column equals marginal entry. | > [!IMPORTANT] A function satisfying properties 1 and 2 is a **valid PMF**. Property 3 is the *bridge* between joint and marginal. --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | S.No. | Concept | Formula | Conditions / Notes | |:---:|---------|---------|-------------------| | 1 | Marginal PMF of $X$ from joint | $p_X(x) = \sum_{y \in S_Y} p_{X,Y}(x,y)$ | $S_Y$ must be finite or countably summable. | | 2 | Marginal PMF of $Y$ from joint | $p_Y(y) = \sum_{x \in S_X} p_{X,Y}(x,y)$ | Symmetric counterpart of (1). | | 3 | Marginals of $n$ variables | $p_{X_1}(x_1) = \sum_{x_2, \dots, x_n} p(x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)$ | $n-1$ nested summations. | | 4 | Marginal of continuous-mixed case | $f_X(x) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_{X,Y}(x,y)\,dy$ | Replaces sum with integral. | | 5 | Law of Total Probability (discrete) | $P(A) = \sum_i P(A \cap B_i)$ | Foundation of marginalisation. | | 6 | Marginal expectation | $E[X] = \sum_x x \cdot p_X(x)$ | Computed from marginal, not joint. | | 7 | Inversion formula (check) | $p_{X,Y}(x,y) = p_{X \vert Y}(x \vert y)\, p_Y(y)$ | Used to verify independence: $p_{X,Y} = p_X \cdot p_Y$. | --- ## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering - **Medical Imaging (PET/CT):** Marginal distributions are extracted from joint histograms of pixel intensities across two imaging modalities. - **Communication Systems:** Marginal of received signal amplitude when transmitted amplitude and noise are jointly distributed. - **Inventory Management:** Marginal demand distribution when both price and demand are random. - **Structural Reliability:** Marginal failure probability with respect to load when both load and resistance are random. - **Stochastic Processes:** Recovering one-dimensional state distributions from two-dimensional transition kernels. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Master Example ŌĆö The Reference Joint PMF Let $X$ and $Y$ be two discrete random variables with the following joint PMF: $$p_{X,Y}(x,y) = \frac{x + y}{12}, \quad x \in \{0, 1\}, \ y \in \{0, 1, 2\}$$ | $x \setminus y$ | $y = 0$ | $y = 1$ | $y = 2$ | **Row Sum** | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | **x = 0** | $0/12 = 0$ | $1/12$ | $2/12$ | ŌĆö | | **x = 1** | $1/12$ | $2/12$ | $3/12$ | ŌĆö | | **Column Sum** | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | **Step 1 ŌĆö Verify the joint PMF is valid:** $$\sum_{x=0}^{1} \sum_{y=0}^{2} \frac{x + y}{12} = \frac{(0+0)+(0+1)+(0+2)+(1+0)+(1+1)+(1+2)}{12} = \frac{0+1+2+1+2+3}{12} = \frac{9}{12}$$ The total is $9/12 \ne 1$, so this is **NOT a valid PMF**. We must renormalise. Let us redefine: $$p_{X,Y}(x,y) = \frac{x + y}{9}, \quad x \in \{0, 1\}, \ y \in \{0, 1, 2\}$$ Now the total mass is $9/9 = 1$. The verified joint PMF table is: | $x \setminus y$ | $y = 0$ | $y = 1$ | $y = 2$ | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | **x = 0** | $0/9$ | $1/9$ | $2/9$ | | **x = 1** | $1/9$ | $2/9$ | $3/9$ | **Verification of non-negativity:** Every cell is $\ge 0$. Ō£ō **Verification of unity:** $0 + 1 + 2 + 1 + 2 + 3 = 9$, divided by 9 gives $1$. Ō£ō --- ## 3.2 Derivation of Marginal PMF of $X$ **Step 1** ŌĆö Fix $X = 0$ and sum over all $y \in \{0, 1, 2\}$: $$p_X(0) = \sum_{y=0}^{2} p_{X,Y}(0, y) = p_{X,Y}(0,0) + p_{X,Y}(0,1) + p_{X,Y}(0,2)$$ Substituting the values: $$p_X(0) = \frac{0}{9} + \frac{1}{9} + \frac{2}{9} = \frac{0 + 1 + 2}{9} = \frac{3}{9} = \frac{1}{3}$$ **Step 2** ŌĆö Fix $X = 1$ and sum over all $y$: $$p_X(1) = \sum_{y=0}^{2} p_{X,Y}(1, y) = p_{X,Y}(1,0) + p_{X,Y}(1,1) + p_{X,Y}(1,2)$$ Substituting: $$p_X(1) = \frac{1}{9} + \frac{2}{9} + \frac{3}{9} = \frac{1 + 2 + 3}{9} = \frac{6}{9} = \frac{2}{3}$$ **Result ŌĆö Marginal PMF of $X$:** $$\boxed{p_X(0) = \frac{1}{3}, \quad p_X(1) = \frac{2}{3}}$$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation of Marginal PMF of $Y$ **Step 1** ŌĆö Fix $Y = 0$ and sum over all $x \in \{0, 1\}$: $$p_Y(0) = \sum_{x=0}^{1} p_{X,Y}(x, 0) = p_{X,Y}(0,0) + p_{X,Y}(1,0)$$ $$p_Y(0) = \frac{0}{9} + \frac{1}{9} = \frac{1}{9}$$ **Step 2** ŌĆö Fix $Y = 1$ and sum over all $x$: $$p_Y(1) = \sum_{x=0}^{1} p_{X,Y}(x, 1) = p_{X,Y}(0,1) + p_{X,Y}(1,1)$$ $$p_Y(1) = \frac{1}{9} + \frac{2}{9} = \frac{3}{9} = \frac{1}{3}$$ **Step 3** ŌĆö Fix $Y = 2$ and sum over all $x$: $$p_Y(2) = \sum_{x=0}^{1} p_{X,Y}(x, 2) = p_{X,Y}(0,2) + p_{X,Y}(1,2)$$ $$p_Y(2) = \frac{2}{9} + \frac{3}{9} = \frac{5}{9}$$ **Result ŌĆö Marginal PMF of $Y$:** $$\boxed{p_Y(0) = \frac{1}{9}, \quad p_Y(1) = \frac{3}{9} = \frac{1}{3}, \quad p_Y(2) = \frac{5}{9}}$$ --- ## 3.4 Verification of Marginal Validity **For $X$:** $p_X(0) + p_X(1) = \dfrac{1}{3} + \dfrac{2}{3} = 1$ Ō£ō **For $Y$:** $p_Y(0) + p_Y(1) + p_Y(2) = \dfrac{1}{9} + \dfrac{3}{9} + \dfrac{5}{9} = \dfrac{9}{9} = 1$ Ō£ō **Non-negativity:** All entries are non-negative. Ō£ō Both functions are valid PMFs. --- ## 3.5 Exhaustive Marginal Table (Compact View) | Variable | Value $k$ | $p(k)$ | Computation | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | $X$ | 0 | $1/3$ | $(0 + 1 + 2)/9$ | | $X$ | 1 | $2/3$ | $(1 + 2 + 3)/9$ | | $Y$ | 0 | $1/9$ | $(0 + 1)/9$ | | $Y$ | 1 | $3/9$ | $(1 + 2)/9$ | | $Y$ | 2 | $5/9$ | $(2 + 3)/9$ | --- ## 3.6 Analytical Extension ŌĆö Marginals from Marginals If we are given only the joint PMF, the following **inverses** are useful: $$p_Y(y) = \sum_{x} p_{X,Y}(x,y)$$ $$p_{X,Y}(x,y) = p_Y(y)\, p_{X \mid Y}(x \mid y) \quad \text{(product rule)}$$ This shows that the marginal $p_Y(y)$ acts as a *weight* in the conditional factorisation of the joint PMF. --- ## 3.7 Edge Case ŌĆö Independence Test Using Marginals Two discrete random variables $X$ and $Y$ are **independent** if and only if: $$p_{X,Y}(x,y) = p_X(x) \cdot p_Y(y) \quad \forall\, (x, y)$$ **Test on our example:** Check $p_{X,Y}(0,2) \stackrel{?}{=} p_X(0) \cdot p_Y(2)$ $$\text{LHS} = \frac{2}{9}, \quad \text{RHS} = \frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{5}{9} = \frac{5}{27}$$ Since $\dfrac{2}{9} = \dfrac{6}{27} \ne \dfrac{5}{27}$, $X$ and $Y$ are **not independent**. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture ŌĆö Marginalisation Flow The following Mermaid block diagram maps the structural flow of how a joint PMF decomposes into its two marginal PMFs, and how marginals combine with conditional PMFs to reconstruct the joint. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Joint PMF pXY of x comma y
defined on SX times SY] --> B{Choose variable
to marginalise} B -- Sum over y --> C[Marginal PMF of X
pX of x equals sum of pXY over y] B -- Sum over x --> D[Marginal PMF of Y
pY of y equals sum of pXY over x] C --> E[Validate Non Negativity
pX of x greater equal 0] C --> F[Validate Unity
sum of pX of x equals 1] D --> G[Validate Non Negativity
pY of y greater equal 0] D --> H[Validate Unity
sum of pY of y equals 1] A --> I[Conditional PMF
pXgivenY of x given y] D --> J[Marginal of Y
pY of y] I --> K[Product Rule
pXY of x comma y equals pXgivenY times pY] J --> K K --> A C --> L[Independence Test
Check pXY equals pX times pY] D --> L A --> L ``` --- ## 4.2 Conceptual Block Diagram ŌĆö Joint, Marginal, Conditional ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph JOINT["Joint Probability Layer"] J1[Cell pXY of x comma y
Full 2D Information] end subgraph MARG["Marginal Probability Layer"] M1[Marginal of X
Row Totals] M2[Marginal of Y
Column Totals] end subgraph COND["Conditional Probability Layer"] C1[Conditional of X given Y
pXgivenY of x given y] C2[Conditional of Y given X
pYgivenX of y given x] end J1 -->|Sum over y| M1 J1 -->|Sum over x| M2 J1 -->|Normalise by pY| C1 J1 -->|Normalise by pX| C2 M2 -->|Multiply| C1 M1 -->|Multiply| C2 C1 -->|Multiply by pY| J1 C2 -->|Multiply by pX| J1 ``` --- ## 4.3 Table-Style Schematic of the Marginalisation Mechanism ```mermaid flowchart TB T1[Joint Table
Rows X values times Columns Y values] --> T2[Column Summation
Sum each column across all X] T2 --> T3[Bottom Margin Row
Equals Marginal of Y] T1 --> T4[Row Summation
Sum each row across all Y] T4 --> T5[Right Margin Column
Equals Marginal of X] ``` The diagrams collectively capture the *causal chain*: the joint PMF is the source; summation (over the unwanted dimension) produces the marginals; multiplication with conditionals reconstructs the joint. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define the marginal probability mass function of a discrete random variable $X$ given a joint PMF $p_{X,Y}(x,y)$. State its two essential properties.** **Model Answer:** > The **marginal PMF** of $X$, denoted $p_X(x)$, is the probability distribution of $X$ alone, obtained from the joint PMF by summing over all admissible values of the companion random variable $Y$: > > $$p_X(x) = \sum_{y \in S_Y} p_{X,Y}(x, y), \quad \forall\, x \in S_X$$ > > **Two Essential Properties:** > > 1. **Non-negativity:** $p_X(x) \ge 0$ for all $x \in S_X$. > 2. **Unity of total probability:** $\sum_{x \in S_X} p_X(x) = 1$. --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Distinguish between a Joint PMF and a Marginal PMF. Why is the joint PMF considered a "richer" description?** **Model Answer:** > | Feature | Joint PMF $p_{X,Y}(x,y)$ | Marginal PMF $p_X(x)$ | > |---|---|---| > | Variables involved | Two (or more) | One | > | Domain | $S_X \times S_Y$ (2D grid) | $S_X$ (1D list) | > | Information content | Full probabilistic coupling between $X$ and $Y$ | Only the distribution of $X$, with $Y$ ignored | > | Computation | Given as input | Derived by summing over $Y$ | > > The **joint PMF is richer** because it captures the *dependence structure* between $X$ and $Y$. Two different joint PMFs can yield the *same* marginals but describe entirely different dependence relations (e.g., independence vs. correlation). Hence, the marginal PMF alone is an **incomplete description** of the random pair. --- ## 5.2 Part B Question ŌĆö Module Internal Choice (14 Marks) ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **The joint PMF of two discrete random variables $X$ and $Y$ is given by:** $$p_{X,Y}(x,y) = k(x + y), \quad x \in \{0, 1, 2\}, \quad y \in \{1, 2\}$$ **(a)** Find the value of the constant $k$. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Determine the marginal PMFs of $X$ and $Y$ and verify that each sums to 1. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Question A **Part (a) ŌĆö Finding $k$** For $p_{X,Y}$ to be a valid PMF, the total probability must equal 1: $$\sum_{x=0}^{2} \sum_{y=1}^{2} p_{X,Y}(x,y) = 1$$ $$\sum_{x=0}^{2} \sum_{y=1}^{2} k(x + y) = 1$$ Factor out $k$: $$k \sum_{x=0}^{2} \sum_{y=1}^{2} (x + y) = 1$$ Compute the inner sum (over $y$): $$\sum_{y=1}^{2} (x + y) = (x + 1) + (x + 2) = 2x + 3$$ Now sum over $x$: $$\sum_{x=0}^{2} (2x + 3) = (0 + 3) + (2 + 3) + (4 + 3) = 3 + 5 + 7 = 15$$ Hence: $$15k = 1 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \boxed{k = \frac{1}{15}}$$ **Part (b) ŌĆö Marginal PMFs of $X$ and $Y$** The complete joint PMF table (with $k = 1/15$): | $x \setminus y$ | $y = 1$ | $y = 2$ | **Row Sum** | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | **x = 0** | $1/15$ | $2/15$ | $3/15$ | | **x = 1** | $2/15$ | $3/15$ | $5/15$ | | **x = 2** | $3/15$ | $4/15$ | $7/15$ | | **Column Sum** | $6/15$ | $9/15$ | $15/15$ | **Marginal PMF of $X$** (row sums): $$p_X(0) = p_{X,Y}(0,1) + p_{X,Y}(0,2) = \frac{1}{15} + \frac{2}{15} = \frac{3}{15} = \frac{1}{5}$$ $$p_X(1) = p_{X,Y}(1,1) + p_{X,Y}(1,2) = \frac{2}{15} + \frac{3}{15} = \frac{5}{15} = \frac{1}{3}$$ $$p_X(2) = p_{X,Y}(2,1) + p_{X,Y}(2,2) = \frac{3}{15} + \frac{4}{15} = \frac{7}{15}$$ **Verification for $X$:** $\dfrac{1}{5} + \dfrac{1}{3} + \dfrac{7}{15} = \dfrac{3}{15} + \dfrac{5}{15} + \dfrac{7}{15} = \dfrac{15}{15} = 1$ Ō£ō **Marginal PMF of $Y$** (column sums): $$p_Y(1) = p_{X,Y}(0,1) + p_{X,Y}(1,1) + p_{X,Y}(2,1) = \frac{1 + 2 + 3}{15} = \frac{6}{15} = \frac{2}{5}$$ $$p_Y(2) = p_{X,Y}(0,2) + p_{X,Y}(1,2) + p_{X,Y}(2,2) = \frac{2 + 3 + 4}{15} = \frac{9}{15} = \frac{3}{5}$$ **Verification for $Y$:** $\dfrac{2}{5} + \dfrac{3}{5} = \dfrac{5}{5} = 1$ Ō£ō **Summary:** $$\boxed{p_X(x): \ p_X(0) = \tfrac{1}{5},\ p_X(1) = \tfrac{1}{3},\ p_X(2) = \tfrac{7}{15}}$$ $$\boxed{p_Y(y): \ p_Y(1) = \tfrac{2}{5},\ p_Y(2) = \tfrac{3}{5}}$$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **The joint PMF of $(X, Y)$ is given by the table:** | $x \setminus y$ | $y = 0$ | $y = 1$ | $y = 2$ | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | **x = 1** | $0.10$ | $0.20$ | $0.10$ | | **x = 2** | $0.15$ | $0.25$ | $0.20$ | **(a)** Find the marginal PMFs of $X$ and $Y$. Also compute $E[X]$ and $E[Y]$ using the marginals. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Determine $P(X > Y)$ and $P(X + Y \le 3)$ from the joint PMF. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Question B **Step 0 ŌĆö Verify the joint PMF sums to 1:** $$0.10 + 0.20 + 0.10 + 0.15 + 0.25 + 0.20 = 1.00 \checkmark$$ **Part (a) ŌĆö Marginal PMFs** **Marginal PMF of $X$** (sum across each row): $$p_X(1) = 0.10 + 0.20 + 0.10 = 0.40$$ $$p_X(2) = 0.15 + 0.25 + 0.20 = 0.60$$ **Verification:** $0.40 + 0.60 = 1.00$ Ō£ō **Marginal PMF of $Y$** (sum down each column): $$p_Y(0) = 0.10 + 0.15 = 0.25$$ $$p_Y(1) = 0.20 + 0.25 = 0.45$$ $$p_Y(2) = 0.10 + 0.20 = 0.30$$ **Verification:** $0.25 + 0.45 + 0.30 = 1.00$ Ō£ō **Expected value of $X$:** $$E[X] = \sum_x x \cdot p_X(x) = (1)(0.40) + (2)(0.60) = 0.40 + 1.20 = 1.60$$ **Expected value of $Y$:** $$E[Y] = \sum_y y \cdot p_Y(y) = (0)(0.25) + (1)(0.45) + (2)(0.30) = 0 + 0.45 + 0.60 = 1.05$$ **Part (b) ŌĆö Event Probabilities from the Joint PMF** **Event $\{X > Y\}$:** List the favourable $(x, y)$ pairs satisfying $x > y$: - $(1, 0)$: $x=1 > y=0$ Ō£ō with probability $0.10$ - $(2, 0)$: $x=2 > y=0$ Ō£ō with probability $0.15$ - $(2, 1)$: $x=2 > y=1$ Ō£ō with probability $0.25$ Pairs that do **not** satisfy $x > y$: $(1, 1), (1, 2), (2, 2)$ ŌĆö these are excluded. $$P(X > Y) = 0.10 + 0.15 + 0.25 = 0.50$$ **Event $\{X + Y \le 3\}$:** List the favourable pairs satisfying $x + y \le 3$: - $(1, 0)$: $1 + 0 = 1 \le 3$ Ō£ō with probability $0.10$ - $(1, 1)$: $1 + 1 = 2 \le 3$ Ō£ō with probability $0.20$ - $(1, 2)$: $1 + 2 = 3 \le 3$ Ō£ō with probability $0.10$ - $(2, 0)$: $2 + 0 = 2 \le 3$ Ō£ō with probability $0.15$ - $(2, 1)$: $2 + 1 = 3 \le 3$ Ō£ō with probability $0.25$ - $(2, 2)$: $2 + 2 = 4 \not\le 3$ Ō£Ś ŌĆö excluded $$P(X + Y \le 3) = 0.10 + 0.20 + 0.10 + 0.15 + 0.25 = 0.80$$ **Summary of Answers:** $$\boxed{p_X(1) = 0.40,\ p_X(2) = 0.60;\quad p_Y(0)=0.25,\ p_Y(1)=0.45,\ p_Y(2)=0.30}$$ $$\boxed{E[X] = 1.60;\quad E[Y] = 1.05;\quad P(X > Y) = 0.50;\quad P(X+Y \le 3) = 0.80}$$ --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Valuation** > > 1. **Forgetting to normalise the constant $k$:** When $p_{X,Y}(x,y)$ contains an unknown constant, you MUST first enforce $\sum\sum p_{X,Y} = 1$ *before* computing the marginal. Marginals computed before normalisation are **wrong by a constant factor**. > 2. **Summing over the wrong axis:** A frequent slip is summing $p_{X,Y}$ over $x$ when asked for the marginal of $X$ (it should be over $y$). Always cross-check: *the marginal of $X$ is a function of $x$ only ŌĆö so $y$ must be summed out*. > 3. **Skipping the unity verification:** KTU examiners award marks for verifying $\sum p_X(x) = 1$. Omitting this step costs at least 1 mark in long-answer scripts. > 4. **Confusing marginal with conditional:** $p_X(x)$ is the **marginal** (no condition). $p_{X \mid Y}(x \mid y)$ is the **conditional** (with a fixed $y$). These are entirely different objects. > 5. **Treating $E[X]$ as needing the joint PMF:** $E[X]$ can (and should) be computed from the marginal $p_X(x)$ alone. Using the joint PMF for $E[X]$ works but is unnecessarily complex and may introduce summation errors. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Marginal PMF** = projection of the joint PMF onto a single axis by summation over the *other* variable. - The defining formula is $p_X(x) = \sum_{y} p_{X,Y}(x, y)$; analogously for $p_Y(y)$. - **Always normalise first** when the joint PMF contains an unknown constant ŌĆö enforce $\sum\sum p = 1$ before any other computation. - A valid marginal PMF must satisfy **two properties**: non-negativity and unity (totals to 1). - Marginal PMFs are **partial** descriptions ŌĆö they do not capture dependence between $X$ and $Y$. - To test **independence**, verify $p_{X,Y}(x,y) = p_X(x)\, p_Y(y)$ for *all* $(x,y)$. - Expected values, variances, and moments of $X$ can all be computed from the **marginal** $p_X(x)$ alone. - The terminology "marginal" comes from the **table margins** (row/column sums) of a contingency table. - The concept extends from 2 variables to $n$ variables by **nested summation** over the unwanted dimensions. - In engineering practice, marginals are used to extract single-variable behaviour from complex multi-dimensional stochastic models (signals, communications, reliability, ML priors).

topics.title: Independent Random Variables
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GCMAT401 - MATHEMATICS FOR PHYSICAL SCIENCE AND LIFE SCIENCE ŌĆō 4
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Independent Random Variables

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

Let $(\Omega, \mathcal{F}, P)$ be a probability space, and let $X : \Omega \to \mathbb{R}$ and $Y : \Omega \to \mathbb{R}$ be two random variables defined on this space. The random variables $X$ and $Y$ are said to be **statistically independent** if and only if the joint distribution of $(X, Y)$ factorizes into the product of their individual marginal distributions.

**Definition (Discrete Case):**
Two discrete random variables $X$ and $Y$ are independent if and only if for every pair of values $(x, y)$ in their respective ranges:

$$P(X = x, Y = y) = P(X = x) \cdot P(Y = y)$$

**Definition (Continuous Case):**
Two continuous random variables $X$ and $Y$ with joint density $f_{X,Y}(x, y)$ and marginal densities $f_X(x)$, $f_Y(y)$ are independent if and only if:

$$f_{X,Y}(x, y) = f_X(x) \cdot f_Y(y) \quad \text{for all } (x, y) \in \mathbb{R}^2$$

**Definition (CDF Characterization ŌĆō Universal Form):**
Two random variables $X$ and $Y$ are independent if and only if their joint cumulative distribution function factorizes:

$$F_{X,Y}(x, y) = F_X(x) \cdot F_Y(y) \quad \text{for all } (x, y) \in \mathbb{R}^2$$

where $F_{X,Y}(x, y) = P(X \leq x, Y \leq y)$, $F_X(x) = P(X \leq x)$, and $F_Y(y) = P(Y \leq y)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 ŌĆō GCMAT401 Module 1):**
> The CDF-based definition is the *most general* and applies to **mixed** random variables (one discrete, one continuous) where joint PDF or PMF may not exist in classical form. Always verify independence using the joint distribution first, *not* the converse.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Real-World Intuition

Imagine two experiments conducted simultaneously and far apart, with absolutely no physical or causal connection between them:

- **Experiment 1:** You flip a fair coin in **Kochi, Kerala**. Let $X = 1$ if Heads, $0$ if Tails.
- **Experiment 2:** A die is rolled in **Thiruvananthapuram, Kerala**. Let $Y$ be the face value (1 to 6).

The outcome of the coin flip in Kochi has **no influence** whatsoever on the die roll in Thiruvananthapuram. The events $\{X = x\}$ and $\{Y = y\}$ are governed by separate physical mechanisms, so $P(X = x, Y = y) = P(X = x) \cdot P(Y = y)$ holds perfectly. Hence $X$ and $Y$ are **independent**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Counter-analogy (Dependence):**
> Now consider the temperature $T_1$ in Kochi at noon and the ice-cream sales $S$ in Kochi at noon. When $T_1$ is high, $S$ tends to be high. The events $\{T_1 > 30^\circ C\}$ and $\{S > 100 \text{ units}\}$ are *not* independent ŌĆö knowing one tells you something about the other. The joint PMF/PDF does **not** factorize.

## 1.3 Engineering Significance

Independence is a workhorse assumption across engineering disciplines:
- **Signal Processing:** Noise samples taken at widely separated time instants are often modeled as independent.
- **Communications:** Bit errors in a communication channel are typically assumed independent (the *i.i.d.* ŌĆö independent and identically distributed ŌĆö assumption).
- **Reliability Engineering:** Failure of independent components in parallel/series systems.
- **Machine Learning:** Feature independence underlies the **Naive Bayes classifier**.
- **Physics:** Quantum measurements on non-entangled systems yield independent random variables.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Independence vs. Uncorrelated (a common student confusion):**
> Independence $\Rightarrow$ Uncorrelated, but Uncorrelated $\not\Rightarrow$ Independence. We will revisit this critical distinction in Section 2.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Conditions for Independence

### (A) Joint Distribution Factorization
The single most fundamental test: compute the joint PMF/PDF and check whether it splits as a product of two single-variable functions.

**Discrete Test:**

$$P(X = x_i, Y = y_j) \stackrel{?}{=} P(X = x_i) \cdot P(Y = y_j) \quad \forall \, i, j$$

**Continuous Test:**

$$f_{X,Y}(x, y) \stackrel{?}{=} g(x) \cdot h(y)$$

where $g$ and $h$ depend on different arguments, and after normalization, $g = f_X$, $h = f_Y$.

### (B) CDF Factorization
The cleanest *unified* test that works for all random variables:

$$F_{X,Y}(x, y) = F_X(x) \cdot F_Y(y) \quad \forall (x, y)$$

### (C) Event-Independence Equivalence
$X$ and $Y$ are independent $\iff$ for *every* pair of Borel sets $A, B \subseteq \mathbb{R}$:

$$P(X \in A, \, Y \in B) = P(X \in A) \cdot P(Y \in B)$$

This is the deepest measure-theoretic form.

## 2.2 Major Theorems on Independent Random Variables

> [!NOTE]
> **Theorem 1 (Functions of Independent RVs):**
> If $X$ and $Y$ are independent, then for *any* measurable (Borel) functions $g$ and $h$:
> - $g(X)$ and $h(Y)$ are independent.
> - $E[g(X) h(Y)] = E[g(X)] \cdot E[h(Y)]$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Theorem 2 (Expectation of Product):**
> If $X$ and $Y$ are independent, then $E[XY]$ exists iff both $E[X]$ and $E[Y]$ exist, and:
> $$E[XY] = E[X] \cdot E[Y]$$
> In particular, $\text{Cov}(X, Y) = E[XY] - E[X]E[Y] = 0$ (uncorrelated).

> [!NOTE]
> **Theorem 3 (Variance Additivity):**
> If $X$ and $Y$ are independent (each with finite variance), then:
> $$\text{Var}(X + Y) = \text{Var}(X) + \text{Var}(Y)$$
> $$\text{Var}(X - Y) = \text{Var}(X) + \text{Var}(Y)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Theorem 4 (MGF Property ŌĆö Most Powerful Test):**
> If $X$ and $Y$ are independent, the MGF of $Z = X + Y$ is the product of MGFs:
> $$M_{X+Y}(t) = M_X(t) \cdot M_Y(t)$$
> **Conversely**, if a joint MGF $M_{X,Y}(t_1, t_2) = M_X(t_1) \cdot M_Y(t_2)$ for all $t_1, t_2$ in a neighbourhood of 0, then $X$ and $Y$ are independent.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the single most important reference for solving KTU exam questions on this topic. Memorize and apply.**

| **#** | **Concept** | **Formula / Condition** |
|:-----:|:------------|:------------------------|
| 1 | Independence ŌĆō Discrete | $P(X=x, Y=y) = P_X(x) \cdot P_Y(y) \quad \forall x,y$ |
| 2 | Independence ŌĆō Continuous | $f_{X,Y}(x,y) = f_X(x) \cdot f_Y(y) \quad \forall x,y$ |
| 3 | Independence ŌĆō CDF | $F_{X,Y}(x,y) = F_X(x) \cdot F_Y(y) \quad \forall x,y$ |
| 4 | Independence ŌĆō Events | $P(X \in A, Y \in B) = P(X \in A) \cdot P(Y \in B) \quad \forall A, B$ |
| 5 | Mean of Product | $E[XY] = E[X] \cdot E[Y]$ |
| 6 | Variance of Sum | $\text{Var}(X \pm Y) = \text{Var}(X) + \text{Var}(Y)$ |
| 7 | Covariance | $\text{Cov}(X,Y) = 0$ (uncorrelated consequence) |
| 8 | MGF of Sum | $M_{X+Y}(t) = M_X(t) \cdot M_Y(t)$ |
| 9 | Joint MGF | $M_{X,Y}(t_1, t_2) = M_X(t_1) \cdot M_Y(t_2)$ |
| 10 | PDF of Sum (Convolution) | $f_{X+Y}(z) = \displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_X(x) \, f_Y(z - x) \, dx$ |
| 11 | PMF of Sum (Discrete Conv.) | $P(X+Y = z) = \displaystyle\sum_{x} P_X(x) \, P_Y(z - x)$ |
| 12 | CDF of Sum | $F_{X+Y}(z) = \displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_X(x) \, F_Y(z-x) \, dx$ |
| 13 | Product of Functions | If $X \perp Y$, then $g(X) \perp h(Y)$ |
| 14 | Uncorrelated $\not\Rightarrow$ Independent | $E[XY] = E[X]E[Y]$ alone is insufficient |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

**Why this matters in engineering practice:**

- **Communication Systems (BER Analysis):** In a Binary Symmetric Channel, bit errors are *i.i.d.* (independent and identically distributed Bernoulli). Computing the Bit Error Rate (BER) over $n$ bits reduces to a Binomial distribution built on independence.
- **Structural Reliability:** For a system of $n$ *independent* components with reliabilities $R_i$, system reliability is $R_{\text{system}} = \prod_i R_i$ (series) or $1 - \prod_i (1 - R_i)$ (parallel).
- **Statistical Inference:** Sample means $\bar{X}_n = \frac{1}{n}\sum X_i$ rely on independence of $X_i$ for the Central Limit Theorem and variance formula $\text{Var}(\bar{X}_n) = \sigma^2 / n$.
- **Machine Learning:** Naive Bayes assumes *conditional* independence of features given the class ŌĆö a direct application of the chain rule under independence.
- **Physics Experiments:** Measurement errors across repeated trials are modeled as independent zero-mean Gaussians.

## 2.5 Independence vs. Uncorrelated ŌĆö The Critical Distinction

> [!WARNING]
> **Most Common Student Mistake in KTU Exams:**
> Students often write "Since $\text{Cov}(X,Y) = 0$, $X$ and $Y$ are independent." This is **FALSE** in general. Zero covariance only implies *linear* uncorrelatedness. Two random variables can have a strong nonlinear dependence yet zero covariance.

**Classic Counterexample:** Let $(X, Y)$ be uniform on the unit circle $x^2 + y^2 \leq 1$.
- $E[X] = 0$, $E[Y] = 0$
- $E[XY] = 0 \Rightarrow \text{Cov}(X,Y) = 0$ (uncorrelated)
- But $Y$ is clearly *not* independent of $X$ ŌĆö knowing $X$ restricts the possible values of $Y$ to $[-\sqrt{1-X^2}, \sqrt{1-X^2}]$.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: $E[XY] = E[X] E[Y]$ for Independent Continuous Random Variables

**Goal:** Show that if $X$ and $Y$ are independent continuous random variables, then the expectation of their product equals the product of expectations.

**Given:** Joint density $f_{X,Y}(x,y) = f_X(x) \cdot f_Y(y)$ for all $(x,y) \in \mathbb{R}^2$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Start with the formal definition of $E[XY]$:**

By the definition of expectation for a function of two random variables:

$$E[XY] = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x \cdot y \cdot f_{X,Y}(x, y) \, dy \, dx$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply the independence condition:**

Substitute $f_{X,Y}(x, y) = f_X(x) \cdot f_Y(y)$:

$$E[XY] = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x \cdot y \cdot f_X(x) \cdot f_Y(y) \, dy \, dx$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Separate the integrand into two single-variable pieces:**

The integrand $x \cdot y \cdot f_X(x) \cdot f_Y(y)$ can be reorganized as $[x \cdot f_X(x)] \cdot [y \cdot f_Y(y)]$, so the double integral becomes a product of two single integrals:

$$E[XY] = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x \, f_X(x) \left[ \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} y \, f_Y(y) \, dy \right] dx$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Recognize the inner integral as $E[Y]$:**

The bracketed term is precisely $E[Y]$ by definition:

$$E[XY] = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x \, f_X(x) \cdot E[Y] \, dx$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Pull the constant $E[Y]$ outside:**

Since $E[Y]$ is a constant with respect to $x$:

$$E[XY] = E[Y] \cdot \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x \, f_X(x) \, dx$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Recognize the remaining integral as $E[X]$:**

$$\boxed{E[XY] = E[X] \cdot E[Y]}$$

**Conclusion:** Independence of $X$ and $Y$ implies their product's expectation factorizes. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: $\text{Var}(X + Y) = \text{Var}(X) + \text{Var}(Y)$ for Independent Random Variables

**Goal:** Show that the variance of the sum of two independent random variables equals the sum of variances.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Expand the variance of the sum using the definition:**

$$\text{Var}(X + Y) = E[(X + Y - E[X + Y])^2]$$

Since $E[X + Y] = E[X] + E[Y]$ always holds (linearity of expectation, requires *no* independence), let $\mu_X = E[X]$, $\mu_Y = E[Y]$:

$$\text{Var}(X + Y) = E[((X - \mu_X) + (Y - \mu_Y))^2]$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Expand the square:**

$$((X - \mu_X) + (Y - \mu_Y))^2 = (X - \mu_X)^2 + 2(X - \mu_X)(Y - \mu_Y) + (Y - \mu_Y)^2$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Take expectation term by term:**

$$\text{Var}(X + Y) = E[(X - \mu_X)^2] + 2E[(X - \mu_X)(Y - \mu_Y)] + E[(Y - \mu_Y)^2]$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Identify the first and third terms:**

$$E[(X - \mu_X)^2] = \text{Var}(X), \qquad E[(Y - \mu_Y)^2] = \text{Var}(Y)$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Simplify the cross term using independence:**

The middle term is $2 \cdot \text{Cov}(X, Y)$. We expand it:

$$E[(X - \mu_X)(Y - \mu_Y)] = E[XY] - \mu_X E[Y] - \mu_Y E[X] + \mu_X \mu_Y$$

$$= E[XY] - \mu_X \mu_Y - \mu_X \mu_Y + \mu_X \mu_Y = E[XY] - \mu_X \mu_Y$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Apply independence to get $E[XY] = \mu_X \mu_Y$:**

By the result of Derivation 1:

$$E[(X - \mu_X)(Y - \mu_Y)] = \mu_X \mu_Y - \mu_X \mu_Y = 0$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Conclude:**

$$\text{Var}(X + Y) = \text{Var}(X) + 2 \cdot 0 + \text{Var}(Y) = \text{Var}(X) + \text{Var}(Y)$$

$$\boxed{\text{Var}(X + Y) = \text{Var}(X) + \text{Var}(Y)} \qquad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Moment Generating Function of the Sum of Independent Random Variables

**Goal:** Show that $M_{X+Y}(t) = M_X(t) \cdot M_Y(t)$ when $X$ and $Y$ are independent.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Start from the MGF definition of $Z = X + Y$:**

$$M_{X+Y}(t) = E[e^{t(X+Y)}] = E[e^{tX} \cdot e^{tY}]$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply the "functions of independent RVs" theorem:**

Since $X$ and $Y$ are independent, the random variables $e^{tX}$ and $e^{tY}$ are also independent. For independent RVs $U$ and $V$:

$$E[UV] = E[U] \cdot E[V]$$

Setting $U = e^{tX}$ and $V = e^{tY}$:

$$M_{X+Y}(t) = E[e^{tX}] \cdot E[e^{tY}]$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Recognize the individual MGFs:**

$$M_{X+Y}(t) = M_X(t) \cdot M_Y(t)$$

$$\boxed{M_{X+Y}(t) = M_X(t) \cdot M_Y(t)} \qquad \blacksquare$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** This identity is the foundation for proving that the sum of two *independent* Normal random variables is Normal: $X \sim N(\mu_1, \sigma_1^2)$ and $Y \sim N(\mu_2, \sigma_2^2)$ (independent) $\Rightarrow X + Y \sim N(\mu_1 + \mu_2, \sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2)$. The same trick proves that the sum of $n$ *i.i.d.* Exponential RVs is Gamma, and the sum of $n$ *i.i.d.* chi-squared RVs is chi-squared.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Convolution Formula for the Sum of Independent Continuous Random Variables

**Goal:** Derive the formula for the PDF of $Z = X + Y$ where $X$ and $Y$ are independent continuous random variables.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Write the CDF of $Z$:**

$$F_Z(z) = P(X + Y \leq z) = \iint_{\{(x,y) : x + y \leq z\}} f_{X,Y}(x, y) \, dy \, dx$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply independence:**

$$F_Z(z) = \iint_{x + y \leq z} f_X(x) \cdot f_Y(y) \, dy \, dx$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Convert the region to an iterated integral:**

For each $x \in (-\infty, \infty)$, $y$ ranges over $(-\infty, z - x]$:

$$F_Z(z) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_X(x) \left[ \int_{-\infty}^{z - x} f_Y(y) \, dy \right] dx$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Recognize the inner integral as a CDF:**

$$\int_{-\infty}^{z - x} f_Y(y) \, dy = F_Y(z - x)$$

So:

$$F_Z(z) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_X(x) \cdot F_Y(z - x) \, dx$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Differentiate with respect to $z$ to obtain the PDF:**

Using Leibniz's rule: $\frac{d}{dz} F_Y(z - x) = f_Y(z - x)$.

$$f_Z(z) = \frac{d}{dz} F_Z(z) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_X(x) \cdot f_Y(z - x) \, dx$$

$$\boxed{f_{X+Y}(z) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_X(x) \, f_Y(z - x) \, dx} \qquad \blacksquare$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This integral is called the **convolution** of $f_X$ and $f_Y$, denoted $(f_X * f_Y)(z)$. It is the workhorse formula for finding the distribution of sums of independent random variables in KTU board exams.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 1: Sum of Two Independent Uniform Random Variables

**Problem:** Let $X \sim U(0, 1)$ and $Y \sim U(0, 1)$ be independent. Find the PDF of $Z = X + Y$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Write the marginal densities:**

$$f_X(x) = \begin{cases} 1, & 0 \leq x \leq 1 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}, \qquad f_Y(y) = \begin{cases} 1, & 0 \leq y \leq 1 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply the convolution formula:**

$$f_Z(z) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_X(x) \, f_Y(z - x) \, dx$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Determine the limits of integration:**

$f_X(x) = 1$ requires $0 \leq x \leq 1$.  
$f_Y(z - x) = 1$ requires $0 \leq z - x \leq 1$, i.e., $z - 1 \leq x \leq z$.

So both are nonzero when $x$ lies in the intersection $[0, 1] \cap [z - 1, z]$.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Case-by-case analysis on $z$:**

**Case 1: $z < 0$.** No overlap, $f_Z(z) = 0$.

**Case 2: $0 \leq z \leq 1$.** Intersection is $[0, z]$, length $z$:

$$f_Z(z) = \int_0^z 1 \, dx = z$$

**Case 3: $1 \leq z \leq 2$.** Intersection is $[z - 1, 1]$, length $2 - z$:

$$f_Z(z) = \int_{z-1}^{1} 1 \, dx = 1 - (z - 1) = 2 - z$$

**Case 4: $z > 2$.** No overlap, $f_Z(z) = 0$.

**Step 5 ŌĆö Final answer:**

$$\boxed{f_Z(z) = \begin{cases} z, & 0 \leq z \leq 1 \\ 2 - z, & 1 \leq z \leq 2 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}}$$

This is the famous **triangular distribution** (IrwinŌĆōHall distribution for $n = 2$).

---

## 3.6 Worked Example 2: Independence Test for a Joint Density

**Problem:** Given the joint PDF:

$$f_{X,Y}(x, y) = \begin{cases} 4xy, & 0 \leq x \leq 1, \; 0 \leq y \leq 1 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

Determine whether $X$ and $Y$ are independent.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute the marginal $f_X(x)$:**

$$f_X(x) = \int_0^1 4xy \, dy = 4x \left[ \frac{y^2}{2} \right]_0^1 = 4x \cdot \frac{1}{2} = 2x, \quad 0 \leq x \leq 1$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute the marginal $f_Y(y)$:**

$$f_Y(y) = \int_0^1 4xy \, dx = 4y \left[ \frac{x^2}{2} \right]_0^1 = 4y \cdot \frac{1}{2} = 2y, \quad 0 \leq y \leq 1$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Check the product $f_X(x) \cdot f_Y(y)$:**

$$f_X(x) \cdot f_Y(y) = (2x)(2y) = 4xy$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Conclude:**

$$f_{X,Y}(x, y) = 4xy = f_X(x) \cdot f_Y(y) \quad \forall (x, y)$$

Therefore $X$ and $Y$ are **independent**. $\checkmark$

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024]`

**Define independence of two random variables. State the difference between uncorrelatedness and independence.**

**Model Answer:**

Two random variables $X$ and $Y$ are said to be **independent** if for all $(x, y) \in \mathbb{R}^2$:
$$F_{X,Y}(x, y) = F_X(x) \cdot F_Y(y)$$
Equivalently (for the continuous case): $f_{X,Y}(x,y) = f_X(x) \cdot f_Y(y)$; and (for the discrete case): $P(X=x, Y=y) = P(X=x) \cdot P(Y=y)$.

**Difference from Uncorrelatedness:**
- **Independent** $\Rightarrow$ $\text{Cov}(X, Y) = 0$, i.e., $E[XY] = E[X]E[Y]$.
- But **Uncorrelated** ($\text{Cov} = 0$) $\not\Rightarrow$ Independent in general (e.g., $X$ and $X^2$ where $X \sim U(-1, 1)$).
- Independence is a *much stronger* condition ŌĆö it implies no dependence of *any* kind, not just linear.

**(3 marks: 1 mark definition, 1 mark discrete/continuous form, 1 mark distinction)** Ō£ō

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]`

**If $X$ and $Y$ are independent random variables with $E[X] = 2$, $E[Y] = -3$, $\text{Var}(X) = 5$, $\text{Var}(Y) = 4$, find $E[3X - 2Y + 7]$ and $\text{Var}(3X - 2Y)$.**

**Model Answer:**

Using linearity of expectation:
$$E[3X - 2Y + 7] = 3E[X] - 2E[Y] + 7 = 3(2) - 2(-3) + 7 = 6 + 6 + 7 = \boxed{19}$$

Using the variance property $\text{Var}(aX + bY) = a^2 \text{Var}(X) + b^2 \text{Var}(Y)$ (holds for independent RVs):
$$\text{Var}(3X - 2Y) = 3^2 \cdot \text{Var}(X) + (-2)^2 \cdot \text{Var}(Y) = 9(5) + 4(4) = 45 + 16 = \boxed{61}$$

**(3 marks: 2 marks expectation, 1 mark variance)** Ō£ō

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Internal Choice)

### Question B-A `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Paper 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Define independence of two random variables $X$ and $Y$ in terms of their joint and marginal probability density functions. State and prove the result $E[XY] = E[X] E[Y]$ for two independent continuous random variables. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Let $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n$ be independent and identically distributed (i.i.d.) random variables with mean $\mu$ and variance $\sigma^2$. Show that $E[\bar{X}] = \mu$ and $\text{Var}(\bar{X}) = \sigma^2 / n$, where $\bar{X} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Definition:** Two continuous random variables $X$ and $Y$ are independent if their joint PDF $f_{X,Y}(x, y)$ equals the product of the marginal PDFs $f_X(x) \cdot f_Y(y)$ for all $(x, y) \in \mathbb{R}^2$:
$$f_{X,Y}(x, y) = f_X(x) \cdot f_Y(y)$$

**Proof of $E[XY] = E[X]E[Y]$:**

By definition:
$$E[XY] = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} xy \, f_{X,Y}(x, y) \, dx \, dy$$

Using independence $f_{X,Y}(x, y) = f_X(x) f_Y(y)$:
$$E[XY] = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} xy \, f_X(x) f_Y(y) \, dx \, dy = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x f_X(x) \, dx \cdot \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} y f_Y(y) \, dy = E[X] E[Y]$$
$\blacksquare$

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Mean of the sample mean:**

$$E[\bar{X}] = E\left[\frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i\right] = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} E[X_i] = \frac{1}{n} \cdot n\mu = \mu$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Variance of the sample mean:**

Using independence, $\text{Cov}(X_i, X_j) = 0$ for $i \neq j$:

$$\text{Var}(\bar{X}) = \text{Var}\left(\frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i\right) = \frac{1}{n^2} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{Var}(X_i) = \frac{1}{n^2} \cdot n\sigma^2 = \frac{\sigma^2}{n}$$

(using $\text{Var}(aX) = a^2 \text{Var}(X)$ and additivity of variance for independent RVs).

$\blacksquare$

---

### Question B-B `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2023]`

**(a)** State and prove the convolution theorem: if $X$ and $Y$ are independent continuous random variables, the PDF of $Z = X + Y$ is given by
$$f_Z(z) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_X(x) \, f_Y(z - x) \, dx$$
Hence, find the PDF of the sum of two independent Exponential random variables with parameters $\lambda_1$ and $\lambda_2$ (with $\lambda_1 \neq \lambda_2$). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A system consists of two components connected in series. The lifetimes $X$ and $Y$ of the components (in hours) are independent Exponential random variables with means $100$ and $200$ hours respectively. Find the probability that the system functions for at least $150$ hours. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Convolution Proof:**

$$F_Z(z) = P(X + Y \leq z) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_X(x) \left[ \int_{-\infty}^{z - x} f_Y(y) \, dy \right] dx = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_X(x) F_Y(z - x) \, dx$$

Differentiating with respect to $z$ using Leibniz's rule:
$$f_Z(z) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_X(x) f_Y(z - x) \, dx \quad \blacksquare$$

**Application ŌĆö Sum of two independent Exponentials:**

$$f_X(x) = \lambda_1 e^{-\lambda_1 x} \mathbf{1}_{x \geq 0}, \qquad f_Y(y) = \lambda_2 e^{-\lambda_2 y} \mathbf{1}_{y \geq 0}$$

$$f_Z(z) = \int_0^z \lambda_1 e^{-\lambda_1 x} \cdot \lambda_2 e^{-\lambda_2 (z - x)} \, dx = \lambda_1 \lambda_2 e^{-\lambda_2 z} \int_0^z e^{-(\lambda_1 - \lambda_2) x} \, dx$$

$$= \lambda_1 \lambda_2 e^{-\lambda_2 z} \cdot \frac{1 - e^{-(\lambda_1 - \lambda_2) z}}{\lambda_1 - \lambda_2}$$

For $z \geq 0$:

$$f_Z(z) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{\lambda_1 \lambda_2}{\lambda_1 - \lambda_2} \left( e^{-\lambda_2 z} - e^{-\lambda_1 z} \right), & \lambda_1 \neq \lambda_2 \\[2mm] \lambda^2 z e^{-\lambda z}, & \lambda_1 = \lambda_2 = \lambda \end{cases}$$

$\blacksquare$

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Identify parameters:**

Mean of exponential is $1/\lambda$. So:
$$\lambda_1 = \frac{1}{100} = 0.01, \qquad \lambda_2 = \frac{1}{200} = 0.005$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Use independence:**

The system lifetime $Z = X + Y$ is the sum. From the formula derived in part (a):

$$f_Z(z) = \frac{\lambda_1 \lambda_2}{\lambda_1 - \lambda_2}\left(e^{-\lambda_2 z} - e^{-\lambda_1 z}\right) = \frac{(0.01)(0.005)}{0.005}\left(e^{-0.005 z} - e^{-0.01 z}\right) = 0.01\left(e^{-0.005 z} - e^{-0.01 z}\right)$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute $P(Z \geq 150)$:**

$$P(Z \geq 150) = \int_{150}^{\infty} 0.01 \left(e^{-0.005 z} - e^{-0.01 z}\right) dz$$

$$= 0.01 \left[ \frac{-e^{-0.005 z}}{0.005} - \frac{-e^{-0.01 z}}{0.01} \right]_{150}^{\infty}$$

$$= 0.01 \left[ 0 - \left(\frac{-e^{-0.75}}{0.005} + \frac{e^{-1.5}}{0.01}\right) \right]$$

$$= 0.01 \left( \frac{e^{-0.75}}{0.005} - \frac{e^{-1.5}}{0.01} \right) = 2 e^{-0.75} - e^{-1.5}$$

Computing numerically: $e^{-0.75} \approx 0.4724$, $e^{-1.5} \approx 0.2231$.

$$P(Z \geq 150) = 2(0.4724) - 0.2231 = 0.9448 - 0.2231 = \boxed{0.7217}$$

$\blacksquare$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **The independence assumption is critical.** Without it, you cannot split $P(X \in A, Y \in B)$ or write $E[XY] = E[X]E[Y]$ or $\text{Var}(X+Y) = \text{Var}(X) + \text{Var}(Y)$. Always **state explicitly** that you are using independence.
> 2. **Convolution limits:** When computing $f_{X+Y}(z)$, students often forget to determine the correct limits of integration (which depend on $z$). Draw a region of integration in the $(x, y)$-plane.
> 3. **Do not assume Uncorrelated $\Rightarrow$ Independent.** This is a **deduction mark of 1ŌĆō2 marks** if used uncritically.
> 4. **For discrete sums:** Use $P(X+Y = z) = \sum_x P_X(x) P_Y(z - x)$ ŌĆö make sure the summation index is over a value where *both* $P_X(x) > 0$ and $P_Y(z - x) > 0$.
> 5. **Units & domains:** If the random variable represents time in hours, the final probability is dimensionless, but the parameters $\lambda$ have units of $\text{hour}^{-1}$. Always sanity-check dimensions.
> 6. **Mentioning the convolution theorem by name** and citing the independence assumption explicitly fetches full marks ŌĆö examiners reward "show me you know what you're doing."

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision ŌĆö Pin This on Your Wall Before the Exam:**

- **Definition (Universal):** $F_{X,Y}(x, y) = F_X(x) \cdot F_Y(y)$ for all $(x, y)$.
- **Discrete Test:** $P(X = x, Y = y) = P_X(x) \cdot P_Y(y)$ for *all* $(x, y)$.
- **Continuous Test:** $f_{X,Y}(x, y) = f_X(x) \cdot f_Y(y)$ for *all* $(x, y)$.
- **Mean of Product:** $E[XY] = E[X] E[Y]$ (requires independence).
- **Variance of Sum:** $\text{Var}(X + Y) = \text{Var}(X) + \text{Var}(Y)$ (requires independence).
- **Variance of Linear Combination:** $\text{Var}(aX \pm bY) = a^2 \text{Var}(X) + b^2 \text{Var}(Y)$ for independent $X, Y$.
- **MGF Trick:** $M_{X+Y}(t) = M_X(t) \cdot M_Y(t)$ ŌĆö extremely useful for proving Normal + Normal = Normal.
- **Joint MGF Criterion:** $M_{X,Y}(t_1, t_2) = M_X(t_1) M_Y(t_2)$ near 0 $\iff$ Independent.
- **Convolution (Continuous):** $f_{X+Y}(z) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_X(x) f_Y(z - x) dx$.
- **Convolution (Discrete):** $P(X+Y = z) = \sum_x P_X(x) P_Y(z - x)$.
- **Functions Preserved:** $X \perp Y \Rightarrow g(X) \perp h(Y)$ for any Borel $g, h$.
- **Common i.i.d. Sums:** Bernoulli$(p)$ + Bernoulli$(p)$ $\sim$ Binomial$(2, p)$; Exponential$(\lambda)$ + Exponential$(\lambda)$ $\sim$ Gamma$(2, \lambda)$; Normal$(\mu_1, \sigma_1^2)$ + Normal$(\mu_2, \sigma_2^2)$ $\sim$ Normal$(\mu_1+\mu_2, \sigma_1^2+\sigma_2^2)$.
- **Counterexample Memorized:** Uniform on unit circle $\Rightarrow$ Uncorrelated but NOT independent.
- **Key Trap:** $\text{Cov}(X, Y) = 0$ does **NOT** imply independence. Always check via joint PDF/PMF.
- **CDF criterion is the most general** ŌĆö works for mixed distributions.
- **Don't forget to specify the support (range)** of $X, Y$ explicitly when using the convolution formula.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** In KTU 14-mark questions, structure your answer as **Given ŌåÆ Formula ŌåÆ Step-by-step Calculation ŌåÆ Final Answer with Units/Interpretation**. A clean, logically ordered solution typically receives 1ŌĆō2 marks more than a correct-but-jumbled one.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Expected Value of a Function of Two Discrete Variables
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GCMAT401 - MATHEMATICS FOR PHYSICAL SCIENCE AND LIFE SCIENCE ŌĆō 4
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Expected Value of a Function of Two Discrete Variables

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

Let $(X, Y)$ be a **two-dimensional discrete random vector** defined on a common probability space. The joint behaviour of $X$ and $Y$ is completely described by the **Joint Probability Mass Function (Joint PMF)**:

$$p_{X,Y}(x, y) = P(X = x, Y = y)$$

which satisfies:
- $p(x, y) \ge 0$ for all $(x, y) \in S$, where $S$ is the support set.
- $\displaystyle\sum_{x}\sum_{y} p(x, y) = 1$

The **expected value (mathematical expectation)** of a real-valued function $g(X, Y)$ is defined as:

$$E[g(X, Y)] = \sum_{x}\sum_{y} g(x, y)\, p(x, y)$$

provided the double series converges absolutely, i.e.:

$$\sum_{x}\sum_{y} \vert g(x, y) \vert\, p(x, y) < \infty$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 HIGHLIGHT:** The convergence condition is **absolute convergence**, not ordinary convergence. This is a standard board-exam tested point ŌĆö students often forget to mention it.

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview ŌĆö The "Profit from Two Machines" Analogy

Imagine a small workshop with **two machines, A and B**. Each day, machine $A$ produces $X$ items and machine $B$ produces $Y$ items, where $(X, Y)$ is a random pair (their outputs are correlated ŌĆö perhaps they share power supply, so when one fails, the other may too).

- If each item yields a profit of Ōé╣$5$, the total profit is $g(X, Y) = 5X + 5Y$.
- If items from $A$ give Ōé╣$5$ and items from $B$ give Ōé╣$8$, the profit is $g(X, Y) = 5X + 8Y$.

The **expected value of $g(X, Y)$** answers the question: *"On an average day, what profit should I expect from this two-machine setup?"* It is the **long-run average** of the function $g$ computed over the joint distribution of the two random variables.

The key insight: we **do not** sum over $X$ and $Y$ separately. We sum over **every joint outcome pair** $(x, y)$, weight each by its joint probability $p(x, y)$, and then apply the function $g$ to that pair.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a function of two variables?** Many engineering quantities (total cost, combined failure rate, joint stress, total power) depend on **two** random inputs simultaneously. We cannot analyse $X$ and $Y$ in isolation when they are dependent.

## 1.3 Special Case ŌĆö Choosing the Right $g(X, Y)$

The formula is **universal** ŌĆö the same double-sum works for *any* function $g$:

| Choice of $g(X, Y)$ | Quantity Computed |
|---|---|
| $g(X, Y) = X$ | Mean of $X$ alone |
| $g(X, Y) = Y$ | Mean of $Y$ alone |
| $g(X, Y) = X + Y$ | Mean of the sum |
| $g(X, Y) = X^2$ | Second moment of $X$ |
| $g(X, Y) = XY$ | Mixed moment $E[XY]$ |
| $g(X, Y) = (X - \mu_X)(Y - \mu_Y)$ | Covariance $\text{Cov}(X, Y)$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Logic ŌĆö Step-by-Step

**Step 1 ŌĆö Identify the joint distribution.** The joint PMF $p(x, y)$ must be known explicitly (either as a formula or as a two-way table of probabilities).

**Step 2 ŌĆö Identify the support set $S$.** Determine the finite (or countable) set of $(x, y)$ pairs where $p(x, y) > 0$. Outside $S$, $p(x, y) = 0$, so those terms vanish in the sum.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Evaluate $g(x, y)$ at every support point.** Plug in each valid pair.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Form the product $g(x, y) \cdot p(x, y)$.** This is the "weighted contribution" of outcome $(x, y)$ to the expectation.

**Step 5 ŌĆö Sum over all $(x, y) \in S$.** Carry out the double summation ŌĆö order of summation (first over $x$ then over $y$, or vice versa) does not matter for absolutely convergent series.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** Expected value of a function of two variables is the mathematical foundation of **risk analysis** (expected loss = probability $\times$ severity), **reliability engineering** (mean time to failure of a parallel/series system), and **portfolio theory** (expected return of a two-asset portfolio).

## 2.2 Important Specialisations (KTU Board Favourites)

### 2.2.1 Mean of $X$ and Mean of $Y$

$$E[X] = \sum_{x}\sum_{y} x\, p(x, y)$$

$$E[Y] = \sum_{x}\sum_{y} y\, p(x, y)$$

### 2.2.2 Mean of the Sum (Always True ŌĆö No Independence Required)

$$E[X + Y] = E[X] + E[Y]$$

### 2.2.3 Linearity of Expectation

For **any** constants $a, b, c$ and **any** function $g$:

$$E[a\, g(X, Y) + b\, h(X, Y) + c] = a\, E[g(X, Y)] + b\, E[h(X, Y)] + c$$

### 2.2.4 Mean of a Linear Combination

$$E[aX + bY + c] = a\, E[X] + b\, E[Y] + c$$

### 2.2.5 Covariance

$$\text{Cov}(X, Y) = E[(X - \mu_X)(Y - \mu_Y)] = E[XY] - E[X]\,E[Y]$$

### 2.2.6 Variance of a Linear Combination

$$\text{Var}(aX + bY) = a^2\, \text{Var}(X) + b^2\, \text{Var}(Y) + 2ab\, \text{Cov}(X, Y)$$

If $X$ and $Y$ are **independent**, then $\text{Cov}(X, Y) = 0$ and:

$$\text{Var}(aX + bY) = a^2\, \text{Var}(X) + b^2\, \text{Var}(Y)$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas ŌĆö they form the backbone of every 14-mark question on this topic.**

| # | Formula | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E[g(X,Y)] = \sum_x \sum_y g(x,y)\, p(x,y)$ | Master definition |
| 2 | $\sum_x \sum_y p(x,y) = 1$ | Sanity check on a joint PMF |
| 3 | $E[X] = \sum_x \sum_y x\, p(x,y)$ | Marginal mean via joint sum |
| 4 | $E[Y] = \sum_x \sum_y y\, p(x,y)$ | Marginal mean via joint sum |
| 5 | $E[aX + bY + c] = aE[X] + bE[Y] + c$ | Linearity (always true) |
| 6 | $E[XY] = \sum_x \sum_y xy\, p(x,y)$ | Mixed moment |
| 7 | $\text{Cov}(X,Y) = E[XY] - E[X]E[Y]$ | Covariance |
| 8 | $\text{Var}(X) = E[X^2] - (E[X])^2$ | Variance from moments |
| 9 | $\text{Var}(aX+bY) = a^2 \text{Var}(X) + b^2 \text{Var}(Y) + 2ab\, \text{Cov}(X,Y)$ | Variance of linear combination |
| 10 | $\rho(X,Y) = \dfrac{\text{Cov}(X,Y)}{\sigma_X \sigma_Y}$ | Correlation coefficient ($-1 \le \rho \le 1$) |
| 11 | If $X \perp Y$: $E[XY] = E[X]E[Y]$ | Independence simplification |
| 12 | $P(X = x) = \sum_y p(x,y)$ | Marginal from joint |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL: Use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX (NOT the pipe character `\|`) inside table cells** to avoid breaking the markdown table structure. The KTU 2024 valuation script flags formatting errors.

## 2.4 Physical / Engineering Interpretation

| Field | Application |
|---|---|
| **Electrical Engineering** | Expected power in a circuit with two correlated noise sources $E[V^2/R]$ |
| **Mechanical Engineering** | Mean stress in a two-axial loading $\sigma_{xx}, \sigma_{yy}$ |
| **Civil Engineering** | Expected load = $E[w \cdot L]$ on a beam |
| **Quality Control** | Expected defect cost $= E[c_1 X + c_2 Y]$ |
| **Economics / Finance** | Expected return on a two-asset portfolio |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: $E[X + Y] = E[X] + E[Y]$ (No Independence Assumption)

This is the most asked "prove that" question in KTU exams for this topic. We derive it directly from the double-sum definition.

**Starting Point:** By definition of expected value of the function $g(X, Y) = X + Y$:

$$E[X + Y] = \sum_{x}\sum_{y} (x + y)\, p(x, y)$$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Distribute the function inside the sum:**

$$E[X + Y] = \sum_{x}\sum_{y} x\, p(x, y) + \sum_{x}\sum_{y} y\, p(x, y)$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Split the double sum into two double sums:** This is a basic property of finite/countable double series; it is valid whenever the series converges absolutely.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Recognise the first term as $E[X]$:** In the first sum, $x$ is constant with respect to the inner summation over $y$, so we can rewrite:

$$\sum_{x}\sum_{y} x\, p(x, y) = \sum_{x} x \left(\sum_{y} p(x, y)\right)$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Use the marginalisation identity:** $\sum_y p(x, y) = p_X(x)$, the marginal PMF of $X$. Thus:

$$\sum_{x} x\, p_X(x) = E[X]$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Symmetric argument for $Y$:** Following the same logic on the second term:

$$\sum_{x}\sum_{y} y\, p(x, y) = \sum_{y} y\, p_Y(y) = E[Y]$$

**Conclusion:**

$$E[X + Y] = E[X] + E[Y] \qquad \blacksquare$$

> [!NOTE]
> The result holds **regardless** of whether $X$ and $Y$ are independent. This is the **defining feature** of linearity of expectation and is the most commonly exploited property in KTU board problems.

## 3.2 Derivation: $E[XY] = E[X]\,E[Y]$ **only if** $X \perp Y$

**Assumption:** $X$ and $Y$ are independent, so $p(x, y) = p_X(x) \cdot p_Y(y)$.

$$E[XY] = \sum_{x}\sum_{y} xy\, p(x, y) = \sum_{x}\sum_{y} xy\, p_X(x)\, p_Y(y)$$

Since the product $p_X(x) \cdot p_Y(y)$ factors:

$$E[XY] = \left(\sum_{x} x\, p_X(x)\right)\left(\sum_{y} y\, p_Y(y)\right) = E[X]\, E[Y] \qquad \blacksquare$$

> [!WARNING]
> **PITFALL:** Many students write $E[XY] = E[X]E[Y]$ even when $X$ and $Y$ are **not** independent. This is **false in general**. The KTU examiner deducts 2ŌĆō3 marks for this.

## 3.3 Derivation: $\text{Var}(aX + bY)$

$$\text{Var}(aX + bY) = E[(aX + bY)^2] - (E[aX + bY])^2$$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Expand the square:**

$$(aX + bY)^2 = a^2 X^2 + 2ab\, XY + b^2 Y^2$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Take expectation term by term (using linearity):**

$$E[(aX + bY)^2] = a^2 E[X^2] + 2ab\, E[XY] + b^2 E[Y^2]$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute the mean squared:**

$$E[aX + bY] = aE[X] + bE[Y] \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; (E[aX+bY])^2 = a^2 (E[X])^2 + 2ab\, E[X]E[Y] + b^2 (E[Y])^2$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Subtract:**

$$\text{Var}(aX + bY) = a^2 \bigl(E[X^2] - (E[X])^2\bigr) + b^2 \bigl(E[Y^2] - (E[Y])^2\bigr) + 2ab\bigl(E[XY] - E[X]E[Y]\bigr)$$

**Conclusion:**

$$\text{Var}(aX + bY) = a^2 \text{Var}(X) + b^2 \text{Var}(Y) + 2ab\, \text{Cov}(X, Y) \qquad \blacksquare$$

## 3.4 Worked Example 1 ŌĆö Direct Application of the Master Formula (Full Solution)

**Problem:** The joint PMF of $(X, Y)$ is given by:

$$p(x, y) = \dfrac{xy}{36}, \quad x \in \{1, 2, 3\},\; y \in \{1, 2, 3\}$$

Find $E[2X - 3Y + 5]$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Verify that it is a valid PMF:**

$$\sum_{x=1}^{3}\sum_{y=1}^{3} \dfrac{xy}{36} = \dfrac{1}{36} \left(\sum_{x=1}^{3} x\right)\left(\sum_{y=1}^{3} y\right) = \dfrac{1}{36} \cdot 6 \cdot 6 = 1 \quad\checkmark$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Use linearity to avoid double-summing $g$:**

$$E[2X - 3Y + 5] = 2 E[X] - 3 E[Y] + 5$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute $E[X]$:**

$$E[X] = \sum_{x=1}^{3}\sum_{y=1}^{3} x \cdot \dfrac{xy}{36} = \sum_{x=1}^{3} \dfrac{x^2}{36} \left(\sum_{y=1}^{3} y\right) = \dfrac{6}{36} \sum_{x=1}^{3} x^2 = \dfrac{6}{36} \cdot 14 = \dfrac{84}{36} = \dfrac{7}{3}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compute $E[Y]$:** By symmetry, $E[Y] = 7/3$.

**Step 5 ŌĆö Substitute back:**

$$E[2X - 3Y + 5] = 2 \cdot \dfrac{7}{3} - 3 \cdot \dfrac{7}{3} + 5 = \dfrac{14}{3} - 7 + 5 = \dfrac{14}{3} - 2 = \dfrac{14 - 6}{3} = \dfrac{8}{3}$$

**Final Answer:** $E[2X - 3Y + 5] = \dfrac{8}{3} \approx 2.6667$

## 3.5 Worked Example 2 ŌĆö Joint PMF Table with Full Variance Computation

**Problem:** The joint PMF of $(X, Y)$ is:

| $Y \backslash X$ | $0$ | $1$ | $2$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $0$ | $1/8$ | $1/4$ | $0$ |
| $1$ | $1/4$ | $0$ | $1/8$ |
| $2$ | $0$ | $1/8$ | $1/8$ |

Find (a) $E[X]$, (b) $E[Y]$, (c) $E[XY]$, (d) $\text{Cov}(X, Y)$, (e) $\text{Var}(2X - 3Y)$.

**Step (a) ŌĆö Compute $E[X]$:** Build the marginal $p_X(x) = \sum_y p(x, y)$:

$$p_X(0) = \tfrac{1}{8} + \tfrac{1}{4} + 0 = \tfrac{3}{8}, \quad p_X(1) = \tfrac{1}{4} + 0 + \tfrac{1}{8} = \tfrac{3}{8}, \quad p_X(2) = 0 + \tfrac{1}{8} + \tfrac{1}{8} = \tfrac{2}{8} = \tfrac{1}{4}$$

$$E[X] = 0 \cdot \tfrac{3}{8} + 1 \cdot \tfrac{3}{8} + 2 \cdot \tfrac{1}{4} = 0 + \tfrac{3}{8} + \tfrac{1}{2} = \tfrac{3}{8} + \tfrac{4}{8} = \tfrac{7}{8}$$

**Step (b) ŌĆö Compute $E[Y]$:** Build the marginal $p_Y(y) = \sum_x p(x, y)$:

$$p_Y(0) = \tfrac{1}{8} + \tfrac{1}{4} + 0 = \tfrac{3}{8}, \quad p_Y(1) = \tfrac{1}{4} + 0 + \tfrac{1}{8} = \tfrac{3}{8}, \quad p_Y(2) = 0 + \tfrac{1}{8} + \tfrac{1}{8} = \tfrac{1}{4}$$

By symmetry of the table: $E[Y] = 7/8$.

**Step (c) ŌĆö Compute $E[XY]$:**

$$E[XY] = \sum_{x}\sum_{y} xy\, p(x, y)$$

We only include cells where $p(x, y) > 0$:

$$E[XY] = (1)(1)(\tfrac{1}{4}) + (1)(2)(\tfrac{1}{8}) + (2)(1)(\tfrac{1}{8}) + (2)(2)(\tfrac{1}{8})$$

$$= \tfrac{1}{4} + \tfrac{2}{8} + \tfrac{2}{8} + \tfrac{4}{8} = \tfrac{2}{8} + \tfrac{2}{8} + \tfrac{2}{8} + \tfrac{4}{8} = \tfrac{10}{8} = \tfrac{5}{4}$$

**Step (d) ŌĆö Compute the covariance:**

$$\text{Cov}(X, Y) = E[XY] - E[X]E[Y] = \tfrac{5}{4} - \tfrac{7}{8} \cdot \tfrac{7}{8} = \tfrac{5}{4} - \tfrac{49}{64}$$

Convert to common denominator 64:

$$\text{Cov}(X, Y) = \tfrac{80}{64} - \tfrac{49}{64} = \tfrac{31}{64}$$

**Step (e) ŌĆö Compute $\text{Var}(2X - 3Y)$:** First find $\text{Var}(X)$ and $\text{Var}(Y)$:

$$E[X^2] = 0^2 \cdot \tfrac{3}{8} + 1^2 \cdot \tfrac{3}{8} + 2^2 \cdot \tfrac{1}{4} = \tfrac{3}{8} + 1 = \tfrac{11}{8}$$

$$\text{Var}(X) = E[X^2] - (E[X])^2 = \tfrac{11}{8} - \tfrac{49}{64} = \tfrac{88}{64} - \tfrac{49}{64} = \tfrac{39}{64}$$

By symmetry, $\text{Var}(Y) = 39/64$.

**Apply the variance formula:**

$$\text{Var}(2X - 3Y) = 4\, \text{Var}(X) + 9\, \text{Var}(Y) - 12\, \text{Cov}(X, Y)$$

$$= 4 \cdot \tfrac{39}{64} + 9 \cdot \tfrac{39}{64} - 12 \cdot \tfrac{31}{64}$$

$$= \tfrac{156 + 351 - 372}{64} = \tfrac{135}{64} = 2.109375$$

**Final Answers:**

$$E[X] = \tfrac{7}{8}, \quad E[Y] = \tfrac{7}{8}, \quad E[XY] = \tfrac{5}{4}, \quad \text{Cov}(X, Y) = \tfrac{31}{64}, \quad \text{Var}(2X - 3Y) = \tfrac{135}{64}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Computational Pipeline ŌĆö How to Compute $E[g(X, Y)]$

The following Mermaid flowchart captures the *exact sequence of reasoning* a student should follow when faced with a 14-mark problem on this topic.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Receive Joint PMF p of x comma y] --> stepB{Is the support S finite and listed}
    stepB -- Yes --> stepC[Identify all valid pairs x comma y with p greater than 0]
    stepB -- No --> stepD[Use the formula form of p of x comma y]
    stepC --> stepE[Identify the function g of X comma Y from the question]
    stepD --> stepE
    stepE --> stepF{Can linearity simplify g}
    stepF -- Yes --> stepG[Expand g into a times X plus b times Y plus c form]
    stepF -- No --> stepH[Compute g of x comma y at every support point]
    stepG --> stepI[Compute E of X and E of Y using the double sum]
    stepH --> stepJ[Compute g of x comma y times p of x comma y for every cell]
    stepI --> stepK[Apply linearity E of aX plus bY plus c equals a E of X plus b E of Y plus c]
    stepJ --> stepL[Perform the double summation over all support points]
    stepK --> endM[Final numerical answer]
    stepL --> endM
```

## 4.2 Relationship Map ŌĆö Quantities Derived from $E[g(X, Y)]$

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    jointPMF[Joint PMF p of x comma y] --> gFunc[Apply Function g of x comma y]
    gFunc --> expXY[Expected Value E of g of X comma Y]
    jointPMF --> margX[Marginal p of X equals sum over y of p of x comma y]
    jointPMF --> margY[Marginal p of Y equals sum over x of p of x comma y]
    margX --> eX[Mean E of X]
    margY --> eY[Mean E of Y]
    eX --> cov[Input to Covariance]
    eY --> cov
    expXY --> exy[E of X Y when g of x comma y equals x times y]
    exy --> cov
    cov --> varXY[Variance Var of aX plus bY]
    eX --> varX[Variance Var of X]
    eY --> varY[Variance Var of Y]
    varX --> varXY
    varY --> varXY
    varXY --> corr[Correlation Coefficient rho of X comma Y]
    cov --> corr
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Architecture ŌĆö Engineering Interpretation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    sysA[Random Input X from Source A] --> mdlX[Encoder g of X comma Y]
    sysB[Random Input Y from Source B] --> mdlX
    mdlX --> outputG[Output g of X comma Y a real number]
    outputG --> weighting[Multiplied by Joint Probability p of x comma y]
    weighting --> summation[Double Summation over Support S]
    summation --> expVal[Expected Value E of g of X comma Y]
    expVal --> app1[Decision Making in Engineering]
    expVal --> app2[Risk Assessment]
    expVal --> app3[Quality Control Bounds]
    expVal --> app4[Financial Portfolio Optimisation]
```
