<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]**
> **Q1.** Define the expected value of a function $g(X, Y)$ of two discrete random variables. State the condition under which it exists.

**Model Answer:**

The expected value of a function $g(X, Y)$ of two discrete random variables $X$ and $Y$, with joint PMF $p(x, y)$, is defined as:

$$E[g(X, Y)] = \sum_{x}\sum_{y} g(x, y)\, p(x, y)$$

It **exists** if and only if the series converges absolutely, i.e.:

$$\sum_{x}\sum_{y} \vert g(x, y) \vert\, p(x, y) < \infty$$

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]**
> **Q2.** State and explain the **linearity of expectation** for two random variables. Why is it valid even when the variables are not independent?

**Model Answer:**

**Statement:** For any constants $a, b, c$ and any functions $g, h$:

$$E[a\,g(X,Y) + b\,h(X,Y) + c] = a\,E[g(X,Y)] + b\,E[h(X,Y)] + c$$

**Explanation:** It follows directly from the linearity of the summation operator ŌĆö we can split the double sum into separate double sums, and the inner $y$-summation yields the marginal PMF, which is well-defined whether or not $X$ and $Y$ are independent.

**Why independence is not required:** Because expectation is a *linear* operator, and linearity is a property of the sum, not of independence. The identity $E[XY] = E[X]E[Y]$ is what *requires* independence, not the linearity of $E[X + Y] = E[X] + E[Y]$.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) ŌĆö Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
>
> **Question A (14 Marks)**
>
> The joint probability mass function of $(X, Y)$ is given by:
>
> $$p(x, y) = \dfrac{(x + y)}{21}, \quad x = 1, 2;\; y = 1, 2, 3$$
>
> (a) Verify that $p(x, y)$ is a valid joint PMF.  
> (b) Compute $E[X]$, $E[Y]$, $E[XY]$, and hence find $\text{Cov}(X, Y)$ and the correlation coefficient $\rho(X, Y)$.

### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) ŌĆö Verification (3 marks):**

$$\sum_{x=1}^{2}\sum_{y=1}^{3} \dfrac{(x+y)}{21} = \dfrac{1}{21}\left[\sum_{x=1}^{2}\sum_{y=1}^{3} x + \sum_{x=1}^{2}\sum_{y=1}^{3} y\right]$$

$$= \dfrac{1}{21}\left[3(1+2) + 2(1+2+3)\right] = \dfrac{1}{21}[9 + 12] = \dfrac{21}{21} = 1 \quad\checkmark$$

All terms $p(x, y) = (x+y)/21 > 0$ for the given support, so it is a valid joint PMF.

**Part (b) ŌĆö Moments and Covariance (11 marks):**

**Compute $E[X]$:**

$$E[X] = \sum_{x=1}^{2}\sum_{y=1}^{3} x \cdot \dfrac{(x+y)}{21} = \dfrac{1}{21} \sum_{x=1}^{2} x \sum_{y=1}^{3} (x+y)$$

Inner sum: $\sum_{y=1}^{3}(x + y) = 3x + (1+2+3) = 3x + 6$.

$$E[X] = \dfrac{1}{21} \sum_{x=1}^{2} x(3x + 6) = \dfrac{1}{21} \left[1 \cdot 9 + 2 \cdot 12\right] = \dfrac{9 + 24}{21} = \dfrac{33}{21} = \dfrac{11}{7}$$

**Compute $E[Y]$:**

$$E[Y] = \sum_{x=1}^{2}\sum_{y=1}^{3} y \cdot \dfrac{(x+y)}{21} = \dfrac{1}{21} \sum_{y=1}^{3} y \sum_{x=1}^{2} (x+y)$$

Inner sum: $\sum_{x=1}^{2}(x + y) = (1+2) + 2y = 3 + 2y$.

$$E[Y] = \dfrac{1}{21} \sum_{y=1}^{3} y(3 + 2y) = \dfrac{1}{21}\left[1 \cdot 5 + 2 \cdot 7 + 3 \cdot 9\right] = \dfrac{5 + 14 + 27}{21} = \dfrac{46}{21}$$

**Compute $E[XY]$:**

$$E[XY] = \dfrac{1}{21} \sum_{x=1}^{2}\sum_{y=1}^{3} xy(x+y) = \dfrac{1}{21} \sum_{x=1}^{2}\sum_{y=1}^{3} (x^2 y + x y^2)$$

Split into two sums:

$$E[XY] = \dfrac{1}{21}\left[\left(\sum_{x=1}^{2} x^2\right)\left(\sum_{y=1}^{3} y\right) + \left(\sum_{x=1}^{2} x\right)\left(\sum_{y=1}^{3} y^2\right)\right]$$

$$= \dfrac{1}{21}\left[(1+4)(1+2+3) + (1+2)(1+4+9)\right] = \dfrac{1}{21}[5 \cdot 6 + 3 \cdot 14] = \dfrac{30 + 42}{21} = \dfrac{72}{21} = \dfrac{24}{7}$$

**Compute $\text{Cov}(X, Y)$:**

$$\text{Cov}(X, Y) = E[XY] - E[X]E[Y] = \dfrac{24}{7} - \dfrac{11}{7} \cdot \dfrac{46}{21} = \dfrac{24}{7} - \dfrac{506}{147}$$

Common denominator 147:

$$\text{Cov}(X, Y) = \dfrac{504}{147} - \dfrac{506}{147} = -\dfrac{2}{147}$$

**Compute $\rho(X, Y)$:** Need $\sigma_X, \sigma_Y$:

$$E[X^2] = \dfrac{1}{21} \sum_{x=1}^{2} x^2 (3x+6) = \dfrac{1}{21}\left[1 \cdot 9 + 4 \cdot 12\right] = \dfrac{57}{21} = \dfrac{19}{7}$$

$$\text{Var}(X) = \dfrac{19}{7} - \left(\dfrac{11}{7}\right)^2 = \dfrac{19}{7} - \dfrac{121}{49} = \dfrac{133 - 121}{49} = \dfrac{12}{49}$$

$$E[Y^2] = \dfrac{1}{21} \sum_{y=1}^{3} y^2(3+2y) = \dfrac{1}{21}\left[1 \cdot 5 + 4 \cdot 7 + 9 \cdot 9\right] = \dfrac{5+28+81}{21} = \dfrac{114}{21} = \dfrac{38}{7}$$

$$\text{Var}(Y) = \dfrac{38}{7} - \left(\dfrac{46}{21}\right)^2 = \dfrac{38}{7} - \dfrac{2116}{441} = \dfrac{2394 - 2116}{441} = \dfrac{278}{441} = \dfrac{278}{441}$$

Simplify: $\dfrac{278}{441}$ ŌĆö check GCD(278, 441) = 1, so it is already in lowest terms.

$$\sigma_X = \sqrt{12/49} = \dfrac{2\sqrt{3}}{7}, \quad \sigma_Y = \sqrt{278/441} = \dfrac{\sqrt{278}}{21}$$

$$\rho(X, Y) = \dfrac{\text{Cov}(X, Y)}{\sigma_X \sigma_Y} = \dfrac{-2/147}{(2\sqrt{3}/7)(\sqrt{278}/21)} = \dfrac{-2/147}{2\sqrt{834}/147} = \dfrac{-1}{\sqrt{834}}$$

$$\rho(X, Y) \approx -0.0346$$

---

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Paper 2024 Scheme ŌĆö Alternative]**
>
> **Question B (14 Marks)**
>
> The joint PMF of two discrete random variables $X$ and $Y$ is given by:
>
> $$p(x, y) = k(x + y), \quad x = 0, 1, 2;\; y = 0, 1$$
>
> (a) Find the value of $k$ and the marginal PMFs of $X$ and $Y$.  
> (b) Compute $E[X]$, $E[Y]$, $\text{Var}(X)$, $\text{Var}(Y)$, and $\text{Cov}(X, Y)$. Is the covariance zero? Comment.

### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) ŌĆö Find $k$ and marginal PMFs (7 marks):**

Using $\sum_x \sum_y p(x, y) = 1$:

$$\sum_{x=0}^{2}\sum_{y=0}^{1} k(x + y) = k\left[\sum_{x=0}^{2}\sum_{y=0}^{1} x + \sum_{x=0}^{2}\sum_{y=0}^{1} y\right]$$

$$= k\left[2(0+1+2) + 3(0+1)\right] = k[6 + 3] = 9k = 1 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; k = \dfrac{1}{9}$$

**Compute the joint PMF table:**

| $Y \backslash X$ | $0$ | $1$ | $2$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $0$ | $0$ | $1/9$ | $2/9$ |
| $1$ | $1/9$ | $2/9$ | $3/9$ |

**Marginal of $X$:**

$$p_X(0) = 0 + \tfrac{1}{9} = \tfrac{1}{9}, \quad p_X(1) = \tfrac{1}{9} + \tfrac{2}{9} = \tfrac{3}{9} = \tfrac{1}{3}, \quad p_X(2) = \tfrac{2}{9} + \tfrac{3}{9} = \tfrac{5}{9}$$

**Marginal of $Y$:**

$$p_Y(0) = 0 + \tfrac{1}{9} + \tfrac{2}{9} = \tfrac{3}{9} = \tfrac{1}{3}, \quad p_Y(1) = \tfrac{1}{9} + \tfrac{2}{9} + \tfrac{3}{9} = \tfrac{6}{9} = \tfrac{2}{3}$$

**Part (b) ŌĆö Moments and Covariance (7 marks):**

$$E[X] = 0 \cdot \tfrac{1}{9} + 1 \cdot \tfrac{3}{9} + 2 \cdot \tfrac{5}{9} = \tfrac{3 + 10}{9} = \dfrac{13}{9}$$

$$E[Y] = 0 \cdot \tfrac{1}{3} + 1 \cdot \tfrac{2}{3} = \dfrac{2}{3}$$

$$E[X^2] = 0 + 1 \cdot \tfrac{3}{9} + 4 \cdot \tfrac{5}{9} = \tfrac{3 + 20}{9} = \dfrac{23}{9}$$

$$\text{Var}(X) = \dfrac{23}{9} - \left(\dfrac{13}{9}\right)^2 = \dfrac{23}{9} - \dfrac{169}{81} = \dfrac{207 - 169}{81} = \dfrac{38}{81}$$

$$E[Y^2] = 0 + 1 \cdot \tfrac{2}{3} = \dfrac{2}{3} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \text{Var}(Y) = \dfrac{2}{3} - \dfrac{4}{9} = \dfrac{2}{9}$$

**Compute $E[XY]$:**

$$E[XY] = \sum_{x}\sum_{y} xy \cdot \tfrac{1}{9}(x + y) = \tfrac{1}{9} \sum_{x}\sum_{y} (x^2 y + x y^2)$$

$$= \tfrac{1}{9}\left[\left(\sum_{x=0}^{2} x^2\right)\left(\sum_{y=0}^{1} y\right) + \left(\sum_{x=0}^{2} x\right)\left(\sum_{y=0}^{1} y^2\right)\right]$$

$$= \tfrac{1}{9}\left[5 \cdot 1 + 3 \cdot 1\right] = \dfrac{8}{9}$$

$$\text{Cov}(X, Y) = E[XY] - E[X]E[Y] = \dfrac{8}{9} - \dfrac{13}{9} \cdot \dfrac{2}{3} = \dfrac{8}{9} - \dfrac{26}{27}$$

$$= \dfrac{24 - 26}{27} = -\dfrac{2}{27}$$

**Comment:** The covariance is **not zero** ($= -2/27$). Therefore $X$ and $Y$ are **not independent** ŌĆö they are in fact *negatively correlated*. The condition $\text{Cov}(X,Y) = 0$ is necessary but not sufficient for independence in general.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls ŌĆö Read Carefully!**
> 1. **Do NOT compute $p_X$ and $p_Y$ incorrectly by missing cells.** Many students forget that the marginal is the **row sum** (for $p_X$) or **column sum** (for $p_Y$) of the joint table.
> 2. **Do NOT write $E[XY] = E[X]E[Y]$ unless independence is explicitly given.** This single mistake costs **2 full marks** in most valuation schemes.
> 3. **Do NOT forget to verify the joint PMF sums to 1.** This is the first sanity check; failure to do so wastes 2ŌĆō3 marks if $k$ is wrong.
> 4. **Always state the unit and form of the final answer** (fraction, decimal). Examiners penalise "calculator dumps".
> 5. **In correlation coefficient questions**, present the final $\rho$ value in the range $[-1, 1]$ ŌĆö if it falls outside, you have made an arithmetic error.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist ŌĆö KTU 2024 Module 1**

- **Master Definition:** $E[g(X,Y)] = \sum_x \sum_y g(x, y) p(x, y)$ ŌĆö must converge **absolutely**.
- **Validity Check:** Always confirm $\sum_x \sum_y p(x, y) = 1$ and $p(x, y) \ge 0$ for all $(x, y) \in S$.
- **Marginalisation:** $p_X(x) = \sum_y p(x, y)$ and $p_Y(y) = \sum_x p(x, y)$.
- **Linearity of $E$:** $E[aX + bY + c] = aE[X] + bE[Y] + c$ ŌĆö **no independence needed**.
- **Independence Multiplication:** $E[XY] = E[X]E[Y]$ ŌĆö **only when** $X \perp Y$.
- **Covariance Identity:** $\text{Cov}(X, Y) = E[XY] - E[X]E[Y]$ ŌĆö sign indicates direction of linear association.
- **Variance of a Linear Combination:** $\text{Var}(aX + bY) = a^2 \text{Var}(X) + b^2 \text{Var}(Y) + 2ab\, \text{Cov}(X, Y)$.
- **Correlation Coefficient:** $\rho(X, Y) = \text{Cov}(X, Y) / (\sigma_X \sigma_Y)$, with $-1 \le \rho \le 1$.
- **Independent Ō¤╣ Uncorrelated**, but **Uncorrelated ŌćÅ Independent** (in general).
- **Variance Shortcut:** $\text{Var}(X) = E[X^2] - (E[X])^2$ ŌĆö the most-used computational formula.
- **Engineering Interpretation:** $E[g(X, Y)]$ is the long-run average outcome of a two-source random system ŌĆö used in risk analysis, portfolio theory, and reliability engineering.
- **Common Board Trap:** When $g$ is **not linear** (e.g. $g(X,Y) = X^2 Y$), you **cannot** use linearity ŌĆö you must compute the full double sum.
- **Symmetry Exploit:** If the joint PMF table is symmetric about the diagonal, then $E[X] = E[Y]$, $\text{Var}(X) = \text{Var}(Y)$, and $\text{Cov}(X, Y)$ is easy to compute.
- **Order of Summation:** Reversing the order of $\sum_x$ and $\sum_y$ gives the same answer (Fubini for finite/countable absolutely convergent series).
- **Final Answer Hygiene:** Reduce fractions; express decimals only when the problem demands; always box the final numerical value.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Continuous Random Variables and Their Probability Distributions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GCMAT401 - MATHEMATICS FOR PHYSICAL SCIENCE AND LIFE SCIENCE ŌĆō 4
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Continuous Random Variables and Their Probability Distributions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of a Continuous Random Variable

A **Continuous Random Variable (CRV)** is a random variable $X$ that can take an uncountably infinite number of possible values within a given interval (typically $\mathbb{R}$ or a subset of $\mathbb{R}$). Unlike discrete random variables, the probability that a continuous random variable takes any *single* exact value is **zero**. Instead, probabilities are assigned to *intervals* or *ranges* of values.

Mathematically, $X$ is continuous if its **Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF)**, denoted $F(x) = P(X \leq x)$, is a continuous function for all $x \in \mathbb{R}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (GCMAT401 ŌĆō Module 2):**
> A random variable $X$ is said to be continuous if there exists a function $f(x) \geq 0$ such that for every interval $[a, b]$,
> $$P(a \leq X \leq b) = \int_{a}^{b} f(x) \, dx$$
> The function $f(x)$ is called the **Probability Density Function (PDF)** of $X$.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are measuring the **exact height** of every student in your KTU college.
- You will get values like 165.4 cm, 172.83 cm, 158.912 cm... ŌĆö these are **uncountably infinite** possibilities.
- The probability that a randomly chosen student is *exactly* 170.0000000... cm tall is practically **zero**.
- But, the probability that a student is between **165 cm and 175 cm** is meaningful and measurable.

Think of the PDF as a **"smooth histogram"** ŌĆö instead of bars, imagine a continuous curve where the *area under the curve* between two points gives the probability. The total area under the entire PDF curve must equal **1** (certainty).

### 1.3 Probability Density Function (PDF) ŌĆö Properties

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Properties of a Valid PDF $f(x)$:**
> 1. **Non-negativity:** $f(x) \geq 0$ for all $x \in \mathbb{R}$
> 2. **Normalization:** $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x) \, dx = 1$
> 3. **Point Probability:** $P(X = c) = 0$ for any single value $c$
> 4. **Interval Probability:** $P(a < X < b) = P(a \leq X \leq b) = \int_{a}^{b} f(x) \, dx$

### 1.4 Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF)

The CDF gives the probability that $X$ is *less than or equal to* a given value $x$:
$$F(x) = P(X \leq x) = \int_{-\infty}^{x} f(t) \, dt$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key CDF Properties:**
> - $0 \leq F(x) \leq 1$ for all $x$
> - $F(-\infty) = 0$ and $F(\infty) = 1$
> - $F(x)$ is **non-decreasing**
> - $F(x)$ is **right-continuous**
> - $F'(x) = f(x)$ (the PDF is the derivative of the CDF wherever it exists)

### 1.5 Real-World Engineering Examples

| Phenomenon | Type of Continuous Variable |
|---|---|
| Lifetime of an electronic component | Time (hours) |
| Voltage fluctuation in a circuit | Voltage (V) |
| Wind speed measurement | Speed (m/s) |
| Reaction time in chemical processes | Time (s) |
| Noise amplitude in communication | Amplitude (dB) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic: How Continuous Probability Works

The key paradigm shift from discrete to continuous probability:

- **Discrete:** Probabilities are *sums* of point masses: $P(X = k) = p_k$
- **Continuous:** Probabilities are *integrals* of density: $P(a \leq X \leq b) = \int_a^b f(x) \, dx$

Think of $f(x)$ as a **"probability concentration rate"** at the point $x$. High $f(x)$ means values near $x$ are *more likely*; low $f(x)$ means they are *less likely*. But $f(x)$ itself is *not* a probability ŌĆö only the *area under $f$* over an interval represents probability.

### 2.2 Expected Value (Mean) of a Continuous Random Variable

The expected value is the **"center of mass"** of the probability distribution, analogous to a weighted average where $f(x)$ acts as the weight function:
$$E(X) = \mu = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x \cdot f(x) \, dx$$

More generally, for a function $g(X)$:
$$E[g(X)] = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} g(x) \cdot f(x) \, dx$$

### 2.3 Variance and Standard Deviation

The **variance** measures the average squared deviation from the mean ŌĆö a measure of *spread*:
$$\text{Var}(X) = \sigma^2 = E[(X - \mu)^2] = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} (x - \mu)^2 \, f(x) \, dx$$

Equivalently (computational formula):
$$\text{Var}(X) = E(X^2) - [E(X)]^2$$

where $E(X^2) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x^2 \cdot f(x) \, dx$.

The **standard deviation** is $\sigma = \sqrt{\text{Var}(X)}$.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Formula Reference for Module 2 ŌĆö Continuous Distributions**

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | PDF Normalization | $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x) \, dx = 1$ | Mandatory for valid PDF |
| 2 | CDF Definition | $F(x) = \int_{-\infty}^{x} f(t) \, dt$ | Cumulative probability |
| 3 | PDF from CDF | $f(x) = \dfrac{d}{dx} F(x)$ | At points of differentiability |
| 4 | Interval Probability | $P(a \leq X \leq b) = F(b) - F(a) = \int_{a}^{b} f(x) \, dx$ | Core calculation |
| 5 | Mean | $\mu = E(X) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x \cdot f(x) \, dx$ | First moment |
| 6 | $E(X^2)$ | $E(X^2) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x^2 \cdot f(x) \, dx$ | Second raw moment |
| 7 | Variance | $\sigma^2 = E(X^2) - \mu^2$ | Computational formula |
| 8 | Median | $F(m) = 0.5$ | Half the mass on each side |
| 9 | **Uniform PDF** | $f(x) = \dfrac{1}{b-a}, \quad a \leq x \leq b$ | Constant density |
| 10 | **Uniform Mean** | $\mu = \dfrac{a+b}{2}$ | Midpoint |
| 11 | **Uniform Variance** | $\sigma^2 = \dfrac{(b-a)^2}{12}$ | Spread formula |
| 12 | **Exponential PDF** | $f(x) = \lambda e^{-\lambda x}, \quad x \geq 0, \lambda > 0$ | Memoryless property |
| 13 | **Exponential Mean** | $\mu = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Decay parameter inverse |
| 14 | **Exponential Variance** | $\sigma^2 = \dfrac{1}{\lambda^2}$ | Square of mean |
| 15 | **Normal PDF** | $f(x) = \dfrac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{x-\mu}{\sigma}\right)^2}$ | Bell curve |
| 16 | **Standard Normal** | $Z = \dfrac{X - \mu}{\sigma}$ | Zero mean, unit variance |
| 17 | **Standardization** | $P(a \leq X \leq b) = P\!\left(\dfrac{a-\mu}{\sigma} \leq Z \leq \dfrac{b-\mu}{\sigma}\right)$ | Convert to Z |

### 2.5 The Three Pillars of Continuous Distributions (KTU Focus)

**Distribution 1: Uniform Distribution $U(a, b)$**
- Models situations where *every value in an interval is equally likely*
- **Example:** Random arrival of a bus in a 10-minute window
- Density is **flat** ŌĆö same height $1/(b-a)$ throughout the interval

**Distribution 2: Exponential Distribution $\text{Exp}(\lambda)$**
- Models the **waiting time** between events in a Poisson process
- **Key Property:** Memoryless ŌĆö $P(X > s + t \mid X > s) = P(X > t)$
- **Example:** Lifetime of a capacitor, time between radioactive decays
- Always **decreasing** density starting from $\lambda$ at $x=0$

**Distribution 3: Normal (Gaussian) Distribution $N(\mu, \sigma^2)$**
- The **most important** distribution in statistics ŌĆö Central Limit Theorem
- Symmetric, bell-shaped curve centered at $\mu$
- **Example:** Measurement errors, heights, IQ scores, blood pressure
- 68-95-99.7 rule (empirical rule)

### 2.6 Why This Matters in Engineering

In real KTU engineering fields:
- **Electronics:** Gaussian noise analysis in communication channels
- **Mechanical:** Tolerance stacks using normal distributions
- **Civil:** Material strength variability, load distributions
- **Computer Science:** Queueing theory uses exponential distributions
- **Reliability Engineering:** Component lifetime modeling with exponential/Weibull
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Why $F'(x) = f(x)$

We start from the definition of the CDF:
$$F(x) = \int_{-\infty}^{x} f(t) \, dt$$

By the **Fundamental Theorem of Calculus** (Leibniz rule), differentiating an integral with respect to its upper limit yields the integrand evaluated at that limit:

$$F'(x) = \frac{d}{dx} \int_{-\infty}^{x} f(t) \, dt = f(x)$$

This holds at every point $x$ where $f(x)$ is continuous. This relationship is the **bridge** between CDF and PDF ŌĆö knowing one lets you find the other.

### 3.2 Derivation: Mean of the Uniform Distribution

For $X \sim U(a, b)$, the PDF is:
$$f(x) = \frac{1}{b-a}, \quad a \leq x \leq b$$

**Step 1:** Apply the definition of mean
$$\mu = E(X) = \int_{a}^{b} x \cdot f(x) \, dx$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the PDF
$$\mu = \int_{a}^{b} x \cdot \frac{1}{b-a} \, dx = \frac{1}{b-a} \int_{a}^{b} x \, dx$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the integral using $\int x \, dx = \frac{x^2}{2}$
$$\mu = \frac{1}{b-a} \left[ \frac{x^2}{2} \right]_{a}^{b}$$

**Step 4:** Apply limits
$$\mu = \frac{1}{b-a} \cdot \frac{b^2 - a^2}{2}$$

**Step 5:** Factor using difference of squares: $b^2 - a^2 = (b-a)(b+a)$
$$\mu = \frac{1}{b-a} \cdot \frac{(b-a)(b+a)}{2}$$

**Step 6:** Cancel $(b-a)$
$$\boxed{\mu = \frac{a+b}{2}}$$

This confirms the intuitive result: the mean of a uniform distribution is the **midpoint** of the interval.

### 3.3 Derivation: Variance of the Uniform Distribution

**Step 1:** Compute $E(X^2)$ first
$$E(X^2) = \int_{a}^{b} x^2 \cdot \frac{1}{b-a} \, dx = \frac{1}{b-a} \left[ \frac{x^3}{3} \right]_{a}^{b}$$

$$E(X^2) = \frac{1}{b-a} \cdot \frac{b^3 - a^3}{3}$$

**Step 2:** Factor: $b^3 - a^3 = (b-a)(b^2 + ab + a^2)$
$$E(X^2) = \frac{b^2 + ab + a^2}{3}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the computational formula
$$\sigma^2 = E(X^2) - \mu^2 = \frac{b^2 + ab + a^2}{3} - \left(\frac{a+b}{2}\right)^2$$

**Step 4:** Expand $\left(\frac{a+b}{2}\right)^2 = \frac{a^2 + 2ab + b^2}{4}$
$$\sigma^2 = \frac{b^2 + ab + a^2}{3} - \frac{a^2 + 2ab + b^2}{4}$$

**Step 5:** Find common denominator 12
$$\sigma^2 = \frac{4(b^2 + ab + a^2) - 3(a^2 + 2ab + b^2)}{12}$$

$$\sigma^2 = \frac{4b^2 + 4ab + 4a^2 - 3a^2 - 6ab - 3b^2}{12}$$

**Step 6:** Combine like terms
$$\sigma^2 = \frac{(4b^2 - 3b^2) + (4ab - 6ab) + (4a^2 - 3a^2)}{12} = \frac{b^2 - 2ab + a^2}{12}$$

**Step 7:** Recognize perfect square: $b^2 - 2ab + a^2 = (b-a)^2$
$$\boxed{\sigma^2 = \frac{(b-a)^2}{12}}$$

### 3.4 Derivation: Mean of the Exponential Distribution

For $X \sim \text{Exp}(\lambda)$: $f(x) = \lambda e^{-\lambda x}, x \geq 0$.

**Step 1:** Set up the integral
$$\mu = \int_{0}^{\infty} x \cdot \lambda e^{-\lambda x} \, dx$$

**Step 2:** Use integration by parts with $u = x$, $dv = \lambda e^{-\lambda x} \, dx$
- $du = dx$, $v = -e^{-\lambda x}$

$$\mu = \left[ x \cdot (-e^{-\lambda x}) \right]_{0}^{\infty} - \int_{0}^{\infty} (-e^{-\lambda x}) \, dx$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the boundary term
- At $x = \infty$: $x e^{-\lambda x} \to 0$ (exponential dominates polynomial)
- At $x = 0$: $0 \cdot (-1) = 0$
- So boundary contribution is $0$

$$\mu = 0 + \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda x} \, dx$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate the remaining integral
$$\mu = \left[ \frac{-e^{-\lambda x}}{\lambda} \right]_{0}^{\infty} = 0 - \left(\frac{-1}{\lambda}\right) = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

$$\boxed{\mu = \frac{1}{\lambda}}$$

### 3.5 Derivation: Mean and Variance of the Normal Distribution

For $X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$:
$$f(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left( -\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2} \right)$$

**Mean Derivation:** Use substitution $z = \frac{x-\mu}{\sigma}$, so $x = \mu + \sigma z$, $dx = \sigma \, dz$:
$$E(X) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x \cdot \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-z^2/2} \, dx$$

$$E(X) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} (\mu + \sigma z) \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-z^2/2} \, dz$$

$$E(X) = \mu \underbrace{\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-z^2/2} \, dz}_{= 1} + \sigma \underbrace{\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{z}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-z^2/2} \, dz}_{= 0 \text{ (odd function)}}$$

$$\boxed{E(X) = \mu}$$

The second integral vanishes because $z e^{-z^2/2}$ is an **odd function** integrated over a symmetric interval. This is a fundamental symmetry property of the normal distribution.

**Variance Derivation:** Similarly, for $E(X^2) = \mu^2 + \sigma^2$:
$$\text{Var}(X) = E(X^2) - \mu^2 = \sigma^2$$

$$\boxed{\text{Var}(X) = \sigma^2}$$

### 3.6 Derivation: Memoryless Property of Exponential

We prove: $P(X > s + t \mid X > s) = P(X > t)$ for $s, t \geq 0$.

**Step 1:** Apply the conditional probability formula
$$P(X > s + t \mid X > s) = \frac{P(X > s + t \text{ and } X > s)}{P(X > s)}$$

**Step 2:** Since $s + t \geq s$, the intersection is $X > s + t$
$$= \frac{P(X > s + t)}{P(X > s)}$$

**Step 3:** For exponential, $P(X > x) = e^{-\lambda x}$
$$= \frac{e^{-\lambda(s+t)}}{e^{-\lambda s}} = \frac{e^{-\lambda s} \cdot e^{-\lambda t}}{e^{-\lambda s}} = e^{-\lambda t}$$

**Step 4:** Recognize the result
$$= P(X > t) \quad \blacksquare$$

This means: *the exponential distribution "forgets" the past*. A component that has already lasted $s$ hours behaves like a brand new component.

### 3.7 The 68-95-99.7 Empirical Rule (Normal Distribution)

For $X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$:
- $P(\mu - \sigma \leq X \leq \mu + \sigma) \approx 0.6827$ (**68.27%**)
- $P(\mu - 2\sigma \leq X \leq \mu + 2\sigma) \approx 0.9545$ (**95.45%**)
- $P(\mu - 3\sigma \leq X \leq \mu + 3\sigma) \approx 0.9973$ (**99.73%**)

These are derived from integrating the standard normal PDF numerically and are **extremely high-yield** for KTU problems.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: The Relationship Between PDF, CDF, and Probability

This diagram shows how a continuous random variable's properties are interconnected through calculus operations.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Continuous Random Variable X] --> B[Define Range and Density f of x]
    B --> C{Is f of x Valid PDF?}
    C -->|No| D[Check non-negativity and total area equals 1]
    D --> B
    C -->|Yes| E[Compute CDF by integration]
    E --> F[F of x equals integral from minus infinity to x of f of t dt]
    F --> G[Find Interval Probability]
    G --> H[P of a to X to b equals integral from a to b of f of x dx]
    H --> I[Also equals F of b minus F of a]
    I --> J[Compute Mean]
    J --> K[Mean equals integral of x times f of x dx]
    K --> L[Compute Variance]
    L --> M[Variance equals E of X squared minus mean squared]
    M --> N[Identify Distribution Type]
    N --> O[Uniform: f of x equals 1 over b minus a]
    N --> P[Exponential: f of x equals lambda e power minus lambda x]
    N --> Q[Normal: Bell shaped with mean mu and variance sigma squared]
    O --> R[Apply KTU Problem Formulas]
    P --> R
    Q --> R
    R --> S[Final Numerical Answer]
```

### 4.2 Flow Diagram: Standardization Process for Normal Distribution

This shows the systematic procedure to convert any normal random variable to the standard normal $Z \sim N(0, 1)$.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Given X with mean mu and variance sigma squared] --> B[Subtract mu from X]
    B --> C[Divide by sigma]
    C --> D[Standard Normal Variable Z]
    D --> E[Z has mean 0 and variance 1]
    E --> F[Look up Phi of z in Z table]
    F --> G{Compute Required Probability}
    G --> H[Case 1: P of X less than a equals Phi of a minus mu over sigma]
    G --> I[Case 2: P of X greater than a equals 1 minus Phi of a minus mu over sigma]
    G --> J[Case 3: P of a to X to b equals Phi of b minus mu over sigma minus Phi of a minus mu over sigma]
    H --> K[Final Answer using Z values]
    I --> K
    J --> K
```

### 4.3 Block Architecture: Decision Tree for Identifying Continuous Distribution

This block-level functional architecture helps KTU students identify which continuous distribution to apply based on the problem's characteristics.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Read Problem Statement] --> B{What is being measured?}
    B -->|Time Duration| C{Is it time between events?}
    C -->|Yes| D[Use Exponential Distribution]
    C -->|No| E{Equal likelihood in interval?}
    E -->|Yes| F[Use Uniform Distribution]
    B -->|Measurement Error or Natural Variation| G[Use Normal Distribution]
    B -->|Position in Fixed Interval| H{Finite range given?}
    H -->|Yes| I[Use Uniform Distribution]
    B -->|Unknown but Symmetric and Bell Shaped| J[Use Normal Distribution]
    D --> K[Apply Formulas mean equals 1 by lambda, variance equals 1 by lambda squared]
    F --> L[Apply Formulas mean equals a plus b over 2, variance equals b minus a squared over 12]
    G --> M[Apply Standardization Z equals X minus mu over sigma]
    I --> L
    J --> M
    K --> N[Solve for KTU Problem]
    L --> N
    M --> N
```

### 4.4 Conceptual Map: Memoryless Property Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Component Starts Operating] --> B[Survives s hours]
    B --> C{Probability of surviving additional t hours?}
    C --> D[For Exponential: Equal to P of X greater than t]
    C --> E[For Normal or Uniform: Not Equal, Depends on Age s]
    D --> F[Memoryless Property Holds]
    E --> G[Memoryless Property Fails]
    F --> H[Future is Independent of Past]
    G --> I[Future Depends on Current Age]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** These mermaid diagrams serve as visual study aids to help KTU students understand the logical flow when solving continuous probability problems. The arrows represent the procedural sequence to follow during examination.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
>
> **Q1.** Define a continuous random variable. State any two properties of its probability density function.
>
> **Model Answer:**
> A **continuous random variable (CRV)** is a random variable that can take all possible values in a given interval (uncountably infinite set of values).
>
> **Two properties of PDF $f(x)$:**
> 1. $f(x) \geq 0$ for all $x \in \mathbb{R}$ (non-negativity)
> 2. $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x) \, dx = 1$ (total probability equals 1)
>
> *(2 marks for definition, 1 mark for properties ŌĆö typical KTU marking scheme)*

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024, Model Paper 2]**
>
> **Q2.** If $X \sim N(50, 16)$, find $P(45 \leq X \leq 55)$.
>
> **Model Answer:**
> Here $\mu = 50$, $\sigma^2 = 16$, so $\sigma = 4$.
>
> Standardize:
> $$Z = \frac{X - 50}{4}$$
>
> $$P(45 \leq X \leq 55) = P\left(\frac{45-50}{4} \leq Z \leq \frac{55-50}{4}\right) = P(-1.25 \leq Z \leq 1.25)$$
>
> Using standard normal table:
> $$= \Phi(1.25) - \Phi(-1.25) = 0.8944 - 0.1056 = 0.7888$$
>
> **Answer:** $P(45 \leq X \leq 55) \approx 0.7888$

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [Choice 1]**

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Paper, Module 2]**
>
> **(a)** A continuous random variable $X$ has the probability density function:
> $$f(x) = \begin{cases} kx^2, & 0 \leq x \leq 3 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$
> Find: **(i)** the value of $k$, **(ii)** $P(1 \leq X \leq 2)$, **(iii)** the CDF $F(x)$. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** The time (in hours) until a machine fails follows an exponential distribution with mean $4$ hours. Find: **(i)** the probability that the machine will last more than $5$ hours, **(ii)** the probability that it will last between $2$ and $6$ hours given it has already lasted $3$ hours. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a)(i): Find $k$**

Apply the normalization condition:
$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x) \, dx = 1$$

$$\int_{0}^{3} kx^2 \, dx = 1$$

$$k \left[ \frac{x^3}{3} \right]_{0}^{3} = 1$$

$$k \cdot \frac{27}{3} = 1 \implies 9k = 1 \implies k = \frac{1}{9}$$

**Part (a)(ii): Find $P(1 \leq X \leq 2)$**

$$P(1 \leq X \leq 2) = \int_{1}^{2} \frac{1}{9} x^2 \, dx$$

$$= \frac{1}{9} \left[ \frac{x^3}{3} \right]_{1}^{2} = \frac{1}{9} \cdot \frac{8 - 1}{3} = \frac{7}{27} \approx 0.2593$$

**Part (a)(iii): Find $F(x)$**

For $x < 0$: $F(x) = 0$

For $0 \leq x \leq 3$:
$$F(x) = \int_{0}^{x} \frac{1}{9} t^2 \, dt = \frac{1}{9} \cdot \frac{x^3}{3} = \frac{x^3}{27}$$

For $x > 3$: $F(x) = 1$

$$\boxed{F(x) = \begin{cases} 0, & x < 0 \\ \dfrac{x^3}{27}, & 0 \leq x \leq 3 \\ 1, & x > 3 \end{cases}}$$

**Part (b)(i): Probability machine lasts more than 5 hours**

Given mean $= 4 = 1/\lambda$, so $\lambda = 1/4 = 0.25$.

For exponential distribution: $P(X > x) = e^{-\lambda x}$

$$P(X > 5) = e^{-(0.25)(5)} = e^{-1.25} \approx 0.2865$$

**Part (b)(ii): Memoryless property application**

We need $P(2 \leq X \leq 6 \mid X \geq 3)$.

By the **memoryless property** of exponential:
$$P(2 \leq X \leq 6 \mid X \geq 3) = P(X \leq 3 \mid X \geq 3) = P(0 \leq X' \leq 3)$$

where $X'$ is a fresh exponential. Using $\mu = 4$:

$$P(X' \leq 3) = 1 - P(X' > 3) = 1 - e^{-0.25 \times 3} = 1 - e^{-0.75}$$

$$= 1 - 0.4724 = 0.5276$$

**Answers:**
- (a)(i) $k = 1/9$, (a)(ii) $P = 7/27$, (a)(iii) CDF as above
- (b)(i) $P(X>5) = e^{-1.25} \approx 0.2865$, (b)(ii) $\approx 0.5276$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [Choice 2 ŌĆö Alternative]**

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Paper, Module 2]**
>
> **(a)** The diameters of ball bearings produced in a factory are normally distributed with mean $\mu = 2$ cm and standard deviation $\sigma = 0.01$ cm. Find: **(i)** the probability that a randomly selected ball bearing has diameter between $1.98$ and $2.02$ cm, **(ii)** the value of $d$ such that $P(X > d) = 0.05$. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** The waiting time at a hospital follows a uniform distribution between $0$ and $30$ minutes. Find: **(i)** the probability density function, **(ii)** the mean waiting time, **(iii)** the probability that a patient waits more than $20$ minutes. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a)(i): Probability between 1.98 and 2.02 cm**

$\mu = 2$, $\sigma = 0.01$.

$$Z_1 = \frac{1.98 - 2}{0.01} = -2, \quad Z_2 = \frac{2.02 - 2}{0.01} = 2$$

$$P(1.98 \leq X \leq 2.02) = P(-2 \leq Z \leq 2) = \Phi(2) - \Phi(-2)$$

$$= 0.9772 - 0.0228 = 0.9544$$

(Alternatively, by the 95.45% empirical rule.)

**Part (a)(ii): Find $d$ such that $P(X > d) = 0.05$**

From Z-table, $P(Z > 1.645) = 0.05$, so $z = 1.645$.

$$d = \mu + z\sigma = 2 + (1.645)(0.01) = 2 + 0.01645 = 2.01645 \text{ cm}$$

**Part (b)(i): PDF of Uniform Distribution**

For $X \sim U(0, 30)$:
$$f(x) = \frac{1}{b - a} = \frac{1}{30 - 0} = \frac{1}{30}, \quad 0 \leq x \leq 30$$

**Part (b)(ii): Mean waiting time**

$$\mu = \frac{a + b}{2} = \frac{0 + 30}{2} = 15 \text{ minutes}$$

**Part (b)(iii): $P(X > 20)$**

$$P(X > 20) = \int_{20}^{30} \frac{1}{30} \, dx = \frac{1}{30} \cdot (30 - 20) = \frac{10}{30} = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.3333$$

**Answers:**
- (a)(i) $0.9544$, (a)(ii) $d = 2.01645$ cm
- (b)(i) $f(x) = 1/30$, (b)(ii) $\mu = 15$ min, (b)(iii) $1/3$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to verify the PDF:** Always check that $f(x) \geq 0$ AND $\int f(x)\,dx = 1$. Examiners deduct 1ŌĆō2 marks if normalization is skipped.
> 2. **Confusing PDF with probability:** $f(x)$ is *not* a probability ŌĆö only $\int_a^b f(x)\,dx$ is. Writing "$P(X=x) = f(x)$" will lose full marks.
> 3. **Standardization errors:** Always compute $z = (x - \mu)/\sigma$ with the correct sign. Common mistake: subtracting in the wrong order.
> 4. **Forgetting $P(X=c) = 0$:** For continuous variables, do not include or exclude single points when computing interval probabilities.
> 5. **Memoryless property misuse:** Only applies to *exponential* distribution, not normal or uniform.
> 6. **Unit errors:** Always specify units (cm, hours, minutes) in the final answer for engineering problems.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Module 2:**

- **Continuous Random Variable (CRV):** Takes uncountably infinite values; $P(X = c) = 0$
- **PDF $f(x)$:** Two mandatory conditions: $f(x) \geq 0$ and $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x)\,dx = 1$
- **CDF $F(x)$:** $F(x) = P(X \leq x) = \int_{-\infty}^{x} f(t)\,dt$; non-decreasing; $F'(x) = f(x)$
- **Interval Probability:** $P(a \leq X \leq b) = F(b) - F(a) = \int_a^b f(x)\,dx$
- **Mean:** $E(X) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x \cdot f(x)\,dx$
- **Variance:** $\sigma^2 = E(X^2) - [E(X)]^2$
- **Uniform $U(a,b)$:** $f(x) = 1/(b-a)$; $\mu = (a+b)/2$; $\sigma^2 = (b-a)^2/12$
- **Exponential $\text{Exp}(\lambda)$:** $f(x) = \lambda e^{-\lambda x}$; $\mu = 1/\lambda$; $\sigma^2 = 1/\lambda^2$; **memoryless**
- **Normal $N(\mu, \sigma^2)$:** Bell curve; symmetric; standardized as $Z = (X-\mu)/\sigma$
- **68-95-99.7 Rule:** Probability within 1, 2, 3 standard deviations of mean
- **Standardization:** Always convert to $Z \sim N(0,1)$ before using tables
- **Memoryless:** $P(X > s+t \mid X > s) = P(X > t)$ ŌĆö exclusive to exponential
- **Z-Table Usage:** $\Phi(-z) = 1 - \Phi(z)$; $P(Z > a) = 1 - \Phi(a)$
- **Engineering Applications:** Quality control (normal), reliability (exponential), random arrivals (uniform)
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cumulative Distribution Function
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GCMAT401 - MATHEMATICS FOR PHYSICAL SCIENCE AND LIFE SCIENCE ŌĆō 4
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Cumulative Distribution Function

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition

The **Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF)**, denoted by $F_X(x)$ for a random variable $X$, is defined as the function that gives the probability that the random variable $X$ takes a value **less than or equal to** a particular value $x$. Mathematically:

$$F_X(x) = P(X \leq x), \quad \forall x \in \mathbb{R}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The CDF is the most fundamental tool in probability theory. It uniquely characterizes the distribution of any random variable ŌĆö whether discrete, continuous, or mixed. Every probability statement about $X$ can be expressed through its CDF.

The CDF is also sometimes called the **Distribution Function** of $X$. The subscript $X$ is often omitted when the random variable is clear from context, and we simply write $F(x)$.

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Filling Glass of Water**
> Imagine you are filling a glass with water drop by drop. At any given moment:
> - The **current water level** in the glass represents the value $x$.
> - The **total amount of water filled so far** (below the current level) represents $F(x)$.
> - The CDF is essentially a running total (cumulative count) of probability mass accumulated as we move along the number line.
> - For $x = -\infty$, the glass is empty, so $F(-\infty) = 0$.
> - For $x = +\infty$, the glass is completely full (all probability captured), so $F(+\infty) = 1$.

Another way to think: $F(x)$ answers the question ŌĆö **"What is the probability that $X$ falls in the range $(-\infty, x]$?"** It accumulates all probability from $-\infty$ up to $x$.

## Definition for Discrete and Continuous Random Variables

### Discrete Case
If $X$ is discrete with probability mass function (PMF) $P(X = x_i) = p_i$, then:

$$F(x) = P(X \leq x) = \sum_{x_i \leq x} p_i$$

### Continuous Case
If $X$ is continuous with probability density function (PDF) $f(x)$, then:

$$F(x) = P(X \leq x) = \int_{-\infty}^{x} f(t) \, dt$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Existence Guarantee (RiemannŌĆōStieltjes):** By the **Lebesgue Decomposition Theorem**, every CDF can be uniquely decomposed as $F(x) = F_d(x) + F_c(x) + F_s(x)$, where $F_d$, $F_c$, and $F_s$ are the discrete, absolutely continuous, and singular components respectively. This guarantees that the CDF is a complete descriptor of any probability law.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## The Four Fundamental Properties of a CDF

A function $F(x)$ is a valid CDF **if and only if** it satisfies the following four properties:

### Property 1: Non-Negativity / Monotonicity
$F(x)$ is a **monotonically non-decreasing** function. That is, for any $x_1 < x_2$:

$$F(x_1) \leq F(x_2)$$

**Why?** Adding more probability mass as we expand the interval cannot decrease total probability.

### Property 2: Right-Continuity
$F(x)$ is **right-continuous** at every point $x$. That is:

$$\lim_{h \to 0^+} F(x + h) = F(x)$$

**Why?** The defining event $\{X \leq x\}$ is a right-closed interval. Probability is continuous from the right by the axioms of measure theory (specifically, continuity from above of probability measures applied to decreasing sequences of sets $A_n = \{X \leq x + 1/n\}$).

### Property 3: Limit at Negative Infinity

$$\lim_{x \to -\infty} F(x) = 0$$

**Why?** As $x \to -\infty$, the event $\{X \leq x\}$ becomes the empty set, whose probability is **0**.

### Property 4: Limit at Positive Infinity

$$\lim_{x \to +\infty} F(x) = 1$$

**Why?** As $x \to +\infty$, the event $\{X \leq x\}$ covers the entire sample space, whose total probability is **1**.

## Key Probability Computations Using CDF

Once the CDF $F(x)$ is known, any probability statement about $X$ can be derived:

| Probability Statement | CDF Expression |
|-----------------------|----------------|
| $P(X \leq a)$ | $F(a)$ |
| $P(X < a)$ | $\lim_{h \to 0^+} F(a - h) = F(a^-)$ |
| $P(X \geq a)$ | $1 - F(a^-)$ |
| $P(X > a)$ | $1 - F(a)$ |
| $P(a < X \leq b)$ | $F(b) - F(a)$ |
| $P(a \leq X \leq b)$ | $F(b) - F(a^-)$ |
| $P(a \leq X < b)$ | $F(b^-) - F(a^-)$ |
| $P(a < X < b)$ | $F(b^-) - F(a)$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** $P(X = a) = F(a) - F(a^-)$ ŌĆö the **jump** (or saltus) of $F$ at the point $a$. For continuous distributions, this jump is **zero**, hence $P(X = a) = 0$ for any specific value $a$.

## KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---------|---------|-------|
| **CDF Definition** | $F(x) = P(X \leq x)$ | Universal definition |
| **From PMF (Discrete)** | $F(x) = \sum_{x_i \leq x} p_i$ | Sum over all outcomes $\leq x$ |
| **From PDF (Continuous)** | $F(x) = \int_{-\infty}^{x} f(t) \, dt$ | Integral of density |
| **PDF from CDF** | $f(x) = \dfrac{d}{dx} F(x)$ | At points where $F$ is differentiable |
| **Range** | $0 \leq F(x) \leq 1$ | Always bounded |
| **Tail Probability** | $P(X > x) = 1 - F(x)$ | Survival function |
| **Interval Probability** | $P(a < X \leq b) = F(b) - F(a)$ | Most common form |
| **Monotonicity** | $F'(x) \geq 0$ | Non-decreasing |
| **Boundary 1** | $F(-\infty) = 0$ | Empty event |
| **Boundary 2** | $F(+\infty) = 1$ | Certain event |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** The expression $F(b) - F(a)$ giving $P(a < X \leq b)$ is the single most tested formula in KTU board exams on this topic. Master it thoroughly.

## Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Reliability Engineering:** The CDF models the probability of failure of a component by time $t$ (e.g., MTBF analysis). $F(t) = P(\text{lifetime} \leq t)$.
- **Signal Processing:** The CDF of noise amplitudes helps determine bit error rates in digital communication channels.
- **Quality Control:** The CDF characterizes the distribution of manufactured product measurements, enabling specification compliance checks.
- **Financial Risk:** Value-at-Risk (VaR) calculations use CDFs to estimate portfolio loss probabilities.
- **Biostatistics:** Survival analysis in clinical trials uses the empirical CDF to estimate survival probabilities over time.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Derivation 1: Equivalence of $P(a < X \leq b) = F(b) - F(a)$

**Statement to prove:** For any random variable $X$ with CDF $F(x)$ and any $a < b$:

$$P(a < X \leq b) = F(b) - F(a)$$

### Step-by-Step Proof

**Step 1:** Express the event $\{X \leq b\}$ as a disjoint union.

The event that $X \leq b$ can be partitioned into two mutually exclusive events:
- $A = \{X \leq a\}$ ŌĆö the values of $X$ that are at most $a$
- $B = \{a < X \leq b\}$ ŌĆö the values of $X$ strictly greater than $a$ but at most $b$

Therefore:

$$\{X \leq b\} = A \cup B \quad \text{with} \quad A \cap B = \emptyset$$

**Step 2:** Apply the additivity axiom of probability.

By the additivity axiom for disjoint events:

$$P(X \leq b) = P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B)$$

$$P(X \leq b) = P(X \leq a) + P(a < X \leq b)$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the CDF definition.

By definition, $P(X \leq b) = F(b)$ and $P(X \leq a) = F(a)$. Substituting:

$$F(b) = F(a) + P(a < X \leq b)$$

**Step 4:** Solve for the desired probability.

Rearranging:

$$P(a < X \leq b) = F(b) - F(a) \qquad \blacksquare$$

---

## Derivation 2: $P(X = a) = F(a) - F(a^-)$ (Jump/Saltus Formula)

**Statement:** For any point $a \in \mathbb{R}$:

$$P(X = a) = F(a) - F(a^-)$$

where $F(a^-) = \lim_{h \to 0^+} F(a - h)$ is the left-hand limit.

### Step-by-Step Proof

**Step 1:** Decompose the event $\{X \leq a\}$ into two disjoint events.

$$\{X \leq a\} = \{X < a\} \cup \{X = a\}$$

These two events are mutually exclusive (a value cannot be simultaneously strictly less than $a$ and equal to $a$).

**Step 2:** Apply additivity.

$$P(X \leq a) = P(X < a) + P(X = a)$$

**Step 3:** Identify each term with a CDF expression.

- $P(X \leq a) = F(a)$
- $P(X < a) = \lim_{n \to \infty} P\left(X \leq a - \tfrac{1}{n}\right) = \lim_{n \to \infty} F\left(a - \tfrac{1}{n}\right) = F(a^-)$

**Step 4:** Combine.

$$F(a) = F(a^-) + P(X = a)$$

Solving for the probability at the point $a$:

$$P(X = a) = F(a) - F(a^-) \qquad \blacksquare$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Consequence:** For **continuous random variables**, $F$ has no jumps, so $F(a) = F(a^-)$ for all $a$, which means $P(X = a) = 0$ for any specific value $a$. This is the fundamental reason why endpoints of intervals can be open or closed interchangeably in continuous probability computations.

---

## Derivation 3: PDF from CDF (Inverse Relationship)

**Statement:** If $X$ is a continuous random variable with CDF $F(x)$ and PDF $f(x)$, then:

$$f(x) = \frac{d}{dx} F(x)$$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Start with the definition.

$$F(x) = \int_{-\infty}^{x} f(t) \, dt$$

**Step 2:** Apply the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus (Part 1).

If $f(t)$ is continuous (Riemann integrable) on $(-\infty, x]$, then the integral is differentiable and:

$$\frac{d}{dx} \int_{-\infty}^{x} f(t) \, dt = f(x)$$

**Step 3:** Conclude.

$$F'(x) = f(x) \qquad \blacksquare$$

---

## Worked Example: Constructing CDF from PMF

**Problem:** A discrete random variable $X$ has the PMF:

$$P(X = -1) = 0.2, \quad P(X = 0) = 0.5, \quad P(X = 2) = 0.3$$

Construct the CDF $F(x)$.

### Solution

**Step 1:** Identify the support: $\{-1, 0, 2\}$.

**Step 2:** For $x < -1$: $F(x) = 0$ (no probability mass accumulated).

**Step 3:** For $-1 \leq x < 0$: $F(x) = P(X \leq x) = P(X = -1) = 0.2$.

**Step 4:** For $0 \leq x < 2$: $F(x) = P(X = -1) + P(X = 0) = 0.2 + 0.5 = 0.7$.

**Step 5:** For $x \geq 2$: $F(x) = 0.2 + 0.5 + 0.3 = 1.0$.

**Final CDF (piecewise):**

$$F(x) = \begin{cases} 0, & x < -1 \\ 0.2, & -1 \leq x < 0 \\ 0.7, & 0 \leq x < 2 \\ 1.0, & x \geq 2 \end{cases}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This CDF is a **right-continuous step function** with jumps of magnitudes $0.2$, $0.5$, and $0.3$ at $x = -1$, $0$, and $2$ respectively. Each jump height equals $P(X = x_i)$ at that point.

---

## Worked Example: Constructing PDF from CDF (Continuous)

**Problem:** Given the CDF of a continuous random variable:

$$F(x) = \begin{cases} 0, & x < 0 \\ x^2, & 0 \leq x \leq 1 \\ 1, & x > 1 \end{cases}$$

Find: (i) the PDF $f(x)$, and (ii) $P(0.3 \leq X \leq 0.7)$.

### Solution

**(i) Differentiate $F(x)$ piecewise:**

$$f(x) = F'(x) = \begin{cases} 0, & x < 0 \\ 2x, & 0 \leq x \leq 1 \\ 0, & x > 1 \end{cases}$$

**Verification:** $\int_{0}^{1} 2x \, dx = \left[ x^2 \right]_{0}^{1} = 1$ Ō£ō

**(ii) Compute the probability:**

$$P(0.3 \leq X \leq 0.7) = F(0.7) - F(0.3)$$

$$= (0.7)^2 - (0.3)^2 = 0.49 - 0.09 = 0.40$$

$$\boxed{P(0.3 \leq X \leq 0.7) = 0.40}$$

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## Diagram 1: Conceptual Flow of CDF Construction

```mermaid

    A["Random Variable X with PMF or PDF"] --> B["Identify the Sample Space"]
    B --> C["For Discrete: Sum PMF values less than or equal to x"]
    B --> D["For Continuous: Integrate PDF from negative infinity to x"]
    C --> E["Build Piecewise Step Function F of x"]
    D --> F["Build Continuous Smooth Function F of x"]
    E --> G["Verify the 4 CDF Properties"]
    F --> G
    G --> H["F is Valid CDF"]
    H --> I["Compute Probabilities: P less than a, P greater than b, P between a and b"]
```

```mermaid
    V --> R["Result: Function is a Valid CDF"]

    R --> APP2["Application: P of a less than X less than b equals F of b minus F of a"]
    R --> APP3["Application: P of X greater than a equals 1 minus F of a"]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF) of a random variable $X$. List its four fundamental properties.**

**Model Answer:**

The Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF) of a random variable $X$ is defined as:

$$F(x) = P(X \leq x), \quad x \in \mathbb{R}$$

**Four Properties:**

1. **Monotonicity (Non-Decreasing):** For $x_1 < x_2$, $F(x_1) \leq F(x_2)$.
2. **Right-Continuity:** $\lim_{h \to 0^+} F(x + h) = F(x)$ for all $x$.
3. **Lower Limit:** $\lim_{x \to -\infty} F(x) = 0$.
4. **Upper Limit:** $\lim_{x \to +\infty} F(x) = 1$.

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**For a continuous random variable, justify why $P(a \leq X \leq b) = P(a < X < b) = F(b) - F(a)$.**

**Model Answer:**

For a continuous random variable, the CDF $F(x)$ has **no jumps**, meaning $F$ is continuous at every point. By the **jump formula**:

$$P(X = a) = F(a) - F(a^-) = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad P(X = b) = F(b) - F(b^-) = 0$$

Since the probability of the random variable equaling any specific point is zero, the inclusion or exclusion of the endpoints $a$ and $b$ does not affect the probability. Therefore:

$$P(a \leq X \leq b) = P(a < X < b) = F(b) - F(a) = 1.0$$

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) ŌĆö Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**The CDF of a continuous random variable $X$ is given by:**

$$F(x) = \begin{cases} 0, & x < 0 \\ \dfrac{x^2}{4}, & 0 \leq x < 2 \\ 1, & x \geq 2 \end{cases}$$

**(a)** Find the probability density function (PDF) $f(x)$ of $X$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute $P(1 \leq X \leq 1.5)$ and $P(X \geq 1.5)$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

**Step 1:** Differentiate $F(x)$ piecewise to obtain $f(x)$.

For $x < 0$: $f(x) = \dfrac{d}{dx}(0) = 0$.

For $0 \leq x < 2$: $f(x) = \dfrac{d}{dx}\left(\dfrac{x^2}{4}\right) = \dfrac{2x}{4} = \dfrac{x}{2}$.

For $x \geq 2$: $f(x) = \dfrac{d}{dx}(1) = 0$.

**Step 2:** Assemble the PDF.

$$f(x) = \begin{cases} 0, & x < 0 \\ \dfrac{x}{2}, & 0 \leq x \leq 2 \\ 0, & x > 2 \end{cases}$$

**Step 3:** Verify normalization.

$$\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} f(x) \, dx = \int_{0}^{2} \dfrac{x}{2} \, dx = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \left[ \dfrac{x^2}{2} \right]_{0}^{2} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \dfrac{4}{2} = 1 \quad \checkmark$$

---

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

**Computing $P(1 \leq X \leq 1.5)$:**

Using the CDF formula for a continuous distribution:

$$P(1 \leq X \leq 1.5) = F(1.5) - F(1)$$

Evaluate $F(1.5)$:
$$F(1.5) = \dfrac{(1.5)^2}{4} = \dfrac{2.25}{4} = 0.5625$$

Evaluate $F(1)$:
$$F(1) = \dfrac{(1)^2}{4} = 0.25$$

Therefore:

$$P(1 \leq X \leq 1.5) = 0.5625 - 0.25 = 0.3125$$

**Computing $P(X \geq 1.5)$:**

Using the complement rule and tail formula:

$$P(X \geq 1.5) = 1 - F(1.5^-) = 1 - F(1.5) = 1 - 0.5625 = 0.4375$$

(For a continuous distribution, $F(1.5^-) = F(1.5)$.)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**A discrete random variable $X$ has the following probability mass function:**

| $x$ | $-2$ | $-1$ | $0$ | $1$ | $2$ |
|-----|------|------|-----|-----|-----|
| $P(X=x)$ | $0.1$ | $0.2$ | $0.3$ | $0.3$ | $0.1$ |

**(a)** Construct the Cumulative Distribution Function $F(x)$ of $X$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the CDF, compute $P(-1 < X \leq 1)$, $P(X \geq 0)$, and $P(X = 1)$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

**Step 1:** Sort the values in ascending order: $-2, -1, 0, 1, 2$.

**Step 2:** Compute the cumulative sum at each value:

- $F(-2) = P(X \leq -2) = 0.1$
- $F(-1) = P(X \leq -1) = 0.1 + 0.2 = 0.3$
- $F(0) = P(X \leq 0) = 0.1 + 0.2 + 0.3 = 0.6$
- $F(1) = P(X \leq 1) = 0.1 + 0.2 + 0.3 + 0.3 = 0.9$
- $F(2) = P(X \leq 2) = 0.1 + 0.2 + 0.3 + 0.3 + 0.1 = 1.0$

**Step 3:** Write the piecewise CDF:

$$F(x) = \begin{cases} 0, & x < -2 \\ 0.1, & -2 \leq x < -1 \\ 0.3, & -1 \leq x < 0 \\ 0.6, & 0 \leq x < 1 \\ 0.9, & 1 \leq x < 2 \\ 1.0, & x \geq 2 \end{cases}$$

---

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

**Computing $P(-1 < X \leq 1)$:**

For a discrete variable, we cannot simply use $F(1) - F(-1)$ because the open endpoint at $-1$ excludes the value $X = -1$:

$$P(-1 < X \leq 1) = F(1) - F(-1^-) = F(1) - P(X \leq -1) = 0.9 - 0.3 = 0.6$$

(Equivalently: $P(X = 0) + P(X = 1) = 0.3 + 0.3 = 0.6$ Ō£ō)

**Computing $P(X \geq 0)$:**

$$P(X \geq 0) = 1 - P(X < 0) = 1 - F(0^-) = 1 - 0.3 = 0.7$$

(Equivalently: $P(X = 0) + P(X = 1) + P(X = 2) = 0.3 + 0.3 + 0.1 = 0.7$ Ō£ō)

**Computing $P(X = 1)$:**

Using the jump formula:

$$P(X = 1) = F(1) - F(1^-) = 0.9 - 0.6 = 0.3 \quad \checkmark$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Endpoint Mismatch Disaster:** The most common mark-losing mistake is using $F(b) - F(a)$ for $P(a \leq X < b)$ or $P(a < X \leq b)$ on **discrete** random variables without accounting for the open endpoint. Always use the jump formula $P(X = a) = F(a) - F(a^-)$ to handle boundary points carefully.
> 2. **Forgetting Right-Continuity:** Many students write the CDF for a discrete variable using the wrong interval convention (e.g., writing $0.1$ for $x = -1$ instead of $-2 \leq x < -1$). Remember: **CDF is right-continuous**, so the constant value $F(c)$ holds for the interval $c \leq x < c'$ where $c'$ is the next jump.
> 3. **Misapplying Tail Formula:** $P(X \geq a) = 1 - F(a^-)$ (uses **left** limit), not $1 - F(a)$. The single-point difference is critical in discrete cases.
> 4. **Skipping Verification:** Always verify $\lim_{x \to \infty} F(x) = 1$ in your final answer. Examiners award 1ŌĆō2 marks for the **normalization check** as a mark of conceptual completeness.
> 5. **No "=" Sign in CDF Definition:** The CDF is strictly $F(x) = P(X \leq x)$, **not** $P(X < x)$. The latter would give the **left-continuous** version, which is different.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö CDF Essentials**

- **Definition:** $F(x) = P(X \leq x)$ ŌĆö accumulate all probability mass to the left of (and including) $x$.
- **Four Cardinal Properties:** Monotonic, Right-Continuous, $F(-\infty) = 0$, $F(+\infty) = 1$. Memorize these in order.
- **Discrete Construction:** $F(x) = \sum_{x_i \leq x} p_i$ ŌĆö produces a **right-continuous step function** with jumps equal to PMF values.
- **Continuous Construction:** $F(x) = \int_{-\infty}^{x} f(t)\,dt$ ŌĆö produces a **smooth, continuous, non-decreasing curve**.
- **Inverse Relation:** $f(x) = \dfrac{d}{dx} F(x)$ for continuous distributions; for discrete, $p_i = F(x_i) - F(x_i^-)$.
- **Interval Probability:** $P(a < X \leq b) = F(b) - F(a)$ ŌĆö works for **continuous**; for **discrete**, careful with open endpoints.
- **Point Probability (Jump Formula):** $P(X = a) = F(a) - F(a^-)$. Equals **0** for continuous variables.
- **Tail Probability:** $P(X > a) = 1 - F(a)$; $P(X \geq a) = 1 - F(a^-)$.
- **Complement Symmetry:** Always pair with the survival function $\bar{F}(x) = 1 - F(x^-)$ for "greater than" probabilities.
- **Engineering Application:** Used in reliability (failure probability), signal processing (noise analysis), quality control (tolerance limits), and finance (Value-at-Risk).
- **Common Error:** Confusing $F(a)$ (right-limit) with $F(a^-)$ (left-limit) in discrete problems.
- **Key Takeaway:** The CDF is the **unifying object** in probability ŌĆö it is the only function that fully describes both discrete and continuous random variables in one framework.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GCMAT401 - MATHEMATICS FOR PHYSICAL SCIENCE AND LIFE SCIENCE ŌĆō 4
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Expectation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In probability theory, the **Expectation** (also called the **Expected Value** or **Mathematical Expectation**) of a random variable is a fundamental concept that generalizes the notion of an "average." Formally, the expectation represents the long-run average value of repetitions of the experiment it represents.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (Discrete Random Variable):** Let $X$ be a discrete random variable with probability mass function (pmf) $P(X = x_i) = p_i$. The **mathematical expectation** of $X$ is defined as:
> $$E(X) = \sum_{i=1}^{\infty} x_i \cdot p_i$$
> provided the sum converges absolutely (i.e., $\sum \vert x_i \vert p_i < \infty$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Continuous Random Variable):** Let $X$ be a continuous random variable with probability density function (pdf) $f(x)$. The **mathematical expectation** of $X$ is defined as:
> $$E(X) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x \cdot f(x) \, dx$$
> provided the integral converges absolutely (i.e., $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x \vert f(x) \, dx < \infty$).

The symbol $E(X)$ is sometimes written as $\mu$ or $\mu_X$, and is also called the **mean** of the random variable $X$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are playing a dice game where you win **Ōé╣50** if you roll a **6**, win **Ōé╣20** if you roll a **3** or **4**, and win **Ōé╣0** otherwise. What is a "fair" entry fee for this game?

A "fair" entry fee would be the amount you would expect to win **on average** if you played the game many times. This is precisely the **Expectation** ŌĆö it is the *long-term average outcome* of a random experiment.

| Outcome (Roll) | Prize $x_i$ (Ōé╣) | Probability $p_i$ | Contribution $x_i p_i$ (Ōé╣) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 6 | 50 | 1/6 | 50/6 Ōēł 8.33 |
| 3 or 4 | 20 | 2/6 | 40/6 Ōēł 6.67 |
| Otherwise (1,2,5) | 0 | 3/6 | 0 |
| **Total** | ŌĆö | **1** | **$E(X) = 15$** |

A fair entry fee would be **Ōé╣15** ŌĆö this is the *expected* winnings per game.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight:** Expectation is NOT the value you "expect" to get in a single trial (you cannot roll a 2.5 on a die!). It is the *weighted average* of all possible values, weighted by how likely each is to occur. In a single trial, you get one of the actual values; expectation is what happens **on average over many trials**.

## 1.3 Why is Expectation Important?

- It is the **first central moment** of a random variable and a measure of central tendency.
- It is the foundation for defining **variance**, **higher moments**, and the **Moment Generating Function (MGF)**.
- It underpins concepts in **risk analysis**, **expected payoff in financial engineering**, **signal processing (expected value of noise)**, and **reliability engineering** (Mean Time to Failure, MTTF).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Expectation is foundational for Module 2 of GCMAT401, which also covers *variance, moments, and moment generating functions*. Mastery of $E(X)$ is non-negotiable for the End Semester Examination (ESE).

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Expectation of a Function of a Random Variable

The real power of expectation is that we can find the expected value of **any function** $g(X)$ of a random variable, without needing to find the distribution of $g(X)$ first.

> [!NOTE]
> **Theorem (Law of the Unconscious Statistician ŌĆö LOTUS):** If $X$ is discrete with pmf $p(x_i)$:
> $$E[g(X)] = \sum_{i} g(x_i) \cdot p(x_i)$$
> If $X$ is continuous with pdf $f(x)$:
> $$E[g(X)] = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} g(x) \cdot f(x) \, dx$$

This is a critical tool: we can compute $E(X^2)$, $E(e^X)$, $E(\sin X)$, etc., directly using the distribution of $X$.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown of the Discrete Expectation

1. **Identify** the sample space and list all possible outcomes $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n$.
2. **Determine** the probability $p_i = P(X = x_i)$ for each outcome.
3. **Verify** that $\sum p_i = 1$ (this is a sanity check on your pmf).
4. **Multiply** each $x_i$ by its corresponding $p_i$.
5. **Sum** all the products to obtain $E(X)$.

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown of the Continuous Expectation

1. **Identify** the pdf $f(x)$ of the random variable $X$.
2. **Verify** that $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x) \, dx = 1$.
3. **Set up** the integral $E(X) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x \cdot f(x) \, dx$.
4. **Evaluate** the integral using standard calculus techniques (substitution, integration by parts, etc.).
5. If the integral diverges (i.e., the area under $\vert x \vert f(x)$ is infinite), $E(X)$ **does not exist**.

## 2.4 Fundamental Properties of Expectation

Let $X$ and $Y$ be random variables and $a, b, c$ be constants. Then:

| # | Property | Mathematical Statement |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| P1 | Linearity (Constant) | $E(c) = c$ |
| P2 | Linearity (Scalar Multiplication) | $E(aX) = a \cdot E(X)$ |
| P3 | Linearity (Additivity) | $E(aX + bY) = a \cdot E(X) + b \cdot E(Y)$ |
| P4 | Linearity (General) | $E\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} a_i X_i + c\right) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} a_i E(X_i) + c$ |
| P5 | Independence Multiplication | If $X, Y$ independent: $E(XY) = E(X) \cdot E(Y)$ |
| P6 | Monotonicity | If $X \geq Y$ always, then $E(X) \geq E(Y)$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Distinction (P5):** $E(XY) = E(X)E(Y)$ is **ONLY** guaranteed when $X$ and $Y$ are **independent**. For dependent random variables, $E(XY) \neq E(X)E(Y)$ in general. However, $E(X+Y) = E(X) + E(Y)$ holds **always**, regardless of independence.

## 2.5 Connection to Variance and Moments

The expectation of a random variable is the **first raw moment** $m_1$. Higher-order moments give a complete probabilistic fingerprint of the distribution.

> [!NOTE]
> **$k$-th Moment about the Origin (Raw Moment):**
> $$\mu_k' = E(X^k)$$
> - $\mu_0' = 1$ (always)
> - $\mu_1' = E(X) = \mu$ (the mean)

> [!NOTE]
> **$k$-th Central Moment (Moment about the Mean):**
> $$\mu_k = E\left[(X - \mu)^k\right]$$
> - $\mu_0 = 1$
> - $\mu_1 = 0$ (always, because $E(X - \mu) = 0$)
> - $\mu_2 = E(X^2) - [E(X)]^2 = \text{Variance}$ (this is the famous variance identity)

The **Variance** is defined as $\text{Var}(X) = E\left[(X - \mu)^2\right]$, which measures the *spread* of $X$ around its mean.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table contains the **high-yield formulas** essential for KTU board examinations on Expectation.

| # | Formula / Identity | Type | Conditions |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $E(X) = \sum x_i p_i$ | Discrete Mean | pmf $p_i$ converges absolutely |
| 2 | $E(X) = \int x f(x)\, dx$ | Continuous Mean | pdf $f(x)$ converges absolutely |
| 3 | $E[g(X)] = \sum g(x_i) p_i$ | LOTUS (Discrete) | $g$ is a real function |
| 4 | $E[g(X)] = \int g(x) f(x)\, dx$ | LOTUS (Continuous) | $g$ is a real function |
| 5 | $E(aX + b) = aE(X) + b$ | Linearity | $a, b \in \mathbb{R}$ |
| 6 | $E(X + Y) = E(X) + E(Y)$ | Additivity | Always true (no independence needed) |
| 7 | $E(XY) = E(X)E(Y)$ | Independence | Only if $X \perp\!\!\!\perp Y$ |
| 8 | $\text{Var}(X) = E(X^2) - [E(X)]^2$ | Variance Identity | Always true |
| 9 | $\text{Var}(aX + b) = a^2 \text{Var}(X)$ | Variance Scaling | $a, b \in \mathbb{R}$ |
| 10 | $E(X^2) = \text{Var}(X) + [E(X)]^2$ | Second Moment | Always true |
| 11 | $M_X(t) = E(e^{tX})$ | MGF Definition | Exists in a neighborhood of $t=0$ |
| 12 | $E(X^n) = M_X^{(n)}(0)$ | $n$-th Moment via MGF | Differentiate MGF $n$ times at $t=0$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Notation Reminder:** Throughout KTU evaluations, the absolute value of a quantity $x$ is written as $\vert x \vert$ (using `\vert` in LaTeX) ŌĆö **NOT** as $|x|$ (vertical pipe) ŌĆö to avoid breaking the markdown table structure.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Quality Control:** The expected number of defective items in a batch of $n$ produced items.
- **Reliability Engineering:** The **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)** is the expectation of the failure-time random variable.
- **Communication Systems:** The expected value of **signal-plus-noise** determines the decision threshold in digital receivers.
- **Insurance & Actuarial Science:** The expected claim amount is the basis for setting insurance premiums.
- **Queueing Theory:** Expected waiting time $E(W)$ in an $M/M/1$ queue is $\frac{\rho}{\mu(1-\rho)}$.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Why $E(X) = \sum x_i p_i$?

**Setup:** Consider a discrete random variable $X$ taking values $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n$ with probabilities $p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_n$.

**Step 1: Imagine $N$ independent repetitions of the experiment.**

Let $N$ be a large positive integer. We perform the experiment $N$ times independently, recording the value of $X$ in each trial.

**Step 2: Count the frequency of each outcome.**

By the **Law of Large Numbers**, for large $N$, the proportion of trials yielding $x_i$ approaches $p_i$. Thus, the number of trials yielding $x_i$ is approximately $N \cdot p_i$.

**Step 3: Compute the sample average (arithmetic mean).**

The total sum of all observed values of $X$ across the $N$ trials is:

$$
S_N \approx \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i \cdot (N p_i) = N \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i p_i
$$

The average (sample mean) of the $N$ observations is:

$$
\bar{X}_N = \frac{S_N}{N} \approx \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i p_i
$$

**Step 4: Take the limit as $N \to \infty$.**

As $N$ grows without bound, the sample mean $\bar{X}_N$ converges to the population mean, which we *define* as the expectation:

$$
E(X) = \lim_{N \to \infty} \bar{X}_N = \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i p_i
$$

This completes the intuitive justification. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Linearity of Expectation $E(aX + b) = aE(X) + b$

**Setup:** Let $X$ be a discrete random variable (the proof for continuous is analogous with sums replaced by integrals).

**Step 1: Apply the LOTUS formula.**

Let $g(X) = aX + b$. By the Law of the Unconscious Statistician:

$$
E(aX + b) = \sum_{i} (a x_i + b) \cdot p_i
$$

**Step 2: Distribute the sum.**

$$
E(aX + b) = \sum_{i} (a x_i p_i + b p_i)
$$

**Step 3: Split the sum into two terms.**

$$
E(aX + b) = a \sum_{i} x_i p_i \; + \; b \sum_{i} p_i
$$

**Step 4: Recognize the two summations.**

- The first sum is, by definition, $E(X)$.
- The second sum is the **total probability**, which equals **1** (axiom of probability).

$$
E(aX + b) = a \cdot E(X) + b \cdot 1
$$

$$
\boxed{E(aX + b) = aE(X) + b} \quad \blacksquare
$$

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: Variance Identity $\text{Var}(X) = E(X^2) - [E(X)]^2$

**Step 1: Write the definition of variance.**

By definition, the variance is the second central moment:

$$
\text{Var}(X) = E\left[(X - \mu)^2\right]
$$

where $\mu = E(X)$.

**Step 2: Expand the squared term using the binomial identity.**

Recall that $(X - \mu)^2 = X^2 - 2\mu X + \mu^2$. Substituting:

$$
\text{Var}(X) = E\left[X^2 - 2\mu X + \mu^2\right]
$$

**Step 3: Apply the linearity of expectation (Property P4).**

$$
\text{Var}(X) = E(X^2) - 2\mu E(X) + E(\mu^2)
$$

**Step 4: Simplify using $\mu = E(X)$ and $E(\mu^2) = \mu^2$.**

Since $\mu$ is a constant, $E(\mu) = \mu$ and $E(\mu^2) = \mu^2$. Also, $E(X) = \mu$:

$$
\text{Var}(X) = E(X^2) - 2\mu \cdot \mu + \mu^2
$$

$$
\text{Var}(X) = E(X^2) - 2\mu^2 + \mu^2
$$

$$
\text{Var}(X) = E(X^2) - \mu^2
$$

**Step 5: Substitute back $\mu = E(X)$.**

$$
\boxed{\text{Var}(X) = E(X^2) - [E(X)]^2} \quad \blacksquare
$$

## 3.4 Worked Example 1: Discrete Expectation (Binomial Distribution)

> **Problem:** Let $X \sim B(n, p)$, the number of successes in $n$ Bernoulli trials with success probability $p$. Compute $E(X)$ and $\text{Var}(X)$.

**Step 1: Decompose $X$ into sum of indicator variables.**

A Binomial random variable can be written as the sum of $n$ independent Bernoulli trials:

$$
X = X_1 + X_2 + \ldots + X_n
$$

where $X_i = 1$ if trial $i$ is a success, and $X_i = 0$ otherwise.

**Step 2: Compute $E(X_i)$ for each Bernoulli trial.**

For a single Bernoulli trial, $E(X_i) = 1 \cdot p + 0 \cdot (1-p) = p$.

**Step 3: Apply linearity of expectation.**

$$
E(X) = E\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i\right) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} E(X_i) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p = np
$$

**Step 4: Compute $\text{Var}(X_i)$.**

$$
E(X_i^2) = 1^2 \cdot p + 0^2 \cdot (1-p) = p
$$

$$
\text{Var}(X_i) = E(X_i^2) - [E(X_i)]^2 = p - p^2 = p(1-p) = pq
$$

where $q = 1-p$.

**Step 5: Apply independence for variance.**

Since the $X_i$ are independent, variances add:

$$
\text{Var}(X) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{Var}(X_i) = n \cdot pq = npq
$$

$$
\boxed{E(X) = np \quad \text{and} \quad \text{Var}(X) = npq}
$$

## 3.5 Worked Example 2: Continuous Expectation (Exponential Distribution)

> **Problem:** Let $X \sim \text{Exp}(\lambda)$ with pdf $f(x) = \lambda e^{-\lambda x}$ for $x \geq 0$. Compute $E(X)$ and $\text{Var}(X)$.

**Step 1: Set up the integral for $E(X)$.**

$$
E(X) = \int_{0}^{\infty} x \cdot \lambda e^{-\lambda x} \, dx
$$

**Step 2: Use integration by parts.**

Let $u = x$, $dv = \lambda e^{-\lambda x} dx$. Then $du = dx$, $v = -e^{-\lambda x}$.

$$
E(X) = \left[-x e^{-\lambda x}\right]_{0}^{\infty} + \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda x} \, dx
$$

**Step 3: Evaluate the boundary term.**

As $x \to \infty$, the exponential dominates, so $x e^{-\lambda x} \to 0$. At $x = 0$, the term is $0$. Thus, the boundary term is $0$.

**Step 4: Evaluate the remaining integral.**

$$
E(X) = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda x} \, dx = \left[-\frac{1}{\lambda} e^{-\lambda x}\right]_{0}^{\infty} = 0 - \left(-\frac{1}{\lambda}\right) = \frac{1}{\lambda}
$$

$$
\boxed{E(X) = \frac{1}{\lambda}}
$$

**Step 5: Compute $E(X^2)$ for variance.**

$$
E(X^2) = \int_{0}^{\infty} x^2 \cdot \lambda e^{-\lambda x} \, dx
$$

Using the standard gamma integral $\int_{0}^{\infty} x^n e^{-\lambda x} dx = \frac{n!}{\lambda^{n+1}}$, we get:

$$
E(X^2) = \lambda \cdot \frac{2!}{\lambda^3} = \frac{2}{\lambda^2}
$$

**Step 6: Apply the variance identity.**

$$
\text{Var}(X) = E(X^2) - [E(X)]^2 = \frac{2}{\lambda^2} - \frac{1}{\lambda^2} = \frac{1}{\lambda^2}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Var}(X) = \frac{1}{\lambda^2}}
$$

## 3.6 Worked Example 3: Standard Uniform Distribution

> **Problem:** Let $X \sim U(0, 1)$ with pdf $f(x) = 1$ for $0 \leq x \leq 1$. Compute $E(X)$.

**Step 1: Set up the integral.**

$$
E(X) = \int_{0}^{1} x \cdot 1 \, dx = \int_{0}^{1} x \, dx
$$

**Step 2: Evaluate.**

$$
E(X) = \left[\frac{x^2}{2}\right]_{0}^{1} = \frac{1}{2} - 0 = \frac{1}{2}
$$

This matches the well-known result $E(X) = \frac{a+b}{2}$ for $X \sim U(a, b)$. $\blacksquare$

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix: Mean and Variance of Common Distributions

The following is a high-density reference table for KTU exam preparation. **Memorizing this matrix is non-negotiable for full marks.**

| Distribution | Notation | $E(X)$ | $\text{Var}(X)$ | MGF $M_X(t)$ |
| :---: | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Bernoulli | $B(1, p)$ | $p$ | $p(1-p)$ | $1 - p + p e^t$ |
| Binomial | $B(n, p)$ | $np$ | $np(1-p)$ | $(1-p+pe^t)^n$ |
| Poisson | $\text{Poi}(\lambda)$ | $\lambda$ | $\lambda$ | $e^{\lambda(e^t - 1)}$ |
| Geometric | $\text{Geo}(p)$ | $1/p$ | $(1-p)/p^2$ | $\frac{pe^t}{1 - (1-p)e^t}$ |
| Uniform (continuous) | $U(a, b)$ | $(a+b)/2$ | $(b-a)^2 / 12$ | $\frac{e^{tb} - e^{ta}}{t(b-a)}$ |
| Exponential | $\text{Exp}(\lambda)$ | $1/\lambda$ | $1/\lambda^2$ | $\frac{\lambda}{\lambda - t}$, $t < \lambda$ |
| Normal | $N(\mu, \sigma^2)$ | $\mu$ | $\sigma^2$ | $e^{\mu t + \sigma^2 t^2 / 2}$ |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram: How to Compute Expectation

The following **Mermaid block diagram** illustrates the complete decision-flow for computing the expectation of a random variable in a KTU-style problem.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Given Random Variable X] --> B{Is X Discrete or Continuous?}
    B -- Discrete --> C[Identify pmf: P_X = x_i equals p_i]
    B -- Continuous --> D[Identify pdf: f of x on support S]
    C --> E[Sanity Check: Sum of all p_i equals 1]
    D --> F[Sanity Check: Integral of f x dx from neg inf to pos inf equals 1]
    E --> G[Apply Discrete Formula: E of X equals Sum x_i times p_i]
    F --> H[Apply Continuous Formula: E of X equals Integral x f x dx]
    G --> I[Check Absolute Convergence: Sum of absolute value of x_i times p_i is finite]
    H --> J[Check Absolute Convergence: Integral of absolute value of x f x dx is finite]
    I --> K[Result: E of X computed]
    J --> K
    I -- Diverges --> L[Conclusion: E of X does not exist]
    J -- Diverges --> L
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture: The Expectation Operator as a "Machine"

This diagram models the **expectation operator** as a processing machine that takes a probability distribution and outputs a single scalar value (the mean).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[Input: Probability Distribution] --> node2{Type Detector}
    node2 -- Discrete --> node3[Weighted Sum Engine: Sum x_i p_i]
    node2 -- Continuous --> node4[Integral Engine: Integral x f x dx]
    node3 --> node5[Convergence Checker]
    node4 --> node5
    node5 -- Converges --> node6[Output: Scalar Value mu equals E of X]
    node5 -- Diverges --> node7[Output: Undefined or Does Not Exist]
```

## 4.3 Hierarchical Concept Map: Expectation in Context

This block diagram shows how **Expectation** fits into the broader hierarchy of probability concepts covered in KTU Module 2.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root1[Random Variable X] --> root2[Distribution Function]
    root2 --> root3[Probability Measures]
    root1 --> root4[Numerical Characteristics]
    root4 --> root5[Central Tendency]
    root4 --> root6[Dispersion]
    root5 --> root7[EXPECTATION E of X]
    root5 --> root8[Median]
    root5 --> root9[Mode]
    root6 --> root10[Variance Var X]
    root6 --> root11[Standard Deviation sigma]
    root4 --> root12[Higher Moments]
    root12 --> root13[MGF M_X t]
    root12 --> root14[Skewness and Kurtosis]
    root10 --> root15[Std Deviation equals square root of Var X]
    root7 --> root16[First Raw Moment mu_1]
    root10 --> root17[Second Central Moment mu_2]
    root13 --> root18[E of X equals M_X prime 0]
```

## 4.4 Property Dependency Diagram

This block diagram shows the logical dependencies between the major properties and theorems of expectation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    p1[Linearity of Expectation] --> p2[E of aX plus b equals aE of X plus b]
    p1 --> p3[E of X plus Y equals E of X plus E of Y]
    p4[LOTUS Theorem] --> p5[Computing E of g of X without finding distribution of g of X]
    p6[Independence] --> p7[E of XY equals E of X times E of Y]
    p2 --> p8[Variance Identity: Var X equals E of X squared minus mu squared]
    p3 --> p8
    p8 --> p9[Var aX plus b equals a squared Var X]
    p5 --> p10[MGF M_X t equals E of e to the tX]
    p10 --> p11[E of X to the n equals n-th derivative of MGF at t equals 0]
```
