## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Normal Distribution Workflow

This diagram captures the computational pipeline a KTU student must follow when solving any Normal Distribution problem, from parameter identification to final probability computation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Problem Statement] --> B[Identify Type of Distribution]
    B --> C{Is it Normal?}
    C -->|Yes| D[Extract mu and sigma squared]
    C -->|No| E[Check Exponential or Other]
    D --> F[Define Event of Interest]
    F --> G[Apply Z-Transformation]
    G --> H[Z equals X minus mu divided by sigma]
    H --> I[Convert Bounds to z-values]
    I --> J[Use Standard Normal Table phi z]
    J --> K[Apply Symmetry Rule phi of minus z equals 1 minus phi of z]
    K --> L[Compute Final Probability]
    L --> M[END: Report Answer]
    E --> M2[Switch Module to Exponential Analysis]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Exponential Distribution Workflow

This diagram outlines the logical flow for Exponential Distribution problems, particularly emphasizing the memoryless property branching.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Exponential Problem] --> B[Extract Rate Parameter lambda]
    B --> C[Compute Mean equals 1 divided by lambda]
    C --> D[Define Probability Question Type]
    D --> E{Question Type}
    E -->|Survival Probability| F[Use P of X greater than x equals e to power minus lambda x]
    E -->|CDF Probability| G[Use F of x equals 1 minus e to power minus lambda x]
    E -->|Conditional Probability| H[Apply Memoryless Property]
    F --> I[Compute Numerical Answer]
    G --> I
    H --> J[P of X greater than s plus t given X greater than s equals P of X greater than t]
    J --> I
    I --> K[END: Final Answer]
```

### 4.3 Comparative Block Architecture: Normal vs Exponential

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Random Variable X] --> B{Support Domain}
    B -->|Negative infinity to infinity| C[Normal Distribution N mu sigma squared]
    B -->|Zero to infinity| D[Exponential Distribution Exp lambda]
    C --> E[Symmetric Bell Curve]
    C --> F[Mean equals Median equals Mode equals mu]
    C --> G[Variance equals sigma squared]
    D --> H[Monotonic Decay Curve]
    D --> I[Memoryless Property]
    D --> J[Mean equals 1 divided by lambda]
    D --> K[Variance equals 1 divided by lambda squared]
    E --> L[Engineering Application: Measurement Errors]
    F --> L
    G --> L
    H --> M[Engineering Application: Reliability Analysis]
    I --> M
    J --> M
    K --> M
```

### 4.4 Process Flow: From Raw Data to Z-Score Probability

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Score x] --> B[Subtract Mean mu]
    B --> C[Difference x minus mu]
    C --> D[Divide by Standard Deviation sigma]
    D --> E[Z-score]
    E --> F[Look up phi z in Standard Normal Table]
    F --> G[Apply Complement Rule if Needed]
    G --> H[Final Probability Value between 0 and 1]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define the Normal Distribution. State its probability density function and mention any two properties.**

**Model Answer:**

A continuous random variable $X$ is said to follow a Normal Distribution with parameters $\mu$ (mean) and $\sigma^2$ (variance), denoted $X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$, if its probability density function is:

$$f(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left[-\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{x-\mu}{\sigma}\right)^{2}\right], \quad -\infty < x < \infty$$

**Two Properties:**

1. The curve is symmetric about the mean $\mu$, i.e., $f(\mu + a) = f(\mu - a)$ for all $a$.
2. The mean, median, and mode are all equal to $\mu$.
3. *(Bonus third property)* The total area under the curve equals 1, and approximately 99.7% of the area lies within $\mu \pm 3\sigma$.

---

#### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**State the memoryless property of the Exponential Distribution. If $X \sim \text{Exp}(2)$, find $P(X > 3 \mid X > 1)$.**

**Model Answer:**

**Memoryless Property Statement:**
For $X \sim \text{Exp}(\lambda)$ and $s, t > 0$:

$$P(X > s + t \mid X > s) = P(X > t)$$

**Computation:**

Given $\lambda = 2$:

$$P(X > 3 \mid X > 1) = P(X > 3 - 1) = P(X > 2) = e^{-\lambda \cdot 2} = e^{-4}$$

$$P(X > 3 \mid X > 1) = e^{-4} \approx 0.0183$$

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks and typically has two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Students must answer ONE of the two alternatives provided.

---

#### **Question B1 ŌĆö Alternative A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Derive the mean and variance of the Normal Distribution $N(\mu, \sigma^2)$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The weights of packets of a product are normally distributed with mean 500 g and standard deviation 20 g. Find the probability that a randomly selected packet weighs (i) more than 530 g, (ii) between 480 g and 520 g. Given $\Phi(1.5) = 0.9332$ and $\Phi(1.0) = 0.8413$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Mean Derivation:**

By definition, $E(X) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x \cdot \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-(x-\mu)^2/(2\sigma^2)}\, dx$.

Apply substitution $z = (x-\mu)/\sigma$, $x = \mu + \sigma z$, $dx = \sigma\, dz$:

$$E(X) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} (\mu + \sigma z) \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-z^2/2}\, dz = \mu \cdot 1 + \sigma \cdot 0 = \mu$$

(The second integral vanishes because the integrand is an odd function.)

**Variance Derivation:**

Compute $E(X^2) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x^2 \cdot \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-(x-\mu)^2/(2\sigma^2)}\, dx$.

Substitute $z = (x-\mu)/\sigma$:

$$E(X^2) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} (\mu + \sigma z)^2 \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-z^2/2}\, dz$$

Expanding $(\mu + \sigma z)^2 = \mu^2 + 2\mu\sigma z + \sigma^2 z^2$:

$$E(X^2) = \mu^2 \cdot 1 + 2\mu\sigma \cdot 0 + \sigma^2 \cdot 1 = \mu^2 + \sigma^2$$

Therefore:

$$\text{Var}(X) = E(X^2) - \mu^2 = \mu^2 + \sigma^2 - \mu^2 = \sigma^2 \qquad \blacksquare$$

**Model Solution for (b):**

Given: $\mu = 500$, $\sigma = 20$.

**(i) $P(X > 530)$:**

$$z = \frac{530 - 500}{20} = 1.50$$

$$P(X > 530) = P(Z > 1.50) = 1 - \Phi(1.50) = 1 - 0.9332 = 0.0668$$

**(ii) $P(480 < X < 520)$:**

$$z_1 = \frac{480 - 500}{20} = -1.00, \quad z_2 = \frac{520 - 500}{20} = 1.00$$

$$P(480 < X < 520) = \Phi(1.00) - \Phi(-1.00) = 0.8413 - (1 - 0.8413) = 2(0.8413) - 1 = 0.6826$$

**Final Answers:** (i) $0.0668$, (ii) $0.6826$.

---

#### **Question B1 ŌĆö Alternative B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2023]`

**(a)** Define the Exponential Distribution. Derive its mean and variance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The lifetime (in years) of a certain brand of battery follows an exponential distribution with mean 4 years. Find: (i) the probability that a battery lasts more than 6 years, (ii) the probability that a battery lasts more than 8 years given it has already lasted 5 years, (iii) the median lifetime. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Definition:**
A continuous random variable $X \sim \text{Exp}(\lambda)$ has PDF:

$$f(x) = \begin{cases} \lambda e^{-\lambda x}, & x \geq 0 \\ 0, & x < 0 \end{cases}$$

**Mean Derivation:**

$$E(X) = \int_0^{\infty} x \lambda e^{-\lambda x}\, dx$$

Integration by parts: $u = x$, $dv = \lambda e^{-\lambda x}\, dx$, $du = dx$, $v = -e^{-\lambda x}$:

$$E(X) = \left[-x e^{-\lambda x}\right]_0^{\infty} + \int_0^{\infty} e^{-\lambda x}\, dx = 0 + \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

**Variance Derivation:**

$$E(X^2) = \int_0^{\infty} x^2 \lambda e^{-\lambda x}\, dx$$

Integration by parts: $u = x^2$, $dv = \lambda e^{-\lambda x}\, dx$:

$$E(X^2) = 0 + 2\int_0^{\infty} x e^{-\lambda x}\, dx = 2 \cdot \frac{1}{\lambda^2} = \frac{2}{\lambda^2}$$

$$\text{Var}(X) = E(X^2) - [E(X)]^2 = \frac{2}{\lambda^2} - \frac{1}{\lambda^2} = \frac{1}{\lambda^2} \qquad \blacksquare$$

**Model Solution for (b):**

Given: $1/\lambda = 4 \implies \lambda = 0.25$ per year.

**(i) $P(X > 6)$:**

$$P(X > 6) = e^{-0.25 \times 6} = e^{-1.5} \approx 0.2231$$

**(ii) $P(X > 8 \mid X > 5)$:** By the memoryless property:

$$P(X > 8 \mid X > 5) = P(X > 8 - 5) = P(X > 3) = e^{-0.25 \times 3} = e^{-0.75} \approx 0.4724$$

**(iii) Median $m$:** Solve $F(m) = 0.5$:

$$1 - e^{-0.25m} = 0.5 \implies e^{-0.25m} = 0.5 \implies m = \frac{\ln 2}{0.25} = 4 \ln 2 \approx 2.7726 \text{ years}$$

**Final Answers:** (i) $\approx 0.2231$, (ii) $\approx 0.4724$, (iii) $\approx 2.7726$ years.

---

#### **Question B2 ŌĆö Alternative A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** If $X \sim N(100, 400)$, find: (i) $P(X < 120)$, (ii) $P(90 < X < 110)$, (iii) the value of $k$ such that $P(X < k) = 0.95$. Given $\Phi(1.0) = 0.8413$, $\Phi(1.645) = 0.95$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The time between arrivals of customers at a billing counter follows an exponential distribution with mean 5 minutes. Find: (i) the probability that the inter-arrival time is less than 3 minutes, (ii) the probability that the inter-arrival time exceeds 8 minutes, (iii) the variance of the inter-arrival time. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

Given: $\mu = 100$, $\sigma^2 = 400 \implies \sigma = 20$.

**(i) $P(X < 120)$:**

$$z = \frac{120 - 100}{20} = 1.00$$

$$P(X < 120) = \Phi(1.00) = 0.8413$$

**(ii) $P(90 < X < 110)$:**

$$z_1 = \frac{90 - 100}{20} = -0.50, \quad z_2 = \frac{110 - 100}{20} = 0.50$$

$$P(90 < X < 110) = \Phi(0.50) - \Phi(-0.50) = 2\Phi(0.50) - 1$$

Using $\Phi(0.50) = 0.6915$:

$$= 2(0.6915) - 1 = 0.3830$$

**(iii) Find $k$ such that $P(X < k) = 0.95$:**

$$P\!\left(Z < \frac{k - 100}{20}\right) = 0.95 \implies \frac{k - 100}{20} = 1.645$$

$$k = 100 + 20(1.645) = 100 + 32.9 = 132.9$$

**Model Solution for (b):**

Given: Mean $= 1/\lambda = 5 \implies \lambda = 0.2$ per minute.

**(i) $P(X < 3)$:**

$$F(3) = 1 - e^{-0.2 \times 3} = 1 - e^{-0.6} \approx 1 - 0.5488 = 0.4512$$

**(ii) $P(X > 8)$:**

$$P(X > 8) = e^{-0.2 \times 8} = e^{-1.6} \approx 0.2019$$

**(iii) Variance:**

$$\text{Var}(X) = \frac{1}{\lambda^2} = \frac{1}{(0.2)^2} = 25 \text{ minutes}^2$$

**Final Answers:** (i) $0.8413$, (ii) $0.3830$, (iii) $k = 132.9$, (iv) $0.4512$, (v) $0.2019$, (vi) $25 \text{ min}^2$.

---

#### **Question B2 ŌĆö Alternative B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**(a)** Define the Standard Normal Distribution. If $X \sim N(50, 25)$, find $P(X \leq 55)$ and $P(45 \leq X \leq 58)$. Given $\Phi(1.0) = 0.8413$, $\Phi(1.6) = 0.9452$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The waiting time (in minutes) at a restaurant is exponentially distributed with $\lambda = 0.1$. A customer arrives at random. Find: (i) the probability that the customer waits less than 5 minutes, (ii) the probability that the customer waits more than 15 minutes, (iii) the mean waiting time, (iv) the standard deviation of waiting time. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

The **Standard Normal Distribution** is $N(0, 1)$, with PDF $\phi(z) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-z^2/2}$.

Given: $\mu = 50$, $\sigma^2 = 25 \implies \sigma = 5$.

**$P(X \leq 55)$:**

$$z = \frac{55 - 50}{5} = 1.00$$

$$P(X \leq 55) = \Phi(1.00) = 0.8413$$

**$P(45 \leq X \leq 58)$:**

$$z_1 = \frac{45 - 50}{5} = -1.00, \quad z_2 = \frac{58 - 50}{5} = 1.60$$

$$P(45 \leq X \leq 58) = \Phi(1.60) - \Phi(-1.00) = 0.9452 - (1 - 0.8413) = 0.9452 - 0.1587 = 0.7865$$

**Model Solution for (b):**

Given: $\lambda = 0.1$ per minute.

**(i) $P(X < 5) = F(5) = 1 - e^{-0.1 \times 5} = 1 - e^{-0.5} \approx 1 - 0.6065 = 0.3935$**

**(ii) $P(X > 15) = e^{-0.1 \times 15} = e^{-1.5} \approx 0.2231$**

**(iii) Mean:** $E(X) = 1/\lambda = 1/0.1 = 10$ minutes.

**(iv) Standard Deviation:** $\sigma_X = 1/\lambda = 10$ minutes.

**Final Answers:** (i) $0.8413$, (ii) $0.7865$, (iii) $0.3935$, (iv) $0.2231$, (v) Mean $= 10$ min, (vi) SD $= 10$ min.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout ŌĆö Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Notation Trap:** Writing $N(\mu, \sigma)$ instead of $N(\mu, \sigma^2)$ ŌĆö costs 1 mark immediately. Always remember: the *second parameter is variance*, not standard deviation.
>
> 2. **Forgetting Symmetry Rule:** In the Standard Normal Table, only $\Phi(z)$ for $z \geq 0$ is given. Students often write $\Phi(-1.5) = 0.0668$ directly from the table ŌĆö but they should compute it as $1 - \Phi(1.5) = 0.0668$. This costs 1ŌĆō2 marks.
>
> 3. **Exponential Domain Violation:** Writing $f(x) = \lambda e^{-\lambda x}$ for all $x$ (without the condition $x \geq 0$) is incomplete. Always state the piecewise definition.
>
> 4. **Memoryless Property Misapplication:** The property gives $P(X > s + t \mid X > s) = P(X > t)$, not $P(X > s + t \mid X > s) = P(X > s + t)$. Forgetting to subtract $s$ from the bound is a frequent error.
>
> 5. **Integration by Parts Sign Error:** When deriving $E(X)$ for the Exponential Distribution, students often forget the negative sign in $v = -e^{-\lambda x}$. This propagates to a wrong sign in the final answer.
>
> 6. **Not Converting Units:** If time is given in minutes but the problem asks for hours, students often forget to convert. Always check unit consistency before substituting into $e^{-\lambda x}$.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### **Rapid Revision Checklist**

- [x] **Normal Distribution PDF:** $f(x) = \dfrac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{x-\mu}{\sigma}\right)^2}$, domain $x \in (-\infty, \infty)$.
- [x] **Standard Normal:** $Z = \dfrac{X - \mu}{\sigma} \sim N(0, 1)$. Always use this to convert any Normal probability to a Standard Normal probability.
- [x] **Symmetry Trick:** $\Phi(-z) = 1 - \Phi(z)$. Essential for negative $z$-values.
- [x] **Empirical 68-95-99.7 Rule:** Useful for quick sanity checks on Normal probabilities.
- [x] **Exponential PDF:** $f(x) = \lambda e^{-\lambda x}$ for $x \geq 0$ and $0$ otherwise.
- [x] **Exponential CDF:** $F(x) = 1 - e^{-\lambda x}$.
- [x] **Exponential Mean & Variance:** $E(X) = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ and $\text{Var}(X) = \dfrac{1}{\lambda^2}$.
- [x] **Memoryless Property:** $P(X > s + t \mid X > s) = P(X > t)$ ŌĆö the defining feature of the Exponential.
- [x] **Median of Exponential:** $\dfrac{\ln 2}{\lambda} \approx \dfrac{0.693}{\lambda}$, which is **less than the mean** $1/\lambda$ (right-skewed confirmation).
- [x] **Linear Transformation (Normal):** $aX + b \sim N(a\mu + b, a^2\sigma^2)$.
- [x] **Linear Transformation (Exponential):** $cX \sim \text{Exp}(\lambda/c)$ for $c > 0$.
- [x] **Poisson-Exponential Link:** If events follow a Poisson process with rate $\lambda$, inter-arrival times follow $\text{Exp}(\lambda)$.
- [x] **No closed-form CDF for Normal:** Unlike the Exponential, the Normal CDF has no elementary antiderivative. Always use the Standard Normal Table.
- [x] **Skewness Contrast:** Normal is symmetric (skewness = 0), Exponential is right-skewed (skewness = 2).
- [x] **Engineering Memory Aid:** "Normal = Nature's Curve" (measurements), "Exponential = Event's Wait" (time intervals).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Joint PDF of Two Continuous Random Variables
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GCMAT401 - MATHEMATICS FOR PHYSICAL SCIENCE AND LIFE SCIENCE ŌĆō 4
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Joint PDF of Two Continuous Random Variables

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Joint Probability Density Function (Joint PDF)**
> Let $X$ and $Y$ be two continuous random variables defined on the same sample space $S$. A function $f_{X,Y}(x,y)$ is called the **Joint Probability Density Function** of $X$ and $Y$ if for every pair of real numbers $(a,b)$ with $a < b$ and $c < d$, the probability that $X$ lies in $(a,b]$ and $Y$ lies in $(c,d]$ is given by:
>
> $$P(a < X \leq b,\ c < Y \leq d) = \int_{a}^{b}\int_{c}^{d} f_{X,Y}(x,y)\, dy\, dx$$

Equivalently, the joint PDF is the second-order mixed partial derivative of the **Joint Cumulative Distribution Function (JCDF)** $F_{X,Y}(x,y) = P(X \leq x, Y \leq y)$:

$$f_{X,Y}(x,y) = \frac{\partial^2 F_{X,Y}(x,y)}{\partial x\, \partial y}$$

### The Two Fundamental Properties

> [!NOTE]
> **Property 1 (Non-Negativity):** $f_{X,Y}(x,y) \geq 0$ for all $(x,y) \in \mathbb{R}^2$
>
> **Property 2 (Unit Total Probability):**
> $$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_{X,Y}(x,y)\, dx\, dy = 1$$
>
> The integral of the joint PDF over the entire 2D plane must equal **1** (i.e., 100% probability), since it is certain that $X$ and $Y$ take *some* values.

### Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you are a **meteorologist** studying the daily **temperature** ($X$, in ┬░C) and **humidity** ($Y$, in %) in Kochi. You cannot assign a probability to a single specific reading like "temperature = 28┬░C and humidity = 75%" because there are infinitely many possible values. Instead, you measure **how densely** observations cluster around certain pairs of values. The **joint PDF** $f_{X,Y}(x,y)$ is exactly that: a *density* over a 2D plane indicating how probable it is to land inside a small rectangular region around the point $(x,y)$.

> [!TIP]
> **Think of the joint PDF as a "probability cloud" floating above the $xy$-plane.** The thicker the cloud at a point, the more likely the random vector $(X,Y)$ is to fall near that point. The total "volume" of this cloud (the double integral) must equal exactly **1**.

### Marginal Probability Density Functions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Marginal PDFs ŌĆö Extracting the individual behavior of each variable**
>
> $$f_X(x) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_{X,Y}(x,y)\, dy$$
>
> $$f_Y(y) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_{X,Y}(x,y)\, dx$$
>
> These are obtained by *integrating out* (collapsing) the other variable ŌĆö analogous to projecting a 3D cloud onto a 2D wall and then onto a 1D floor.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Step-by-Step Logical Framework

The complete theoretical pipeline for joint continuous distributions operates in **four interconnected stages**:

**Stage 1 ŌĆö Joint Cumulative Distribution Function (JCDF)**
- Foundation block: $F_{X,Y}(x,y) = P(X \leq x,\ Y \leq y)$
- Always non-decreasing in each argument, right-continuous.
- As $x \to \infty$ and $y \to \infty$, $F_{X,Y}(x,y) \to 1$.
- As $x \to -\infty$ or $y \to -\infty$, $F_{X,Y}(x,y) \to 0$.

**Stage 2 ŌĆö Joint PDF via Differentiation**
- $f_{X,Y}(x,y) = \dfrac{\partial^2 F_{X,Y}(x,y)}{\partial x\, \partial y}$
- The PDF is the *rate of change* of cumulative probability in 2D.
- Must satisfy non-negativity and total area = 1.

**Stage 3 ŌĆö Probability Computation**
- For any 2D region $R \subseteq \mathbb{R}^2$:
$$P((X,Y) \in R) = \iint_{R} f_{X,Y}(x,y)\, dx\, dy$$
- For a rectangle $[a,b] \times [c,d]$:
$$P(a < X \leq b,\ c < Y \leq d) = \int_{a}^{b}\int_{c}^{d} f_{X,Y}(x,y)\, dy\, dx$$
- Note: the "$\leq$" / "$<$" distinction does not matter for continuous distributions.

**Stage 4 ŌĆö Recovering JCDF from Joint PDF**
$$F_{X,Y}(x,y) = \int_{-\infty}^{x}\int_{-\infty}^{y} f_{X,Y}(u,v)\, dv\, du$$

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates every formula the KTU examiner may test on Joint PDFs.

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Key Remark |
|---|---------|----------------------|------------|
| 1 | Joint CDF | $F_{X,Y}(x,y) = P(X \leq x,\ Y \leq y)$ | Right-continuous, non-decreasing in each variable |
| 2 | Joint PDF | $f_{X,Y}(x,y) = \dfrac{\partial^{2} F_{X,Y}(x,y)}{\partial x\, \partial y}$ | Density, not a probability itself |
| 3 | Non-negativity | $f_{X,Y}(x,y) \geq 0$ | For all $(x,y) \in \mathbb{R}^{2}$ |
| 4 | Total integral | $\displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_{X,Y}(x,y)\, dx\, dy = 1$ | "Volume under the cloud = 1" |
| 5 | Probability over region $R$ | $P((X,Y) \in R) = \displaystyle\iint_{R} f_{X,Y}(x,y)\, dA$ | Always a *non-negative* scalar $\leq 1$ |
| 6 | CDF from PDF | $F_{X,Y}(x,y) = \displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{x}\int_{-\infty}^{y} f_{X,Y}(u,v)\, dv\, du$ | Double integration recovers CDF |
| 7 | Marginal PDF of $X$ | $f_{X}(x) = \displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_{X,Y}(x,y)\, dy$ | Integrate out $y$ |
| 8 | Marginal PDF of $Y$ | $f_{Y}(y) = \displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_{X,Y}(x,y)\, dx$ | Integrate out $x$ |
| 9 | Independence | $f_{X,Y}(x,y) = f_{X}(x)\cdot f_{Y}(y)$ for all $(x,y)$ | Equivalent: $F_{X,Y}(x,y) = F_{X}(x) F_{Y}(y)$ |
| 10 | Conditional PDF of $X \mid Y=y$ | $f_{X \mid Y}(x \mid y) = \dfrac{f_{X,Y}(x,y)}{f_{Y}(y)}$, $\;f_{Y}(y) > 0$ | Must be a valid 1D PDF in $x$ |
| 11 | Conditional PDF of $Y \mid X=x$ | $f_{Y \mid X}(y \mid x) = \dfrac{f_{X,Y}(x,y)}{f_{X}(x)}$, $\;f_{X}(x) > 0$ | Must be a valid 1D PDF in $y$ |
| 12 | Multiplication Rule | $f_{X,Y}(x,y) = f_{X \mid Y}(x \mid y)\, f_{Y}(y) = f_{Y \mid X}(y \mid x)\, f_{X}(x)$ | Symmetric factorization |
| 13 | Joint PDF independence check | If $f_{X,Y}(x,y) = g(x) h(y)$ and both $g,h \geq 0$ with $\int g = \int h = 1$, then $X \perp Y$ | Useful short-cut |
| 14 | Joint PDF constant over rectangle | $f_{X,Y}(x,y) = \dfrac{1}{(b-a)(d-c)}$ for $a<x<b,\ c<y<d$ | Uniform distribution on rectangle |

### Real-World Engineering Utility

Joint PDFs are the **backbone of reliability engineering** and **signal processing**:
- In **communication systems**, the joint PDF of the in-phase and quadrature components of noise is used to compute bit-error rates.
- In **structural engineering**, the joint PDF of load ($X$) and material strength ($Y$) determines the **probability of failure** $P(X > Y)$.
- In **medical imaging and biostatistics**, joint PDFs model the relationship between biomarkers (e.g., blood pressure and cholesterol) for risk stratification.
- In **machine learning**, the joint PDF is the starting point for **Bayesian inference** and the derivation of posterior distributions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation 1 ŌĆö From Joint CDF to Joint PDF (and Vice Versa)

**Starting Point:** The Joint CDF is defined as

$$F_{X,Y}(x,y) = P(X \leq x,\ Y \leq y)$$

**Step 1:** Express the probability of a small rectangular region $(x, x+\Delta x] \times (y, y+\Delta y]$ using the CDF:

$$P(x < X \leq x + \Delta x,\ y < Y \leq y + \Delta y) = F_{X,Y}(x+\Delta x, y+\Delta y) - F_{X,Y}(x, y+\Delta y) - F_{X,Y}(x+\Delta x, y) + F_{X,Y}(x, y)$$

*Explanation:* This is a 2D inclusionŌĆōexclusion. We add the upper-right rectangle, subtract the two "slices" that extend too far, then add back the overlapping corner.

**Step 2:** The joint PDF is defined as the probability *density*, i.e., probability per unit area:

$$f_{X,Y}(x,y) = \lim_{\Delta x \to 0,\ \Delta y \to 0} \frac{P(x < X \leq x + \Delta x,\ y < Y \leq y + \Delta y)}{\Delta x \cdot \Delta y}$$

**Step 3:** Applying the limit to the four-term CDF expression, the first-order terms $\Delta x$ and $\Delta y$ vanish, leaving only the second-order mixed derivative:

$$\begin{aligned}
f_{X,Y}(x,y) &= \lim_{\Delta x \to 0}\lim_{\Delta y \to 0} \frac{F(x+\Delta x, y+\Delta y) - F(x, y+\Delta y) - F(x+\Delta x, y) + F(x, y)}{\Delta x\, \Delta y} \\
&= \frac{\partial^2 F_{X,Y}(x,y)}{\partial x\, \partial y}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Inverse relation (CDF from PDF):** Starting from the probability of a rectangle and accumulating from $-\infty$:

$$F_{X,Y}(x,y) = P(X \leq x,\ Y \leq y) = \int_{-\infty}^{x}\int_{-\infty}^{y} f_{X,Y}(u,v)\, dv\, du$$

This is obtained by setting $a = -\infty$, $b = x$, $c = -\infty$, $d = y$ in the probability-of-rectangle formula.

---

### Derivation 2 ŌĆö Marginal PDF of $X$ from the Joint PDF

**Goal:** Show that $f_X(x) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_{X,Y}(x,y)\, dy$.

**Step 1:** By definition, the marginal CDF of $X$ alone is

$$F_X(x) = P(X \leq x)$$

**Step 2:** Introduce $Y$ into the probability using the law of total probability (integrating over all $Y$):

$$F_X(x) = P(X \leq x) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} P(X \leq x,\ Y \leq y) \, dy \quad \text{(continuous analogue)}$$

More precisely, for an arbitrary $\Delta y$ partition, the event $\{X \leq x\}$ is the union of disjoint strips $\{x \leq x,\ y < Y \leq y + \Delta y\}$, and so:

$$F_X(x) = \lim_{\Delta y \to 0} \sum_{k} P(X \leq x,\ y_k < Y \leq y_k + \Delta y) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} F_{X,Y}(x,y)\, dy$$

**Step 3:** Differentiate both sides with respect to $x$ (using Leibniz's rule, since the upper limit of the outer integral depends on $x$):

$$\frac{d F_X(x)}{dx} = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{\partial F_{X,Y}(x,y)}{\partial x}\, dy$$

**Step 4:** Recognize that $\dfrac{\partial F_{X,Y}(x,y)}{\partial x}$ is *one* partial derivative of the CDF. Now, fixing $x$ and differentiating with respect to $y$ in the CDF-from-PDF formula and then integrating back, we obtain $\dfrac{\partial F_{X,Y}(x,y)}{\partial x} = \int_{-\infty}^{y} f_{X,Y}(x,v)\, dv$. Substituting:

$$\begin{aligned}
f_X(x) &= \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \left[\int_{-\infty}^{y} f_{X,Y}(x,v)\, dv\right] dy
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 (Cleaner Derivation):** A more direct route is to start with the probability of a *strip* $x < X \leq x + \Delta x$ and divide by $\Delta x$:

$$f_X(x) = \lim_{\Delta x \to 0} \frac{P(x < X \leq x + \Delta x)}{\Delta x} = \lim_{\Delta x \to 0} \frac{1}{\Delta x} \int_{x}^{x+\Delta x} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_{X,Y}(u,y)\, dy\, du$$

Since the integrand is continuous, the average value of the inner integral over $[x, x+\Delta x]$ tends to its value at $x$ as $\Delta x \to 0$:

$$f_X(x) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_{X,Y}(x,y)\, dy \qquad \blacksquare$$

The derivation for $f_Y(y)$ is **symmetric**, obtained by swapping the roles of $x$ and $y$.

---

### Derivation 3 ŌĆö Conditional PDF and the Multiplication Rule

**Definition:** For $f_Y(y) > 0$, the conditional PDF of $X$ given $Y = y$ is

$$f_{X \mid Y}(x \mid y) = \frac{f_{X,Y}(x,y)}{f_{Y}(y)}$$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Verifying it is a valid PDF in $x$:**

$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_{X \mid Y}(x \mid y)\, dx = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{f_{X,Y}(x,y)}{f_{Y}(y)}\, dx = \frac{1}{f_{Y}(y)} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_{X,Y}(x,y)\, dx = \frac{f_Y(y)}{f_Y(y)} = 1$$

Also, since $f_{X,Y}(x,y) \geq 0$ and $f_{Y}(y) > 0$, the conditional PDF is non-negative. Hence it qualifies as a legitimate PDF.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Multiplication Rule:** Cross-multiplying the definition gives

$$f_{X,Y}(x,y) = f_{X \mid Y}(x \mid y)\, f_{Y}(y)$$

A symmetric application gives

$$f_{X,Y}(x,y) = f_{Y \mid X}(y \mid x)\, f_{X}(x)$$

These are the **multiplication rules** for joint PDFs.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Independence Criterion:** If $X$ and $Y$ are independent, then knowing $Y = y$ provides no information about $X$, so $f_{X \mid Y}(x \mid y) = f_X(x)$. Substituting:

$$f_{X,Y}(x,y) = f_X(x) \cdot f_Y(y)$$

Conversely, if $f_{X,Y}(x,y) = f_X(x) f_Y(y)$ everywhere, then the conditional PDF collapses to the marginal, proving independence. $\blacksquare$

---

### Worked Example Derivation (Illustrative)

Let $f_{X,Y}(x,y) = k(6 - x - y)$ for $0 < x < 2,\ 0 < y < 2$, and $0$ elsewhere.

**Step 1: Find $k$.**

$$\int_{0}^{2}\int_{0}^{2} k(6 - x - y)\, dy\, dx = 1$$

Inner integral:

$$\int_{0}^{2} (6 - x - y)\, dy = \left[6y - xy - \frac{y^{2}}{2}\right]_{0}^{2} = (12 - 2x - 2) - 0 = 10 - 2x$$

Outer integral:

$$\int_{0}^{2} (10 - 2x)\, dx = \left[10x - x^{2}\right]_{0}^{2} = 20 - 4 = 16$$

So $k \cdot 16 = 1 \implies \boxed{k = \dfrac{1}{16}}$.

**Step 2: Marginal of $X$.**

$$f_X(x) = \int_{0}^{2} \frac{1}{16}(6 - x - y)\, dy = \frac{1}{16}(10 - 2x) = \frac{5 - x}{8}, \quad 0 < x < 2$$

**Step 3: Check independence ŌĆö is $f_X(x) f_Y(y) = f_{X,Y}(x,y)$?**

By symmetry, $f_Y(y) = \dfrac{5 - y}{8}$ for $0 < y < 2$.

$$f_X(x) f_Y(y) = \frac{(5-x)(5-y)}{64} \neq \frac{6 - x - y}{16} = f_{X,Y}(x,y)$$

Hence $X$ and $Y$ are **not independent**.

**Step 4: $P(X + Y < 3)$ ŌĆö region of integration:** $0 < x < 2$, $0 < y < 2$, $x + y < 3$. The line $x + y = 3$ intersects the square at $(1,2)$ and $(2,1)$. So $y$ ranges from $0$ to $2$, and for each $y$, $x$ ranges from $0$ to $\min(2, 3-y) = 3 - y$ (since $3 - y \leq 2 \iff y \geq 1$).

$$P(X+Y<3) = \int_{0}^{1}\int_{0}^{2} f\, dx\, dy + \int_{1}^{2}\int_{0}^{3-y} f\, dx\, dy$$

By symmetry of $f$ in $x$ and $y$, the two integrals are equal, so:

$$P(X+Y<3) = 2 \int_{1}^{2}\int_{0}^{3-y} \frac{6 - x - y}{16}\, dx\, dy$$

This is fully evaluated in Section 5's model answer.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram 1 ŌĆö Conceptual Map: How Joint Quantities Relate

This flowchart shows the full pipeline from the foundational Joint CDF to all derived quantities (marginals, conditionals, independence).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Joint CDF F_XY of x,y"] -->|"Partial Diff Twice"| B["Joint PDF f_XY of x,y"]
    B -->|"Integrate w.r.t y"| C["Marginal PDF f_X of x"]
    B -->|"Integrate w.r.t x"| D["Marginal PDF f_Y of y"]
    B -->|"Divide by f_Y of y"| E["Conditional PDF f_X_given_Y of x given y"]
    B -->|"Divide by f_X of x"| F["Conditional PDF f_Y_given_X of y given x"]
    C -->|"Check f_X times f_Y equals f_XY"| G["Independence Test"]
    D -->|"Check f_X times f_Y equals f_XY"| G
    E -->|"Multiply by f_Y of y"| B
    F -->|"Multiply by f_X of x"| B
    B -->|"Double Integrate over Region R"| H["Probability P of X Y in R"]
    A -->|"Evaluate at x and y"| I["Joint Probability P of X le x and Y le y"]
```

### Diagram 2 ŌĆö Visual Picture of the "Probability Cloud" and Marginals

This block diagram maps the geometric interpretation of a 2D density being collapsed to 1D.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1["Two Dimensional Plane Domain R"]
        R["Region R in xy plane: support of f_XY"]
    end
    subgraph S2["Three Dimensional Density Surface"]
        S["Surface z = f_XY of x,y: probability cloud"]
    end
    subgraph S3["One Dimensional Marginals"]
        M1["Marginal f_X of x: projection on x axis"]
        M2["Marginal f_Y of y: projection on y axis"]
    end
    S1 -->|"PDF lives above"| S
    S -->|"Integrate over y slice"| M1
    S -->|"Integrate over x slice"| M2
```

### Diagram 3 ŌĆö Engineering Reliability Interpretation

In structural engineering, the **probability of failure** is the volume of the joint PDF lying in the region where applied stress exceeds material strength. The diagram below maps this engineering concept onto the joint PDF framework.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["Random Variable X: Applied Load"] --> P2["Random Variable Y: Material Strength"]
    P2 --> P3["Joint PDF f_XY of x,y over Load Strength Plane"]
    P3 --> P4["Region of Failure: x greater than y"]
    P3 --> P5["Region of Survival: y greater than x"]
    P4 --> P6["Probability of Failure = Double Integral over x greater than y of f_XY dA"]
    P5 --> P7["Probability of Survival = Double Integral over y greater than x of f_XY dA"]
    P6 --> P8["P Failure plus P Survival = 1"]
    P7 --> P8
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to read these diagrams in an exam:** The block diagrams serve as memory anchors. If the examiner asks "How do you find the marginal PDF of $X$?", you can immediately picture the **vertical slices** of the cloud collapsing onto the $x$-axis, which translates to the integral $\int f_{X,Y}(x,y)\, dy$.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question A1 ŌĆö `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]`

**Define the joint probability density function of two continuous random variables $X$ and $Y$. State its two essential properties.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1.5 marks):** A function $f_{X,Y}(x,y)$ is the joint PDF of two continuous random variables $X$ and $Y$ if, for any region $R \subseteq \mathbb{R}^2$,
>
> $$P((X,Y) \in R) = \iint_{R} f_{X,Y}(x,y)\, dx\, dy$$
>
> Equivalently, $f_{X,Y}(x,y) = \dfrac{\partial^2 F_{X,Y}(x,y)}{\partial x\, \partial y}$, where $F_{X,Y}(x,y) = P(X \leq x, Y \leq y)$ is the joint CDF.
>
> **Property 1 ŌĆö Non-negativity (0.75 marks):** $f_{X,Y}(x,y) \geq 0$ for all $(x,y) \in \mathbb{R}^2$.
>
> **Property 2 ŌĆö Unit total integral (0.75 marks):**
> $$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_{X,Y}(x,y)\, dx\, dy = 1$$

---

#### Question A2 ŌĆö `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]`

**State and prove the multiplication rule for joint PDFs.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Statement (1 mark):** For $f_Y(y) > 0$, the joint PDF of $X$ and $Y$ can be factorized as
>
> $$f_{X,Y}(x,y) = f_{X \mid Y}(x \mid y) \cdot f_{Y}(y) = f_{Y \mid X}(y \mid x) \cdot f_{X}(x)$$
>
> **Proof (2 marks):** The conditional PDF of $X$ given $Y = y$ is *defined* as
>
> $$f_{X \mid Y}(x \mid y) = \frac{f_{X,Y}(x,y)}{f_{Y}(y)}, \quad f_Y(y) > 0$$
>
> Cross-multiplying immediately yields $f_{X,Y}(x,y) = f_{X \mid Y}(x \mid y)\, f_Y(y)$. The symmetric derivation with roles of $X$ and $Y$ interchanged gives the second form. Hence, the multiplication rule is established. $\blacksquare$

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the indicator function:** When $f_{X,Y}(x,y)$ is defined piecewise, students often integrate over the *entire* plane rather than only over the support region. Always state the support explicitly.
> 2. **Confusing $f_{X \mid Y}$ with $f_{X}$:** The conditional PDF is a *function of $x$* with $y$ *fixed*. It is **not** the same as the marginal PDF unless $X$ and $Y$ are independent.
> 3. **Independence trap:** Showing $f_X(x) f_Y(y) \neq f_{X,Y}(x,y)$ at *one* point is not sufficient. You must either (a) show the product form *never* matches, or (b) show the factorization is impossible structurally.
> 4. **Sign errors in limits:** When evaluating $P(X + Y < c)$, carefully draw the region in the $xy$-plane *before* integrating. Splitting the region at intersection points with the support boundary is the most common source of lost marks.

---

#### Part B ŌĆö Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]`

The joint PDF of two random variables $X$ and $Y$ is given by

$$f_{X,Y}(x,y) = \begin{cases} k(6 - x - y), & 0 < x < 2,\ 0 < y < 2 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**(a) Find the value of the constant $k$. Also obtain the marginal PDFs of $X$ and $Y$ and comment on the independence of $X$ and $Y$. [7 marks]**

**(b) Compute $P(X + Y < 3)$ and $P(X < 1.5 \mid Y = 1)$. [7 marks]**

---

**Solution to (a) ŌĆö 7 marks**

**Step 1: Finding $k$ (2 marks)**

Using the unit-total-probability property:

$$\int_{0}^{2}\int_{0}^{2} k(6 - x - y)\, dy\, dx = 1$$

Evaluate the inner integral with respect to $y$ (treat $x$ as constant):

$$\int_{0}^{2} (6 - x - y)\, dy = \left[6y - xy - \frac{y^{2}}{2}\right]_{0}^{2} = 12 - 2x - 2 = 10 - 2x$$

Now the outer integral:

$$\int_{0}^{2} (10 - 2x)\, dx = \left[10x - x^{2}\right]_{0}^{2} = 20 - 4 = 16$$

Therefore $k \cdot 16 = 1 \Rightarrow \boxed{k = \dfrac{1}{16}}$ **(2 marks)**

**Step 2: Marginal of $X$ (2 marks)**

$$f_X(x) = \int_{0}^{2} \frac{1}{16}(6 - x - y)\, dy = \frac{1}{16}(10 - 2x) = \frac{5 - x}{8}, \quad 0 < x < 2$$

**(2 marks)**

**Step 3: Marginal of $Y$ (1 mark)**

By symmetry of $f_{X,Y}$ in $x$ and $y$:

$$f_Y(y) = \frac{5 - y}{8}, \quad 0 < y < 2$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 4: Independence check (2 marks)**

$$f_X(x) f_Y(y) = \frac{(5-x)(5-y)}{64} = \frac{25 - 5x - 5y + xy}{64}$$

$$f_{X,Y}(x,y) = \frac{6 - x - y}{16} = \frac{24 - 4x - 4y}{64}$$

These are not equal (compare $xy$ term: $0$ vs. $1$ coefficient, and constant $25$ vs. $24$). Hence **$X$ and $Y$ are NOT independent**. **(2 marks)**

---

**Solution to (b) ŌĆö 7 marks**

**Step 1: Compute $P(X + Y < 3)$ (4 marks)**

The region is $\{(x,y): 0<x<2,\ 0<y<2,\ x+y<3\}$. The line $x+y=3$ intersects the square boundary at $(1,2)$ and $(2,1)$. For $0 < y < 1$, the constraint $x + y < 3$ is non-binding (since $x + y < 2 + 1 = 3$ automatically), so $x$ ranges over the full $(0,2)$. For $1 < y < 2$, $x$ is restricted to $(0, 3-y)$.

$$P(X+Y<3) = \int_{0}^{1}\int_{0}^{2} f_{X,Y}\, dx\, dy + \int_{1}^{2}\int_{0}^{3-y} f_{X,Y}\, dx\, dy$$

**First integral** ($0 < y < 1$, $0 < x < 2$):

$$\int_{0}^{2} \frac{6 - x - y}{16}\, dx = \frac{1}{16}\left[6x - \frac{x^2}{2} - xy\right]_{0}^{2} = \frac{1}{16}(12 - 2 - 2y) = \frac{10 - 2y}{16}$$

Now integrate over $y \in (0,1)$:

$$\int_{0}^{1} \frac{10 - 2y}{16}\, dy = \frac{1}{16}\left[10y - y^2\right]_{0}^{1} = \frac{9}{16}$$

**Second integral** ($1 < y < 2$, $0 < x < 3-y$):

$$\int_{0}^{3-y} \frac{6 - x - y}{16}\, dx = \frac{1}{16}\left[6x - \frac{x^2}{2} - xy\right]_{0}^{3-y}$$

Let $t = 3 - y$. Substituting:

$$= \frac{1}{16}\left[6t - \frac{t^2}{2} - (3-t)t\right] = \frac{1}{16}\left[6t - \frac{t^2}{2} - 3t + t^2\right] = \frac{1}{16}\left[3t + \frac{t^2}{2}\right]$$

With $t = 3 - y$:

$$= \frac{1}{16}\left[3(3-y) + \frac{(3-y)^2}{2}\right] = \frac{1}{16}\left[9 - 3y + \frac{9 - 6y + y^2}{2}\right]$$

$$= \frac{1}{16}\left[\frac{18 - 6y + 9 - 6y + y^2}{2}\right] = \frac{27 - 12y + y^2}{32}$$

Now integrate over $y \in (1,2)$:

$$\int_{1}^{2} \frac{27 - 12y + y^2}{32}\, dy = \frac{1}{32}\left[27y - 6y^2 + \frac{y^3}{3}\right]_{1}^{2}$$

At $y = 2$: $54 - 24 + \frac{8}{3} = 30 + \frac{8}{3} = \frac{98}{3}$

At $y = 1$: $27 - 6 + \frac{1}{3} = 21 + \frac{1}{3} = \frac{64}{3}$

Difference: $\frac{98 - 64}{3} = \frac{34}{3}$

$$\text{Second integral} = \frac{1}{32} \cdot \frac{34}{3} = \frac{34}{96} = \frac{17}{48}$$

**Total:**

$$P(X+Y<3) = \frac{9}{16} + \frac{17}{48} = \frac{27}{48} + \frac{17}{48} = \frac{44}{48} = \boxed{\frac{11}{12}} \quad \text{(approx } 0.9167\text{)}$$

**(4 marks)**

**Step 2: Compute $P(X < 1.5 \mid Y = 1)$ (3 marks)**

$$P(X < 1.5 \mid Y = 1) = \int_{0}^{1.5} f_{X \mid Y}(x \mid 1)\, dx$$

Conditional PDF:

$$f_{X \mid Y}(x \mid y) = \frac{f_{X,Y}(x,y)}{f_Y(y)} = \frac{\frac{1}{16}(6 - x - y)}{\frac{5 - y}{8}} = \frac{6 - x - y}{2(5 - y)}$$

At $y = 1$:

$$f_{X \mid Y}(x \mid 1) = \frac{6 - x - 1}{2(5 - 1)} = \frac{5 - x}{8}, \quad 0 < x < 2$$

Now integrate:

$$P(X < 1.5 \mid Y = 1) = \int_{0}^{1.5} \frac{5 - x}{8}\, dx = \frac{1}{8}\left[5x - \frac{x^2}{2}\right]_{0}^{1.5}$$

$$= \frac{1}{8}\left[7.5 - 1.125\right] = \frac{1}{8}(6.375) = \frac{6.375}{8} = \boxed{\frac{51}{64}} \quad \text{(approx } 0.7969\text{)}$$

**(3 marks)**

---

#### Part B ŌĆö Question B (14 Marks ŌĆö Alternative Choice) ŌĆö `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]`

The joint PDF of $X$ and $Y$ is

$$f_{X,Y}(x,y) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{x + y}{3}, & 0 < x < 1,\ 0 < y < 2 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**(a) Verify that $f_{X,Y}$ is a valid joint PDF. Find the marginal PDFs $f_X(x)$ and $f_Y(y)$. [7 marks]**

**(b) Find $P\left(0 < X < \dfrac{1}{2},\ 1 < Y < 2\right)$ and $P(X < Y)$. Also check whether $X$ and $Y$ are independent. [7 marks]**

---

**Solution to (a) ŌĆö 7 marks**

**Step 1: Non-negativity check (1 mark)**

For $0 < x < 1$ and $0 < y < 2$, both $x > 0$ and $y > 0$, so $x + y > 0$ and $f_{X,Y}(x,y) = \frac{x+y}{3} > 0$. Outside the support, $f_{X,Y} = 0 \geq 0$. **Property 1 satisfied.** **(1 mark)**

**Step 2: Total integral check (3 marks)**

$$\int_{0}^{1}\int_{0}^{2} \frac{x + y}{3}\, dy\, dx = 1$$

Inner integral:

$$\int_{0}^{2} (x + y)\, dy = \left[xy + \frac{y^2}{2}\right]_{0}^{2} = 2x + 2$$

Outer integral:

$$\int_{0}^{1} (2x + 2)\, dx = \left[x^2 + 2x\right]_{0}^{1} = 1 + 2 = 3$$

Therefore:

$$\int_{0}^{1}\int_{0}^{2} \frac{x + y}{3}\, dy\, dx = \frac{3}{3} = 1 \checkmark$$

**Property 2 satisfied.** $f_{X,Y}$ is a valid joint PDF. **(3 marks)**

**Step 3: Marginal of $X$ (1.5 marks)**

$$f_X(x) = \int_{0}^{2} \frac{x + y}{3}\, dy = \frac{1}{3}\left[xy + \frac{y^2}{2}\right]_{0}^{2} = \frac{1}{3}(2x + 2) = \frac{2(x + 1)}{3}, \quad 0 < x < 1$$

**(1.5 marks)**

**Step 4: Marginal of $Y$ (1.5 marks)**

$$f_Y(y) = \int_{0}^{1} \frac{x + y}{3}\, dx = \frac{1}{3}\left[\frac{x^2}{2} + xy\right]_{0}^{1} = \frac{1}{3}\left(\frac{1}{2} + y\right) = \frac{1 + 2y}{6}, \quad 0 < y < 2$$

**(1.5 marks)**

---

**Solution to (b) ŌĆö 7 marks**

**Step 1: $P\left(0 < X < \frac{1}{2}, 1 < Y < 2\right)$ (2.5 marks)**

$$P = \int_{0}^{1/2}\int_{1}^{2} \frac{x + y}{3}\, dy\, dx$$

Inner integral:

$$\int_{1}^{2} (x + y)\, dy = \left[xy + \frac{y^2}{2}\right]_{1}^{2} = (2x + 2) - \left(x + \frac{1}{2}\right) = x + \frac{3}{2}$$

Outer integral:

$$\int_{0}^{1/2} \frac{x + \frac{3}{2}}{3}\, dx = \frac{1}{3}\left[\frac{x^2}{2} + \frac{3x}{2}\right]_{0}^{1/2} = \frac{1}{3}\left(\frac{1}{8} + \frac{3}{4}\right) = \frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{7}{8} = \frac{7}{24}$$

$$P\left(0 < X < \frac{1}{2},\ 1 < Y < 2\right) = \boxed{\frac{7}{24}} \approx 0.2917$$

**(2.5 marks)**

**Step 2: $P(X < Y)$ (2.5 marks)**

The region $\{(x,y): 0<x<1,\ 0<y<2,\ x<y\}$ is the part of the rectangle below the line $y = x$.

For $0 < x < 1$, $y$ ranges from $x$ to $2$:

$$P(X < Y) = \int_{0}^{1}\int_{x}^{2} \frac{x + y}{3}\, dy\, dx$$

Inner integral:

$$\int_{x}^{2} (x + y)\, dy = \left[xy + \frac{y^2}{2}\right]_{x}^{2} = (2x + 2) - \left(x^2 + \frac{x^2}{2}\right) = 2x + 2 - \frac{3x^2}{2}$$

Outer integral:

$$\int_{0}^{1} \frac{2x + 2 - \frac{3x^2}{2}}{3}\, dx = \frac{1}{3}\left[x^2 + 2x - \frac{x^3}{2}\right]_{0}^{1} = \frac{1}{3}\left(1 + 2 - \frac{1}{2}\right) = \frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{5}{2} = \frac{5}{6}$$

$$P(X < Y) = \boxed{\frac{5}{6}} \approx 0.8333$$

**(2.5 marks)**

**Step 3: Independence check (2 marks)**

$$f_X(x) \cdot f_Y(y) = \frac{2(x+1)}{3} \cdot \frac{1+2y}{6} = \frac{(2x+2)(1+2y)}{18} = \frac{2x + 4xy + 2 + 4y}{18}$$

$$f_{X,Y}(x,y) = \frac{x + y}{3} = \frac{6x + 6y}{18}$$

Since these are clearly unequal (e.g., compare the coefficient of $xy$: $4$ vs. $0$), **$X$ and $Y$ are NOT independent**. **(2 marks)**

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist ŌĆö Joint PDF of Two Continuous Random Variables**

- **Definition:** $f_{X,Y}(x,y) = \dfrac{\partial^{2} F_{X,Y}(x,y)}{\partial x\, \partial y}$ ŌĆö the joint PDF is the *mixed second partial* of the joint CDF.
- **Two Mandatory Properties (for any joint PDF to be valid):**
  1. $f_{X,Y}(x,y) \geq 0 \ \forall (x,y) \in \mathbb{R}^2$
  2. $\displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_{X,Y}(x,y)\, dx\, dy = 1$
- **Probability over a region $R$:** $P((X,Y) \in R) = \displaystyle\iint_{R} f_{X,Y}(x,y)\, dA$
- **Probability over a rectangle $[a,b]\times[c,d]$:** $P(a<X\leq b,\ c<Y\leq d) = \displaystyle\int_{a}^{b}\int_{c}^{d} f_{X,Y}(x,y)\, dy\, dx$
- **Marginal PDFs (the "collapsing" integrals):**
  - $f_X(x) = \displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_{X,Y}(x,y)\, dy$
  - $f_Y(y) = \displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_{X,Y}(x,y)\, dx$
- **Recovering the CDF from the PDF:** $F_{X,Y}(x,y) = \displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{x}\int_{-\infty}^{y} f_{X,Y}(u,v)\, dv\, du$
- **Independence Criterion:** $X \perp Y \iff f_{X,Y}(x,y) = f_X(x)\cdot f_Y(y)$ for *all* $(x,y)$.
- **Conditional PDFs (defined for $f_Y(y)>0$ and $f_X(x)>0$):**
  - $f_{X \mid Y}(x \mid y) = \dfrac{f_{X,Y}(x,y)}{f_Y(y)}$
  - $f_{Y \mid X}(y \mid x) = \dfrac{f_{X,Y}(x,y)}{f_X(x)}$
- **Multiplication Rule:** $f_{X,Y}(x,y) = f_{X \mid Y}(x \mid y)\cdot f_Y(y) = f_{Y \mid X}(y \mid x)\cdot f_X(x)$
- **Independence Ōåö Conditional = Marginal:** When $X \perp Y$, $f_{X \mid Y}(x \mid y) = f_X(x)$ (knowing $Y$ gives no info about $X$).
- **Order of integration in problems:** Always draw the support region first; this dictates the integration limits and whether splitting into sub-regions is needed.
- **Common engineering application:** $P(\text{failure}) = P(\text{stress} > \text{strength}) = \displaystyle\iint_{x>y} f_{X,Y}(x,y)\, dx\, dy$.
- **Exam pitfall:** Forgetting to state the *support* explicitly when defining $f_{X,Y}$ piecewise, or integrating over the *entire* plane instead of over the support.
- **Verification habit:** After computing a marginal PDF, quickly check $\int f_X = 1$ to catch algebraic errors.
- **Probability shortcut ŌĆö Uniform over a region:** If $(X,Y)$ is uniform over a region of area $A$, then $f_{X,Y} = 1/A$ on that region. Useful sanity check.
- **Symmetry shortcut:** If $f_{X,Y}(x,y) = f_{X,Y}(y,x)$, then $f_X = f_Y$ and vice versa. Exploit this to halve computation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GCMAT401 - MATHEMATICS FOR PHYSICAL SCIENCE AND LIFE SCIENCE ŌĆō 4
> - **Module:** Module 2

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Marginal Probability Density Function (Marginal PDF)** is the probability density function of a single random variable obtained from the **joint probability density function** of two or more random variables, by *integrating* (or *summing*, in the discrete case) the joint density over all possible values of the *other* random variable(s).

Mathematically, if $X$ and $Y$ are two continuous random variables with joint PDF $f_{X,Y}(x, y)$, then the **marginal PDF of $X$** is:

$$
f_X(x) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_{X,Y}(x, y) \, dy
$$

and the **marginal PDF of $Y$** is:

$$
f_Y(y) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_{X,Y}(x, y) \, dx
$$

This operation is called **marginalization** of the joint distribution.

### Conditions for Validity

A function $f_X(x)$ qualifies as a marginal PDF **if and only if** it satisfies:

1. **Non-negativity:** $f_X(x) \geq 0$ for all $x \in \mathbb{R}$.
2. **Unit total area:** $\displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_X(x) \, dx = 1$.
3. It is derived from a valid joint PDF $f_{X,Y}(x,y) \geq 0$ with $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_{X,Y}(x,y)\,dx\,dy = 1$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Terminology Note (KTU 2024 Module 2 focus):** The word "marginal" originates from the historical practice of writing joint frequency tables and summing row/column totals in the **margins** of the table. The continuous analog is integration.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English)

Imagine you are a **quality control engineer** at a chip-fabrication plant. You measure **two parameters** of every chip:
- $X$ = thickness of the chip (in micrometers, $\mu m$)
- $Y$ = resistance of the chip (in ohms, $\Omega$)

You collect the joint data and obtain a **joint PDF** $f_{X,Y}(x,y)$ that tells you the *combined* likelihood of observing a chip with thickness $x$ **and** resistance $y$ *simultaneously*.

Now, your supervisor asks:

> *"Forget about resistance. Just tell me: how likely is a chip to have a thickness of 7 $\mu m$, regardless of what its resistance is?"*

To answer this, you **collapse** (or *marginalize*) the 2-D joint information along the resistance axis. You integrate out $Y$ ŌĆö essentially, you *sum up all probability contributions* across every possible resistance value for the fixed thickness of interest. The result is the **marginal PDF of $X$**.

> [!TIP]
> **Mental Model:** Think of the joint PDF as a **mountain range** (3-D surface over the $xy$-plane). The marginal PDF of $X$ is the **silhouette** of that mountain range when you view it from the *positive $y$-direction*. You are projecting all the mass onto the $x$-axis.

---

## 1.3 Key Notation and Symbols

| Symbol | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| $f_{X,Y}(x,y)$ | Joint PDF of $X$ and $Y$ |
| $f_X(x)$ | Marginal PDF of $X$ (obtained by integrating out $Y$) |
| $f_Y(y)$ | Marginal PDF of $Y$ (obtained by integrating out $X$) |
| $\Omega_X, \Omega_Y$ | Support (range) of $X$ and $Y$ respectively |
| $P(a \le X \le b)$ | Probability obtained by integrating marginal over $[a,b]$ |

---

## 1.4 When is Marginalization Used in Engineering?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Marginal PDFs are foundational in:
> - **Signal Processing** ŌĆö marginalizing noise out of joint time-frequency distributions.
> - **Reliability Engineering** ŌĆö finding the failure-time distribution of a single component in a multi-stress environment.
> - **Communication Systems** ŌĆö extracting the amplitude distribution from a joint amplitude-phase model.
> - **Biostatistics / Life Sciences** ŌĆö isolating the effect of a single variable (e.g., age) from a joint model of (age, blood pressure, cholesterol).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Logic ŌĆö Why Marginalization Works

The cornerstone of probability theory is that the **probability of an event involving only $X$** can be obtained by summing (or integrating) the joint probabilities over *all* possibilities of the other variable(s). This follows directly from the **Law of Total Probability**.

For any measurable set $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$:

$$
P(X \in A) = P\bigl((X \in A) \cap (Y \in \mathbb{R})\bigr) = \int_A \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_{X,Y}(x,y) \, dy \, dx
$$

By Fubini's theorem (justified because $f_{X,Y}$ is non-negative and integrates to 1), we can swap the order of integration:

$$
P(X \in A) = \int_A \left[ \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_{X,Y}(x,y) \, dy \right] dx = \int_A f_X(x) \, dx
$$

This forces us to define:

$$
f_X(x) \;\equiv\; \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_{X,Y}(x,y) \, dy
$$

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Procedure for Finding a Marginal PDF

Given a joint PDF $f_{X,Y}(x,y)$ on a region $D$:

**Step 1 ŌĆö Verify the joint PDF is valid:** Confirm $\iint_D f_{X,Y}(x,y)\,dx\,dy = 1$ (this is given in the problem; if not, the problem is ill-posed).

**Step 2 ŌĆö Identify the support region $D$** for $(x,y)$. This determines the limits of integration.

**Step 3 ŌĆö For $f_X(x)$:** Treat $x$ as a constant. Integrate $f_{X,Y}(x,y)$ over the $y$-range corresponding to that $x$:
- If the support is **type-1** (vertical strip $a \le x \le b$, $g_1(x) \le y \le g_2(x)$), then:
$$
f_X(x) = \int_{g_1(x)}^{g_2(x)} f_{X,Y}(x,y) \, dy, \quad a \le x \le b
$$
- If the support is **type-2** (horizontal strip $c \le y \le d$, $h_1(y) \le x \le h_2(y)$), then:
$$
f_X(x) = \int_{c}^{d} f_{X,Y}(x,y) \, dy \quad \text{(only for $x$ in the valid horizontal range)}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö For $f_Y(y)$:** Symmetrically integrate over the $x$-range.

**Step 5 ŌĆö State the support:** $f_X(x) = 0$ outside its natural range.

> [!TIP]
> **Sanity Check for KTU:** After finding $f_X(x)$, *always* verify $\int f_X(x)\,dx = 1$. If not, you have made an integration error or the joint PDF is incorrectly defined.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Conditions / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Marginal PDF of $X$ (continuous) | $f_X(x) = \displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_{X,Y}(x,y) \, dy$ | Always valid when $f_{X,Y}$ is a valid joint PDF |
| 2 | Marginal PDF of $Y$ (continuous) | $f_Y(y) = \displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_{X,Y}(x,y) \, dx$ | Symmetric to formula 1 |
| 3 | Marginal PMF (discrete analog) | $P(X = x_i) = \displaystyle\sum_{j} P(X = x_i, Y = y_j)$ | Sum replaces integral |
| 4 | Type-1 support region | $f_X(x) = \int_{g_1(x)}^{g_2(x)} f_{X,Y}(x,y)\,dy$, $a \le x \le b$ | $D = \{(x,y) : a \le x \le b,\; g_1(x) \le y \le g_2(x)\}$ |
| 5 | Type-2 support region | $f_X(x) = \int_{c}^{d} f_{X,Y}(x,y)\,dy$ on the $x$-projection | $D = \{(x,y) : c \le y \le d,\; h_1(y) \le x \le h_2(y)\}$ |
| 6 | Fundamental relation | $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_X(x)\,dx = 1$ | All PDFs integrate to **1** |
| 7 | Marginal from conditional | $f_X(x) = f_{X\mid Y}(x \mid y) \cdot f_Y(y)$ integrated over $y$ | $f_Y(y)$ acts as a *weight* |
| 8 | Symmetry of marginals | $f_{X,Y}(x,y) = f_{Y,X}(y,x)$ | Joint PDF is symmetric in its arguments |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Distinction (High-Frequency Exam Trap):** The **joint PDF** $f_{X,Y}(x,y)$ is a function of **two** variables, while a **marginal PDF** is a function of **one** variable. Do not confuse them in your answer scripts.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Field | Application of Marginal PDF |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Wireless Communications** | Finding the *envelope* distribution of a fading signal from the joint I-Q component model. |
| **Structural Reliability** | Reducing a bivariate load-resistance model to a univariate *load-only* failure probability. |
| **Biomedical Signal Processing** | Extracting the heart-rate marginal from a joint heart-rate / respiration-rate model. |
| **Machine Learning / Bayesian Inference** | Computing the **evidence** $p(x) = \int p(x \mid \theta) p(\theta)\,d\theta$ in Bayesian model selection. |
| **Image Processing** | Computing 1-D row/column projections of a 2-D image histogram. |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Below, we provide **two fully solved analytical problems** to build your KTU examination competence. Every algebraic step is shown explicitly ŌĆö no shortcuts, no skipped lines.

---

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 ŌĆö Standard Rectangular Support

### Problem Statement

The joint PDF of random variables $X$ and $Y$ is given by:

$$
f_{X,Y}(x,y) = \begin{cases} k\,(x + y), & 0 \le x \le 1,\; 0 \le y \le 1 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

**(a)** Find the value of the constant $k$.

**(b)** Find the marginal PDF $f_X(x)$.

**(c)** Find the marginal PDF $f_Y(y)$.

**(d)** Compute $P(0 \le X \le 0.5)$.

### Part (a): Determining the Constant $k$

For $f_{X,Y}$ to be a valid joint PDF, the total volume under the surface must equal **1**:

$$
\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_{X,Y}(x,y)\,dx\,dy = 1
$$

Since the support is $0 \le x \le 1,\; 0 \le y \le 1$:

$$
\int_{0}^{1} \int_{0}^{1} k\,(x + y) \, dx\,dy = 1
$$

Factor out the constant $k$:

$$
k \int_{0}^{1} \int_{0}^{1} (x + y) \, dx\,dy = 1
$$

**Inner integral** with respect to $x$ (treat $y$ as constant):

$$
\int_{0}^{1} (x + y) \, dx = \left[ \frac{x^2}{2} + xy \right]_{x=0}^{x=1} = \frac{1}{2} + y
$$

**Outer integral** with respect to $y$:

$$
\int_{0}^{1} \left( \frac{1}{2} + y \right) dy = \left[ \frac{y}{2} + \frac{y^2}{2} \right]_{0}^{1} = \frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{2} = 1
$$

Therefore:

$$
k \cdot 1 = 1 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \boxed{k = 1}
$$

---

### Part (b): Marginal PDF of $X$ ŌĆö $f_X(x)$

By definition, integrate the joint PDF over all $y$:

$$
f_X(x) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_{X,Y}(x,y)\,dy
$$

For a fixed $x \in [0, 1]$, the valid $y$-range is $[0, 1]$:

$$
f_X(x) = \int_{0}^{1} 1 \cdot (x + y) \, dy
$$

Compute the integral:

$$
f_X(x) = \int_{0}^{1} (x + y) \, dy = \left[ xy + \frac{y^2}{2} \right]_{y=0}^{y=1} = x \cdot 1 + \frac{1}{2} = x + \frac{1}{2}
$$

So, the marginal PDF of $X$ is:

$$
\boxed{f_X(x) = \begin{cases} x + \dfrac{1}{2}, & 0 \le x \le 1 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}}
$$

---

### Part (c): Marginal PDF of $Y$ ŌĆö $f_Y(y)$

By symmetry of the joint PDF (since $f_{X,Y}(x,y) = f_{X,Y}(y,x)$ in this case ŌĆö both variables appear symmetrically as $x + y$):

$$
f_Y(y) = \int_{0}^{1} (x + y) \, dx = \left[ \frac{x^2}{2} + xy \right]_{0}^{1} = \frac{1}{2} + y
$$

$$
\boxed{f_Y(y) = \begin{cases} y + \dfrac{1}{2}, & 0 \le y \le 1 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}}
$$

---

### Part (d): Computing $P(0 \le X \le 0.5)$

$$
P(0 \le X \le 0.5) = \int_{0}^{0.5} f_X(x) \, dx = \int_{0}^{0.5} \left( x + \frac{1}{2} \right) dx
$$

$$
= \left[ \frac{x^2}{2} + \frac{x}{2} \right]_{0}^{0.5} = \frac{(0.5)^2}{2} + \frac{0.5}{2} = \frac{0.25}{2} + 0.25 = 0.125 + 0.25 = 0.375
$$

$$
\boxed{P(0 \le X \le 0.5) = 0.375 = \frac{3}{8}}
$$

---

### Verification

To confirm the marginal is valid:

$$
\int_{0}^{1} \left( x + \frac{1}{2} \right) dx = \left[ \frac{x^2}{2} + \frac{x}{2} \right]_{0}^{1} = \frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{2} = 1 \quad \checkmark
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 ŌĆö Triangular Support Region (Type-1)

### Problem Statement

The joint PDF of $X$ and $Y$ is:

$$
f_{X,Y}(x,y) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{3}{2}\,(x^2 + y^2), & 0 \le x \le 1,\; 0 \le y \le x \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

**(a)** Verify it is a valid joint PDF.

**(b)** Find $f_X(x)$.

**(c)** Find $f_Y(y)$.

### Part (a): Verification

$$
\int_{0}^{1} \int_{0}^{x} \frac{3}{2}\,(x^2 + y^2)\,dy\,dx = 1 \;\; ?
$$

**Inner integral** (with respect to $y$):

$$
\int_{0}^{x} (x^2 + y^2) \, dy = \left[ x^2 y + \frac{y^3}{3} \right]_{0}^{x} = x^3 + \frac{x^3}{3} = \frac{4x^3}{3}
$$

**Outer integral** (with respect to $x$):

$$
\int_{0}^{1} \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{4x^3}{3} \, dx = \int_{0}^{1} 2x^3 \, dx = 2 \cdot \frac{x^4}{4} \bigg|_{0}^{1} = \frac{1}{2}
$$

**The result is $\dfrac{1}{2} \ne 1$.** Therefore this is **not a valid joint PDF as stated** (the constant should be **3** to make it valid). However, in an exam, the problem may not ask for verification ŌĆö so let's *assume* the constant is $3$ and proceed to illustrate the method. (We will use $c = 3$ instead.)

> [!NOTE]
> **Correction:** The correct constant is $c = 3$, not $\dfrac{3}{2}$. We proceed with:
$$
f_{X,Y}(x,y) = 3\,(x^2 + y^2), \quad 0 \le x \le 1,\; 0 \le y \le x
$$

---

### Part (b): Marginal PDF of $X$

For a fixed $x \in [0, 1]$, the $y$-range is $[0, x]$:

$$
f_X(x) = \int_{0}^{x} 3\,(x^2 + y^2)\,dy
$$

$$
= 3 \left[ x^2 y + \frac{y^3}{3} \right]_{0}^{x} = 3 \left( x^3 + \frac{x^3}{3} \right) = 3 \cdot \frac{4x^3}{3} = 4x^3
$$

$$
\boxed{f_X(x) = \begin{cases} 4x^3, & 0 \le x \le 1 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}}
$$

**Verify:** $\int_0^1 4x^3\,dx = 4 \cdot \frac{1}{4} = 1$ Ō£ō

---

### Part (c): Marginal PDF of $Y$

This is more interesting. The support region is the triangle $\{(x,y) : 0 \le y \le 1,\; y \le x \le 1\}$. So for a fixed $y \in [0, 1]$, $x$ ranges from $y$ to $1$:

$$
f_Y(y) = \int_{y}^{1} 3\,(x^2 + y^2)\,dx
$$

$$
= 3 \left[ \frac{x^3}{3} + xy^2 \right]_{x=y}^{x=1} = 3 \left[ \left( \frac{1}{3} + y^2 \right) - \left( \frac{y^3}{3} + y^3 \right) \right]
$$

Simplify term by term:

$$
= 3 \left( \frac{1}{3} + y^2 - \frac{y^3}{3} - y^3 \right) = 3 \left( \frac{1}{3} + y^2 - \frac{4y^3}{3} \right)
$$

$$
= 1 + 3y^2 - 4y^3
$$

$$
\boxed{f_Y(y) = \begin{cases} 1 + 3y^2 - 4y^3, & 0 \le y \le 1 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}}
$$

**Verify:** $\int_0^1 (1 + 3y^2 - 4y^3)\,dy = 1 + 1 - 1 = 1$ Ō£ō

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Common Support Types and Their Marginal Limits

| Support Shape | Description | $f_X(x)$ limits (over $y$) | $f_Y(y)$ limits (over $x$) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Rectangle** | $a \le x \le b,\; c \le y \le d$ | $y$ from $c$ to $d$ | $x$ from $a$ to $b$ |
| **Triangle (above diag.)** | $0 \le x \le 1,\; 0 \le y \le x$ | $y$ from $0$ to $x$ | $x$ from $y$ to $1$ |
| **Triangle (below diag.)** | $0 \le x \le 1,\; x \le y \le 1$ | $y$ from $x$ to $1$ | $x$ from $0$ to $y$ |
| **Disk** | $x^2 + y^2 \le r^2$ | $y$ from $-\sqrt{r^2 - x^2}$ to $\sqrt{r^2 - x^2}$ | $x$ from $-\sqrt{r^2 - y^2}$ to $\sqrt{r^2 - y^2}$ |
| **Infinite quadrant** | $0 \le x < \infty,\; 0 \le y < \infty$ | $y$ from $0$ to $\infty$ | $x$ from $0$ to $\infty$ |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram ŌĆö Marginalization Workflow

The following Mermaid flowchart depicts the **functional pipeline** for extracting a marginal PDF from a joint PDF in a KTU-style exam setting.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Given Joint PDF f of X and Y"] --> B["Step 1: Identify Support Region D"]
    B --> C{"Step 2: Is support a rectangle?"}
    C -- Yes --> D["Step 3a: Set constant limits - integrate f over y in fixed range"]
    C -- No --> E["Step 3b: Determine y-limits as functions of x (Type-1) or x-limits as functions of y (Type-2)"]
    D --> F["Step 4: Perform Integration over y to obtain f of X"]
    E --> F
    F --> G["Step 5: State f of X with explicit support"]
    G --> H["Step 6: Verify by integration that total area equals 1"]
    H --> I["Step 7: Repeat symmetrically to obtain f of Y"]
    I --> J["End: Marginal PDFs Ready"]
```

---

## 4.2 Schematic ŌĆö Geometric Interpretation of Marginalization

The diagram below shows the **relationship between the joint PDF surface** and its **marginal projections** in a 3-D-to-2-D reduction.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1["3-D Joint PDF Surface"]
        JPDF["f of X, Y - Mountain over xy-plane"]
    end
    subgraph S2["Marginal Projections onto Coordinate Axes"]
        FX["f of X - Silhouette on x-axis"]
        FY["f of Y - Silhouette on y-axis"]
    end
    JPDF -->|"Integrate along y-axis"| FX
    JPDF -->|"Integrate along x-axis"| FY
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The joint PDF lives over the 2-D $xy$-plane. The marginal PDF $f_X$ is the **1-D shadow** cast on the $x$-axis when light shines parallel to the $y$-axis. The integral performs exactly this shadowing operation.

---

## 4.3 Concept Map ŌĆö Marginal PDF in the Hierarchy of Distributions

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Bivariate Random Variable (X, Y)"] --> B["Joint PDF: f of X, Y"]
    B --> C["Marginal PDF of X: integrate over y"]
    B --> D["Marginal PDF of Y: integrate over x"]
    B --> E["Conditional PDF: f of X given Y"]
    C --> F["Cumulative Distribution Function F of X"]
    D --> G["Cumulative Distribution Function F of Y"]
    E --> H["Independence Test: f of X, Y equals f of X times f of Y"]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]`

**Define marginal PDF of a random variable. If the joint PDF of $X$ and $Y$ is $f_{X,Y}(x,y)$, write the formula for the marginal PDF of $X$.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks ŌĆö Allocation: 2 + 1)

The **marginal probability density function (PDF)** of a random variable $X$ is the probability density function of $X$ alone, obtained from the joint PDF $f_{X,Y}(x,y)$ of $X$ and $Y$ by integrating (or summing out) the other variable $Y$ over its entire range. **(2 marks)**

The formula is:

$$
f_X(x) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_{X,Y}(x,y) \, dy
$$

**(1 mark)**

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]`

**State two essential properties that any marginal PDF must satisfy.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks ŌĆö Allocation: 1.5 + 1.5)

**Property 1: Non-negativity.** A marginal PDF must be non-negative for all values in its domain:

$$
f_X(x) \geq 0, \quad \forall x \in \mathbb{R}
$$

**(1.5 marks)**

**Property 2: Unit Total Probability.** The total area under the marginal PDF curve must equal **1**:

$$
\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_X(x) \, dx = 1
$$

**(1.5 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Regulation:** Part B questions carry **14 marks** with **internal choice**. Each sub-part is typically worth **7 marks**. The complete, step-by-step model answer for both choices is provided below.

---

### Question B1 ŌĆö Choice A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2022]`

The joint PDF of random variables $X$ and $Y$ is given by:

$$
f_{X,Y}(x,y) = \begin{cases} 2, & 0 \le x \le 1,\; 0 \le y \le 1,\; x + y \le 1 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

**(a)** Find the marginal PDF of $X$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Find the marginal PDF of $Y$ and compute $P(Y \le 0.25)$. **(7 marks)**

#### Solution to Part (a) ŌĆö Marginal of $X$

**Step 1: Identify the support region.**
The support is the triangle with vertices $(0,0)$, $(1,0)$, $(0,1)$, defined by:
- $0 \le x \le 1$
- $0 \le y \le 1 - x$

This is a **Type-1 region** (for fixed $x$, $y$ has a definite range from $0$ to $1-x$).

**Step 2: Integrate the joint PDF over $y$.**

$$
f_X(x) = \int_{0}^{1-x} 2 \, dy = 2 \cdot \bigl[ y \bigr]_{0}^{1-x} = 2(1-x)
$$

**Step 3: State the support.**

$$
\boxed{f_X(x) = \begin{cases} 2(1-x), & 0 \le x \le 1 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}}
$$

**Step 4: Verification.**

$$
\int_{0}^{1} 2(1-x) \, dx = 2 \left[ x - \frac{x^2}{2} \right]_0^1 = 2 \left( 1 - \frac{1}{2} \right) = 1 \quad \checkmark
$$

**(7 marks ŌĆö 2 for setup, 3 for integration, 2 for verification and statement of support)**

---

#### Solution to Part (b) ŌĆö Marginal of $Y$ and Probability

**Step 1: For a fixed $y \in [0, 1]$, identify the $x$-range.**
The triangle constraint $x + y \le 1$ gives $x \le 1 - y$. Combined with $x \ge 0$:

$$
0 \le x \le 1 - y
$$

**Step 2: Integrate.**

$$
f_Y(y) = \int_{0}^{1-y} 2 \, dx = 2(1-y)
$$

**Step 3: State the support.**

$$
\boxed{f_Y(y) = \begin{cases} 2(1-y), & 0 \le y \le 1 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}}
$$

**Step 4: Compute $P(Y \le 0.25)$.**

$$
P(Y \le 0.25) = \int_{0}^{0.25} 2(1-y) \, dy = 2 \left[ y - \frac{y^2}{2} \right]_0^{0.25}
$$

$$
= 2 \left( 0.25 - \frac{0.0625}{2} \right) = 2 \left( 0.25 - 0.03125 \right) = 2 \cdot 0.21875 = 0.4375
$$

$$
\boxed{P(Y \le 0.25) = 0.4375 = \frac{7}{16}}
$$

**(7 marks ŌĆö 2 for setup, 2 for integration, 2 for probability computation, 1 for final answer)**

---

### Question B2 ŌĆö Choice B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2023]`

The joint PDF of $X$ and $Y$ is:

$$
f_{X,Y}(x,y) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{1}{8}\,(x + y), & 0 \le x \le 2,\; 0 \le y \le 2 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

**(a)** Verify that this is a valid joint PDF. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Find the marginal PDFs $f_X(x)$ and $f_Y(y)$, and compute $P(X + Y \le 1)$. **(7 marks)**

#### Solution to Part (a) ŌĆö Verification

**Step 1:** Integrate over the full support $[0,2] \times [0,2]$:

$$
I = \int_{0}^{2} \int_{0}^{2} \frac{1}{8}\,(x + y) \, dx\,dy
$$

**Step 2: Factor out the constant.**

$$
I = \frac{1}{8} \int_{0}^{2} \int_{0}^{2} (x + y) \, dx\,dy
$$

**Step 3: Inner integral with respect to $x$.**

$$
\int_{0}^{2} (x + y) \, dx = \left[ \frac{x^2}{2} + xy \right]_0^2 = \frac{4}{2} + 2y = 2 + 2y
$$

**Step 4: Outer integral with respect to $y$.**

$$
\int_{0}^{2} (2 + 2y) \, dy = \left[ 2y + y^2 \right]_0^2 = 4 + 4 = 8
$$

**Step 5: Final result.**

$$
I = \frac{1}{8} \cdot 8 = 1 \quad \checkmark
$$

The function is also non-negative on the support, so it is a **valid joint PDF**.

**(7 marks ŌĆö 3 for setting up, 3 for evaluating, 1 for conclusion)**

---

#### Solution to Part (b) ŌĆö Marginals and Probability

**Step 1: Find $f_X(x)$.** For fixed $x \in [0, 2]$:

$$
f_X(x) = \int_{0}^{2} \frac{1}{8}\,(x + y) \, dy = \frac{1}{8} \left[ xy + \frac{y^2}{2} \right]_0^2 = \frac{1}{8}\bigl(2x + 2\bigr) = \frac{x+1}{4}
$$

$$
\boxed{f_X(x) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{x+1}{4}, & 0 \le x \le 2 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}}
$$

**Step 2: Find $f_Y(y)$.** By symmetry:

$$
\boxed{f_Y(y) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{y+1}{4}, & 0 \le y \le 2 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}}
$$

**Step 3: Compute $P(X + Y \le 1)$.** The region is $\{(x,y) : 0 \le x \le 1,\; 0 \le y \le 1 - x\}$.

$$
P(X + Y \le 1) = \int_{0}^{1} \int_{0}^{1-x} \frac{1}{8}\,(x + y) \, dy\,dx
$$

**Inner integral:**

$$
\int_{0}^{1-x} (x + y) \, dy = \left[ xy + \frac{y^2}{2} \right]_0^{1-x} = x(1-x) + \frac{(1-x)^2}{2}
$$

Simplify:

$$
= x - x^2 + \frac{1 - 2x + x^2}{2} = x - x^2 + \frac{1}{2} - x + \frac{x^2}{2} = \frac{1}{2} - \frac{x^2}{2}
$$

**Outer integral:**

$$
P(X + Y \le 1) = \frac{1}{8} \int_{0}^{1} \left( \frac{1}{2} - \frac{x^2}{2} \right) dx = \frac{1}{16} \int_{0}^{1} (1 - x^2) \, dx
$$

$$
= \frac{1}{16} \left[ x - \frac{x^3}{3} \right]_0^1 = \frac{1}{16} \left( 1 - \frac{1}{3} \right) = \frac{1}{16} \cdot \frac{2}{3} = \frac{2}{48} = \frac{1}{24}
$$

$$
\boxed{P(X + Y \le 1) = \frac{1}{24} \approx 0.0417}
$$

**(7 marks ŌĆö 2 for $f_X$, 2 for $f_Y$, 3 for the probability calculation)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU 2024 Scheme Valuation:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to state the support explicitly** ŌĆö A marginal PDF without its range is incomplete. *Penalty: Up to 1ŌĆō2 marks.*
> 2. **Confusing Type-1 and Type-2 limits** ŌĆö When the support is non-rectangular, students often use rectangular limits. *Penalty: 3ŌĆō4 marks.*
> 3. **Missing the unit-area verification** ŌĆö KTU examiners value the "sanity check" step $\int f_X(x)\,dx = 1$. *Penalty: 1 mark if omitted.*
> 4. **Sign errors in integration of piecewise functions** ŌĆö When the joint PDF changes form over different sub-regions, students forget to split the integral. *Penalty: Variable, often 3+ marks.*
> 5. **Writing $f_X(x) = f_{X,Y}(x, y)$** ŌĆö Confusing the joint PDF with the marginal. *Penalty: 2 marks for conceptual error.*
> 6. **Forgetting to substitute the correct limits** ŌĆö Especially when transitioning from joint to marginal integration in a triangular/disk support. *Penalty: 4+ marks.*

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Marginal PDF Module:**

- **Definition:** A marginal PDF is the PDF of a *single* variable obtained by integrating the joint PDF over all values of the *other* variable(s).
- **Core Formula:** $f_X(x) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_{X,Y}(x,y)\,dy$ and $f_Y(y) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_{X,Y}(x,y)\,dx$.
- **Two Mandatory Properties:** (i) $f_X(x) \geq 0$ everywhere; (ii) $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_X(x)\,dx = 1$.
- **Always State Support:** Without the support, the marginal PDF is incomplete. Always write the piecewise definition.
- **Type-1 vs. Type-2 Regions:** Identify whether the region is naturally expressed with $x$ as the outer variable (Type-1) or $y$ as the outer variable (Type-2). Draw a quick sketch.
- **Sanity Check:** Verify your answer by integrating ŌĆö it should give 1.
- **Symmetry Exploitation:** If $f_{X,Y}(x,y) = f_{X,Y}(y,x)$, then $f_X(x)$ and $f_Y(y)$ have the *same functional form* ŌĆö compute one and write the other by symmetry.
- **Common Support Shapes:** Rectangle (constant limits), Triangle (variable limits), Disk ($\pm\sqrt{r^2 - x^2}$).
- **Discrete Analog:** Marginal PMF is obtained by *summing* over the other variable: $P(X = x_i) = \sum_j P(X = x_i, Y = y_j)$.
- **Independence Test:** $X$ and $Y$ are independent if and only if $f_{X,Y}(x,y) = f_X(x) \cdot f_Y(y)$.
- **Always verify the joint PDF integrates to 1** before finding marginals ŌĆö if it doesn't, the problem has a typo (as in Example 2 above).
- **KTU 2024 Module 2 weightage** typically places marginals as a 7ŌĆō14 mark question within Bivariate/Multivariate Random Variables.

---

> **End of Module 2 Topic: Marginal PDF**
> *Prepared under KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 | NEP 2020 / 2024 Scheme Aligned*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Independent Random Variables
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GCMAT401 - MATHEMATICS FOR PHYSICAL SCIENCE AND LIFE SCIENCE ŌĆō 4
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Independent Random Variables

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

Let $X$ and $Y$ be two random variables defined on the same probability space $(\Omega, \mathcal{F}, P)$ with joint cumulative distribution function (CDF) $F_{X,Y}(x, y)$, marginal CDFs $F_X(x)$ and $F_Y(y)$. The random variables $X$ and $Y$ are said to be **statistically independent** if, and only if, for every pair of real numbers $x, y \in \mathbb{R}$, the joint CDF factorises into the product of the marginal CDFs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition ŌĆö Two Random Variables**
> Two random variables $X$ and $Y$ are said to be *independent* if, for all $x, y \in \mathbb{R}$,
> $$F_{X,Y}(x, y) = F_X(x) \cdot F_Y(y)$$
> Equivalently, in terms of the joint probability mass function (PMF) for discrete variables, or joint probability density function (PDF) for continuous variables:
> $$p_{X,Y}(x, y) = p_X(x) \cdot p_Y(y) \quad \text{(Discrete case)}$$
> $$f_{X,Y}(x, y) = f_X(x) \cdot f_Y(y) \quad \text{(Continuous case)}$$

## 1.2 Generalisation to $n$ Random Variables

A family $\{X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n\}$ of random variables is **mutually independent** if, for every choice of $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n \in \mathbb{R}$, the joint CDF factorises completely.

$$F_{X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n}(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} F_{X_i}(x_i)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Pairwise vs. Mutual Independence ŌĆö Examiner's Hot Topic**
> - **Pairwise Independence**: Every *subset of size 2* behaves independently: $F_{X_i, X_j}(x_i, x_j) = F_{X_i}(x_i) F_{X_j}(x_j)$ for all $i \ne j$.
> - **Mutual Independence**: The factorisation holds for *every finite sub-collection*, not just pairs.
> - Mutual independence ŌćÆ Pairwise independence, but the converse is **not** always true.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy: Rolling Two Dice Together**
> Imagine rolling a fair red die and a fair blue die simultaneously on a table. The number that appears on the red die **has absolutely no influence** on the number that appears on the blue die. Knowing the red die shows a 4 does not change your belief about the blue die ŌĆö it is still uniformly distributed over $\{1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6\}$. This "no influence" property is precisely what independence captures mathematically. The probability of getting $(X = x)$ **AND** $(Y = y)$ is simply the product of individual probabilities, just as the chance of "red = 4 AND blue = 3" is $\frac{1}{6} \times \frac{1}{6} = \frac{1}{36}$.

In engineering terms: if the output of one sensor does not affect the output of another sensor, we can safely model them as independent random variables. This simplifies joint distributions, expectations, variances, and signal-processing chains enormously.

## 1.4 Engineering Significance

| Domain | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|
| **Signal Processing** | Noise samples across different time instants are modelled as independent identically distributed (i.i.d.) random variables. |
| **Communication Systems** | Bit errors in different packet transmissions are independent Bernoulli trials. |
| **Reliability Engineering** | Failure of one component (e.g., a fuse) does not cause failure of another ŌĆö independence assumption. |
| **Machine Learning** | Naive Bayes classifiers assume feature independence for tractable inference. |
| **Physics & Life Sciences** | Decay events of radioactive nuclei follow an independent Poisson process. |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" and "How" of Independence

The independence assumption is one of the most powerful simplifying tools in probability theory. Here is the underlying logical flow:

**Step 1 ŌĆö The Joint Distribution Problem**
To fully describe two random variables, one needs the **joint** distribution $F_{X,Y}(x, y)$ or $f_{X,Y}(x, y)$. This is a 2D function, which is mathematically cumbersome.

**Step 2 ŌĆö The Marginals**
Each random variable has its own **marginal** distribution $F_X(x)$ and $F_Y(y)$ ŌĆö these are 1D functions and easier to work with.

**Step 3 ŌĆö The Independence Factorisation**
When $X$ and $Y$ are independent, the entire 2D joint information collapses into a product of 1D marginals. We "lose no information" because, under independence, knowledge of $X$ tells us nothing new about $Y$.

**Step 4 ŌĆö The Operational Consequence**
We can compute expectations, variances, and distributions of derived quantities (e.g., $X + Y$, $X - Y$, $XY$) using **convolutions** or **moment generating functions** rather than solving 2D integrals.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master the following table ŌĆö these are the formulas KTU examiners test most frequently.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Independence of 2 r.v.s (CDF) | $F_{X,Y}(x, y) = F_X(x) F_Y(y)$ | All $x, y \in \mathbb{R}$ |
| 2 | Independence (Discrete PMF) | $P(X = x_i, Y = y_j) = P(X = x_i) P(Y = y_j)$ | All $i, j$ |
| 3 | Independence (Continuous PDF) | $f_{X,Y}(x, y) = f_X(x) f_Y(y)$ | All $x, y \in \mathbb{R}$ |
| 4 | Conditional expectation (independent) | $E[X \mid Y = y] = E[X]$ | $X \perp Y$ |
| 5 | Expected product | $E[XY] = E[X] E[Y]$ | $X, Y$ independent |
| 6 | Variance of sum | $\text{Var}(X + Y) = \text{Var}(X) + \text{Var}(Y)$ | $X, Y$ independent |
| 7 | Variance of linear combo | $\text{Var}(aX + bY) = a^2 \text{Var}(X) + b^2 \text{Var}(Y)$ | $X, Y$ independent |
| 8 | MGF of sum | $M_{X+Y}(t) = M_X(t) M_Y(t)$ | $X, Y$ independent |
| 9 | PDF of sum (continuous) | $f_{X+Y}(z) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_X(x) f_Y(z - x) \, dx$ | Convolution |
| 10 | PDF of product | $f_{XY}(z) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{1}{\vert x \vert} f_X(x) f_Y(z/x) \, dx$ | $x \ne 0$ |
| 11 | Independence of functions | $g(X) \perp h(Y)$ | If $X \perp Y$ |
| 12 | Mutual independence ($n$ r.v.s) | $F_{X_1, \ldots, X_n} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} F_{X_i}$ | Factorisation for all tuples |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Pitfall: Covariance vs. Independence**
> $X$ and $Y$ are independent $\Rightarrow \text{Cov}(X, Y) = 0$.
> But $\text{Cov}(X, Y) = 0 \not\Rightarrow X$ and $Y$ are independent.
> The converse fails for **non-linear dependence**. KTU examiners love to test this!

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility of These Formulas

- **Formula 6 & 7 (Variance of Sum)**: Used in quality control to compute the total variance of a process that is the sum of independent stages. If a manufacturing line has 3 independent stations each with variance $\sigma^2$, the total variance of the output dimension is $3\sigma^2$.
- **Formula 8 (MGF of Sum)**: In communication theory, the MGF of the sum of independent noise sources is the product of individual MGFs. This is the cornerstone of the **Central Limit Theorem** proof.
- **Formula 9 (Convolution)**: In digital signal processing, the output of a Linear Time-Invariant (LTI) system is the convolution of the input signal with the impulse response ŌĆö when both are treated as random, the PDF of the output is the convolution of their PDFs.

## 2.4 The Sum of Independent Random Variables ŌĆö Detailed Setup

Let $X$ and $Y$ be two **independent** continuous random variables with PDFs $f_X(x)$ and $f_Y(y)$. Define the new random variable $Z = X + Y$. We seek $f_Z(z)$.

**Derivation of the Convolution Formula (Setup):**

We need to compute $F_Z(z) = P(X + Y \le z)$.

$$F_Z(z) = P(X + Y \le z) = \iint\limits_{x+y \le z} f_{X,Y}(x, y) \, dx \, dy$$

By independence, $f_{X,Y}(x, y) = f_X(x) f_Y(y)$, so:

$$F_Z(z) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{z - x} f_X(x) f_Y(y) \, dy \, dx$$

Differentiating w.r.t. $z$ using Leibniz's rule (since the integrand is continuous and the integration limit involves $z$):

$$f_Z(z) = \frac{d}{dz} F_Z(z) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_X(x) f_Y(z - x) \, dx$$

This is the **convolution**: $f_Z = f_X * f_Y$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Mean and Variance of the Sum of Two Independent Random Variables

### Theorem
If $X$ and $Y$ are independent random variables with finite second moments, then:

$$E[X + Y] = E[X] + E[Y]$$
$$\text{Var}(X + Y) = \text{Var}(X) + \text{Var}(Y)$$

### Proof ŌĆö Expected Value

We start from the definition of expected value for a continuous joint distribution. (The discrete case is analogous.)

$$E[X + Y] = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} (x + y) f_{X,Y}(x, y) \, dx \, dy$$

Split the integrand into two separate integrals:

$$E[X + Y] = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x \, f_{X,Y}(x, y) \, dx \, dy + \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} y \, f_{X,Y}(x, y) \, dx \, dy$$

Apply the **independence** factorisation $f_{X,Y}(x, y) = f_X(x) f_Y(y)$:

$$E[X + Y] = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x f_X(x) \left[ \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_Y(y) \, dy \right] dx + \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} y f_Y(y) \left[ \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_X(x) \, dx \right] dy$$
