[KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2023, Model]** **Define a confidence interval. State any two situations where one-sided confidence intervals are preferred over two-sided ones.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **confidence interval** is an interval estimate for an unknown population parameter, constructed from sample data using a procedure such that the interval contains the true parameter with a preassigned probability called the **confidence level** $1 - \alpha$. Mathematically, for a parameter $\theta$ and interval $[L, U]$: $$P(L \le \theta \le U) = 1 - \alpha$$ **Two situations favouring one-sided confidence intervals:** 1. **Lower confidence bound ($\mu \ge L$):** When the parameter must satisfy a *minimum* specification. Example: Estimating the minimum yield strength of steel; manufacturers need to know the lowest plausible value. 2. **Upper confidence bound ($\mu \le U$):** When the parameter must not exceed a *maximum* tolerance. Example: Estimating the upper limit of pollutant concentration in a river, to ensure it is below legal thresholds. *(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark each for the two situations ŌĆö total 3 marks.)* --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2022, Model]** **When is the t-distribution used in place of the normal distribution for constructing a confidence interval for the mean? Mention the degrees of freedom used.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Student's t-distribution** is used in place of the standard normal distribution for constructing a confidence interval for the population mean $\mu$ when **all** the following conditions hold: 1. The sample size is **small** (conventionally $n < 30$). 2. The **population standard deviation $\sigma$ is unknown** (and is estimated by the sample standard deviation $s$). 3. The underlying population is **approximately normal**. **Degrees of Freedom:** $\nu = n - 1$, where $n$ is the sample size. The t-distribution is symmetric and bell-shaped like the normal distribution but has **heavier tails**. As the sample size increases, the t-distribution approaches the standard normal distribution. *(Award 1 mark for stating small sample size, 1 mark for unknown sigma with normal population assumption, 1 mark for degrees of freedom.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö 14-Mark Questions (Module 3 Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > Following the KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Evaluation) pattern, Part B questions of Module 3 carry **14 marks** with an **internal choice**. Below, both options are provided with sub-parts and complete model solutions. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023, Model]** **(a)** The compressive strength of concrete cubes (in $\text{N/mm}^2$) for a sample of $n = 25$ batches is found to have mean $\bar{x} = 32.5$ and standard deviation $s = 4.2$. Assuming the strength is normally distributed, construct a **$95\%$ two-sided confidence interval** for the true mean strength. **(7 marks)** **(b)** The mean lifetime of $n = 16$ CFL bulbs is $\bar{x} = 1200$ hours with $s = 80$ hours. Construct a **$99\%$ one-sided lower confidence interval** for the true mean lifetime. State the engineering interpretation. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Solution to Q.A(a):** **Given:** $n = 25$, $\bar{x} = 32.5$, $s = 4.2$, $1 - \alpha = 0.95$, population is normal. Since $n = 25 < 30$ and $\sigma$ is unknown, we use the **t-distribution** with $\nu = n - 1 = 24$ degrees of freedom. **Critical value:** $t_{\alpha/2, n-1} = t_{0.025, 24} = 2.064$ **Step 1 ŌĆö Standard Error:** $$SE = \frac{s}{\sqrt{n}} = \frac{4.2}{\sqrt{25}} = \frac{4.2}{5} = 0.84$$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Margin of Error:** $$E = t_{0.025, 24} \cdot SE = 2.064 \times 0.84 = 1.73376 \approx 1.734$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Confidence Interval:** $$\bar{x} \pm E = 32.5 \pm 1.734$$ $$\boxed{[\,30.77,\ 34.23\,]\,\text{N/mm}^2}$$ **Interpretation:** We are $95\%$ confident that the true mean compressive strength of the concrete lies between $30.77$ and $34.23\,\text{N/mm}^2$. *(Award 2 marks for identifying t-distribution, 1 mark for correct critical value, 2 marks for SE and E calculation, 2 marks for final interval and interpretation.)* --- #### **Solution to Q.A(b):** **Given:** $n = 16$, $\bar{x} = 1200$, $s = 80$, $1 - \alpha = 0.99$ (one-sided lower). **Identification:** $n = 16 < 30$, $\sigma$ unknown, normal population ŌĆö use **t-distribution** with $\nu = 15$. **Critical value (one-sided):** $t_{\alpha, n-1} = t_{0.01, 15} = 2.602$ **Step 1 ŌĆö Standard Error:** $$SE = \frac{s}{\sqrt{n}} = \frac{80}{\sqrt{16}} = \frac{80}{4} = 20$$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Lower Confidence Bound:** $$L = \bar{x} - t_{\alpha, n-1} \cdot SE = 1200 - 2.602 \times 20 = 1200 - 52.04 = 1147.96$$ $$\boxed{\,\mu \ge 1147.96\,\text{hours}\,}$$ **Engineering Interpretation:** The manufacturer can claim with $99\%$ confidence that the true mean lifetime of the CFL bulbs is at least $1147.96$ hours. This is the *minimum* guaranteed lifetime under the worst-case statistical tolerance ŌĆö important for warranty declarations to customers. *(Award 2 marks for correct t-distribution and critical value, 2 marks for SE, 2 marks for the lower bound, 1 mark for engineering interpretation.)* --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024, Model]** **(a)** A random sample of $n = 64$ measurements of the diameter (in mm) of machine-produced ball bearings gives $\bar{x} = 25.02$ mm and $\sigma = 0.16$ mm. Construct a **$99\%$ two-sided confidence interval** for the true mean diameter. Also find the **sample size** required to halve the margin of error. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A water quality lab measures the lead concentration (in ppb) in $n = 9$ water samples. The data are: $12.1, 11.8, 12.5, 12.0, 11.9, 12.3, 12.2, 12.4, 12.0$. Assuming normality, construct a **$90\%$ two-sided confidence interval** for the mean lead concentration. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Solution to Q.B(a):** **Part 1: Two-Sided CI** **Given:** $n = 64$ (large), $\bar{x} = 25.02$, $\sigma = 0.16$, $1 - \alpha = 0.99$ so $z_{\alpha/2} = z_{0.005} = 2.576$. **Step 1 ŌĆö Standard Error:** $$SE = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} = \frac{0.16}{\sqrt{64}} = \frac{0.16}{8} = 0.02\,\text{mm}$$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Margin of Error:** $$E = z_{\alpha/2} \cdot SE = 2.576 \times 0.02 = 0.05152 \approx 0.0515\,\text{mm}$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Confidence Interval:** $$\bar{x} \pm E = 25.02 \pm 0.0515$$ $$\boxed{[\,24.9685,\ 25.0715\,]\,\text{mm}\,}$$ **Part 2: New Sample Size to Halve $E$** If $E_{\text{new}} = E/2 = 0.02576$, then: $$n_{\text{new}} = \left(\frac{z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma}{E_{\text{new}}}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{2.576 \times 0.16}{0.02576}\right)^2$$ $$n_{\text{new}} = \left(\frac{0.41216}{0.02576}\right)^2 = (16)^2 = 256$$ $$\boxed{\,n_{\text{new}} = 256\,}$$ **Verification by formula:** Since $n \propto 1/E^2$, halving $E$ requires $4$ times the sample: $4 \times 64 = 256$. Ō£ō *(Award 2 marks for correct use of Z with $n=64$, 2 marks for SE/E, 1 mark for interval, 1 mark for sample size formula setup, 1 mark for final answer.)* --- #### **Solution to Q.B(b):** **Given:** $n = 9$, data: $12.1, 11.8, 12.5, 12.0, 11.9, 12.3, 12.2, 12.4, 12.0$, normal population. **Step 1 ŌĆö Sample Mean:** $$\bar{x} = \frac{12.1+11.8+12.5+12.0+11.9+12.3+12.2+12.4+12.0}{9} = \frac{109.2}{9} = 12.1333\,\text{ppb}$$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Sample Standard Deviation:** Compute deviations $(x_i - \bar{x})$: $(12.1 - 12.1333)^2 = 0.001111$ $(11.8 - 12.1333)^2 = 0.111111$ $(12.5 - 12.1333)^2 = 0.134444$ $(12.0 - 12.1333)^2 = 0.017778$ $(11.9 - 12.1333)^2 = 0.054444$ $(12.3 - 12.1333)^2 = 0.027778$ $(12.2 - 12.1333)^2 = 0.004444$ $(12.4 - 12.1333)^2 = 0.071111$ $(12.0 - 12.1333)^2 = 0.017778$ Sum of squared deviations $= 0.500$ $$s^2 = \frac{0.500}{n-1} = \frac{0.500}{8} = 0.0625 \quad\Rightarrow\quad s = 0.25\,\text{ppb}$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Standard Error:** $$SE = \frac{s}{\sqrt{n}} = \frac{0.25}{\sqrt{9}} = \frac{0.25}{3} = 0.08333\,\text{ppb}$$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Critical t-value:** For $1 - \alpha = 0.90$, $\alpha/2 = 0.05$, $\nu = 8$. From t-tables: $t_{0.05, 8} = 1.860$. **Step 5 ŌĆö Margin of Error:** $$E = t_{0.05, 8} \cdot SE = 1.860 \times 0.08333 = 0.15500\,\text{ppb}$$ **Step 6 ŌĆö Confidence Interval:** $$\bar{x} \pm E = 12.1333 \pm 0.1550$$ $$\boxed{[\,11.978,\ 12.288\,]\,\text{ppb}\,}$$ **Interpretation:** With $90\%$ confidence, the true mean lead concentration in the water supply lies between $11.98$ and $12.29$ ppb. *(Award 1 mark for $\bar{x}$, 2 marks for $s$ with correct $n-1$, 1 mark for SE, 1 mark for correct t-critical value, 1 mark for E, 1 mark for final interval.)* --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Student Mistakes That Cost Marks:** > > 1. **Using Z for small samples with unknown $\sigma$:** This is the #1 error. When $n < 30$ AND $\sigma$ is unknown, you **MUST** use the t-distribution, not Z. Marks lost: 2-3 per question. > > 2. **Wrong critical value direction:** For a $95\%$ two-sided CI, students often use $z_{0.05} = 1.645$ instead of $z_{0.025} = 1.96$. Always split $\alpha$ into two tails for a *two-sided* CI. > > 3. **Forgetting $\alpha$ vs $\alpha/2$ in one-sided CIs:** A $95\%$ one-sided CI uses $z_{0.05} = 1.645$, NOT $z_{0.025} = 1.96$. The half-angle is for two-sided only. > > 4. **Using $n$ instead of $n-1$ in sample variance:** Always use $n-1$ in the denominator. This is the **Bessel correction** and is mandatory. > > 5. **Not stating the assumption of normality** for small samples: KTU evaluators expect you to explicitly write "assuming the population is approximately normal" before applying the t-distribution. > > 6. **Wrong rounding of sample size:** Always **round up** ($n = 165.9 \to n = 166$). Rounding down violates the margin of error requirement. > > 7. **Confusing one-sided CI with one-tailed hypothesis test:** They are related but distinct. A one-sided CI of level $1-\alpha$ corresponds to a one-tailed test at level $\alpha$. Don't interchange the critical values. > > 8. **No units in the final answer:** Always state units (e.g., $\text{mm}$, $\text{hours}$, $\text{N/mm}^2$). Missing units can cost 0.5ŌĆō1 mark. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Summary for Last-Minute Revision:** ### Core Definitions - **Point Estimate:** A single number estimating a parameter (e.g., $\bar{x}$ for $\mu$). - **Interval Estimate (CI):** A range $[L, U]$ likely to contain the parameter. - **Confidence Level $1-\alpha$:** The long-run proportion of CIs that contain the true parameter. - **Significance Level $\alpha$:** The probability of *missing* the true parameter. - **Standard Error:** $SE = \sigma/\sqrt{n}$ (or $s/\sqrt{n}$). - **Margin of Error $E$:** Critical value $\times$ Standard Error. - **Degrees of Freedom:** $\nu = n - 1$ for t-distribution. ### Four Master Formulas 1. **Large sample, $\sigma$ known:** $\bar{x} \pm z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$ 2. **Large sample, $\sigma$ unknown:** $\bar{x} \pm z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \dfrac{s}{\sqrt{n}}$ 3. **Small sample, $\sigma$ unknown (t):** $\bar{x} \pm t_{\alpha/2,\,n-1} \cdot \dfrac{s}{\sqrt{n}}$ 4. **Small sample, $\sigma$ known:** $\bar{x} \pm z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$ (Z still valid) ### One-Sided Formulas - **Lower bound:** $L = \bar{x} - z_\alpha \cdot \sigma/\sqrt{n}$ (or t version) - **Upper bound:** $U = \bar{x} + z_\alpha \cdot \sigma/\sqrt{n}$ (or t version) - **Critical value uses $\alpha$, NOT $\alpha/2$** ### Critical Values to Memorize | Confidence | $z_{\alpha/2}$ (two-sided) | $z_\alpha$ (one-sided) | |------------|---------------------------|----------------------| | $90\%$ | $1.645$ | $1.28$ | | $95\%$ | $1.96$ | $1.645$ | | $99\%$ | $2.576$ | $2.33$ | ### Sample Size Formula $$n = \left(\frac{z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma}{E}\right)^2$$ Always **round up** to the next integer. ### When to Use Z vs t (Decision Rules) - **Use Z** when: $\sigma$ is known, OR $n \ge 30$ (regardless of $\sigma$). - **Use t** when: $n < 30$ AND $\sigma$ is unknown AND population is normal. - **Degrees of freedom for t:** Always $n - 1$. ### Properties of the t-Distribution - Symmetric, bell-shaped, mean $= 0$. - **Heavier tails** than normal (more area in the extremes). - As $\nu = n - 1 \to \infty$, t $\to$ Z. - For $n \ge 30$, t-values are essentially equal to Z-values. ### Interpretation Format (Always Use This Wording) > *"We are $(1-\alpha) \times 100\%$ confident that the true population mean lies between $L$ and $U$."* ### Common Engineering Applications - Quality control of manufactured parts (tensile strength, diameter) - Estimating battery/LED lifetimes - Concrete strength analysis - Pharmaceutical efficacy (drug relief time) - Environmental pollutant limits ### Key Equations Summary Card $$\text{CI} = \bar{x} \pm (\text{critical value}) \times \frac{(\sigma \text{ or } s)}{\sqrt{n}}$$ $$\text{Margin of Error } E = z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \quad \text{or} \quad t_{\alpha/2, n-1} \cdot \frac{s}{\sqrt{n}}$$ $$n = \left(\frac{z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma}{E}\right)^2 \quad \text{(sample size for desired margin of error)}$$ > **END OF MODULE 3 NOTES ŌĆö CONFIDENCE INTERVALS**

topics.title: Hypotheses and Test Procedures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GCMAT401 - MATHEMATICS FOR PHYSICAL SCIENCE AND LIFE SCIENCE ŌĆō 4
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Hypotheses and Test Procedures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Statistical Hypothesis ŌĆö Formal Definition

A **statistical hypothesis** is a definite statement (or assertion) about the unknown population parameter(s) that can be tested using sample data drawn from the population. The parameter of interest may be the population mean $\mu$, the population proportion $p$, the population variance $\sigma^2$, or any other unknown constant governing the distribution.

The process of deciding whether to **accept or reject** this statement, based on sample evidence, is known as **Hypothesis Testing** or **Test of Hypothesis**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Exact Terminology):**
> "A *hypothesis* is a statement about the nature of a population. It is often a statement about the value of an unknown population parameter. A *test of hypothesis* (or *test procedure*) is a rule, based on sample data, for deciding whether to reject or fail to reject a given hypothesis."

### 1.2 The Two Competing Hypotheses

Every test of hypothesis involves a **pair of complementary statements** about the population:

| Symbol | Name | Role in Test | Intuitive Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $H_0$ | **Null Hypothesis** | The "status quo" ŌĆö assumed true unless evidence strongly contradicts it. Always contains equality ($=$, $\leq$, $\geq$). | The "innocent until proven guilty" assumption. |
| $H_1$ (or $H_a$) | **Alternative Hypothesis** | The "research claim" or "innovation" ŌĆö the statement we wish to establish. Contains strict inequality ($<$, $>$, $\neq$). | The "claim of guilt" that must be backed by strong evidence. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Golden Rule for KTU Exams:** The null hypothesis $H_0$ ALWAYS contains the equality sign. The alternative hypothesis $H_1$ is the negation (or research hypothesis) we are trying to prove.

### 1.3 Real-World Analogy: The Courtroom Trial

Imagine a criminal trial. The judge does not know whether the accused is guilty or innocent.

- **$H_0$ (Null Hypothesis):** "The accused is **innocent**" (the default assumption of the law).
- **$H_1$ (Alternative Hypothesis):** "The accused is **guilty**" (the prosecution's claim).
- **Evidence:** Sample data collected by the investigator.
- **Burden of Proof:** The prosecution must provide **overwhelming evidence** to reject $H_0$.

Just as a court errs when it convicts an innocent person (**Type I error**) or acquits a guilty person (**Type II error**), statistical tests err in two corresponding ways ŌĆö we will quantify these later.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick:** $H_0$ is the "**H**o-hum" ŌĆö the boring, no-change, nothing-special hypothesis. $H_1$ is the "**H**ot" ŌĆö the exciting, different, research-claim hypothesis.

### 1.4 Types of Hypotheses

> [!NOTE]
> **Classification by Structure:**

**1. Simple Hypothesis:** A hypothesis that completely specifies the value(s) of the population parameter(s).
- *Example:* $H_0: \mu = 50$ (specifies the mean exactly).
- *Example:* $H_0: \mu = 50$ AND $\sigma^2 = 100$ (specifies every parameter).

**2. Composite Hypothesis:** A hypothesis that does NOT completely specify the parameter; it specifies a range or a set of values.
- *Example:* $H_0: \mu \geq 50$ (a range of values).
- *Example:* $H_1: \mu \neq 50$ (values on both sides of 50).

**3. One-Sided (One-Tailed) Test:** $H_1$ contains a single inequality sign ($<$ or $>$).
- *Example:* $H_0: \mu = 100$  vs.  $H_1: \mu > 100$ (Right-tailed).
- *Example:* $H_0: \mu = 100$  vs.  $H_1: \mu < 100$ (Left-tailed).

**4. Two-Sided (Two-Tailed) Test:** $H_1$ contains the "not equal to" sign ($\neq$).
- *Example:* $H_0: \mu = 100$  vs.  $H_1: \mu \neq 100$ (Both tails).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Anatomy of a Test Procedure

A **test procedure** (or **test rule**) is a complete decision-making recipe. It consists of the following five essential components:

**Step 1 ŌĆö State the Hypotheses:**
Clearly write $H_0$ (with equality) and $H_1$ (the research claim).

**Step 2 ŌĆö Choose the Test Statistic:**
A test statistic $T = T(X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n)$ is a function of the sample whose distribution is known (or asymptotically known) when $H_0$ is true.
- *Examples:* $Z = \frac{\bar{X} - \mu_0}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}}$, $t = \frac{\bar{X} - \mu_0}{S/\sqrt{n}}$, $\chi^2$, $F$.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Determine the Critical Region (Rejection Region) $W$:**
This is the set of values of the test statistic for which $H_0$ is **rejected**. The complement of $W$ is the **Acceptance Region** $A$, where we **fail to reject** $H_0$.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Specify the Level of Significance $\alpha$:**
The probability of rejecting $H_0$ when it is actually true.
$$\alpha = P(\text{Type I Error}) = P(T \in W \mid H_0 \text{ is true})$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Compute the Test Statistic and Decide:**
Compute $T_{\text{obs}}$ from the sample. If $T_{\text{obs}} \in W$, **Reject $H_0$**. Otherwise, **Fail to reject $H_0$**.

### 2.2 The Two Types of Errors

Since we are making a decision based on incomplete sample data, errors are inevitable. There are exactly two possible errors:

| | **$H_0$ is TRUE (Reality)** | **$H_0$ is FALSE (Reality)** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Fail to Reject $H_0$** | Correct Decision (Probability = $1 - \alpha$) | **Type II Error** (Probability = $\beta$) |
| **Reject $H_0$** | **Type I Error** (Probability = $\alpha$) | Correct Decision (Probability = $1 - \beta$) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definitions:**
> - **Type I Error ($\alpha$):** Rejecting a TRUE null hypothesis. "Convicting the innocent."
> - **Type II Error ($\beta$):** Failing to reject a FALSE null hypothesis. "Letting the guilty go free."
> - **Power of the Test ($1 - \beta$):** The probability of correctly rejecting a FALSE null hypothesis.

### 2.3 The Power Function

The **Power Function** of a test is the probability of rejecting $H_0$ as a function of the true (unknown) parameter $\theta$. It is the master tool for evaluating a test's performance:

$$\pi(\theta) = P_{\theta}(T \in W)$$

When evaluated at a value $\theta \in H_0$, it gives the **Type I error rate**. When evaluated at $\theta \in H_1$, it gives the **Power**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Property Check:** A "good" test satisfies:
> 1. $\pi(\theta) \leq \alpha$ for all $\theta \in H_0$ (size constraint).
> 2. $\pi(\theta)$ is as large as possible for $\theta \in H_1$ (maximum power).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Symbol / Domain | KTU Remarks |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Type I Error | $\alpha = P(\text{Reject } H_0 \mid H_0 \text{ true})$ | $0 < \alpha < 1$ | Also called **significance level**. |
| Type II Error | $\beta = P(\text{Accept } H_0 \mid H_1 \text{ true})$ | $0 < \beta < 1$ | Depends on the *true* parameter value. |
| Power | $1 - \beta = P(\text{Reject } H_0 \mid H_1 \text{ true})$ | $0 < 1-\beta < 1$ | Higher power = better test. |
| Power Function | $\pi(\theta) = P_{\theta}(X \in W)$ | Function of $\theta$ | Universal test-evaluation tool. |
| Size of Test | $\alpha^* = \sup_{\theta \in H_0} \pi(\theta)$ | Smallest upper bound | The **actual** significance level. |
| $Z$-test (mean) | $Z = \dfrac{\bar{X} - \mu_0}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}}$ | $\sigma$ known, $n \geq 30$ | Standard Normal under $H_0$. |
| $t$-test (mean) | $t = \dfrac{\bar{X} - \mu_0}{S/\sqrt{n}}$ | $\sigma$ unknown, $n < 30$ | $t$-distribution with $n-1$ d.f. |
| $Z$-test (proportion) | $Z = \dfrac{\hat{p} - p_0}{\sqrt{p_0(1-p_0)/n}}$ | Binomial data, large $n$ | $N(0,1)$ approximately. |
| Critical value (right) | $z_{\alpha}$ or $t_{\alpha,\,n-1}$ | Two-sided uses $z_{\alpha/2}$ | Read from statistical tables. |

### 2.5 Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> Hypothesis testing is the **backbone of Quality Control** in manufacturing. When a factory claims that the mean lifetime of a bulb is $\mu = 1000$ hours, the Quality Assurance engineer uses a hypothesis test to verify this claim. **Type I error** would mean recalling a perfectly good batch (waste of money). **Type II error** would mean shipping a defective batch to customers (damage to brand, safety hazard). Engineers must balance both risks.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Power of a $Z$-Test for the Mean (One-Sided, Right-Tailed)

> **Problem Setup (Typical KTU Long-Answer Question):**
> Let $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n$ be a random sample from $N(\mu, \sigma^2)$ where $\sigma^2$ is **known**. We wish to test:
> $$H_0: \mu = \mu_0 \quad \text{versus} \quad H_1: \mu > \mu_0$$
> at significance level $\alpha$. Derive the critical region and the power function of the test.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Identify the Test Statistic.**
Since $\sigma$ is known and the population is Normal, the natural test statistic is the standardized mean:

$$Z = \frac{\bar{X} - \mu_0}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}}$$

When $H_0$ is true ($\mu = \mu_0$), this statistic follows the **standard normal distribution**, i.e., $Z \sim N(0, 1)$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Determine the Critical Region (Rejection Region).**
The alternative hypothesis is $H_1: \mu > \mu_0$. This means large positive values of $\bar{X}$ (and hence large positive values of $Z$) provide evidence **in favor of $H_1$**. Therefore, the critical region must be of the form:

$$W = \left\{ (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n) : Z > z_{\alpha} \right\}$$

where $z_{\alpha}$ is the upper $\alpha$-quantile of the standard normal distribution, defined by:

$$P(Z > z_{\alpha}) = \alpha \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \int_{z_{\alpha}}^{\infty} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-t^2/2} \, dt = \alpha$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Enforce the Size Constraint.**
We must ensure that the probability of Type I error equals exactly $\alpha$:

$$\begin{aligned}
P(\text{Type I Error}) &= P_{\mu_0}(\text{Reject } H_0) \\
&= P_{\mu_0}(Z > z_{\alpha}) \\
&= \alpha \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

This confirms that the test has **size exactly $\alpha$**.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Derive the Power Function $\pi(\mu)$.**
For a general (true) mean $\mu$, the distribution of $\bar{X}$ is $N(\mu, \sigma^2/n)$. Standardizing:

$$Z = \frac{\bar{X} - \mu_0}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}} = \frac{(\bar{X} - \mu) + (\mu - \mu_0)}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}}$$

We rewrite the critical region in terms of the *true* $\mu$:

$$Z > z_{\alpha} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \frac{\bar{X} - \mu}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}} > z_{\alpha} - \frac{\mu - \mu_0}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}}$$

Let $\delta = \dfrac{\mu - \mu_0}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}}$ (this is the *effect size*, dimensionless). Then:

$$\begin{aligned}
\pi(\mu) &= P_{\mu}\left(\frac{\bar{X} - \mu}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}} > z_{\alpha} - \delta\right) \\
&= 1 - \Phi\left(z_{\alpha} - \frac{\mu - \mu_0}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}}\right)
\end{aligned}$$

where $\Phi(\cdot)$ is the CDF of the standard normal distribution. Therefore, the **power function is**:

$$\boxed{\;\pi(\mu) = 1 - \Phi\left(z_{\alpha} - \frac{\mu - \mu_0}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}}\right)\;}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Verify Special Cases.**

- **At $\mu = \mu_0$ (under $H_0$):**
  $$\pi(\mu_0) = 1 - \Phi(z_{\alpha} - 0) = 1 - \Phi(z_{\alpha}) = \alpha \quad \checkmark$$
  This matches the significance level, as expected.

- **At $\mu \to \infty$ (very far from $H_0$):**
  $$\frac{\mu - \mu_0}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}} \to \infty \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \pi(\mu) \to 1 - \Phi(-\infty) = 1 \quad \checkmark$$
  The test correctly rejects $H_0$ with probability approaching 1.

- **Type II Error at a specific $\mu_1 > \mu_0$:**
  $$\beta(\mu_1) = 1 - \pi(\mu_1) = \Phi\left(z_{\alpha} - \frac{\mu_1 - \mu_0}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}}\right)$$

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Worked Numerical Example

> **Worked Example (KTU 2024 Pattern ŌĆö 7-Mark Style):**
> A manufacturer claims that the average weight of a cereal box is $\mu_0 = 500$ g. A random sample of $n = 36$ boxes yields a sample mean $\bar{x} = 495$ g. Assume the population standard deviation is $\sigma = 12$ g. Test at $\alpha = 0.05$ whether the true mean is **less** than 500 g.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Formulate Hypotheses:**
$$H_0: \mu = 500 \text{ g} \quad \text{vs.} \quad H_1: \mu < 500 \text{ g}$$
This is a **left-tailed** test.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Choose Test Statistic:**
Since $\sigma$ is known and the sample is large ($n = 36$):

$$Z = \frac{\bar{X} - \mu_0}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}} = \frac{\bar{X} - 500}{12/\sqrt{36}} = \frac{\bar{X} - 500}{2}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Determine Critical Value:**
For a left-tailed test at $\alpha = 0.05$, we need $z_{0.05}$. From the standard normal table:

$$z_{0.05} = -1.645 \quad \text{(lower 5\% quantile)}$$

Therefore, the critical region is: **$W = \{ Z < -1.645 \}$**.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compute Observed Test Statistic:**

$$Z_{\text{obs}} = \frac{495 - 500}{12/\sqrt{36}} = \frac{-5}{2} = -2.50$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Make Decision:**
Since $Z_{\text{obs}} = -2.50 < -1.645$, the observed value **falls inside the critical region**.

$$\therefore \text{Reject } H_0 \text{ at the 5\% level of significance.}$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Engineering Interpretation:**
There is **sufficient statistical evidence** at the 5% significance level to conclude that the true mean weight of the cereal boxes is **less than 500 g**, contradicting the manufacturer's claim. The production line may need recalibration.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: The Hypothesis Testing Pipeline

This block diagram maps the complete **decision flow** of a hypothesis test procedure from formulation to final inference, including error feedback loops.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[STEP 1: Research Claim Formulated] --> B[STEP 2: State H0 with Equality]
    B --> C[STEP 3: State H1 as Research Claim]
    C --> D[STEP 4: Choose Test Statistic T]
    D --> E[STEP 5: Fix Significance Level alpha]
    E --> F[STEP 6: Determine Critical Region W]
    F --> G[STEP 7: Draw Random Sample and Compute Tobs]
    G --> H{Is Tobs in W ?}
    H -- YES --> I[REJECT H0]
    H -- NO --> J[FAIL TO REJECT H0]
    I --> K{Is H0 actually true ?}
    K -- YES --> L[Type I Error: alpha]
    K -- NO --> M[Correct Rejection: Power 1 - beta]
    J --> N{Is H0 actually true ?}
    N -- YES --> O[Correct Decision: 1 - alpha]
    N -- NO --> P[Type II Error: beta]
    L --> Q[Report p-value and Confidence Interval]
    M --> Q
    O --> Q
    P --> Q
    Q --> R[Final Engineering Conclusion]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Decision Reality Matrix

This schematic visualizes the **2x2 decision matrix** and the two error types, showing the relationship between decisions made and ground truth.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Reality
        R1[H0 is TRUE]
        R2[H0 is FALSE]
    end
    subgraph Decision
        D1[Reject H0]
        D2[Fail to Reject H0]
    end
    R1 --> D1
    R1 --> D2
    R2 --> D1
    R2 --> D2
    D1 -.->|alpha: Type I Error| R1
    D2 -.->|beta: Type II Error| R2
    D1 -.->|Power 1 - beta| R2
    D2 -.->|Specificity 1 - alpha| R1
```

### 4.3 Classification Tree: Types of Hypotheses

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    H[Statistical Hypothesis] --> H1[Simple Hypothesis]
    H --> H2[Composite Hypothesis]
    H1 --> H1A[Example: mu equals 50]
    H2 --> H2A[One Sided: mu greater than 50]
    H2 --> H2B[One Sided: mu less than 50]
    H2 --> H2C[Two Sided: mu not equal 50]
    H2A --> R[Right Tailed Test]
    H2B --> L[Left Tailed Test]
    H2C --> T[Two Tailed Test]
```

### 4.4 Power Curve Schematic (Description)

The power function $\pi(\mu)$ is a **monotonically increasing function** of $\mu$ for a right-tailed test:

- For $\mu \leq \mu_0$ (within $H_0$): $\pi(\mu) \leq \alpha$ (test rejects with small probability).
- For $\mu = \mu_0$: $\pi(\mu_0) = \alpha$ (boundary of $H_0$).
- For $\mu \to \infty$: $\pi(\mu) \to 1$ (test almost surely rejects $H_0$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Visualizing the Trade-off:** If we decrease $\alpha$ (e.g., from 0.05 to 0.01), the critical value $z_{\alpha}$ becomes more extreme, the critical region shrinks, $\pi(\mu_0)$ drops, **but** $\beta$ increases for any fixed $\mu_1 \in H_1$. The power curves for the test become "flatter" ŌĆö the test is more conservative. This is the fundamental $\alpha$-$\beta$ trade-off.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024]**
**Define the following terms with one example each:**
**(a)** Null Hypothesis
**(b)** Alternative Hypothesis
**(c)** Type I Error

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**(a) Null Hypothesis ($H_0$):** A null hypothesis is a statistical hypothesis that states there is **no significant difference** or **no effect** in the population. It always contains the equality sign and represents the "status quo."
*Example:* $H_0: \mu = 100$ (the population mean equals 100).

**(b) Alternative Hypothesis ($H_1$ or $H_a$):** The alternative hypothesis is the statement that **contradicts** the null hypothesis and represents the researcher's claim. It is the hypothesis we are trying to establish evidence for.
*Example:* $H_1: \mu \neq 100$ (the population mean is different from 100).

**(c) Type I Error:** A Type I error occurs when we **reject a true null hypothesis**. It is also called the "false positive" or "significance level," denoted by $\alpha$.
*Example:* Concluding that a medicine is effective when in reality it is not.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish between a one-tailed test and a two-tailed test with examples.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | One-Tailed Test | Two-Tailed Test |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Direction** | Directional (left or right) | Non-directional (both sides) |
| **$H_1$ form** | $\mu > \mu_0$ or $\mu < \mu_0$ | $\mu \neq \mu_0$ |
| **Rejection region** | Only in one tail of distribution | In both tails of distribution |
| **Critical value** | $z_{\alpha}$ or $z_{1-\alpha}$ | $z_{\alpha/2}$ and $z_{1-\alpha/2}$ |
| **When to use** | When researcher knows the direction of effect | When direction is unknown a priori |
| **Example** | $H_0: \mu = 50$ vs. $H_1: \mu > 50$ | $H_0: \mu = 50$ vs. $H_1: \mu \neq 50$ |

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks ŌĆö Choice Based)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024 Model Paper]**

**A manufacturing firm claims that the average lifetime of its LED bulbs is at least $\mu_0 = 10000$ hours. A random sample of $n = 50$ bulbs is tested, and the sample mean lifetime is found to be $\bar{x} = 9800$ hours. Assume the population standard deviation is $\sigma = 400$ hours. Test the firm's claim at the 5% level of significance.**

**(a)** Formulate the null and alternative hypotheses. Identify the type of test (one-sided or two-sided) and find the critical value. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compute the test statistic, state the decision rule, and compute the p-value. State your conclusion. Also calculate the power of the test if the true mean is $\mu_1 = 9750$ hours. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

### Part (a) ŌĆö Hypothesis Formulation & Critical Region (7 Marks)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Stating the Hypotheses:**
The firm claims the mean is "at least 10000 hours," so the null hypothesis (status quo) is:

$$H_0: \mu \geq 10000 \text{ hours} \quad \text{(or equivalently } \mu = 10000\text{)}$$

The research claim to be tested (whether the mean is less than claimed) is:

$$H_1: \mu < 10000 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Type of Test:**
Since $H_1$ contains the strict inequality "$<$", this is a **left-tailed (one-sided) test**.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Test Statistic:**
Since $\sigma$ is known and $n = 50$ is large, we use the **$Z$-test**:

$$Z = \frac{\bar{X} - \mu_0}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}} = \frac{\bar{X} - 10000}{400/\sqrt{50}}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Critical Value:**
For a left-tailed test at $\alpha = 0.05$, the critical value is the lower 5% quantile of the standard normal:

$$z_{0.05} = -1.645$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Critical Region:**
The critical region (rejection region) is:

$$W = \left\{ (x_1, \ldots, x_{50}) : Z < -1.645 \right\}$$

### Part (b) ŌĆö Test Computation, Decision, and Power (7 Marks)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute the Test Statistic:**

$$Z_{\text{obs}} = \frac{9800 - 10000}{400/\sqrt{50}} = \frac{-200}{400/7.0711} = \frac{-200}{56.5685} = -3.536$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Decision Rule:**
Reject $H_0$ if $Z_{\text{obs}} < -1.645$.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Decision:**
Since $Z_{\text{obs}} = -3.536 < -1.645$, the observed value **lies in the critical region**.

$$\therefore \text{We REJECT } H_0 \text{ at the 5\% level of significance.}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö p-value:**
The p-value is the probability of observing a value at least as extreme as $Z_{\text{obs}}$ under $H_0$:

$$\text{p-value} = P(Z < -3.536) \approx 0.0002$$

Since p-value $= 0.0002 \ll 0.05$, this strongly supports rejection.

**Step 5 ŌĆö Conclusion:**
There is **overwhelming evidence** at the 5% level that the true mean lifetime is **less than 10000 hours**. The firm's claim is not supported by the data.

**Step 6 ŌĆö Power Calculation at $\mu_1 = 9750$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\pi(9750) &= 1 - \Phi\left(z_{\alpha} - \frac{\mu_1 - \mu_0}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}}\right) \\
&= 1 - \Phi\left(-1.645 - \frac{9750 - 10000}{400/\sqrt{50}}\right) \\
&= 1 - \Phi\left(-1.645 - \frac{-250}{56.5685}\right) \\
&= 1 - \Phi\left(-1.645 + 4.419\right) \\
&= 1 - \Phi(2.774) \\
&= 1 - 0.9972 \\
&= 0.0028
\end{aligned}$$

The power at $\mu_1 = 9750$ is only $0.28\%$, which is very low ŌĆö this means if the true mean were 9750, the test would rarely detect the difference. This is because $\mu_1 = 9750$ is only slightly less than 10000, and the effect is small relative to the standard error.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the concepts of **Type I error**, **Type II error**, and **Power of a test** in detail. Draw a decision table and explain the relationship between significance level $\alpha$ and Type II error $\beta$. What is meant by the **power function** of a test? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A random sample of size $n = 25$ is drawn from a normal population with mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma = 8$. Test $H_0: \mu = 50$ against $H_1: \mu \neq 50$ at $\alpha = 0.05$. If the true mean is $\mu_1 = 52$, find the probability of Type II error $\beta$ and the power of the test. Use $Z_{0.025} = 1.96$. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

### Part (a) ŌĆö Theory of Errors and Power (7 Marks)

**Type I Error ($\alpha$):** This is the error of **rejecting a true null hypothesis**. The probability is denoted by $\alpha$ and is fixed in advance as the level of significance.

**Type II Error ($\beta$):** This is the error of **failing to reject a false null hypothesis**. The probability is $\beta$ and depends on the true (alternative) value of the parameter.

**Power of a Test ($1 - \beta$):** The probability of **correctly rejecting a false null hypothesis**. A test with high power is desirable.

**Decision Table:**

| Decision | $H_0$ True | $H_0$ False |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Reject $H_0$** | Type I Error ($\alpha$) | Correct (Power = $1-\beta$) |
| **Fail to Reject $H_0$** | Correct ($1-\alpha$) | Type II Error ($\beta$) |

**Relationship between $\alpha$ and $\beta$:** For a fixed sample size $n$, $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are **inversely related**. Decreasing $\alpha$ (stricter rejection criterion) **increases** $\beta$ (more likely to miss a true effect). The only way to reduce both simultaneously is to **increase the sample size $n$**.

**Power Function:** The power function $\pi(\theta)$ of a test is defined as the probability of rejecting $H_0$ as a function of the true parameter $\theta$:

$$\pi(\theta) = P_{\theta}(X \in W)$$

- If $\theta \in H_0$: $\pi(\theta) = \alpha(\theta)$ (Type I error rate).
- If $\theta \in H_1$: $\pi(\theta) = 1 - \beta(\theta)$ (Power).

### Part (b) ŌĆö Two-Tailed Test Computation (7 Marks)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Hypotheses:**
$$H_0: \mu = 50 \quad \text{vs.} \quad H_1: \mu \neq 50$$

This is a **two-tailed test**.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Test Statistic:**
Given $\sigma = 8$ and $n = 25$:

$$Z = \frac{\bar{X} - 50}{8/\sqrt{25}} = \frac{\bar{X} - 50}{1.6}$$

Under $H_0$, $Z \sim N(0, 1)$.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Critical Region for Two-Tailed Test:**
At $\alpha = 0.05$, the critical values are $\pm z_{\alpha/2} = \pm 1.96$:

$$W = \{ Z < -1.96 \text{ or } Z > 1.96 \}$$

The acceptance region is $A = \{ -1.96 \leq Z \leq 1.96 \}$, which translates to:

$$50 - 1.96(1.6) \leq \bar{X} \leq 50 + 1.96(1.6)$$
$$46.864 \leq \bar{X} \leq 53.136$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compute $\beta$ at $\mu_1 = 52$:**
Type II error is the probability of **failing to reject** $H_0$ when the true mean is 52:

$$\begin{aligned}
\beta &= P_{52}(46.864 \leq \bar{X} \leq 53.136) \\
&= P_{52}\left(\frac{46.864 - 52}{1.6} \leq Z \leq \frac{53.136 - 52}{1.6}\right) \\
&= P_{52}(-3.21 \leq Z \leq 0.71) \\
&= \Phi(0.71) - \Phi(-3.21) \\
&= 0.7611 - 0.00067 \\
&\approx 0.7604
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Power:**

$$\text{Power} = 1 - \beta = 1 - 0.7604 = 0.2396 \approx 24\%$$

**Interpretation:** When the true mean is $\mu_1 = 52$ (only 2 units away from $H_0$), the test has only a **24% chance** of correctly detecting the difference. This is because the effect size is small relative to the standard error ($1.6$). Increasing $n$ would substantially increase the power.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the equality in $H_0$:** Many students write $H_0: \mu < 50$ or $H_0: \mu > 50$. This is **WRONG**. $H_0$ MUST contain the equality sign. (Penalty: 1 mark lost instantly.)
> 2. **Confusing one-tailed and two-tailed critical values:** For a two-tailed test at $\alpha = 0.05$, students often use $z_{0.05} = 1.645$ instead of $z_{0.025} = 1.96$. This leads to **wrong critical region** and **wrong decision**. (Penalty: 2-3 marks.)
> 3. **Skipping the engineering interpretation:** KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes **Outcome-Based Education**. Always end with a contextual sentence (e.g., "the firm's claim is rejected", "the medicine is effective", etc.). (Penalty: 1 mark.)
> 4. **Not standardizing the test statistic:** When computing $Z_{\text{obs}}$, students often divide by $\sigma$ instead of $\sigma/\sqrt{n}$. The formula MUST use the **standard error** $\sigma/\sqrt{n}$. (Penalty: 2 marks.)
> 5. **Mixing up Type I and Type II errors:** Type I = Rejecting a true $H_0$ (false positive). Type II = Accepting a false $H_0$ (false negative). Reversing these is a common error. (Penalty: 2 marks.)

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Null Hypothesis ($H_0$):** Status quo statement; ALWAYS contains the equality sign ($=$, $\leq$, $\geq$).
- **Alternative Hypothesis ($H_1$):** The research claim; contains strict inequality ($<$, $>$, $\neq$).
- **Simple Hypothesis:** Completely specifies all parameters (e.g., $\mu = 50$).
- **Composite Hypothesis:** Specifies a range or set of values (e.g., $\mu \geq 50$).
- **One-tailed test:** $H_1$ has $<$ (left-tailed) or $>$ (right-tailed).
- **Two-tailed test:** $H_1$ has $\neq$.
- **Type I Error ($\alpha$):** Rejecting a TRUE $H_0$ ŌĆö probability = significance level.
- **Type II Error ($\beta$):** Failing to reject a FALSE $H_0$.
- **Power of Test ($1 - \beta$):** Probability of correctly rejecting a FALSE $H_0$.
- **Power Function $\pi(\theta)$:** $P_{\theta}(X \in W)$; equals $\alpha$ when $\theta \in H_0$ and $1-\beta$ when $\theta \in H_1$.
- **Critical Region $W$:** Set of sample outcomes that lead to rejection of $H_0$.
- **Test Statistic:** A function of the sample with known distribution under $H_0$ ($Z$, $t$, $\chi^2$, $F$).
- **Trade-off:** For fixed $n$, decreasing $\alpha$ increases $\beta$. To reduce both, **increase sample size $n$**.
- **Standard Error:** Always use $\sigma/\sqrt{n}$ (or $S/\sqrt{n}$), never just $\sigma$.
- **Two-tailed critical value:** Use $z_{\alpha/2}$, NOT $z_{\alpha}$.
- **Decision rule:** If $T_{\text{obs}} \in W$, Reject $H_0$. If $T_{\text{obs}} \notin W$, Fail to reject $H_0$ (NEVER say "Accept $H_0$" ŌĆö that is a KTU-favored deduction).
- **p-value:** Smallest significance level at which $H_0$ is rejected; if p-value $< \alpha$, reject $H_0$.
- **Engineering meaning:** Always conclude with a domain-specific statement (manufacturing quality, medical efficacy, etc.) ŌĆö KTU values OBE-based answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Type I and Type II Error
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GCMAT401 - MATHEMATICS FOR PHYSICAL SCIENCE AND LIFE SCIENCE ŌĆō 4
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Type I and Type II Error

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Foundation: Hypothesis Testing Decisions

In **Statistical Hypothesis Testing**, every test forces the statistician to make one of exactly two mutually exclusive decisions about the population parameter being studied:

1. **Reject the Null Hypothesis ($H_0$)**, OR
2. **Fail to Reject (Accept) the Null Hypothesis ($H_0$)**

These decisions are made based on a calculated test statistic (such as $Z$, $t$, or $\chi^2$) compared against a **critical region** derived from a chosen level of significance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A **Type I Error** ($\alpha$-error) occurs when a **TRUE** Null Hypothesis ($H_0$) is **incorrectly REJECTED** by the test.
> A **Type II Error** ($\beta$-error) occurs when a **FALSE** Null Hypothesis ($H_0$) is **incorrectly ACCEPTED** (failed to reject) by the test.

## 1.2 The Four Possible Outcomes of a Statistical Decision

Because the true state of nature is either "$H_0$ is true" or "$H_0$ is false," and the decision is either "Reject $H_0$" or "Accept $H_0$", there are **exactly four** combinations:

| # | True State of Nature | Decision Made | Outcome | Type |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $H_0$ is True | Reject $H_0$ | Incorrect Decision | **Type I Error** |
| 2 | $H_0$ is True | Accept $H_0$ | Correct Decision | Confidence Level $(1-\alpha)$ |
| 3 | $H_0$ is False | Reject $H_0$ | Correct Decision | Power of Test $(1-\beta)$ |
| 4 | $H_0$ is False | Accept $H_0$ | Incorrect Decision | **Type II Error** |

## 1.3 The Courtroom Analogy (Intuitive Insight)

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö A Criminal Trial:**
> - The **Null Hypothesis ($H_0$):** "The accused is **INNOCENT**" (the default assumption of the court).
> - The **Alternative Hypothesis ($H_1$):** "The accused is **GUILTY**".
> - **Type I Error:** Convicting an **innocent** person (rejecting a true $H_0$).
> - **Type II Error:** Letting a **guilty** person go free (accepting a false $H_0$).
> - **Justice System Bias:** The court is set up to *minimize Type I errors* (a strict "beyond reasonable doubt" threshold), even at the cost of a higher Type II error rate. In statistics, we similarly pre-fix the level of significance $\alpha$ (usually **0.05** or **5%**) to control the Type I error probability.

## 1.4 The Level of Significance ($\alpha$)

The **Level of Significance** $\alpha$ is the *pre-determined* maximum probability of committing a Type I Error that the researcher is willing to accept.

$$\alpha = P(\text{Type I Error}) = P(\text{Reject } H_0 \mid H_0 \text{ is true})$$

Standard values adopted in KTU board examinations:

- **$\alpha = 0.05$** (5%) ŌĆö Most common
- **$\alpha = 0.01$** (1%) ŌĆö For critical applications (medical, aerospace)
- **$\alpha = 0.10$** (10%) ŌĆö For exploratory research

The complementary probability $(1-\alpha)$ is called the **Level of Confidence** (e.g., 95% confidence level when $\alpha = 0.05$).

## 1.5 The Probability of Type II Error ($\beta$) and Power

The probability of a Type II Error is denoted by $\beta$:

$$\beta = P(\text{Type II Error}) = P(\text{Accept } H_0 \mid H_0 \text{ is false})$$

The complement $(1-\beta)$ is the **Power of the Test** ŌĆö the probability of *correctly* rejecting a false null hypothesis. A high-power test is essential for reliable scientific conclusions.

$$\text{Power of Test} = 1 - \beta = P(\text{Reject } H_0 \mid H_0 \text{ is false})$$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logic Flow of a Hypothesis Test

- **Step 1 ŌĆö Formulate Hypotheses:** The researcher sets up the null ($H_0$) and alternative ($H_1$) statements. $H_0$ always contains an equality sign ($=$, $\leq$, or $\geq$).
- **Step 2 ŌĆö Choose Significance Level:** A maximum Type I Error probability $\alpha$ is selected *before* data collection (e.g., $\alpha = 0.05$). This fixes the critical region boundaries.
- **Step 3 ŌĆö Compute Test Statistic:** Using sample data, calculate the test statistic ($Z$, $t$, $F$, or $\chi^2$).
- **Step 4 ŌĆö Decision Rule:** Compare the calculated statistic to the critical value. If it falls in the rejection region $\Rightarrow$ **Reject $H_0$**. Otherwise $\Rightarrow$ **Accept $H_0$**.
- **Step 5 ŌĆö Conclusion in Context:** Translate the statistical decision back to the original research question.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why $\alpha$ is fixed first (not $\beta$):**
> In classical (Neyman-Pearson) hypothesis testing, only one error probability can be directly controlled by the test designer. By convention, we pre-fix $\alpha$ (the Type I risk), and $\beta$ then becomes a derived quantity that depends on the true (unknown) parameter value, the sample size $n$, and the true variance $\sigma^2$.

## 2.2 The Crucial Trade-off: Inverse Relationship

> [!NOTE]
> **The Fundamental Trade-off:** For a **fixed sample size $n$**, decreasing the probability of a Type I Error ($\alpha$) automatically **increases** the probability of a Type II Error ($\beta$), and vice versa. They cannot be minimized simultaneously without increasing $n$.

The only way to reduce BOTH $\alpha$ and $\beta$ simultaneously is to:
1. **Increase the sample size $n$**, OR
2. **Reduce the population variance $\sigma^2$** (e.g., through better experimental control).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Symbol | Full Name | Mathematical Definition | Engineering/Clinical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\alpha$ | Size of Test / Significance Level | $P(\text{Reject } H_0 \mid H_0 \text{ true})$ | Producer's risk (Quality Control) |
| 2 | $\beta$ | Type II Error Probability | $P(\text{Accept } H_0 \mid H_0 \text{ false})$ | Consumer's risk (Quality Control) |
| 3 | $1-\alpha$ | Confidence Level / Coefficient of Confidence | $P(\text{Accept } H_0 \mid H_0 \text{ true})$ | Reliability of accepting a true claim |
| 4 | $1-\beta$ | Power of the Test | $P(\text{Reject } H_0 \mid H_0 \text{ false})$ | Ability to detect a real effect |
| 5 | $n$ | Sample Size | Determined by $z_\alpha$, $z_\beta$, $\sigma$, effect size | Drives precision of inference |
| 6 | $z_\alpha$ | Critical Value (Upper) | $P(Z > z_\alpha) = \alpha$ for standard normal | Boundary of rejection region |

### 2.4 Sample Size Determination Formula (KTU High-Yield)

For a **two-tailed test** of a population mean with known $\sigma$, the minimum sample size required to achieve specific $\alpha$ and $\beta$ values is:

$$n = \left( \frac{(z_{\alpha/2} + z_{\beta}) \, \sigma}{E} \right)^2$$

where $E = \vert \mu - \mu_0 \vert$ is the **effect size** (true difference we wish to detect).

For a **one-tailed test**, replace $z_{\alpha/2}$ with $z_{\alpha}$.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Medical Diagnostics (Life Sciences):** $\alpha$ is a false-positive (healthy person diagnosed with disease); $\beta$ is a false-negative (sick person cleared). Doctors tune $\alpha$ low to avoid unnecessary treatments.
- **Quality Control in Manufacturing (Physical Sciences):** $\alpha$ is the *producer's risk* (rejecting a good batch); $\beta$ is the *consumer's risk* (accepting a defective batch). Engineers balance both using OC curves.
- **Aerospace and Structural Safety:** A Type I Error (falsely rejecting a safe design) is generally preferred over a Type II Error (accepting an unsafe design), so $\alpha$ is set very low (e.g., 0.01).
- **Machine Learning & A/B Testing:** $\alpha$ corresponds to a false positive in classification; $\beta$ to a false negative. Power $(1-\beta)$ is the model's sensitivity.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Mathematical Formulation of the Decision Matrix

We formalize the four outcomes using conditional probability notation. Let $D_R$ = event "Reject $H_0$" and $D_A$ = event "Accept $H_0$".

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{Type I Error}) &= P(D_R \mid H_0 \text{ true}) = \alpha \\[4pt]
P(\text{Correct Acceptance}) &= P(D_A \mid H_0 \text{ true}) = 1 - \alpha \\[4pt]
P(\text{Correct Rejection / Power}) &= P(D_R \mid H_0 \text{ false}) = 1 - \beta \\[4pt]
P(\text{Type II Error}) &= P(D_A \mid H_0 \text{ false}) = \beta
\end{aligned}
$$

Since the probabilities of correct and incorrect decisions on the same row must sum to 1:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha + (1-\alpha) &= 1 \quad \text{(Row 1 ŌĆö H}_0 \text{ true)} \\
\beta + (1-\beta) &= 1 \quad \text{(Row 2 ŌĆö H}_0 \text{ false)}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Worked Example 1: Constructing the Decision Table

**Problem:** A pharmaceutical company claims that a new drug reduces blood pressure by an average of 10 mmHg. State the hypotheses and identify Type I and Type II errors in this context.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Formulate Hypotheses:**

$$H_0: \mu = 10 \text{ mmHg} \quad \text{(Drug is effective as claimed)}$$
$$H_1: \mu \neq 10 \text{ mmHg} \quad \text{(Drug is NOT effective as claimed)}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Identify the Errors (Interpretation):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Type I Error } (\alpha): \quad & \text{Concluding the drug is NOT effective when it actually IS effective.} \\
& \text{(Rejecting a true claim ŌĆö wastes a good drug.)} \\[4pt]
\text{Type II Error } (\beta): \quad & \text{Concluding the drug IS effective when it actually is NOT.} \\
& \text{(Accepting a false claim ŌĆö patients get ineffective treatment.)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Decision Table:**

| | $H_0$ True (Drug Works) | $H_0$ False (Drug Doesn't Work) |
|---|---|---|
| **Reject $H_0$** | Type I Error ($\alpha$) ŌĆö False Alarm | Correct Decision (Power $1-\beta$) ŌĆö Detected Inefficacy |
| **Accept $H_0$** | Correct Decision ($1-\alpha$) ŌĆö Trust the Claim | Type II Error ($\beta$) ŌĆö Missed Inefficacy |

## 3.3 Worked Example 2: Computing $\beta$ for a One-Tailed Mean Test

**Problem:** For a population with $\sigma = 12$, a random sample of size $n = 36$ is drawn. Test $H_0: \mu = 50$ against $H_1: \mu > 50$ at $\alpha = 0.05$. Find the value of $\beta$ if the true mean is $\mu = 52$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Find the Critical Value of the Test Statistic:**

Since $n = 36$ is large, we use the $Z$-test. The standard error of the mean is:

$$SE = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} = \frac{12}{\sqrt{36}} = \frac{12}{6} = 2$$

For a right-tailed test at $\alpha = 0.05$, the critical value is $z_{0.05} = 1.645$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Find the Critical Value of $\bar{X}$ (the decision boundary in the original scale):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z &= \frac{\bar{X}_c - \mu_0}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}} \\[4pt]
1.645 &= \frac{\bar{X}_c - 50}{2} \\[4pt]
\bar{X}_c &= 50 + 1.645 \times 2 = 50 + 3.29 = 53.29
\end{aligned}
$$

Decision Rule: Reject $H_0$ if $\bar{X} > 53.29$.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute $\beta$ Assuming the TRUE Mean is $\mu = 52$:**

A Type II Error occurs when we **accept** $H_0$ (i.e., $\bar{X} \leq 53.29$) even though the true mean is $52$. We standardize using the *true* mean:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\beta &= P(\text{Accept } H_0 \mid \mu = 52) = P(\bar{X} \leq 53.29 \mid \mu = 52) \\[4pt]
Z &= \frac{53.29 - 52}{2} = \frac{1.29}{2} = 0.645 \\[4pt]
\beta &= P(Z \leq 0.645) = \Phi(0.645)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Look up the Standard Normal Table:**

$$\Phi(0.645) \approx 0.7404$$

Therefore:

$$\beta \approx 0.7404, \quad \text{Power} = 1 - \beta \approx 0.2596$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Interpretation:** There is a **74% chance** of failing to detect the true shift from 50 to 52. The test has very low power for this effect size. To increase power, we would need a larger sample $n$.

## 3.4 Worked Example 3: Sample Size Calculation

**Problem:** A manufacturer wants to detect a mean shift of $E = 4$ units in a process where $\sigma = 10$. Find the minimum sample size to achieve $\alpha = 0.05$ (two-tailed) and $\beta = 0.10$ (i.e., Power = 0.90).

**Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Identify the Critical Values:**

$$z_{\alpha/2} = z_{0.025} = 1.96, \quad z_{\beta} = z_{0.10} = 1.28$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply the Sample Size Formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n &= \left( \frac{(z_{\alpha/2} + z_{\beta}) \, \sigma}{E} \right)^2 \\[4pt]
n &= \left( \frac{(1.96 + 1.28) \times 10}{4} \right)^2 \\[4pt]
n &= \left( \frac{3.24 \times 10}{4} \right)^2 \\[4pt]
n &= \left( \frac{32.4}{4} \right)^2 = (8.1)^2 = 65.61
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Round Up (Always):**

$$n = 66 \text{ samples}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Interpretation:** The manufacturer must collect at least 66 observations to simultaneously control Type I Error at 5% and Type II Error at 10% for detecting a true shift of 4 units.

## 3.5 Comprehensive Analytical Matrix

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Master Decision Matrix (for a Two-Tailed Test):} \\[6pt]
\begin{array}{|l|c|c|}
\hline
 & H_0 \text{ is TRUE} & H_0 \text{ is FALSE} \\
\hline
\text{Reject } H_0 & \alpha \text{ (Type I)} & 1-\beta \text{ (Power)} \\
\hline
\text{Accept } H_0 & 1-\alpha \text{ (Confidence)} & \beta \text{ (Type II)} \\
\hline
\textbf{Column Total} & 1 & 1 \\
\hline
\end{array}
\end{aligned}
$$

Key relationships to memorize:

$$P(\text{Correct Decision}) = (1-\alpha) + (1-\beta) - 1 = 1 - \alpha - \beta$$

$$P(\text{Incorrect Decision}) = \alpha + \beta$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Decision Flowchart for Hypothesis Testing

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Define H0 and H1"] --> B["Choose Level of Significance alpha"]
    B --> C["Collect Random Sample of Size n"]
    C --> D["Compute Test Statistic Z, t, or chi2"]
    D --> E{"Does Statistic Fall in Rejection Region?"}
    E -- "YES" --> F["Reject H0 at level alpha"]
    E -- "NO" --> G["Accept H0 at level alpha"]
    F --> H{"Was H0 Actually True?"}
    G --> I{"Was H0 Actually True?"}
    H -- "Yes, H0 Was True" --> J["Type I Error: Probability alpha"]
    H -- "No, H0 Was False" --> K["Correct Rejection: Power 1 minus beta"]
    I -- "Yes, H0 Was True" --> L["Correct Acceptance: Confidence 1 minus alpha"]
    I -- "No, H0 Was False" --> M["Type II Error: Probability beta"]
    J --> N["End: Report Type and Probability of Error"]
    K --> N
    L --> N
    M --> N
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: The Two-Error Subsystem

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT MODULE"]
        P1["Population Parameter mu_true"]
        P2["Pre-fixed alpha Level"]
    end

    subgraph TEST["HYPOTHESIS TEST ENGINE"]
        T1["Null Hypothesis H0: mu = mu0"]
        T2["Alternative H1: mu not equal to mu0"]
        T3["Compute Z Statistic"]
        T4["Compare to Critical Value z_alpha"]
    end

    subgraph DECISION["DECISION OUTPUT MODULE"]
        D1["If Z greater than z_alpha: Reject H0"]
        D2["If Z less than z_alpha: Accept H0"]
    end

    subgraph ERRORS["ERROR CLASSIFICATION MODULE"]
        E1["Type I Error alpha: Reject True H0"]
        E2["Type II Error beta: Accept False H0"]
        E3["Power 1 minus beta: Reject False H0"]
        E4["Confidence 1 minus alpha: Accept True H0"]
    end

    P1 --> T1
    P2 --> T4
    T1 --> T3
    T2 --> T3
    T3 --> T4
    T4 --> D1
    T4 --> D2
    D1 --> E1
    D1 --> E3
    D2 --> E2
    D2 --> E4
```

## 4.3 The Probability Distribution Visualization (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph H0_TRUE["DISTRIBUTION UNDER H0 TRUE: Center at mu0"]
        R1["Rejection Region in Tails: Area equals alpha"]
        C1["Central Acceptance Region: Area equals 1 minus alpha"]
    end

    subgraph H0_FALSE["DISTRIBUTION UNDER H0 FALSE: Shifted to mu1"]
        R2["Correct Rejection Region: Area equals 1 minus beta equals Power"]
        B2["Type II Error Beta: Area in Central Region under H0 FALSE"]
    end

    H0_TRUE -. "Overlap determines beta" .-> H0_FALSE
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** When $H_0$ is true, the test statistic follows the left (centered) distribution. When $H_0$ is actually false with true mean $\mu_1$, the statistic follows the right (shifted) distribution. The overlap area of the right distribution inside the left distribution's *acceptance region* is exactly $\beta$. The complementary area is the **power** $(1-\beta)$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024 Model]

**Define Type I and Type II errors. Explain with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Type I Error ($\alpha$):** Rejecting a true null hypothesis $H_0$. Probability $P(\text{Type I}) = \alpha$.
- **Type II Error ($\beta$):** Accepting a false null hypothesis $H_0$. Probability $P(\text{Type II}) = \beta$.

**Example:** Let $H_0$ = "The new medicine is effective." A **Type I error** occurs if we conclude the medicine is ineffective when in reality it is. A **Type II error** occurs if we conclude the medicine is effective when it actually is not. *(Full marks for clear hypothesis + distinct definitions + one contextual example.)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023 Model]

**What is the power of a test? How is it related to Type II error?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **power of a statistical test** is the probability of **correctly rejecting a false null hypothesis**.

$$\text{Power} = 1 - \beta = P(\text{Reject } H_0 \mid H_0 \text{ is false})$$

A high power (close to 1) means the test is very good at detecting a true effect; a low power (close to 0) means it frequently misses real effects. Power increases with larger sample size $n$ and larger effect size.

## 5.2 Part B: Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2024 Model]

**State the four possible outcomes of a hypothesis test. Construct the decision table and define the size of the test, level of confidence, and power of the test. For a normal population with $\sigma = 8$, a sample of $n = 64$ yields $\bar{x} = 52$. Test $H_0: \mu = 50$ against $H_1: \mu \neq 50$ at $\alpha = 0.05$. If the true mean is $\mu = 53$, find the probability of Type II error $\beta$ and the power of the test.**

#### Part (a) ŌĆö Theory (7 Marks)

**Solution:**

The four possible outcomes arise from the combination of two true states ($H_0$ true / $H_0$ false) and two decisions (Reject / Accept $H_0$).

**Decision Table:**

| Decision \\ Truth | $H_0$ True | $H_0$ False |
|---|---|---|
| **Reject $H_0$** | Type I Error ($\alpha$) | Power ($1-\beta$) |
| **Accept $H_0$** | Confidence ($1-\alpha$) | Type II Error ($\beta$) |

**Definitions:**

- **Size of Test ($\alpha$):** Probability of rejecting $H_0$ when it is true, $P(\text{Type I}) = \alpha$. Pre-fixed by the researcher.
- **Level of Confidence ($1-\alpha$):** Probability of correctly accepting a true $H_0$.
- **Power ($1-\beta$):** Probability of correctly rejecting a false $H_0$.

#### Part (b) ŌĆö Computation (7 Marks)

**Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö State the Hypotheses:**

$$H_0: \mu = 50, \quad H_1: \mu \neq 50 \quad \text{(Two-tailed test)}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Test Statistic Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z &= \frac{\bar{x} - \mu_0}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}} = \frac{52 - 50}{8 / \sqrt{64}} = \frac{2}{8/8} = \frac{2}{1} = 2.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Decision at $\alpha = 0.05$ Two-Tailed:**

Critical values: $z_{\alpha/2} = \pm 1.96$.

Since $|Z| = 2.0 > 1.96$, we **Reject $H_0$** at the 5% level.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Find Critical Sample Mean Boundary:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{X}_c &= \mu_0 \pm z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} = 50 \pm 1.96 \times 1 = (48.04, \; 51.96)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Compute $\beta$ Assuming True Mean $\mu = 53$:**

Type II Error occurs when $H_0$ is accepted, i.e., when $48.04 \leq \bar{X} \leq 51.96$, despite $\mu = 53$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\beta &= P(48.04 \leq \bar{X} \leq 51.96 \mid \mu = 53) \\[4pt]
Z_1 &= \frac{48.04 - 53}{1} = -4.96 \\[4pt]
Z_2 &= \frac{51.96 - 53}{1} = -1.04 \\[4pt]
\beta &= P(-4.96 \leq Z \leq -1.04) = \Phi(-1.04) - \Phi(-4.96) \\[4pt]
\beta &\approx 0.1492 - 0.0000 = 0.1492
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Compute the Power:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Power} = 1 - \beta = 1 - 0.1492 = 0.8508
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The test has an **85.08% chance** of correctly detecting a true shift from 50 to 53. The probability of missing it ($\beta$) is only 14.92%.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024 Model]

**Explain the concept of Type I and Type II errors. A manufacturer claims that the average lifetime of a bulb is 1000 hours. A sample of 64 bulbs gives a mean of 990 hours. Assuming the lifetime is normally distributed with $\sigma = 80$ hours, test at $\alpha = 0.05$ whether the manufacturer's claim is valid. Also find the probability of Type II error if the true mean is $\mu = 980$ hours.**

#### Part (a) ŌĆö Conceptual Setup (7 Marks)

**Solution:**

**Conceptual Framework:**

In testing $H_0: \mu = 1000$ against $H_1: \mu \neq 1000$:

- **Type I Error:** Concluding the bulbs do NOT last 1000 hours when they actually do (false alarm ŌĆö wastes a good product).
- **Type II Error:** Concluding the bulbs DO last 1000 hours when the true mean is actually lower (missed defect ŌĆö defective product reaches customer).

**Hypotheses for this problem:**

$$H_0: \mu = 1000 \text{ hours (claim is valid)}, \quad H_1: \mu \neq 1000 \text{ hours (claim is invalid)}$$

**Test Statistic Setup:** Since $n = 64$ is large, the $Z$-test is appropriate.

**Decision Rule at $\alpha = 0.05$ (Two-tailed):** Reject $H_0$ if $|Z| > 1.96$.

#### Part (b) ŌĆö Numerical Computation (7 Marks)

**Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute the Test Statistic:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z &= \frac{\bar{x} - \mu_0}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}} = \frac{990 - 1000}{80 / \sqrt{64}} = \frac{-10}{80/8} = \frac{-10}{10} = -1.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Decision at $\alpha = 0.05$:**

Since $|Z| = 1.0 < 1.96$, the test statistic does **NOT** fall in the rejection region. We **Accept $H_0$**: the manufacturer's claim is statistically valid at the 5% level.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Find the Acceptance Region in Terms of $\bar{X}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{X}_{\text{lower}} &= \mu_0 - 1.96 \times \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} = 1000 - 1.96 \times 10 = 1000 - 19.6 = 980.4 \\[4pt]
\bar{X}_{\text{upper}} &= \mu_0 + 1.96 \times \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} = 1000 + 19.6 = 1019.6
\end{aligned}
$$

Acceptance region: $980.4 \leq \bar{X} \leq 1019.6$.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compute $\beta$ Assuming True Mean $\mu = 980$:**

Type II Error occurs when we **accept** $H_0$ (i.e., $980.4 \leq \bar{X} \leq 1019.6$) despite the true mean being 980.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\beta &= P(980.4 \leq \bar{X} \leq 1019.6 \mid \mu = 980) \\[4pt]
Z_1 &= \frac{980.4 - 980}{10} = \frac{0.4}{10} = 0.04 \\[4pt]
Z_2 &= \frac{1019.6 - 980}{10} = \frac{39.6}{10} = 3.96 \\[4pt]
\beta &= P(0.04 \leq Z \leq 3.96) = \Phi(3.96) - \Phi(0.04) \\[4pt]
\beta &\approx 1.0000 - 0.5160 = 0.4840
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Compute the Power:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Power} = 1 - \beta = 1 - 0.4840 = 0.5160
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The probability of a Type II error is **0.4840** (about 48.4%), meaning the test has only a 51.6% chance of detecting a true drop of 20 hours from the claimed 1000. The test has **low power** for this effect size ŌĆö a larger sample is recommended.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls (Lose 1ŌĆō2 Marks Each):**
> 1. **Confusing $H_0$ and $H_1$:** $H_0$ ALWAYS contains the equality sign. Markers deduct marks if the alternative is written with equality.
> 2. **One-tailed vs Two-tailed confusion:** Forgetting to use $z_{\alpha/2}$ for two-tailed tests is a common 1-mark loss. The denominator stays the same; only the critical value changes.
> 3. **Standard Error Formula:** Students frequently write $\sigma \sqrt{n}$ instead of $\sigma/\sqrt{n}$. This single error cascades into wrong Z-values and wrong $\beta$ calculations.
> 4. **Standardizing for $\beta$ using the WRONG mean:** When computing $\beta$, the $Z$-value is computed using the *TRUE* mean $\mu_1$ (e.g., 980 or 52), not the null hypothesis mean $\mu_0$. This is the single most common error in Part (b) numericals.
> 5. **Final Conclusion Missing:** A numerical answer without a concluding sentence in plain English (e.g., "The test has a 48.4% chance of missing a real defect") loses 1 mark.
> 6. **Rounding Errors:** $\Phi$ values must be read accurately to 4 decimal places. Off-by-one errors in table lookups (e.g., reading $\Phi(0.04)$ as 0.5080 instead of 0.5160) propagate heavily.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Type I Error ($\alpha$):** Rejecting a TRUE $H_0$ ŌĆö "False Alarm." Probability = Level of Significance. Pre-fixed by the experimenter.
- **Type II Error ($\beta$):** Accepting a FALSE $H_0$ ŌĆö "Missed Detection." Probability depends on true parameter value, sample size, and variance.
- **Power of Test** = $1-\beta$: The probability of correctly detecting a real effect. **Higher is better.** A well-designed test has Power $\geq 0.80$ (80%).
- **Confidence Level** = $1-\alpha$: The probability of correctly accepting a true claim. Standard is 0.95.
- **Trade-off Rule:** For fixed $n$, **decreasing $\alpha$ increases $\beta$** and vice versa. They are inversely related.
- **Joint Reduction Rule:** Increase sample size $n$ to **simultaneously reduce** both $\alpha$ and $\beta$.
- **Sample Size Formula (Two-tailed Mean Test):** $n = \left( \dfrac{(z_{\alpha/2} + z_{\beta}) \sigma}{E} \right)^2$, where $E = \vert \mu - \mu_0 \vert$.
- **OC Curve:** A plot of $\beta$ against the true parameter value. Steeper OC curves indicate more powerful tests.
- **One-tailed test** uses $z_\alpha$; **Two-tailed test** uses $z_{\alpha/2}$. Always divide $\alpha$ by 2 for two-tailed rejection regions.
- **For $\beta$ calculation:** Use the TRUE mean $\mu_1$ (not $\mu_0$) when standardizing $\bar{X}$ in the Type II error probability integral.
- **Standard Normal Table:** $\Phi(z) = P(Z \leq z)$. For negative $z$, use $\Phi(-z) = 1 - \Phi(z)$.
- **Always round up** the sample size $n$ to the next whole number ŌĆö fractional samples are meaningless.
- **Engineering labels:** $\alpha$ = Producer's Risk; $\beta$ = Consumer's Risk (in Quality Control contexts).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: z Tests for Hypotheses about a Population Mean (for Large Sample)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GCMAT401 - MATHEMATICS FOR PHYSICAL SCIENCE AND LIFE SCIENCE ŌĆō 4
> - **Module:** Module Module 3

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **z-test for a population mean (large sample)** is a statistical hypothesis test that uses the standard normal distribution to determine whether the sample mean $\bar{x}$ differs significantly from a hypothesized population mean $\mu_0$. The test is classified as a **large sample test** when the sample size $n \geq 30$ (the Central Limit Theorem guarantees approximate normality of $\bar{x}$), and the test statistic follows the standard normal variate $Z \sim N(0, 1)$.

The general form of the test statistic is:

$$Z = \frac{\bar{x} - \mu_0}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}}$$

where the population standard deviation $\sigma$ may be replaced by the sample standard deviation $s$ when $\sigma$ is unknown (valid for large $n$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> The z-test applies when **(a)** the population is normal with **known** $\sigma$, or **(b)** the sample size is **large ($n \geq 30$)** even if $\sigma$ is unknown (substitute $s$ for $\sigma$). This is the principal large-sample test for a single population mean.

## 1.2 Foundational Concepts: The Hypothesis Testing Framework

Before applying the z-test, the following foundational vocabulary must be mastered:

### (a) Statistical Hypothesis
A **statistical hypothesis** is an assumption or a statement about a population parameter that may or may not be true. It is tested against sample evidence.

### (b) Null Hypothesis ($H_0$)
The **null hypothesis** is the default assumption of *no effect*, *no difference*, or *no change*. It always contains the equality sign $(=, \leq, \geq)$. It represents the status quo that the test attempts to challenge.

### (c) Alternative Hypothesis ($H_1$ or $H_a$)
The **alternative hypothesis** is the negation or the research claim ŌĆö the statement we want to prove. It contains the strict inequality $(<, >, \neq)$.

### (d) Level of Significance ($\alpha$)
The **level of significance** is the maximum probability of rejecting $H_0$ when it is actually true (Type I error). Common values used in KTU examinations: $\alpha = 0.05$ ($Z_{\text{crit}} = 1.96$ two-tailed, $1.645$ one-tailed) and $\alpha = 0.01$ ($Z_{\text{crit}} = 2.576$ two-tailed, $2.33$ one-tailed).

### (e) Critical Value and Critical Region
The **critical value** is the threshold value of the test statistic that separates the acceptance region from the rejection (critical) region. The **critical region** (rejection region) is the set of all values of the test statistic that lead to rejection of $H_0$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Critical Values to Memorize for KTU Exams:**
> | $\alpha$ | Two-Tailed $Z_\alpha$ | One-Tailed $Z_\alpha$ |
> |---------|----------------------|------------------------|
> | **0.05** | **1.96** | **1.645** |
> | **0.01** | **2.576** | **2.33** |
> | **0.10** | **1.645** | **1.28** |

## 1.3 One-Tailed vs Two-Tailed Tests

| Test Type | Form of $H_1$ | Rejection Region | Use Case |
|-----------|---------------|------------------|----------|
| **Right-Tailed** | $H_1: \mu > \mu_0$ | $Z > Z_\alpha$ | Testing if mean has *increased* |
| **Left-Tailed** | $H_1: \mu < \mu_0$ | $Z < -Z_\alpha$ | Testing if mean has *decreased* |
| **Two-Tailed** | $H_1: \mu \neq \mu_0$ | $\vert Z \vert > Z_{\alpha/2}$ | Testing if mean has *changed* (in either direction) |

## 1.4 Type I and Type II Errors

| Decision \ Reality | $H_0$ is True | $H_0$ is False |
|--------------------|----------------|----------------|
| **Reject $H_0$** | **Type I Error** (probability $= \alpha$) | Correct Decision (Power $= 1-\beta$) |
| **Accept $H_0$** | Correct Decision (probability $= 1-\alpha$) | **Type II Error** (probability $= \beta$) |

## 1.5 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Real-World Engineering Analogy)

> [!TIP]
> **Think of a z-test as a "factory quality control alarm system":**
>
> Imagine a pharmaceutical company claims that the average paracetamol tablet weight is **500 mg** ($\mu_0 = 500$). The Quality Control manager takes a **large sample** of 50 tablets and weighs them. The sample mean comes out to be **497 mg**.
>
> - **$H_0$** (Null): "The machine is working fine; mean weight *is* 500 mg."
> - **$H_1$** (Alternative): "The machine is faulty; mean weight is *not* 500 mg."
> - The **z-test statistic** measures: *"How many standard errors is the sample mean away from the claimed value of 500 mg?"*
> - If this distance is *small* (i.e., $\vert Z \vert \leq 1.96$), we say: "The discrepancy is just random noise ŌĆö accept $H_0$, the machine is fine."
> - If this distance is *large* (i.e., $\vert Z \vert > 1.96$), we trigger the **alarm** (reject $H_0$) and shut down the line for maintenance.
>
> The critical value $1.96$ is essentially the **alarm threshold**: the maximum distance we tolerate before flagging a problem.

## 1.6 Decision Rule (Standard KTU Pattern)

1. **State** $H_0$ and $H_1$.
2. **Choose** the level of significance $\alpha$.
3. **Compute** the test statistic $Z$.
4. **Find** the critical value(s) from the standard normal table.
5. **Decision:** If $\vert Z_{\text{calc}} \vert > Z_{\text{crit}}$, **reject** $H_0$. Otherwise, **accept** $H_0$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Theoretical Foundation: Central Limit Theorem (CLT)

The z-test is mathematically justified by the **Central Limit Theorem**, which states:

> If $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n$ are independent and identically distributed random variables from a population with mean $\mu$ and finite variance $\sigma^2$, then the standardized sample mean converges in distribution to a standard normal variate as $n \to \infty$:

$$Z = \frac{\bar{X} - \mu}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}} \xrightarrow{d} N(0, 1)$$

**Why "Large Sample"?** For $n \geq 30$, the sampling distribution of $\bar{X}$ is approximately normal *regardless of the parent population's shape*. This allows us to use the standard normal table to compute probabilities.

## 2.2 The Standard Error of the Mean (SE)

The **standard error** quantifies the typical fluctuation of the sample mean around the population mean:

$$\text{SE} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

- **Smaller SE** $\Rightarrow$ sample mean is a *more precise* estimator of $\mu$.
- Increasing $n$ by a factor of $4$ halves the SE.

## 2.3 The Test Statistic ŌĆö Three Variants (KTU High-Yield)

| Case | Condition | Test Statistic |
|------|-----------|----------------|
| **Case 1** | $\sigma$ **known**, any $n$ (or $n$ large) | $Z = \dfrac{\bar{x} - \mu_0}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}}$ |
| **Case 2** | $\sigma$ **unknown**, $n \geq 30$ | $Z = \dfrac{\bar{x} - \mu_0}{s / \sqrt{n}}$ (using sample SD $s$) |
| **Case 3** | Comparing two large sample means | $Z = \dfrac{\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2}{\sqrt{\dfrac{s_1^2}{n_1} + \dfrac{s_2^2}{n_2}}}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Convention:** In large-sample problems, if $\sigma$ is *not* explicitly given, always substitute $s = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum x_i^2}{n} - \left(\dfrac{\sum x_i}{n}\right)^2}$ (or the unbiased estimator $s^2 = \dfrac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n-1}$). Either yields the same conclusion for $n \geq 30$.

## 2.4 Step-by-Step Logic of the z-Test Procedure

**Step 1 ŌĆö Formulate Hypotheses:**
- $H_0: \mu = \mu_0$ (the population mean equals the hypothesized value)
- $H_1: \mu \neq \mu_0$ (two-tailed) or $\mu > \mu_0$ / $\mu < \mu_0$ (one-tailed)

**Step 2 ŌĆö Choose Significance Level:**
- Standard KTU choices: $\alpha = 0.05$ or $\alpha = 0.01$.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Determine Critical Value(s):**
- Two-tailed: $Z_{\text{crit}} = Z_{\alpha/2}$ (rejection if $\vert Z \vert > Z_{\alpha/2}$)
- Right-tailed: $Z_{\text{crit}} = Z_\alpha$ (rejection if $Z > Z_\alpha$)
- Left-tailed: $Z_{\text{crit}} = -Z_\alpha$ (rejection if $Z < -Z_\alpha$)

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compute Test Statistic:**

$$Z_{\text{calc}} = \frac{\bar{x} - \mu_0}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}} \quad \text{or} \quad \frac{\bar{x} - \mu_0}{s / \sqrt{n}}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Decision:**
- If $\vert Z_{\text{calc}} \vert > Z_{\text{crit}}$: **Reject $H_0$** (the result is *statistically significant*).
- If $\vert Z_{\text{calc}} \vert \leq Z_{\text{crit}}$: **Accept $H_0$** (insufficient evidence to reject).

**Step 6 ŌĆö Conclusion in Words:**
- Translate the statistical decision into a real-world, domain-specific conclusion (e.g., "There is significant evidence at the 5% level that the mean lifetime of the bulbs exceeds 1000 hours").

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Notes / KTU Tip |
|---|-------------------|--------------------------|------------------|
| 1 | **Test statistic (known $\sigma$)** | $Z = \dfrac{\bar{x} - \mu_0}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}}$ | Use this when $\sigma$ is given. |
| 2 | **Test statistic (unknown $\sigma$, large $n$)** | $Z = \dfrac{\bar{x} - \mu_0}{s / \sqrt{n}}$ | Substitute $s$ when $\sigma$ is not given. |
| 3 | **Sample mean** | $\bar{x} = \dfrac{\sum x_i}{n}$ | Always compute first. |
| 4 | **Sample SD (biased estimator)** | $s = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum x_i^2}{n} - \bar{x}^2}$ | Convenient shortcut. |
| 5 | **Sample SD (unbiased)** | $s = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n-1}}$ | Used when $n < 30$; valid for large $n$. |
| 6 | **Standard error** | $\text{SE} = \dfrac{s}{\sqrt{n}}$ | Measures precision of $\bar{x}$. |
| 7 | **Critical value (two-tailed, $\alpha = 0.05$)** | $Z_{\text{crit}} = 1.96$ | Reject if $\vert Z \vert > 1.96$. |
| 8 | **Critical value (one-tailed, $\alpha = 0.05$)** | $Z_{\text{crit}} = 1.645$ | Reject if $Z > 1.645$ or $Z < -1.645$. |
| 9 | **p-value approach** | Compare $p$ with $\alpha$ | Reject $H_0$ if $p < \alpha$. |
| 10 | **P(Type I error)** | $\alpha$ | Pre-fixed by the analyst. |
| 11 | **P(Type II error)** | $\beta$ | Depends on true $\mu$. |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Engineering Field | Application of z-test on population mean |
|-------------------|-------------------------------------------|
| **Quality Control (Mechanical)** | Testing whether the mean diameter of manufactured bolts deviates from the specification. |
| **Civil Engineering** | Testing whether the mean compressive strength of concrete cubes meets the design value. |
| **Pharmaceutical** | Testing whether the mean drug concentration in a batch matches the labeled dosage. |
| **Electrical Engineering** | Testing whether the mean lifetime of LED bulbs exceeds 10,000 hours. |
| **Process Industries (Chemical)** | Testing whether the mean pH of an effluent stream lies within permissible limits. |
| **Agriculture / Life Sciences** | Testing whether the mean yield of a new fertilizer is greater than the conventional one. |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** In any Six-Sigma or ISO-9001 certified industry, the z-test is the *workhorse* of statistical process control (SPC). Control charts (X-bar charts) for large batches essentially perform z-tests continuously, signaling when a process goes "out of control."
