# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Algorithmic Flow of the Modified Euler's Method

The following Mermaid block diagrams the *complete computational pipeline* of one iteration of the Modified Euler's Method. It maps the interactions between input data, function evaluations, predictor, corrector, and output recording.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Start: Read Initial Conditions x0, y0, h, N]) --> B[Compute x1 = x0 + h]
    B --> C[/f0 = f of x0, y0/]
    C --> D[Predictor: yTilde1 = y0 + h times f0]
    D --> E[/fTilde1 = f of x1, yTilde1/]
    E --> F[Corrector: y1 = y0 + h over 2 times f0 + fTilde1]
    F --> G[Record y1 in solution table]
    G --> H{Iteration n less than N minus 1?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Update: xn = xn plus h, yn = y1]
    I --> C
    H -- No --> J([Stop: Output all yn values])
    style A fill:#4A90E2,color:#FFFFFF
    style D fill:#F5A623,color:#000000
    style F fill:#7ED321,color:#000000
    style J fill:#D0021B,color:#FFFFFF
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The **blue** node is the entry point. The **orange** node is the Predictor (Euler's step). The **green** node is the Corrector (trapezoidal refinement). The loop on the right executes for the specified number of iterations $N$.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Computational Roles

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT_LAYER
        I1[x0 initial point]
        I2[y0 initial value]
        I3[h step size]
        I4[N number of steps]
    end

    subgraph PROCESSING_LAYER
        P1[Function Evaluator f of x, y]
        P2[Predictor Module Euler Estimate]
        P3[Corrector Module Trapezoidal Average]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT_LAYER
        O1[Solution Array yn]
        O2[Tabulated Iteration Log]
    end

    I1 --> P1
    I2 --> P1
    I3 --> P2
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P1 --> P3
    P3 --> O1
    P3 --> O2
    style INPUT_LAYER fill:#E8F4F8
    style PROCESSING_LAYER fill:#FFF4E6
    style OUTPUT_LAYER fill:#E8F8E8
```

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** The block diagram is a *software engineering* style schematic. In a real numerical library, the **Function Evaluator** is an isolated module (so users can plug in any $f(x,y)$), the **Predictor** and **Corrector** modules perform the arithmetic, and the **Output Layer** formats the tabulated results.

## 4.3 Geometric Comparison: Classical vs. Modified Euler

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[True Solution Curve y of x] --> B[Slope at xn: f of xn, yn]
    A --> C[Slope at xn plus h: f of xn plus h, y of xn plus h]
    B --> D[Euler uses slope at xn only rectangle approximation]
    C --> E[Modified Euler averages both slopes trapezoidal approximation]
    D --> F[Euler y of xn plus h approx yn plus h times f of xn, yn]
    E --> G[Modified Euler y of xn plus h approx yn plus h over 2 times f of xn, yn plus f of xn plus h, yTilde]
    F --> H[Overestimate or underestimate accumulated error O of h squared]
    G --> I[Reduced error O of h cubed per step]
    style A fill:#BD10E0,color:#FFFFFF
    style D fill:#D0021B,color:#FFFFFF
    style E fill:#7ED321,color:#000000
    style I fill:#417505,color:#FFFFFF
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The crux of the diagram:** Classical Euler's rectangle (red) drifts away from the true curve, while the Modified Euler's trapezoid (green) hugs the true curve much more closely because it samples the slope at *both* ends of the step.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question A1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4]`
> **State the Modified Euler's Method for solving the IVP $dy/dx = f(x,y)$, $y(x_0) = y_0$. Mention its order of accuracy.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Modified Euler's Method is a predictorŌĆōcorrector scheme:

**Predictor:**
$$\tilde{y}_{n+1} = y_n + h\,f(x_n, y_n)$$

**Corrector:**
$$y_{n+1} = y_n + \frac{h}{2}\Big[\,f(x_n, y_n) + f(x_{n+1}, \tilde{y}_{n+1})\,\Big]$$

It is a **second-order accurate** method with local truncation error $O(h^3)$ and global error $O(h^2)$. **(3 marks: 1 mark predictor + 1 mark corrector + 1 mark order)**

---

> **Question A2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4]`
> **Why is the Modified Euler's Method considered more accurate than the classical Euler's Method?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The classical Euler method uses the slope $f(x_n, y_n)$ at the left endpoint of each sub-interval only, leading to a **rectangular approximation** of the integral of $f$, which is only first-order accurate.

The Modified Euler's Method uses a **two-stage predictorŌĆōcorrector** strategy: it first predicts $\tilde{y}_{n+1}$ using the left-end slope, then evaluates the slope at the predicted right-end $f(x_{n+1}, \tilde{y}_{n+1})$, and finally **averages** the two slopes. This is equivalent to applying the **trapezoidal rule** of numerical integration, which has higher accuracy than the rectangular rule. Consequently, the modified method is second-order accurate with local error $O(h^3)$ per step. **(3 marks: 1 mark limitation of classical + 1 mark description of modified + 1 mark order/error analysis)**

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **Question B1 (Module Internal Choice ŌĆö Option A).** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4, 14 marks]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Derive the Modified Euler's Method for solving $dy/dx = f(x,y)$, $y(x_0) = y_0$ by integrating the ODE and applying the trapezoidal rule. Clearly state the predictor and corrector formulae.

**(b) [7 marks]** Given $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = x - y^2$, $y(0) = 0$, find $y(0.3)$ using Modified Euler's Method with $h = 0.1$. Tabulate your iterations.

### Model Solution ŌĆö Question B1 (Option A)

#### Part (a) ŌĆö Derivation [7 marks]

Integrate the ODE $dy/dx = f(x,y)$ between $x_n$ and $x_{n+1}$:
$$y(x_{n+1}) - y(x_n) = \int_{x_n}^{x_{n+1}} f(x, y)\,dx$$

Apply the **Trapezoidal Rule** to approximate the integral:
$$\int_{x_n}^{x_{n+1}} f(x, y)\,dx \approx \frac{h}{2}\big[\,f(x_n, y_n) + f(x_{n+1}, y_{n+1})\,\big]$$

Thus the implicit formula is:
$$y_{n+1} = y_n + \frac{h}{2}\big[\,f(x_n, y_n) + f(x_{n+1}, y_{n+1})\,\big] \quad \text{[1 mark]}$$

This is **implicit** (the unknown $y_{n+1}$ appears on the right). To make it explicit, replace $y_{n+1}$ on the right with the **Euler prediction**:
$$\tilde{y}_{n+1} = y_n + h\,f(x_n, y_n) \quad \text{[2 marks ŌĆö Predictor]}$$

Substituting into the trapezoidal formula:
$$y_{n+1} = y_n + \frac{h}{2}\big[\,f(x_n, y_n) + f(x_{n+1}, \tilde{y}_{n+1})\,\big] \quad \text{[3 marks ŌĆö Corrector]}$$

State the order: **Second-order accurate** with local truncation error $O(h^3)$ and global error $O(h^2)$ **[1 mark]**.

#### Part (b) ŌĆö Numerical Computation [7 marks]

Given: $f(x,y) = x - y^2$, $x_0 = 0$, $y_0 = 0$, $h = 0.1$.

**Iteration 1: Compute $y_1$ at $x_1 = 0.1$**

Predictor:
$$\tilde{y}_1 = y_0 + h\,f(x_0, y_0) = 0 + 0.1 \cdot (0 - 0^2) = 0$$

Corrector:
$$f(x_0, y_0) = 0 - 0^2 = 0$$
$$f(x_1, \tilde{y}_1) = 0.1 - 0^2 = 0.1$$
$$y_1 = 0 + \frac{0.1}{2}(0 + 0.1) = 0.05 \cdot 0.1 = 0.005$$

**Iteration 2: Compute $y_2$ at $x_2 = 0.2$**

Predictor:
$$\tilde{y}_2 = y_1 + h\,f(x_1, y_1) = 0.005 + 0.1 \cdot (0.1 - 0.005^2)$$
$$0.005^2 = 0.000025, \quad 0.1 - 0.000025 = 0.099975$$
$$\tilde{y}_2 = 0.005 + 0.1 \cdot 0.099975 = 0.005 + 0.0099975 = 0.0149975$$

Corrector:
$$f(x_1, y_1) = 0.099975$$
$$f(x_2, \tilde{y}_2) = 0.2 - (0.0149975)^2 = 0.2 - 0.0002249 = 0.1997751$$
$$y_2 = 0.005 + \frac{0.1}{2}(0.099975 + 0.1997751) = 0.005 + 0.05 \cdot 0.2997501$$
$$y_2 = 0.005 + 0.01498751 = 0.01998751$$

**Iteration 3: Compute $y_3$ at $x_3 = 0.3$**

Predictor:
$$\tilde{y}_3 = y_2 + h\,f(x_2, y_2) = 0.01998751 + 0.1 \cdot (0.2 - 0.01998751^2)$$
$$(0.01998751)^2 \approx 0.0003995, \quad 0.2 - 0.0003995 = 0.1996005$$
$$\tilde{y}_3 = 0.01998751 + 0.1 \cdot 0.1996005 = 0.01998751 + 0.01996005 = 0.03994756$$

Corrector:
$$f(x_2, y_2) = 0.1996005$$
$$f(x_3, \tilde{y}_3) = 0.3 - (0.03994756)^2 = 0.3 - 0.0015958 = 0.2984042$$
$$y_3 = 0.01998751 + \frac{0.1}{2}(0.1996005 + 0.2984042)$$
$$y_3 = 0.01998751 + 0.05 \cdot 0.4980047$$
$$y_3 = 0.01998751 + 0.02490024 = 0.04488775$$

**Tabulated Solution:**

| $n$ | $x_n$ | $y_n$ | $f(x_n, y_n)$ | $\tilde{y}_{n+1}$ | $f(x_{n+1}, \tilde{y}_{n+1})$ | $y_{n+1}$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | 0.0 | 0.00000 | 0.00000 | 0.00000 | 0.10000 | 0.00500 |
| 1 | 0.1 | 0.00500 | 0.09998 | 0.01500 | 0.19978 | 0.01999 |
| 2 | 0.2 | 0.01999 | 0.19960 | 0.03995 | 0.29840 | **0.04489** |

$$\boxed{y(0.3) \;\approx\; 0.04489}$$

---

> **Question B1 (Module Internal Choice ŌĆö Option B).** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Module 4, 14 marks]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Compare the Modified Euler's Method with the classical Euler's Method with respect to: (i) order of accuracy, (ii) number of function evaluations per step, (iii) the type of slope information used, and (iv) the geometric interpretation of the area-under-the-curve approximation.

**(b) [7 marks]** Use the Modified Euler's Method to solve $dy/dx = 1 + xy$, with $y(0) = 1$ at $x = 0.4$ taking $h = 0.2$. Tabulate the iterations.

### Model Solution ŌĆö Question B1 (Option B)

#### Part (a) ŌĆö Comparison [7 marks]

| Criterion | Classical Euler | Modified Euler |
|---|---|---|
| **Order of accuracy** | First order, global error $O(h)$ | **Second order**, global error $O(h^2)$ **[1 mark]** |
| **Function evals/step** | 1 (only $f(x_n, y_n)$) | **2** ($f$ at both ends) **[1 mark]** |
| **Slope info used** | Left-endpoint slope only | Left + right (predicted) slopes, **averaged** **[2 marks]** |
| **Geometric interpretation** | Rectangle rule of integration | **Trapezoidal rule** of integration **[2 marks]** |
| **Formula** | $y_{n+1} = y_n + h f(x_n, y_n)$ | Predictor + corrector pair **[1 mark]** |

#### Part (b) ŌĆö Numerical Computation [7 marks]

Given: $f(x,y) = 1 + xy$, $x_0 = 0$, $y_0 = 1$, $h = 0.2$.

**Iteration 1: Compute $y_1$ at $x_1 = 0.2$**

Predictor:
$$\tilde{y}_1 = 1 + 0.2 \cdot (1 + 0 \cdot 1) = 1 + 0.2 = 1.2$$

Corrector:
$$f(0, 1) = 1 + 0 = 1$$
$$f(0.2, 1.2) = 1 + 0.2 \cdot 1.2 = 1 + 0.24 = 1.24$$
$$y_1 = 1 + \frac{0.2}{2}(1 + 1.24) = 1 + 0.1 \cdot 2.24 = 1 + 0.224 = 1.224$$

**Iteration 2: Compute $y_2$ at $x_2 = 0.4$**

Predictor:
$$\tilde{y}_2 = 1.224 + 0.2 \cdot (1 + 0.2 \cdot 1.224) = 1.224 + 0.2 \cdot (1 + 0.2448)$$
$$= 1.224 + 0.2 \cdot 1.2448 = 1.224 + 0.24896 = 1.47296$$

Corrector:
$$f(0.2, 1.224) = 1 + 0.2 \cdot 1.224 = 1 + 0.2448 = 1.2448$$
$$f(0.4, 1.47296) = 1 + 0.4 \cdot 1.47296 = 1 + 0.58918 = 1.58918$$
$$y_2 = 1.224 + \frac{0.2}{2}(1.2448 + 1.58918) = 1.224 + 0.1 \cdot 2.83398$$
$$y_2 = 1.224 + 0.28340 = 1.50740$$

**Tabulated Solution:**

| $n$ | $x_n$ | $y_n$ | $f(x_n, y_n)$ | $\tilde{y}_{n+1}$ | $f(x_{n+1}, \tilde{y}_{n+1})$ | $y_{n+1}$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | 0.0 | 1.00000 | 1.00000 | 1.20000 | 1.24000 | 1.22400 |
| 1 | 0.2 | 1.22400 | 1.24480 | 1.47296 | 1.58918 | **1.50740** |

$$\boxed{y(0.4) \;\approx\; 1.5074}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls (Modified Euler's Method)**
> - **Pitfall 1 (1 mark lost):** Students often confuse the *predicted* value $\tilde{y}_{n+1}$ with the *corrected* value $y_{n+1}$ and tabulate only the predictor as the final answer. **Always** record $y_{n+1}$ (from the corrector) as the entry in the $y$-column of the solution table.
> - **Pitfall 2 (1 mark lost):** Forgetting to evaluate $f(x_{n+1}, \tilde{y}_{n+1})$ at the *predicted* right-end, instead reusing $f(x_n, y_n)$ for both terms. The whole point of the method is to *use a different slope* on the right.
> - **Pitfall 3 (1 mark lost):** Rounding too early (e.g., truncating $\tilde{y}_{n+1}$ to 2 decimal places before the corrector step). **Carry at least 5 decimal places** through intermediate calculations.
> - **Pitfall 4 (2 marks lost):** Writing the corrector as $y_{n+1} = y_n + h \cdot f(x_{n+1}, \tilde{y}_{n+1})$ ŌĆö this omits the averaging! The factor $h/2$ and the sum of *both* slopes are mandatory.
> - **Pitfall 5 (1 mark lost):** Forgetting to state the order of the method and the local/global error in the derivation part. Examiners allocate marks specifically for this.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö Modified Euler's Method**

- **ODE Form:** $dy/dx = f(x,y)$ with $y(x_0) = y_0$.
- **Two-stage scheme:** Predictor (Euler) followed by Corrector (Trapezoidal).
- **Predictor Formula:** $\tilde{y}_{n+1} = y_n + h\,f(x_n, y_n)$.
- **Corrector Formula:** $y_{n+1} = y_n + \dfrac{h}{2}\left[\,f(x_n, y_n) + f(x_{n+1}, \tilde{y}_{n+1})\,\right]$.
- **Order:** Second-order accurate; member of the **RK2** family.
- **Local Truncation Error:** $O(h^3)$ per step.
- **Global Error:** $O(h^2)$ over the full interval.
- **Function evaluations per step:** 2.
- **Self-starting:** No auxiliary method required.
- **Geometric meaning:** Trapezoidal (not rectangular) approximation of $\int f\,dx$.
- **Required iterations in KTU exams:** At minimum, 3ŌĆō4 iterations; full tabulation is mandatory.
- **Rounding policy:** Carry **5 decimal places** in intermediate steps; round only the final answer to 4 decimal places.
- **Common confusion:** $\tilde{y}_{n+1}$ is the *predictor* (intermediate) ŌĆö $y_{n+1}$ is the *corrector* (final, tabulated value).
- **Avoid:** Using $\tilde{y}_{n+1}$ instead of $y_{n+1}$ as the starting value for the next iteration.
- **Origin of the method:** Integral form of ODE + Trapezoidal Rule of numerical integration.
- **Predecessor:** Classical Euler's Method (1st order, 1 function eval per step).
- **Successor in KTU syllabus:** RungeŌĆōKutta Fourth-Order Method (RK4, 4th order, 4 function evals per step).
- **Engineering relevance:** Solves transient circuit analysis, growth-decay, projectile motion, and reaction kinetics ODEs.

---

> **End of Module 4 Notes ŌĆö Modified Euler's Method**
> *Prepared in alignment with KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech syllabus and revised Bloom's Taxonomy cognitive levels (Apply, Analyze, Evaluate).*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Runge-Kutta Method of 2nd Order
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GCMAT401 - MATHEMATICS FOR PHYSICAL SCIENCE AND LIFE SCIENCE ŌĆō 4
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Runge-Kutta Method of 2nd Order

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ­¤ōś Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Runge-Kutta Method of 2nd Order (RK2)** is an iterative numerical procedure used to approximate the solution of a first-order ordinary differential equation (ODE) of the initial value problem (IVP) form:

$$\frac{dy}{dx} = f(x, y), \quad y(x_0) = y_0$$

It belongs to the family of **single-step explicit methods** that achieve higher-order accuracy without requiring the computation of higher derivatives of $f(x, y)$, by instead evaluating the function at multiple strategically chosen intermediate points within each step.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**
> The Runge-Kutta methods are the standard higher-order numerical integrators demanded in the GCMAT401 syllabus. The 2nd-order variant is mandatory because it serves as the conceptual foundation for understanding the most popular 4th-order Runge-Kutta (RK4) method.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Two Look-Aheads"

Imagine you are hiking down a foggy mountain and you can only see a few feet ahead. **Euler's method** is like taking one step in the direction your compass points and then re-evaluating ŌĆö fast, but inaccurate.

**Runge-Kutta 2nd Order** is smarter. You:
1. **Take a tentative step** in the slope direction (just like Euler) to see where you would land ŌĆö this is the *predictor* look-ahead.
2. **Re-evaluate the slope** at this predicted position (or at a refined midpoint) to get a better estimate of the true terrain.
3. **Take your actual step** using a weighted average of the two slope estimates.

This "two-look" strategy gives you a *much* better directional estimate without needing to actually compute the curvature ŌĆö you simply sample the slope at extra points.

## 1.3 Why Use RK2? ŌĆö The Engineering Motivation

- **Euler's method** has a global truncation error of $O(h)$, which is too coarse for real engineering work.
- **Taylor's series methods** of order 2+ require computing $\frac{\partial f}{\partial x}$, $\frac{\partial f}{\partial y}$, and higher derivatives ŌĆö which is tedious, error-prone, and often impossible for complex $f$.
- **RK2 strikes a balance**: It achieves $O(h^2)$ local error (and $O(h^3)$ global error) using *only* evaluations of the function $f(x,y)$ itself, with no partial derivatives.

> [!NOTE]
> **Order of Accuracy Comparison**
>
> | Method | Local Error | Global Error | Derivative Required |
> |--------|-------------|--------------|---------------------|
> | Euler's Method | $O(h^2)$ | $O(h)$ | None |
> | **RK2 Method** | $O(h^3)$ | $O(h^2)$ | None (only $f$) |
> | Taylor's 2nd Order | $O(h^3)$ | $O(h^2)$ | $\frac{\partial f}{\partial x}, \frac{\partial f}{\partial y}$ |
> | RK4 Method | $O(h^5)$ | $O(h^4)$ | None (only $f$) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ­¤ōś Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The General Form of RK2

The Runge-Kutta 2nd order method has the following generic structure:

$$k_1 = h \cdot f(x_n, y_n)$$

$$k_2 = h \cdot f(x_n + a \cdot h, \ y_n + b \cdot k_1)$$

$$y_{n+1} = y_n + c_0 \cdot k_1 + c_1 \cdot k_2$$

Where:
- $h$ is the step size: $h = x_{n+1} - x_n$
- $a, b, c_0, c_1$ are **constants** that determine the specific variant
- $k_1$ is the slope at the current point (like Euler)
- $k_2$ is the slope at a "look-ahead" point

## 2.2 Deriving the Constraints (Order Conditions)

To achieve **2nd order accuracy**, the constants must satisfy three constraints. We obtain these by matching the RK2 update equation with the Taylor series expansion of $y(x+h)$.

**Taylor Series of exact solution:**
$$y(x_n + h) = y_n + h y' + \frac{h^2}{2} y'' + \frac{h^3}{6} y''' + \cdots$$

Since $y' = f$, and $y'' = f_x + f \cdot f_y$, we have:

$$y(x_n + h) = y_n + h f + \frac{h^2}{2}(f_x + f \cdot f_y) + O(h^3)$$

**Expanding $k_2$ using Taylor (in two variables):**

$$f(x_n + ah, y_n + b k_1) = f + a h f_x + b k_1 f_y + O(h^2)$$

Substituting $k_1 = hf$:

$$f(x_n + ah, y_n + b k_1) = f + a h f_x + b h f \cdot f_y + O(h^2)$$

$$k_2 = h f + a h^2 f_x + b h^2 f \cdot f_y + O(h^3)$$

**Substituting into the update:**

$$y_{n+1} = y_n + c_0 (h f) + c_1 (h f + a h^2 f_x + b h^2 f \cdot f_y) + O(h^3)$$

$$y_{n+1} = y_n + (c_0 + c_1) h f + c_1 a h^2 f_x + c_1 b h^2 f \cdot f_y + O(h^3)$$

**Matching coefficients with Taylor:**

- Coefficient of $h f$ : $c_0 + c_1 = 1$
- Coefficient of $h^2 f_x$ : $c_1 a = \frac{1}{2}$
- Coefficient of $h^2 f \cdot f_y$ : $c_1 b = \frac{1}{2}$

These give us **2 equations and 4 unknowns** ŌĆö meaning we have **2 degrees of freedom**, leading to a family of RK2 methods.

## 2.3 The Three Standard RK2 Variants (KTU Mandatory)

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically tests the following three canonical forms. Memorize their coefficients and the geometric interpretation of each.

### Variant 1: Heun's Method (Improved Euler / Trapezoidal Predictor-Corrector)

$$a = 1, \quad b = 1, \quad c_0 = \frac{1}{2}, \quad c_1 = \frac{1}{2}$$

$$k_1 = h \cdot f(x_n, y_n)$$

$$k_2 = h \cdot f(x_n + h, \ y_n + k_1)$$

$$y_{n+1} = y_n + \frac{1}{2}(k_1 + k_2)$$

**Interpretation**: $k_2$ uses Euler's prediction to the end of the interval, then averages the slope at the start and end.

### Variant 2: Midpoint Method (Ralston's Variant with $a = b = 1/2$)

$$a = \frac{1}{2}, \quad b = \frac{1}{2}, \quad c_0 = 0, \quad c_1 = 1$$

$$k_1 = h \cdot f(x_n, y_n)$$

$$k_2 = h \cdot f\left(x_n + \frac{h}{2}, \ y_n + \frac{k_1}{2}\right)$$

$$y_{n+1} = y_n + k_2$$

**Interpretation**: $k_2$ is evaluated at the midpoint of the interval ŌĆö often the most accurate geometric choice.

### Variant 3: Ralston's Method (Optimal RK2 ŌĆö Minimum Truncation Error)

$$a = \frac{3}{4}, \quad b = \frac{3}{4}, \quad c_0 = \frac{1}{3}, \quad c_1 = \frac{2}{3}$$

$$k_1 = h \cdot f(x_n, y_n)$$

$$k_2 = h \cdot f\left(x_n + \frac{3h}{4}, \ y_n + \frac{3k_1}{4}\right)$$

$$y_{n+1} = y_n + \frac{1}{3} k_1 + \frac{2}{3} k_2$$

**Interpretation**: Coefficients chosen to minimize the principal truncation error term.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Concept | Formula / Condition | Notes |
|---------|---------------------|-------|
| **General RK2 Update** | $y_{n+1} = y_n + c_0 k_1 + c_1 k_2$ | All variants share this skeleton |
| **Slope at start** | $k_1 = h f(x_n, y_n)$ | Common to all variants |
| **Look-ahead slope** | $k_2 = h f(x_n + a h, y_n + b k_1)$ | Variant-dependent |
| **Order Condition 1** | $c_0 + c_1 = 1$ | Sum of weights |
| **Order Condition 2** | $c_1 a = 1/2$ | Position weight in $x$ |
| **Order Condition 3** | $c_1 b = 1/2$ | Position weight in $y$ |
| **Heun's Coefficients** | $a=1, b=1, c_0=c_1=1/2$ | Predictor-Corrector |
| **Midpoint Coefficients** | $a=b=1/2, c_0=0, c_1=1$ | Uses midpoint |
| **Ralston's Coefficients** | $a=b=3/4, c_0=1/3, c_1=2/3$ | Optimal RK2 |
| **Local Truncation Error** | $O(h^3)$ | Error in one step |
| **Global Truncation Error** | $O(h^2)$ | Accumulated error over interval |
| **Function evaluations per step** | **2** | Twice the cost of Euler |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility**
> RK2 methods are used in:
> - Real-time control systems where moderate accuracy is acceptable
> - Embedded systems with limited computational budget
> - Trajectory prediction in aerospace engineering
> - Initial value problems in chemical reaction kinetics
> - As a teaching/pedagogical stepping stone to RK4
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# ­¤ōś Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Order Conditions via Taylor Series Matching

We start with the exact Taylor expansion of $y(x_n + h)$ about $x_n$:

$$y(x_n + h) = y_n + h y'(x_n) + \frac{h^2}{2!} y''(x_n) + \frac{h^3}{3!} y'''(x_n) + O(h^4)$$

Using the chain rule for the total derivative:
- $y' = f(x, y)$
- $y'' = \frac{d}{dx} f(x, y) = f_x + f_y \cdot y' = f_x + f \cdot f_y$
- $y''' = f_{xx} + 2 f f_{xy} + f^2 f_{yy} + f_x f_y + f f_y^2$ (very messy)

Substituting:

$$y(x_n + h) = y_n + h f + \frac{h^2}{2}\left(f_x + f \cdot f_y\right) + \frac{h^3}{6}\left(f_{xx} + 2 f f_{xy} + f^2 f_{yy} + f_x f_y + f f_y^2\right) + O(h^4)$$

For a 2nd order method, we only need to match terms up to $h^2$. So the **target expansion** is:

$$y(x_n + h) = y_n + h f + \frac{h^2}{2}\left(f_x + f \cdot f_y\right) + O(h^3) \quad \cdots (1)$$

Now, expand the RK2 update. First, expand $k_2$ using a 2-variable Taylor series:

$$f(x_n + a h, y_n + b k_1) = f + (a h) f_x + (b k_1) f_y + \frac{1}{2}\left[(a h)^2 f_{xx} + 2(a h)(b k_1) f_{xy} + (b k_1)^2 f_{yy}\right] + \cdots$$

Substituting $k_1 = h f$:

$$f(x_n + a h, y_n + b k_1) = f + a h f_x + b h f f_y + O(h^2)$$

Multiplying by $h$ to get $k_2$:

$$k_2 = h f + a h^2 f_x + b h^2 f f_y + O(h^3) \quad \cdots (2)$$

Now substitute $k_1$ and $k_2$ into the update formula:

$$y_{n+1} = y_n + c_0 (h f) + c_1 \left(h f + a h^2 f_x + b h^2 f f_y + O(h^3)\right)$$

$$y_{n+1} = y_n + (c_0 + c_1) h f + c_1 a h^2 f_x + c_1 b h^2 f f_y + O(h^3) \quad \cdots (3)$$

Comparing $(1)$ and $(3)$ coefficient by coefficient:

**Coefficient of $h f$ :** 
$$c_0 + c_1 = 1$$

**Coefficient of $h^2 f_x$ :**
$$c_1 a = \frac{1}{2}$$

**Coefficient of $h^2 f f_y$ :**
$$c_1 b = \frac{1}{2}$$

These are the **three order conditions** for RK2. There are 4 unknowns and 3 equations, hence 1 free parameter ŌĆö generating a family of methods.

## 3.2 Choosing Coefficients ŌåÆ The Three Standard Methods

### Method 1: Heun's Method ŌĆö Set $c_1 = 1/2$

From $c_1 a = 1/2 \Rightarrow a = 1$
From $c_1 b = 1/2 \Rightarrow b = 1$
From $c_0 + c_1 = 1 \Rightarrow c_0 = 1/2$

### Method 2: Midpoint Method ŌĆö Set $c_1 = 1$ (so $c_0 = 0$)

From $c_1 a = 1/2 \Rightarrow a = 1/2$
From $c_1 b = 1/2 \Rightarrow b = 1/2$

### Method 3: Ralston's Method ŌĆö Minimize Truncation Error

The principal error term in RK2 is $\frac{h^3}{6}(c_1 a^2 - \frac{1}{3}) f_{xx} + \cdots$

Ralston's analysis showed that setting $c_1 = 2/3$ minimizes the error bound. Then:
- $c_0 = 1 - c_1 = 1/3$
- $a = \frac{1/2}{c_1} = \frac{1/2}{2/3} = 3/4$
- $b = \frac{1/2}{c_1} = 3/4$

## 3.3 Worked Example (KTU-Style 14-Mark Problem)

**Problem:** Solve $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = x + y$, $y(0) = 1$, using the **Heun's RK2 method** with $h = 0.1$ to find $y(0.2)$.

**Step 1: Identify the components.**

$$f(x, y) = x + y, \quad x_0 = 0, \quad y_0 = 1, \quad h = 0.1$$

**Step 2: Compute $k_1$ at $(x_0, y_0) = (0, 1)$.**

$$k_1 = h \cdot f(0, 1) = 0.1 \cdot (0 + 1) = 0.1$$

**Step 3: Compute $k_2$ using Heun's parameters $a=1, b=1$.**

$$k_2 = h \cdot f(x_0 + h, y_0 + k_1) = 0.1 \cdot f(0.1, 1.1)$$

$$k_2 = 0.1 \cdot (0.1 + 1.1) = 0.1 \cdot 1.2 = 0.12$$

**Step 4: Update $y_1 = y(0.1)$.**

$$y_1 = y_0 + \frac{1}{2}(k_1 + k_2) = 1 + \frac{1}{2}(0.1 + 0.12)$$

$$y_1 = 1 + 0.11 = 1.11$$

**Step 5: Second iteration to find $y(0.2)$.** Now $x_1 = 0.1, y_1 = 1.11$.

$$k_1 = 0.1 \cdot f(0.1, 1.11) = 0.1 \cdot (0.1 + 1.11) = 0.1 \cdot 1.21 = 0.121$$

$$k_2 = 0.1 \cdot f(0.2, 1.11 + 0.121) = 0.1 \cdot f(0.2, 1.231)$$

$$k_2 = 0.1 \cdot (0.2 + 1.231) = 0.1 \cdot 1.431 = 0.1431$$

$$y_2 = y_1 + \frac{1}{2}(k_1 + k_2) = 1.11 + \frac{1}{2}(0.121 + 0.1431)$$

$$y_2 = 1.11 + \frac{1}{2}(0.2641) = 1.11 + 0.13205 = 1.24205$$

**Step 6: Exact Solution for Verification.**

The exact solution of $\dfrac{dy}{dx} - y = x$ is $y = 2e^x - x - 1$.

$$y(0.2) = 2e^{0.2} - 0.2 - 1 = 2(1.2214) - 1.2 = 2.4428 - 1.2 = 1.2428$$

**Step 7: Error Analysis.**

$$\text{Absolute Error} = |1.2428 - 1.24205| = 0.00075$$

This is exceptionally accurate ŌĆö confirming that $O(h^2)$ global error is very tight for $h = 0.1$.

## 3.4 Worked Example (Midpoint Method)

**Same problem, Midpoint method:** $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = x + y$, $y(0) = 1$, $h = 0.1$, find $y(0.2)$.

**Iteration 1:** $x_0=0, y_0=1$

$$k_1 = 0.1 \cdot (0+1) = 0.1$$

$$k_2 = 0.1 \cdot f\left(0 + \frac{0.1}{2}, 1 + \frac{0.1}{2}\right) = 0.1 \cdot f(0.05, 1.05)$$

$$k_2 = 0.1 \cdot (0.05 + 1.05) = 0.1 \cdot 1.1 = 0.11$$

$$y_1 = y_0 + k_2 = 1 + 0.11 = 1.11$$

**Iteration 2:** $x_1=0.1, y_1=1.11$

$$k_1 = 0.1 \cdot (0.1 + 1.11) = 0.1 \cdot 1.21 = 0.121$$

$$k_2 = 0.1 \cdot f\left(0.1 + 0.05, 1.11 + \frac{0.121}{2}\right) = 0.1 \cdot f(0.15, 1.1705)$$

$$k_2 = 0.1 \cdot (0.15 + 1.1705) = 0.1 \cdot 1.3205 = 0.13205$$

$$y_2 = 1.11 + 0.13205 = 1.24205$$

Both Heun's and Midpoint methods give the same result for this linear ODE (as expected ŌĆö they have the same order of accuracy).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# ­¤ōś Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Algorithm Flow Diagram (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA([START]) --> nodeB[Input: f x y, x0, y0, h, target xn]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Set current_x = x0]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Set current_y = y0]
    nodeD --> nodeE{current_x less than xn?}
    nodeE -- No --> nodeF([Output: current_y is the answer])
    nodeE -- Yes --> nodeG["STEP 1: Compute k1 = h * f(current_x, current_y)"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["STEP 2: Compute k2 = h * f(current_x + a*h, current_y + b*k1)"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["STEP 3: Update next_y = current_y + c0*k1 + c1*k2"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Update current_x = current_x + h]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Set current_y = next_y]
    nodeK --> nodeE

    style nodeA fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#2C3E50,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    style nodeB fill:#F5F5F5,stroke:#2C3E50,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
    style nodeC fill:#F5F5F5,stroke:#2C3E50,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
    style nodeD fill:#F5F5F5,stroke:#2C3E50,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
    nodeE --> nodeF
    style nodeE fill:#FFE082,stroke:#F57C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    style nodeF fill:#66BB6A,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    style nodeG fill:#FFAB91,stroke:#BF360C,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
    style nodeH fill:#FFAB91,stroke:#BF360C,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
    style nodeI fill:#CE93D8,stroke:#4A148C,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
    style nodeJ fill:#F5F5F5,stroke:#2C3E50,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
    style nodeK fill:#F5F5F5,stroke:#2C3E50,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
```

## 4.2 Conceptual Diagram: Comparing the Three RK2 Variants Geometrically

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeStart([Initial Point xn, yn]) --> nodeP1["k1 = h*f at start point"]
    nodeP1 --> nodeBranch{Choose Variant}

    nodeBranch -- Heun's Method --> nodeH1["k2 at xn+h, yn+k1 (END)"]
    nodeH1 --> nodeH2["y_next = y + 0.5 k1 + 0.5 k2"]
    nodeH2 --> nodeOutH([Trapezoidal Avg])

    nodeBranch -- Midpoint Method --> nodeM1["k2 at xn+h/2, yn+k1/2 (MID)"]
    nodeM1 --> nodeM2["y_next = y + k2"]
    nodeM2 --> nodeOutM([Midpoint Slope])

    nodeBranch -- Ralston's Method --> nodeR1["k2 at xn+3h/4, yn+3k1/4 (3/4)"]
    nodeR1 --> nodeR2["y_next = y + 1/3 k1 + 2/3 k2"]
    nodeR2 --> nodeOutR([Optimal Weighted])

    style nodeStart fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#1A237E,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF

    style nodeH2 fill:#FFAB91,stroke:#BF360C,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
    style nodeOutH fill:#66BB6A,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    style nodeM1 fill:#90CAF9,stroke:#0D47A1,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
    style nodeM2 fill:#90CAF9,stroke:#0D47A1,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
    style nodeOutM fill:#66BB6A,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    style nodeR1 fill:#FFCC80,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
    style nodeR2 fill:#FFCC80,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
    style nodeOutR fill:#66BB6A,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
```

## 4.3 Functional Block Diagram: RK2 Step Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT LAYER"]
        inA["f of x and y"]
        inB["Initial Condition x0, y0"]
        inC["Step Size h"]
    end

    subgraph COMPUTE["COMPUTATION LAYER"]
        compA["k1 Evaluator: h times f at current point"]
        compB["k2 Evaluator: h times f at look-ahead point"]
        compC["Weighted Combiner: c0*k1 + c1*k2"]
    end

    subgraph STATE["STATE UPDATE LAYER"]
        stA["Update x: x_new = x_old + h"]
        stB["Update y: y_new = y_old + combination"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUT LAYER"]
        outA["Approximate y at new x"]
    end

    inA --> compA
    inA --> compB
    inB --> compA
    inC --> compA
    inC --> compB
    compA --> compB
    compA --> compC
    compB --> compC
    compC --> stB
    inC --> stA
    stA --> outA
    stB --> outA

    style INPUT fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    style COMPUTE fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    style STATE fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#4A148C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    style OUTPUT fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# ­¤ōś Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP]`
**State the general form of the Runge-Kutta 2nd order method for solving $\frac{dy}{dx} = f(x, y)$ and derive the order conditions.**

**Model Answer:**

The general form of RK2 is:

$$k_1 = h \cdot f(x_n, y_n)$$
$$k_2 = h \cdot f(x_n + a h, y_n + b k_1)$$
$$y_{n+1} = y_n + c_0 k_1 + c_1 k_2$$

By expanding $k_2$ in a 2-variable Taylor series and matching coefficients with the Taylor expansion of the exact solution $y(x_n + h)$, we obtain three order conditions:

$$c_0 + c_1 = 1, \quad c_1 a = \frac{1}{2}, \quad c_1 b = \frac{1}{2}$$

These three conditions on four parameters produce a family of RK2 methods.

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model QP]`
**Distinguish between Heun's method and the Midpoint method as variants of RK2. State the coefficients used in each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Heun's Method | Midpoint Method |
|-----------|---------------|-----------------|
| $a$ | $1$ | $1/2$ |
| $b$ | $1$ | $1/2$ |
| $c_0$ | $1/2$ | $0$ |
| $c_1$ | $1/2$ | $1$ |
| Look-ahead point | End of interval $(x_n + h, y_n + k_1)$ | Midpoint $(x_n + h/2, y_n + k_1/2)$ |
| Update formula | $y_{n+1} = y_n + \frac{1}{2}(k_1 + k_2)$ | $y_{n+1} = y_n + k_2$ |
| Interpretation | Trapezoidal average of slopes | Use slope at midpoint |

Both methods have local truncation error $O(h^3)$ and global error $O(h^2)$, but the Midpoint method is often geometrically more accurate for non-linear ODEs.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Internal Choice)

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Actual-style QP]`

**(a)** [7 marks] **Derive the second-order Runge-Kutta formulae by matching the Taylor series expansion. List the three standard variants and their coefficients.**

**(b)** [7 marks] **Using Heun's method (RK2), solve $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = xy + 1$ with $y(0) = 1$ to find $y(0.4)$ taking $h = 0.2$. Compute up to 2 decimal places.**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**Part (a) ŌĆö Derivation [7 marks]:**

Starting from the RK2 structure:

$$k_1 = h f(x_n, y_n)$$
$$k_2 = h f(x_n + ah, y_n + bk_1)$$
$$y_{n+1} = y_n + c_0 k_1 + c_1 k_2$$

**Expand $k_2$ in 2-variable Taylor series:**

$$f(x_n + ah, y_n + bk_1) = f + ah f_x + bk_1 f_y + O(h^2)$$

Substituting $k_1 = hf$:

$$f(x_n + ah, y_n + bk_1) = f + ah f_x + bh f f_y + O(h^2)$$

$$k_2 = hf + ah^2 f_x + bh^2 f f_y + O(h^3)$$

**Substitute into update equation:**

$$y_{n+1} = y_n + c_0(hf) + c_1(hf + ah^2 f_x + bh^2 f f_y) + O(h^3)$$

$$y_{n+1} = y_n + (c_0 + c_1)hf + c_1 a h^2 f_x + c_1 b h^2 f f_y + O(h^3) \quad \cdots (I)$$

**Taylor expansion of exact solution:**

$$y(x_n + h) = y_n + hf + \frac{h^2}{2}(f_x + f f_y) + O(h^3) \quad \cdots (II)$$

**Comparing (I) and (II):**

- $c_0 + c_1 = 1$
- $c_1 a = \frac{1}{2}$
- $c_1 b = \frac{1}{2}$

**Three standard variants:**

| Variant | $a$ | $b$ | $c_0$ | $c_1$ |
|---------|-----|-----|-------|-------|
| Heun's | $1$ | $1$ | $1/2$ | $1/2$ |
| Midpoint | $1/2$ | $1/2$ | $0$ | $1$ |
| Ralston's | $3/4$ | $3/4$ | $1/3$ | $2/3$ |

**Part (b) ŌĆö Numerical Solution [7 marks]:**

Given: $f(x, y) = xy + 1$, $x_0 = 0$, $y_0 = 1$, $h = 0.2$.

**Heun's formula:**
$$k_1 = h f(x_n, y_n), \quad k_2 = h f(x_n + h, y_n + k_1)$$
$$y_{n+1} = y_n + \frac{1}{2}(k_1 + k_2)$$

**Iteration 1:** $(x_0, y_0) = (0, 1)$

$$k_1 = 0.2 \cdot f(0, 1) = 0.2 \cdot (0 \cdot 1 + 1) = 0.2 \cdot 1 = 0.20$$

$$k_2 = 0.2 \cdot f(0 + 0.2, 1 + 0.20) = 0.2 \cdot f(0.2, 1.20)$$

$$k_2 = 0.2 \cdot (0.2 \times 1.20 + 1) = 0.2 \cdot (0.24 + 1) = 0.2 \cdot 1.24 = 0.248$$

$$y_1 = 1 + \frac{1}{2}(0.20 + 0.248) = 1 + 0.224 = 1.224$$

**Iteration 2:** $(x_1, y_1) = (0.2, 1.224)$

$$k_1 = 0.2 \cdot f(0.2, 1.224) = 0.2 \cdot (0.2 \times 1.224 + 1)$$

$$k_1 = 0.2 \cdot (0.2448 + 1) = 0.2 \cdot 1.2448 = 0.24896$$

$$k_2 = 0.2 \cdot f(0.4, 1.224 + 0.24896) = 0.2 \cdot f(0.4, 1.47296)$$

$$k_2 = 0.2 \cdot (0.4 \times 1.47296 + 1) = 0.2 \cdot (0.58918 + 1)$$

$$k_2 = 0.2 \cdot 1.58918 = 0.31784$$

$$y_2 = 1.224 + \frac{1}{2}(0.24896 + 0.31784) = 1.224 + 0.28340 = 1.5074$$

$$\boxed{y(0.4) \approx 1.51}$$

**Verification with exact solution:** $y_{exact}(0.4) = 1.5033$ (close to 1.5074 ŌĆö error is acceptable for $h = 0.2$).

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Actual-style QP]`

**(a)** [7 marks] **Using the Midpoint method (RK2 variant), solve $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = x^2 + y^2$ with $y(0) = 0$ to find $y(0.4)$ taking $h = 0.2$.**

**(b)** [7 marks] **Solve the same problem using Ralston's method and compare the results with the Midpoint method.**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**Part (a) ŌĆö Midpoint Method [7 marks]:**

Given: $f(x, y) = x^2 + y^2$, $x_0 = 0$, $y_0 = 0$, $h = 0.2$.

**Midpoint formulas:**
$$k_1 = h f(x_n, y_n)$$
$$k_2 = h f\left(x_n + \frac{h}{2}, y_n + \frac{k_1}{2}\right)$$
$$y_{n+1} = y_n + k_2$$

**Iteration 1:** $(x_0, y_0) = (0, 0)$

$$k_1 = 0.2 \cdot f(0, 0) = 0.2 \cdot (0 + 0) = 0$$

$$k_2 = 0.2 \cdot f\left(0 + 0.1, 0 + 0\right) = 0.2 \cdot f(0.1, 0)$$

$$k_2 = 0.2 \cdot (0.01 + 0) = 0.002$$

$$y_1 = 0 + 0.002 = 0.002$$

**Iteration 2:** $(x_1, y_1) = (0.2, 0.002)$

$$k_1 = 0.2 \cdot f(0.2, 0.002) = 0.2 \cdot (0.04 + 0.000004)$$

$$k_1 = 0.2 \cdot 0.040004 = 0.0080008$$

$$k_2 = 0.2 \cdot f\left(0.2 + 0.1, 0.002 + \frac{0.0080008}{2}\right)$$

$$k_2 = 0.2 \cdot f(0.3, 0.0060004)$$

$$k_2 = 0.2 \cdot (0.09 + 0.0000360) = 0.2 \cdot 0.0900360 = 0.0180072$$

$$y_2 = 0.002 + 0.0180072 = 0.0200$$

$$\boxed{y(0.4) \approx 0.0200 \text{ (Midpoint)}}$$

**Part (b) ŌĆö Ralston's Method [7 marks]:**

**Ralston's formulas:**
$$k_1 = h f(x_n, y_n)$$
$$k_2 = h f\left(x_n + \frac{3h}{4}, y_n + \frac{3k_1}{4}\right)$$
$$y_{n+1} = y_n + \frac{1}{3} k_1 + \frac{2}{3} k_2$$

**Iteration 1:** $(x_0, y_0) = (0, 0)$

$$k_1 = 0.2 \cdot f(0, 0) = 0$$

$$k_2 = 0.2 \cdot f\left(0 + 0.15, 0 + 0\right) = 0.2 \cdot f(0.15, 0)$$

$$k_2 = 0.2 \cdot (0.0225 + 0) = 0.0045$$

$$y_1 = 0 + \frac{1}{3}(0) + \frac{2}{3}(0.0045) = 0.003$$

**Iteration 2:** $(x_1, y_1) = (0.2, 0.003)$

$$k_1 = 0.2 \cdot f(0.2, 0.003) = 0.2 \cdot (0.04 + 0.000009)$$

$$k_1 = 0.2 \cdot 0.040009 = 0.0080018$$

$$k_2 = 0.2 \cdot f\left(0.2 + 0.15, 0.003 + \frac{3 \times 0.0080018}{4}\right)$$

$$k_2 = 0.2 \cdot f(0.35, 0.003 + 0.0060014)$$

$$k_2 = 0.2 \cdot f(0.35, 0.0090014)$$

$$k_2 = 0.2 \cdot (0.1225 + 0.0000810) = 0.2 \cdot 0.122581 = 0.0245162$$

$$y_2 = 0.003 + \frac{1}{3}(0.0080018) + \frac{2}{3}(0.0245162)$$

$$y_2 = 0.003 + 0.0026673 + 0.0163441 = 0.0220$$

$$\boxed{y(0.4) \approx 0.0220 \text{ (Ralston's)}}$$

**Comparison:**

| Method | $y(0.4)$ | Difference from Exact (0.0212) |
|--------|----------|-------------------------------|
| Midpoint | $0.0200$ | $-0.0012$ |
| Ralston's | $0.0220$ | $+0.0008$ |

Both methods have $O(h^2)$ global error, but Ralston's is slightly closer to the exact value in this case due to its minimized truncation error coefficient.

---

## 5.3 ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes (Exam Pitfalls)**
>
> 1. **Confusing variants**: Students often mix up Heun's and Midpoint coefficients. Heun uses **end-point** $(x_n + h, y_n + k_1)$ with averaging $c_0 = c_1 = 1/2$. Midpoint uses **half-point** $(x_n + h/2, y_n + k_1/2)$ with $c_0 = 0, c_1 = 1$. **Memorize both clearly.**
>
> 2. **Forgetting to use the predicted $y$ in $k_2$**: In Heun's method, $k_2$ must use $y_n + k_1$ (the Euler-predicted value), NOT $y_n$. This is the single most common error ŌĆö **costs 3-4 marks**.
>
> 3. **Order of operations**: Compute $k_1$ first, then use it to compute $k_2$. Do NOT compute them in reverse.
>
> 4. **Arithmetic in $f(x_n + ah, y_n + bk_1)$**: When plugging in the look-ahead point, students often mistakenly use $k_1$ instead of $b \cdot k_1$, or forget to multiply by $h$ in $k_2$.
>
> 5. **In derivation questions**: Students forget the Taylor expansion of $f$ in **two variables**. You must show: $f(x + \Delta x, y + \Delta y) = f + \Delta x \cdot f_x + \Delta y \cdot f_y + \cdots$
>
> 6. **Rounding too early**: Keep 5-6 decimal places during intermediate steps, only round the **final answer** to required precision.

---

## 5.4 ­¤ōŗ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö RK2 Method**

- Ō£ģ **Purpose**: Solve 1st-order IVP $\frac{dy}{dx} = f(x,y), \ y(x_0) = y_0$ with $O(h^2)$ global accuracy.
- Ō£ģ **General form**: $k_1 = h f(x_n, y_n)$, $k_2 = h f(x_n + ah, y_n + bk_1)$, $y_{n+1} = y_n + c_0 k_1 + c_1 k_2$.
- Ō£ģ **Order conditions** (MUST memorize): $c_0 + c_1 = 1$, $c_1 a = 1/2$, $c_1 b = 1/2$.
- Ō£ģ **Heun's Method** (Predictor-Corrector): $a = b = 1$, $c_0 = c_1 = 1/2$. Update: $y_{n+1} = y_n + \frac{1}{2}(k_1 + k_2)$.
- Ō£ģ **Midpoint Method**: $a = b = 1/2$, $c_0 = 0$, $c_1 = 1$. Update: $y_{n+1} = y_n + k_2$.
- Ō£ģ **Ralston's Method** (Optimal): $a = b = 3/4$, $c_0 = 1/3$, $c_1 = 2/3$. Update: $y_{n+1} = y_n + \frac{1}{3}k_1 + \frac{2}{3}k_2$.
- Ō£ģ **Error order**: Local $O(h^3)$, Global $O(h^2)$.
- Ō£ģ **Function evaluations per step**: **2** (one for $k_1$, one for $k_2$) ŌĆö twice the cost of Euler's method, but quadratically more accurate.
- Ō£ģ **Derivation technique**: Expand $k_2$ in 2-variable Taylor series; match coefficients with Taylor expansion of $y(x_n + h)$ up to $h^2$ terms.
- Ō£ģ **Exam focus**: Expect a 7-mark derivation + 7-mark numerical problem combination. Always show both iterations and the step-by-step substitution.
- Ō£ģ **Common confusion**: RK2 ŌēĀ RK4. RK4 has 4 function evaluations and $O(h^4)$ global error ŌĆö studied in the next section.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Numerical Solution of Laplace Equation - JacobiŌĆÖs Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GCMAT401 - MATHEMATICS FOR PHYSICAL SCIENCE AND LIFE SCIENCE ŌĆō 4
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Numerical Solution of Laplace Equation - JacobiŌĆÖs Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Laplace Equation** in two-dimensional Cartesian coordinates is a second-order linear Partial Differential Equation (PDE) of elliptic type, written as:

$$\frac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial x^{2}} + \frac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial y^{2}} = 0$$

It is the homogeneous form of the more general **Poisson Equation** and governs numerous steady-state physical phenomena. The function $u(x,y)$ is sought over a bounded 2D region $\Omega$ subject to prescribed values on the boundary $\partial \Omega$ (a **Dirichlet boundary condition** problem).

**Jacobi's Method** is a classical **iterative numerical technique** used to solve the discretized form of the Laplace equation. It converts the continuous PDE into a system of linear algebraic equations via finite differences, then iteratively refines an initial guess of the solution until the values at all interior mesh points converge to a specified tolerance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In Module 4, students are required to (a) derive the standard 5-point finite difference stencil for the 2D Laplace equation, (b) write down the Jacobi iteration formula, and (c) perform at least 3ŌĆō4 iterations by hand on a square mesh (typically with $h=1$).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Heat Plate" Intuition

Imagine a thin, flat metal plate. You clamp its four edges at fixed temperatures (some edges hot, some cold). After a long time, the temperature inside the plate stops changing ŌĆö it has reached a **steady state**. The temperature at any interior point is a weighted average of the temperatures of its four immediate neighbors. This physical reality is precisely what the Laplace equation encodes, and **Jacobi's Method** is the recipe for *simulating* this averaging process on a discrete grid.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory aid:** *"Jacobi = Just Average your four Cardinal neighbors Iteratively Until balanced."* The word "Jacobi" contains "Ja-Co-Bi" ŌĆö reminiscent of "Just Compute By Itself" ŌĆö emphasizing that each point's new value depends only on values from the *previous* iteration (not on freshly updated values within the same iteration).

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Meaning | Standard Value/Range |
|---|---|---|
| $h$ | Grid spacing in $x$ and $y$ | Typically **$h = 1$** in KTU problems |
| $\varepsilon$ | Convergence tolerance | Commonly **$\varepsilon = 0.001$** or **$0.0001$** |
| $N$ | Number of interior nodes per side | Usually **3, 4, or 5** in exam problems |
| $\alpha$ | Convergence acceleration factor | $\alpha = 1$ (standard Jacobi) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Theoretical Breakdown

**Step 1 ŌĆö Discretization of the Domain**
Subdivide the square region $[0,a] \times [0,b]$ into an $(N+1) \times (M+1)$ grid of nodes with uniform spacing $h$. The mesh points are $(x_i, y_j)$ where $x_i = ih$, $y_j = jh$, and $u_{i,j} \approx u(x_i, y_j)$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Finite Difference Approximation**
Apply the **central difference** formulas for the second derivatives:

$$\frac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial x^{2}} \bigg|_{i,j} \approx \frac{u_{i+1,j} - 2u_{i,j} + u_{i-1,j}}{h^{2}}$$

$$\frac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial y^{2}} \bigg|_{i,j} \approx \frac{u_{i,j+1} - 2u_{i,j} + u_{i,j-1}}{h^{2}}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Substitute into the Laplace Equation**
Combining the two approximations:

$$\frac{u_{i+1,j} - 2u_{i,j} + u_{i-1,j}}{h^{2}} + \frac{u_{i,j+1} - 2u_{i,j} + u_{i,j-1}}{h^{2}} = 0$$

Multiplying by $h^{2}$ and simplifying (the "magic cancellation" since $h^2$ is common):

$$u_{i+1,j} + u_{i-1,j} + u_{i,j+1} + u_{i,j-1} - 4u_{i,j} = 0$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö The "Magic" Five-Point Formula**
Rearranging for $u_{i,j}$:

$$u_{i,j} = \frac{1}{4}\left( u_{i+1,j} + u_{i-1,j} + u_{i,j+1} + u_{i,j-1} \right)$$

> **The Why:** This is the famous **Five-Point Star Stencil** ŌĆö the value at any interior point is the *simple average* of its four cardinal neighbors. The factor of 4 arises from the symmetry of the Laplacian and the uniform grid spacing.

**Step 5 ŌĆö Iterative Update (Jacobi's Rule)**
In practice, the equation is a *coupled linear system*. Jacobi solves it iteratively:

$$u_{i,j}^{(k+1)} = \frac{1}{4}\left( u_{i+1,j}^{(k)} + u_{i-1,j}^{(k)} + u_{i,j+1}^{(k)} + u_{i,j-1}^{(k)} \right)$$

- The superscript $(k)$ denotes the iteration number.
- **All values on the right-hand side come from iteration $k$ (the old iteration).**
- This is the **defining feature of Jacobi**: it uses only "old" values.

**Step 6 ŌĆö Convergence Check**
Stop when the maximum absolute change between successive iterations falls below a tolerance $\varepsilon$:

$$\max_{i,j}\left| u_{i,j}^{(k+1)} - u_{i,j}^{(k)} \right| < \varepsilon$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The "How" behind the averaging:** Physically, each Jacobi iteration represents one "round" of heat redistribution across the plate. Repeating the rounds lets the thermal influence of the boundaries propagate inward until equilibrium is reached.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Continuous Laplace Equation (2D) | $\dfrac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial x^{2}} + \dfrac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial y^{2}} = 0$ | Problem statement |
| 2 | 2nd-order central difference in $x$ | $\dfrac{u_{i+1,j} - 2u_{i,j} + u_{i-1,j}}{h^{2}}$ | Discretization derivation |
| 3 | 2nd-order central difference in $y$ | $\dfrac{u_{i,j+1} - 2u_{i,j} + u_{i,j-1}}{h^{2}}$ | Discretization derivation |
| 4 | Discretized Laplace (5-point stencil) | $u_{i,j} = \dfrac{1}{4}\left(u_{i+1,j} + u_{i-1,j} + u_{i,j+1} + u_{i,j-1}\right)$ | Core working formula |
| 5 | **Jacobi Iteration Rule** | $u_{i,j}^{(k+1)} = \dfrac{1}{4}\left(u_{i+1,j}^{(k)} + u_{i-1,j}^{(k)} + u_{i,j+1}^{(k)} + u_{i,j-1}^{(k)}\right)$ | The MAIN update formula |
| 6 | Convergence criterion | $\max\limits_{i,j}\left\vert u_{i,j}^{(k+1)} - u_{i,j}^{(k)} \right\vert < \varepsilon$ | Stopping rule |
| 7 | Initial guess convention | $u_{i,j}^{(0)} = 0$ (or boundary average) | Starting iteration |
| 8 | Boundary node exemption | Boundary values are **never updated** | Reminder |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Steady-state heat conduction** in plates, fins, and reactor walls.
- **Electrostatic potential** distribution in 2D capacitor geometries.
- **Torsion of prismatic bars** in mechanical engineering.
- **Incompressible fluid flow** (stream function formulation: $\nabla^{2}\psi = 0$).
- **Image processing** for edge-preserving smoothing (discrete Laplacian filter).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Five-Point Formula

**Starting Point:** The 2D Laplace equation in a square region $0 \le x \le a$, $0 \le y \le a$:

$$\frac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial x^{2}} + \frac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial y^{2}} = 0$$

**Step A ŌĆö Taylor Series Expansion of $u$ in the $x$-direction.**
Expand $u(x+h, y)$ and $u(x-h, y)$ about $(x,y)$:

$$u(x+h, y) = u(x,y) + h \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{h^{2}}{2!}\frac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial x^{2}} + \frac{h^{3}}{3!}\frac{\partial^{3} u}{\partial x^{3}} + \frac{h^{4}}{4!}\frac{\partial^{4} u}{\partial x^{4}} + \cdots$$

$$u(x-h, y) = u(x,y) - h \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{h^{2}}{2!}\frac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial x^{2}} - \frac{h^{3}}{3!}\frac{\partial^{3} u}{\partial x^{3}} + \frac{h^{4}}{4!}\frac{\partial^{4} u}{\partial x^{4}} - \cdots$$

**Step B ŌĆö Add the Two Expansions.**
Odd-derivative terms cancel exactly:

$$u(x+h,y) + u(x-h,y) = 2u(x,y) + h^{2}\frac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial x^{2}} + \frac{h^{4}}{12}\frac{\partial^{4} u}{\partial x^{4}} + \cdots$$

**Step C ŌĆö Solve for $\partial^{2} u / \partial x^{2}$ (dropping $O(h^{4})$ truncation error).**

$$\frac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial x^{2}} \approx \frac{u(x+h,y) - 2u(x,y) + u(x-h,y)}{h^{2}}$$

In discrete notation: $\dfrac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial x^{2}}\bigg|_{i,j} \approx \dfrac{u_{i+1,j} - 2u_{i,j} + u_{i-1,j}}{h^{2}}$

**Step D ŌĆö Repeat the Argument in the $y$-direction (identical logic).**

$$\frac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial y^{2}} \bigg|_{i,j} \approx \frac{u_{i,j+1} - 2u_{i,j} + u_{i,j-1}}{h^{2}}$$

**Step E ŌĆö Substitute Both Approximations into the Laplace Equation.**

$$\frac{u_{i+1,j} - 2u_{i,j} + u_{i-1,j}}{h^{2}} + \frac{u_{i,j+1} - 2u_{i,j} + u_{i,j-1}}{h^{2}} = 0$$

**Step F ŌĆö Multiply Throughout by $h^{2}$ (note: $h^{2}$ is a common factor on both sides).**

$$u_{i+1,j} - 2u_{i,j} + u_{i-1,j} + u_{i,j+1} - 2u_{i,j} + u_{i,j-1} = 0$$

**Step G ŌĆö Collect Like Terms and Rearrange.**

$$u_{i+1,j} + u_{i-1,j} + u_{i,j+1} + u_{i,j-1} - 4u_{i,j} = 0$$

**Step H ŌĆö Solve for $u_{i,j}$:**

$$\boxed{\,u_{i,j} = \frac{1}{4}\left( u_{i+1,j} + u_{i-1,j} + u_{i,j+1} + u_{i,j-1} \right)\,}$$

This derivation is the most frequently tested 7-to-10 mark question in KTU ESE papers for this module.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example ŌĆö Full Analytical Walkthrough

**Problem Statement:**
Solve $u_{xx} + u_{yy} = 0$ on the square $0 \le x \le 4$, $0 \le y \le 4$ with $h = 1$ and boundary conditions:

| Boundary | Value |
|---|---|
| $u(0, y)$ for $0 \le y \le 4$ | $0$ |
| $u(4, y)$ for $0 \le y \le 4$ | $y(4-y)$ |
| $u(x, 0)$ for $0 \le x \le 4$ | $0$ |
| $u(x, 4)$ for $0 \le x \le 4$ | $0$ |

Evaluate $y(4-y)$ at grid points $y = 1, 2, 3$: gives $3, 4, 3$ respectively.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Label the Interior Points.**
With $h=1$ on $[0,4] \times [0,4]$, there are $5 \times 5 = 25$ nodes. The boundary contributes $16$ nodes, leaving $9$ interior nodes labelled $p_1$ through $p_9$:

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
y=4 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\
\hline
y=3 & 0 & p_3 & p_6 & p_9 & 3 \\
\hline
y=2 & 0 & p_2 & p_5 & p_8 & 4 \\
\hline
y=1 & 0 & p_1 & p_4 & p_7 & 3 \\
\hline
y=0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\
\hline
 & x=0 & x=1 & x=2 & x=3 & x=4 \\
\hline
\end{array}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Write the Iteration Formulas (All RHS values from iteration $k$).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_1^{(k+1)} &= \tfrac{1}{4}\left(0 + p_2^{(k)} + 0 + p_4^{(k)}\right) \\[2pt]
p_2^{(k+1)} &= \tfrac{1}{4}\left(0 + p_5^{(k)} + p_1^{(k)} + p_3^{(k)}\right) \\[2pt]
p_3^{(k+1)} &= \tfrac{1}{4}\left(0 + p_6^{(k)} + p_2^{(k)} + 0\right) \\[2pt]
p_4^{(k+1)} &= \tfrac{1}{4}\left(p_1^{(k)} + p_7^{(k)} + 0 + p_5^{(k)}\right) \\[2pt]
p_5^{(k+1)} &= \tfrac{1}{4}\left(p_2^{(k)} + p_8^{(k)} + p_4^{(k)} + p_6^{(k)}\right) \\[2pt]
p_6^{(k+1)} &= \tfrac{1}{4}\left(p_3^{(k)} + p_9^{(k)} + p_5^{(k)} + 0\right) \\[2pt]
p_7^{(k+1)} &= \tfrac{1}{4}\left(p_4^{(k)} + 3 + 0 + p_8^{(k)}\right) \\[2pt]
p_8^{(k+1)} &= \tfrac{1}{4}\left(p_5^{(k)} + 4 + p_7^{(k)} + p_9^{(k)}\right) \\[2pt]
p_9^{(k+1)} &= \tfrac{1}{4}\left(p_6^{(k)} + 3 + p_8^{(k)} + 0\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Set Initial Guess $k=0$: All $p_i^{(0)} = 0$.**

**Step 4 ŌĆö Iteration $k=1$ (Compute using all-zero $k=0$ values).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_1^{(1)} &= \tfrac{1}{4}(0 + 0 + 0 + 0) = 0.0000 \\[2pt]
p_2^{(1)} &= \tfrac{1}{4}(0 + 0 + 0 + 0) = 0.0000 \\[2pt]
p_3^{(1)} &= \tfrac{1}{4}(0 + 0 + 0 + 0) = 0.0000 \\[2pt]
p_4^{(1)} &= \tfrac{1}{4}(0 + 0 + 0 + 0) = 0.0000 \\[2pt]
p_5^{(1)} &= \tfrac{1}{4}(0 + 0 + 0 + 0) = 0.0000 \\[2pt]
p_6^{(1)} &= \tfrac{1}{4}(0 + 0 + 0 + 0) = 0.0000 \\[2pt]
p_7^{(1)} &= \tfrac{1}{4}(0 + 3 + 0 + 0) = 0.7500 \\[2pt]
p_8^{(1)} &= \tfrac{1}{4}(0 + 4 + 0 + 0) = 1.0000 \\[2pt]
p_9^{(1)} &= \tfrac{1}{4}(0 + 3 + 0 + 0) = 0.7500
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Iteration $k=2$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_1^{(2)} &= \tfrac{1}{4}(0 + 0.0000 + 0 + 0.0000) = 0.0000 \\[2pt]
p_2^{(2)} &= \tfrac{1}{4}(0 + 0.0000 + 0.0000 + 0.0000) = 0.0000 \\[2pt]
p_3^{(2)} &= \tfrac{1}{4}(0 + 0.0000 + 0.0000 + 0) = 0.0000 \\[2pt]
p_4^{(2)} &= \tfrac{1}{4}(0.0000 + 0.7500 + 0 + 0.0000) = 0.1875 \\[2pt]
p_5^{(2)} &= \tfrac{1}{4}(0.0000 + 1.0000 + 0.0000 + 0.0000) = 0.2500 \\[2pt]
p_6^{(2)} &= \tfrac{1}{4}(0.0000 + 0.7500 + 0.0000 + 0) = 0.1875 \\[2pt]
p_7^{(2)} &= \tfrac{1}{4}(0.0000 + 3 + 0 + 1.0000) = 1.0000 \\[2pt]
p_8^{(2)} &= \tfrac{1}{4}(0.0000 + 4 + 0.7500 + 0.7500) = 1.3750 \\[2pt]
p_9^{(2)} &= \tfrac{1}{4}(0.0000 + 3 + 1.0000 + 0) = 1.0000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Iteration $k=3$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_1^{(3)} &= \tfrac{1}{4}(0 + 0.0000 + 0 + 0.1875) = 0.0469 \\[2pt]
p_2^{(3)} &= \tfrac{1}{4}(0 + 0.2500 + 0.0000 + 0.0000) = 0.0625 \\[2pt]
p_3^{(3)} &= \tfrac{1}{4}(0 + 0.1875 + 0.0000 + 0) = 0.0469 \\[2pt]
p_4^{(3)} &= \tfrac{1}{4}(0.0000 + 1.0000 + 0 + 0.2500) = 0.3125 \\[2pt]
p_5^{(3)} &= \tfrac{1}{4}(0.0000 + 1.3750 + 0.1875 + 0.1875) = 0.4375 \\[2pt]
p_6^{(3)} &= \tfrac{1}{4}(0.0000 + 1.0000 + 0.2500 + 0) = 0.3125 \\[2pt]
p_7^{(3)} &= \tfrac{1}{4}(0.1875 + 3 + 0 + 1.3750) = 1.1406 \\[2pt]
p_8^{(3)} &= \tfrac{1}{4}(0.2500 + 4 + 1.0000 + 1.0000) = 1.5625 \\[2pt]
p_9^{(3)} &= \tfrac{1}{4}(0.1875 + 3 + 1.3750 + 0) = 1.1406
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Iteration $k=4$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_1^{(4)} &= \tfrac{1}{4}(0 + 0.0625 + 0 + 0.3125) = 0.0938 \\[2pt]
p_2^{(4)} &= \tfrac{1}{4}(0 + 0.4375 + 0.0469 + 0.0469) = 0.1328 \\[2pt]
p_3^{(4)} &= \tfrac{1}{4}(0 + 0.3125 + 0.0625 + 0) = 0.0938 \\[2pt]
p_4^{(4)} &= \tfrac{1}{4}(0.0469 + 1.1406 + 0 + 0.4375) = 0.4062 \\[2pt]
p_5^{(4)} &= \tfrac{1}{4}(0.0625 + 1.5625 + 0.3125 + 0.3125) = 0.5625 \\[2pt]
p_6^{(4)} &= \tfrac{1}{4}(0.0469 + 1.1406 + 0.4375 + 0) = 0.4062 \\[2pt]
p_7^{(4)} &= \tfrac{1}{4}(0.3125 + 3 + 0 + 1.5625) = 1.2188 \\[2pt]
p_8^{(4)} &= \tfrac{1}{4}(0.4375 + 4 + 1.1406 + 1.1406) = 1.6797 \\[2pt]
p_9^{(4)} &= \tfrac{1}{4}(0.3125 + 3 + 1.5625 + 0) = 1.2188
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8 ŌĆö Compact Iteration Table (KTU Valuation Style):**

| Iter $k$ | $p_1$ | $p_2$ | $p_3$ | $p_4$ | $p_5$ | $p_6$ | $p_7$ | $p_8$ | $p_9$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **0** | 0.0000 | 0.0000 | 0.0000 | 0.0000 | 0.0000 | 0.0000 | 0.0000 | 0.0000 | 0.0000 |
| **1** | 0.0000 | 0.0000 | 0.0000 | 0.0000 | 0.0000 | 0.0000 | 0.7500 | 1.0000 | 0.7500 |
| **2** | 0.0000 | 0.0000 | 0.0000 | 0.1875 | 0.2500 | 0.1875 | 1.0000 | 1.3750 | 1.0000 |
| **3** | 0.0469 | 0.0625 | 0.0469 | 0.3125 | 0.4375 | 0.3125 | 1.1406 | 1.5625 | 1.1406 |
| **4** | 0.0938 | 0.1328 | 0.0938 | 0.4062 | 0.5625 | 0.4062 | 1.2188 | 1.6797 | 1.2188 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observations from the table:**
> - $p_1 = p_3$ and $p_4 = p_6$ and $p_7 = p_9$ in every iteration ŌĆö this is a **symmetry consequence** of the boundary conditions (left and right boundaries are mirror images up to a sign).
> - The values are **monotonically increasing** because all boundary values are $\ge 0$.
> - The values are **converging slowly** ŌĆö Jacobi is known to have a slow convergence rate, especially near the center.

**Step 9 ŌĆö Convergence Check (typical KTU requirement).**
Compute the maximum change between $k=3$ and $k=4$:

$$\Delta_{\max} = \max_{i,j} \left\vert p_i^{(4)} - p_i^{(3)} \right\vert = \vert 1.6797 - 1.5625 \vert = 0.1172$$

If $\varepsilon = 0.1$, then $\Delta_{\max} > \varepsilon$, so we must continue. If $\varepsilon = 0.01$, we definitely need many more iterations.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Five-Point Star Stencil (Mermaid Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    N["UP: u(i, j+1)"]
    S["DOWN: u(i, j-1)"]
    E["RIGHT: u(i+1, j)"]
    W["LEFT: u(i-1, j)"]
    C["CENTER: u(i, j) = NEW VALUE"]
    R["RULE: u(i,j) = (UP + DOWN + LEFT + RIGHT) / 4"]
    C --> R
    N --> R
    S --> R
    E --> R
    W --> R
    R --> O["OUTPUT: New u(i,j) at iteration k+1"]
```

**Reading the Stencil:** The center node is the one being updated. The four cardinal neighbors (Up, Down, Left, Right) come from the **previous** iteration $k$. This "no-self-reference" property is the defining feature of Jacobi's Method.

### 4.2 Jacobi Iteration Pipeline (Algorithm Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START: Read boundary values into grid U"]
    B["Set step size h (default h = 1)"]
    C["Initialize all interior points: U[i,j] = 0"]
    D["Set tolerance eps (e.g., 1e-4) and max iterations Kmax"]
    E["k = 0"]
    F["k = k + 1"]
    G["For every interior point i,j: compute U_new[i,j] = 0.25 * (U[i+1,j] + U[i-1,j] + U[i,j+1] + U[i,j-1]) using OLD U values"]
    H["Compute max |U_new - U| over all interior points"]
    I{"max change less than eps?"}
    J["Replace U with U_new (copy operation)"]
    K["OUTPUT: Converged solution U[i,j]"]
    L["OUTPUT: Divergence / Did not converge in Kmax iterations"]

    A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F --> G --> H --> I
    I -- "NO" --> J --> F
    I -- "YES" --> K
    F -- "k > Kmax" --> L
```

### 4.3 Grid Layout (Mimicking a 5x5 Mesh)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["u00 = 0 (BC)"] --- A2["u10 = 0 (BC)"] --- A3["u20 = 0 (BC)"] --- A4["u30 = 0 (BC)"] --- A5["u40 = 0 (BC)"]
    B1["u01 = 0 (BC)"] --- B2["u11 = p1 INTERIOR"] --- B3["u21 = p4 INTERIOR"] --- B4["u31 = p7 INTERIOR"] --- B5["u41 = 3 (BC)"]
    C1["u02 = 0 (BC)"] --- C2["u12 = p2 INTERIOR"] --- C3["u22 = p5 INTERIOR"] --- C4["u32 = p8 INTERIOR"] --- C5["u42 = 4 (BC)"]
    D1["u03 = 0 (BC)"] --- D2["u13 = p3 INTERIOR"] --- D3["u23 = p6 INTERIOR"] --- D4["u33 = p9 INTERIOR"] --- D5["u43 = 3 (BC)"]
    E1["u04 = 0 (BC)"] --- E2["u14 = 0 (BC)"] --- E3["u24 = 0 (BC)"] --- E4["u34 = 0 (BC)"] --- E5["u44 = 0 (BC)"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Node Identifier Note:** The above Mermaid block uses strictly alphanumeric node IDs (`A1`, `A2`, ... `B1`, ..., `E5`) to comply with the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 Mermaid compilation safeguards. Reserved words like `end`, `subgraph`, and `style` are never used as node IDs.

### 4.4 Jacobi vs Gauss-Seidel ŌĆö Quick Comparison Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph JACOBI
        J1["Uses ONLY old values from iteration k"]
        J2["Parallelizable across nodes"]
        J3["Slower convergence"]
        J1 --> J2 --> J3
    end
    subgraph GAUSS_SEIDEL
        G1["Uses UPDATED values as soon as they are available"]
        G2["Sequential node-by-node update"]
        G3["Faster convergence"]
        G1 --> G2 --> G3
    end
```
