## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Model QP]**
*State the standard five-point finite difference approximation for the 2D Laplace equation $u_{xx} + u_{yy} = 0$ on a uniform grid with spacing $h$.*

**Model Answer:**
Using the central difference approximation for the second derivatives, we obtain the five-point stencil:

$$u_{i,j} = \frac{1}{4}\left( u_{i+1,j} + u_{i-1,j} + u_{i,j+1} + u_{i,j-1} \right)$$

This states that the value at any interior mesh point is the arithmetic mean of the four adjacent (left, right, up, down) neighbors. **[3 marks: 1 for stating the formula, 1 for naming "central difference", 1 for explaining the averaging interpretation]**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024, Model QP]**
*What is the role of the initial guess in Jacobi's method? What is the standard choice and why?*

**Model Answer:**
The initial guess $u_{i,j}^{(0)}$ is the starting vector of values at all interior points. The standard choice is **$u_{i,j}^{(0)} = 0$** (or the average of the four surrounding boundary values). This choice is preferred because: (i) it requires no prior knowledge of the solution, (ii) it is the "neutral" starting point that does not bias the iteration, and (iii) for the Laplace equation with non-negative boundary data, it provides a lower bound on the true solution, ensuring monotonic convergence. **[3 marks: 1 for stating initial guess purpose, 1 for standard choice, 1 for reasoning]**

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) ŌĆö Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö Full Derivation + Iteration**

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2022 Style, Adapted for 2024 Scheme]**

*(a)* Derive the standard five-point finite difference approximation for the two-dimensional Laplace equation $u_{xx} + u_{yy} = 0$ on a uniform square mesh of spacing $h$. *(**7 marks**)*

*(b)* Consider the Laplace equation $u_{xx} + u_{yy} = 0$ on the square $0 \le x \le 3$, $0 \le y \le 3$ with $h = 1$. The boundary values are:

| Boundary | Value |
|---|---|
| $u(0, y)$ | $0$ |
| $u(3, y)$ | $0$ |
| $u(x, 0)$ | $0$ |
| $u(x, 3)$ | $100$ |

Apply **Jacobi's method** starting with the initial guess $u_{i,j}^{(0)} = 0$ and compute the first **three iterations**. *(**7 marks**)*

---

**Model Solution to Q-A (a):**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Write the Taylor expansion of $u$ in the $x$-direction at the point $(x_i, y_j)$:**

$$u_{i+1,j} = u_{i,j} + h \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{h^{2}}{2}\frac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial x^{2}} + \frac{h^{3}}{6}\frac{\partial^{3} u}{\partial x^{3}} + O(h^{4})$$

$$u_{i-1,j} = u_{i,j} - h \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{h^{2}}{2}\frac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial x^{2}} - \frac{h^{3}}{6}\frac{\partial^{3} u}{\partial x^{3}} + O(h^{4})$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Add to eliminate odd-derivative terms:**

$$u_{i+1,j} + u_{i-1,j} = 2u_{i,j} + h^{2}\frac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial x^{2}} + O(h^{4})$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Solve for $\partial^{2} u / \partial x^{2}$:**

$$\frac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial x^{2}} \approx \frac{u_{i+1,j} - 2u_{i,j} + u_{i-1,j}}{h^{2}}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö By identical reasoning in the $y$-direction:**

$$\frac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial y^{2}} \approx \frac{u_{i,j+1} - 2u_{i,j} + u_{i,j-1}}{h^{2}}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Substitute into the Laplace equation:**

$$\frac{u_{i+1,j} - 2u_{i,j} + u_{i-1,j}}{h^{2}} + \frac{u_{i,j+1} - 2u_{i,j} + u_{i,j-1}}{h^{2}} = 0$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Multiply by $h^{2}$ and collect terms:**

$$u_{i+1,j} + u_{i-1,j} + u_{i,j+1} + u_{i,j-1} - 4u_{i,j} = 0$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Rearrange (this is the five-point stencil):**

$$\boxed{\,u_{i,j} = \frac{1}{4}\left( u_{i+1,j} + u_{i-1,j} + u_{i,j+1} + u_{i,j-1} \right)\,} \quad \text{[7 marks]}$$

---

**Model Solution to Q-A (b):**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Identify the grid and label interior points.**
With $h=1$ on $[0,3]\times[0,3]$, the grid has $4 \times 4 = 16$ nodes. There is only **one** interior point $(1,1), (1,2), (2,1), (2,2)$ = a $2\times 2$ block of 4 interior points. Label them:
$p_1 = u(1,1)$, $p_2 = u(1,2)$, $p_3 = u(2,1)$, $p_4 = u(2,2)$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Tabulate boundary values:**

| Position | Value |
|---|---|
| $u(0, 1), u(0, 2)$ | $0$ |
| $u(3, 1), u(3, 2)$ | $0$ |
| $u(1, 0), u(2, 0)$ | $0$ |
| $u(1, 3), u(2, 3)$ | $100$ |

**Step 3 ŌĆö Write the four Jacobi update formulas:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_1^{(k+1)} &= \tfrac{1}{4}\left(0 + p_3^{(k)} + 0 + p_2^{(k)}\right) \\[2pt]
p_2^{(k+1)} &= \tfrac{1}{4}\left(0 + p_4^{(k)} + p_1^{(k)} + 100\right) \\[2pt]
p_3^{(k+1)} &= \tfrac{1}{4}\left(p_1^{(k)} + 0 + 0 + p_4^{(k)}\right) \\[2pt]
p_4^{(k+1)} &= \tfrac{1}{4}\left(p_2^{(k)} + 0 + p_3^{(k)} + 100\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Initial guess $k = 0$:** $p_1 = p_2 = p_3 = p_4 = 0$.

**Step 5 ŌĆö Iteration $k = 1$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_1^{(1)} &= \tfrac{1}{4}(0 + 0 + 0 + 0) = 0.00 \\[2pt]
p_2^{(1)} &= \tfrac{1}{4}(0 + 0 + 0 + 100) = 25.00 \\[2pt]
p_3^{(1)} &= \tfrac{1}{4}(0 + 0 + 0 + 0) = 0.00 \\[2pt]
p_4^{(1)} &= \tfrac{1}{4}(0 + 0 + 0 + 100) = 25.00
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Iteration $k = 2$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_1^{(2)} &= \tfrac{1}{4}(0 + 0 + 0 + 25) = 6.25 \\[2pt]
p_2^{(2)} &= \tfrac{1}{4}(0 + 25 + 0 + 100) = 31.25 \\[2pt]
p_3^{(2)} &= \tfrac{1}{4}(0 + 0 + 0 + 25) = 6.25 \\[2pt]
p_4^{(2)} &= \tfrac{1}{4}(25 + 0 + 0 + 100) = 31.25
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Iteration $k = 3$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_1^{(3)} &= \tfrac{1}{4}(0 + 6.25 + 0 + 31.25) = 9.375 \\[2pt]
p_2^{(3)} &= \tfrac{1}{4}(0 + 31.25 + 6.25 + 100) = 34.375 \\[2pt]
p_3^{(3)} &= \tfrac{1}{4}(6.25 + 0 + 0 + 31.25) = 9.375 \\[2pt]
p_4^{(3)} &= \tfrac{1}{4}(31.25 + 0 + 6.25 + 100) = 34.375
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8 ŌĆö Iteration Table:**

| Iter $k$ | $p_1$ | $p_2$ | $p_3$ | $p_4$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **0** | 0.00 | 0.00 | 0.00 | 0.00 |
| **1** | 0.00 | 25.00 | 0.00 | 25.00 |
| **2** | 6.25 | 31.25 | 6.25 | 31.25 |
| **3** | 9.375 | 34.375 | 9.375 | 34.375 |

> **Conclusion:** Values are monotonically increasing and converging toward the analytical mean of $u(1,2) \approx 50$ (the true steady-state value is approximately 50 in the center, since the only non-zero BC is the top edge at 100). The iteration would continue until $\Delta_{\max} < \varepsilon$. **[7 marks: 1 for grid setup, 2 for formulas, 3 for iterations, 1 for final conclusion]**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2023 Style, Adapted for 2024 Scheme]**

*(a)* State and derive the **Jacobi iteration formula** for solving the discretized Laplace equation. Clearly state the role of the superscript $(k)$. *(**7 marks**)*

*(b)* For the Laplace equation on the square $[0,3] \times [0,3]$ with $h=1$ and boundary conditions:
$u(0,y) = 0$, $u(3,y) = 100$, $u(x,0) = 0$, $u(x,3) = 0$,
apply **two iterations** of Jacobi's method starting from $u_{i,j}^{(0)} = 0$. Tabulate your results. *(**7 marks**)*

---

**Model Solution to Q-B (a):**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Starting Point:** The discretized form of the Laplace equation at the interior node $(i,j)$:

$$u_{i+1,j} + u_{i-1,j} + u_{i,j+1} + u_{i,j-1} - 4u_{i,j} = 0$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Rearranging for $u_{i,j}$:**

$$u_{i,j} = \frac{1}{4}\left( u_{i+1,j} + u_{i-1,j} + u_{i,j+1} + u_{i,j-1} \right)$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Recognizing that this is a *coupled* system:** Each equation involves four unknowns simultaneously. Direct inversion is feasible but cumbersome for large grids.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Iterative Solution (Jacobi's Idea):** Start with an initial guess $u_{i,j}^{(0)}$ for all interior points. The superscript $(k)$ denotes the iteration number. Update all points *simultaneously* using only values from iteration $k$:

$$u_{i,j}^{(k+1)} = \frac{1}{4}\left( u_{i+1,j}^{(k)} + u_{i-1,j}^{(k)} + u_{i,j+1}^{(k)} + u_{i,j-1}^{(k)} \right)$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Role of $(k)$:** The superscript $(k)$ indexes the iteration count. At each iteration, every interior point is updated using the *previous* iteration's values. Boundary values are *never* updated (they are fixed inputs).

**Step 6 ŌĆö Convergence:** Repeat until $\max_{i,j}\vert u_{i,j}^{(k+1)} - u_{i,j}^{(k)} \vert < \varepsilon$. The sequence $\{u_{i,j}^{(k)}\}_{k=0}^{\infty}$ converges to the exact solution of the discretized Laplace equation. **[7 marks]**

---

**Model Solution to Q-B (b):**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Grid Setup.** With $h = 1$ on $[0,3]\times[0,3]$, there are 4 interior points: $p_1 = u(1,1)$, $p_2 = u(1,2)$, $p_3 = u(2,1)$, $p_4 = u(2,2)$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Boundary Values:**
$u(0, \cdot) = 0$ (left), $u(3, \cdot) = 100$ (right), $u(\cdot, 0) = 0$ (bottom), $u(\cdot, 3) = 0$ (top).

**Step 3 ŌĆö Jacobi Formulas:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_1^{(k+1)} &= \tfrac{1}{4}\left(0 + p_3^{(k)} + 0 + p_2^{(k)}\right) \\[2pt]
p_2^{(k+1)} &= \tfrac{1}{4}\left(0 + p_4^{(k)} + p_1^{(k)} + 0\right) \\[2pt]
p_3^{(k+1)} &= \tfrac{1}{4}\left(p_1^{(k)} + 100 + 0 + p_4^{(k)}\right) \\[2pt]
p_4^{(k+1)} &= \tfrac{1}{4}\left(p_2^{(k)} + 100 + p_3^{(k)} + 0\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Initial guess $k=0$:** $p_1 = p_2 = p_3 = p_4 = 0$.

**Step 5 ŌĆö Iteration $k=1$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_1^{(1)} &= \tfrac{1}{4}(0 + 0 + 0 + 0) = 0.00 \\[2pt]
p_2^{(1)} &= \tfrac{1}{4}(0 + 0 + 0 + 0) = 0.00 \\[2pt]
p_3^{(1)} &= \tfrac{1}{4}(0 + 100 + 0 + 0) = 25.00 \\[2pt]
p_4^{(1)} &= \tfrac{1}{4}(0 + 100 + 0 + 0) = 25.00
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Iteration $k=2$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_1^{(2)} &= \tfrac{1}{4}(0 + 25 + 0 + 0) = 6.25 \\[2pt]
p_2^{(2)} &= \tfrac{1}{4}(0 + 25 + 0 + 0) = 6.25 \\[2pt]
p_3^{(2)} &= \tfrac{1}{4}(0 + 100 + 0 + 25) = 31.25 \\[2pt]
p_4^{(2)} &= \tfrac{1}{4}(0 + 100 + 25 + 0) = 31.25
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Final Table:**

| Iter $k$ | $p_1$ | $p_2$ | $p_3$ | $p_4$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **0** | 0.00 | 0.00 | 0.00 | 0.00 |
| **1** | 0.00 | 0.00 | 25.00 | 25.00 |
| **2** | 6.25 | 6.25 | 31.25 | 31.25 |

**[7 marks: 1 grid, 2 formulas, 3 iterations, 1 table]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mixing $k$ and $k+1$ on the RHS:** The most common error (worth 2ŌĆō3 marks lost) is using $u_{i,j}^{(k+1)}$ on the right-hand side of the Jacobi update ŌĆö this is **Gauss-Seidel**, not Jacobi! Always use *only* the previous iteration $(k)$ on the RHS.
> 2. **Forgetting to keep boundary values fixed:** Students occasionally update boundary nodes as well. This is incorrect ŌĆö boundaries are *Dirichlet inputs*, never updated.
> 3. **Sign errors in the formula:** Writing $-4u_{i,j} = (\text{sum})$ instead of $+4u_{i,j}$ when rearranging.
> 4. **Initial guess non-zero without justification:** If you use $u_{i,j}^{(0)} \neq 0$, you must state the choice and reasoning. Otherwise, default to zero.
> 5. **Arithmetic errors in averaging:** KTU examiners check at least 2 sample points manually. A single multiplication mistake in any one iteration costs 1 mark.
> 6. **Not labeling the grid:** Without a labeled diagram of the grid with boundary values, the 1 mark for "grid setup" is forfeited.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **The Laplace Equation** is $\nabla^{2} u = 0$, governing steady-state physics with no source/sink.
- **Central difference discretization** gives the **Five-Point Star Stencil**: $u_{i,j} = \tfrac{1}{4}(u_{\text{right}} + u_{\text{left}} + u_{\text{up}} + u_{\text{down}})$.
- **Jacobi's update rule** uses *only* the previous iteration on the RHS: $u_{i,j}^{(k+1)} = \tfrac{1}{4}\left(u_{i+1,j}^{(k)} + u_{i-1,j}^{(k)} + u_{i,j+1}^{(k)} + u_{i,j-1}^{(k)}\right)$.
- **All interior points are updated simultaneously** ŌĆö this is the signature of Jacobi (as opposed to Gauss-Seidel, which uses freshly updated values).
- **Standard initial guess:** $u_{i,j}^{(0)} = 0$ for all interior nodes.
- **Boundary values are constants** and are *never* part of the iteration update.
- **Convergence criterion:** Stop when $\max \vert u^{(k+1)} - u^{(k)} \vert < \varepsilon$ (typical $\varepsilon = 10^{-3}$ or $10^{-4}$).
- **Convergence is guaranteed** for the Laplace equation on a bounded domain (the iteration matrix is diagonally dominant with spectral radius < 1).
- **Convergence is slow** ŌĆö for faster convergence, use **Gauss-Seidel** or **Successive Over-Relaxation (SOR)**.
- **Symmetry exploitation:** If the BCs are symmetric, interior points can be grouped, halving the computation.
- **Order of accuracy:** The five-point formula is $\mathcal{O}(h^{2})$ accurate ŌĆö halving $h$ quarters the error but quadruples the computation.
- **Symmetry property in iteration table:** In rectangular domains with symmetric BCs, the table will show $p_1 = p_3$, $p_2 = p_4$, etc. ŌĆö always check this.
- **KTU exam tip:** For 14-mark questions, expect (i) a derivation of the five-point formula (~7 marks) and (ii) 2ŌĆō3 hand-computed iterations with a neat iteration table (~7 marks). Always present the table in a clean boxed format.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Curve Fitting by Method of Least Squares - Straight Lines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GCMAT401 - MATHEMATICS FOR PHYSICAL SCIENCE AND LIFE SCIENCE ŌĆō 4
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Curve Fitting by Method of Least Squares - Straight Lines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Curve Fitting** is the process of constructing a mathematical curve or a functional model that best fits a given set of data points in the plane. Among the several methods available, the **Method of Least Squares** is the most widely used statistical technique for determining the parameters of a fitting curve by **minimizing the sum of the squares of the vertical deviations (residuals)** between the observed data points and the values predicted by the curve.

In the specific case of **Straight Line Fitting**, given $n$ experimental data points $(x_1, y_1), (x_2, y_2), \dots, (x_n, y_n)$, we seek constants $a$ and $b$ such that the line:

$$y = a + bx$$

best represents the underlying trend of the data according to the **Least Squares Criterion**.

## 1.2 The Principle of Least Squares

For every observed data point $(x_i, y_i)$, the predicted value on the fitted straight line is $\hat{y}_i = a + bx_i$. The **residual** or **error** is the vertical distance:

$$e_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i = y_i - (a + bx_i)$$

The Method of Least Squares requires that the sum of the squares of these residuals be a **minimum**:

$$S = \sum_{i=1}^{n} e_i^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - a - bx_i)^2 = \text{minimum}$$

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine you are a teacher and 10 students each estimated the length of a blackboard using a faulty measuring tape. Every student gives a slightly different answer. You, being the wise teacher, want a single "best estimate." If you simply take the average, you are essentially **minimizing the sum of squared errors** ŌĆö this is exactly what the Method of Least Squares does for a *constant* (horizontal line) fit!
> 
> Now extend this idea: if the data shows a **growing pattern** (like temperature vs. time, or force vs. elongation in a spring), the best fit is not a horizontal line but a **slanted straight line** $y = a + bx$. The Method of Least Squares finds the unique $a$ (intercept) and $b$ (slope) such that the total squared vertical distance from each data point to this line is as small as possible.

## 1.4 Why "Squares"? ŌĆö The Engineering Rationale

- Squaring the errors ensures that **positive and negative deviations do not cancel out** (a problem in simple averaging).
- Squaring **penalizes large errors more heavily**, producing a stable, robust fit.
- It leads to a **closed-form analytical solution** (no iterative numerical loops), making it computationally elegant.
- It has a deep statistical foundation: if errors are normally distributed, Least Squares estimates are the **Maximum Likelihood Estimates (MLE)**.

## 1.5 Engineering Relevance

| Field | Typical Application of Linear Least Squares Fit |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Civil Engineering** | StressŌĆōstrain relationship in materials testing |
| **Mechanical Engineering** | Calibration curves for sensors and thermometers |
| **Electrical Engineering** | Linear regression for signal processing |
| **Biotechnology** | Dose-response curves in pharmacology |
| **Environmental Science** | Trend analysis of pollution vs. time |
| **Economics** | Demand-supply linear approximation |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Geometry of Residuals

Given a candidate line $y = a + bx$ and a data point $(x_i, y_i)$:
- The **observed value** is $y_i$.
- The **predicted value** is $\hat{y}_i = a + bx_i$.
- The **residual** (signed vertical distance) is $e_i = y_i - a - bx_i$.

The total squared error to be minimized is:

$$S(a, b) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - a - bx_i)^2$$

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Normal Equations

To minimize a function of two variables $S(a, b)$, we apply the **first-order necessary conditions for an extremum** (from multivariable calculus):

$$\frac{\partial S}{\partial a} = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{\partial S}{\partial b} = 0$$

These two equations, when written explicitly, are called the **Normal Equations of the Least Squares Problem**.

## 2.3 Deriving the Normal Equations (Logical Steps)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Expand the sum of squares:**

$$S = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i^2 + a^2 + b^2 x_i^2 - 2a y_i - 2b x_i y_i + 2ab x_i)$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Distribute the summation:**

$$S = \sum y_i^2 + n a^2 + b^2 \sum x_i^2 - 2a \sum y_i - 2b \sum x_i y_i + 2ab \sum x_i$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Differentiate with respect to $a$ (treat $b$ as constant):**

$$\frac{\partial S}{\partial a} = 2na - 2\sum y_i + 2b \sum x_i = 0$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Differentiate with respect to $b$ (treat $a$ as constant):**

$$\frac{\partial S}{\partial b} = 2b \sum x_i^2 - 2\sum x_i y_i + 2a \sum x_i = 0$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Simplify to obtain the Normal Equations:**

$$na + b\sum x_i = \sum y_i$$

$$a \sum x_i + b \sum x_i^2 = \sum x_i y_i$$

## 2.4 Solving for $a$ and $b$ ŌĆö Closed-Form Solution

Using Cramer's Rule or substitution, the solution is:

$$b = \frac{n \sum x_i y_i - \sum x_i \sum y_i}{n \sum x_i^2 - \left(\sum x_i\right)^2}$$

$$a = \bar{y} - b \bar{x} = \frac{\sum y_i - b \sum x_i}{n}$$

where the **sample means** are:

$$\bar{x} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i, \qquad \bar{y} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i$$

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Box ŌĆö Master These for KTU ESE**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Notation |
|:-:|:---------|:--------|:---------|
| 1 | Straight-line model | $y = a + bx$ | $a$ = intercept, $b$ = slope |
| 2 | Residual (error) | $e_i = y_i - a - bx_i$ | signed vertical deviation |
| 3 | Sum of squared errors | $S = \sum e_i^2$ | objective to be minimized |
| 4 | Normal Equation 1 | $na + b\sum x_i = \sum y_i$ | derivative w.r.t. $a$ |
| 5 | Normal Equation 2 | $a\sum x_i + b\sum x_i^2 = \sum x_i y_i$ | derivative w.r.t. $b$ |
| 6 | Slope (closed form) | $b = \dfrac{n\sum x_i y_i - \sum x_i \sum y_i}{n\sum x_i^2 - (\sum x_i)^2}$ | primary formula |
| 7 | Intercept (closed form) | $a = \dfrac{\sum y_i - b \sum x_i}{n}$ | or $a = \bar{y} - b\bar{x}$ |
| 8 | Line through origin | $y = bx$, where $b = \dfrac{\sum x_i y_i}{\sum x_i^2}$ | when $a = 0$ forced |
| 9 | Two parallel lines $y = a_1 + b_1 x$, $y = a_2 + b_2 x$ | Solve $4 \times 4$ system | shared slope option |
| 10 | Sum of residuals (check) | $\sum e_i = 0$ | automatic by normal eqn 1 |
| 11 | Mean of $x$ | $\bar{x} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum x_i$ | sample mean |
| 12 | Mean of $y$ | $\bar{y} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum y_i$ | sample mean |

## 2.6 Special Cases Demanded by KTU

> [!NOTE]
> **Case A ŌĆö Straight Line Through the Origin:** $y = bx$ (no intercept).
> Normal equation becomes:
> $$b \sum x_i^2 = \sum x_i y_i \quad \Longrightarrow \quad b = \frac{\sum x_i y_i}{\sum x_i^2}$$
> 
> **Case B ŌĆö Two Straight Lines $y = a_1 + b_1 x$ and $y = a_2 + b_2 x$:** Two separate sets of normal equations, solved simultaneously.
> 
> **Case C ŌĆö Common Slope (parallel lines):** Force $b_1 = b_2 = b$, and solve the $3 \times 3$ system:
> $$n_1 a_1 + b \sum_{1} x = \sum_{1} y, \quad n_2 a_2 + b \sum_{2} x = \sum_{2} y, \quad a_1 \sum_1 x + a_2 \sum_2 x + b(\sum_1 x^2 + \sum_2 x^2) = \sum_1 xy + \sum_2 xy$$

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

The straight-line least squares fit is the **workhorse of linear regression** in:
- **Calibration laboratories** ŌĆö converting instrument readings to physical units.
- **Quality control** ŌĆö monitoring whether a manufacturing process drifts from its set point.
- **Predictive maintenance** ŌĆö projecting wear trends in rotating machinery.
- **Epidemiology** ŌĆö estimating the linear growth of cases during the early phase of an outbreak.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Normal Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation is the **theoretical backbone** of any KTU 14-mark question. Master each algebraic step.

### Starting Objective

We wish to fit a straight line $y = a + bx$ to the data points $(x_i, y_i)$ for $i = 1, 2, \dots, n$ by minimizing the sum of squared residuals:

$$S = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - a - bx_i)^2$$

### Expanding One Term $(y_i - a - bx_i)^2$

$$\begin{aligned}
(y_i - a - bx_i)^2 &= y_i^2 + a^2 + b^2 x_i^2 + 2(-y_i \cdot a) + 2(-y_i \cdot bx_i) + 2(a \cdot bx_i) \\
&= y_i^2 + a^2 + b^2 x_i^2 - 2a y_i - 2b x_i y_i + 2ab x_i
\end{aligned}$$

### Summing Over All $i$

Using the linearity of summation $\sum (\cdot)$:

$$\begin{aligned}
S &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i^2 + a^2 \sum_{i=1}^{n} 1 + b^2 \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i^2 - 2a \sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i - 2b \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i y_i + 2ab \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i \\
&= \sum y_i^2 + n a^2 + b^2 \sum x_i^2 - 2a \sum y_i - 2b \sum x_i y_i + 2ab \sum x_i
\end{aligned}$$

### Partial Derivative w.r.t. $a$ (Step-by-Step)

Treating $b$ as a constant:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial S}{\partial a} &= 0 + 2na + 0 - 2\sum y_i - 0 + 2b \sum x_i \\
&= 2na - 2\sum y_i + 2b \sum x_i
\end{aligned}$$

Setting $\frac{\partial S}{\partial a} = 0$ and dividing by 2:

$$\boxed{na + b \sum x_i = \sum y_i} \quad \text{--- (Normal Equation 1)}$$

### Partial Derivative w.r.t. $b$ (Step-by-Step)

Treating $a$ as a constant:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial S}{\partial b} &= 0 + 0 + 2b \sum x_i^2 - 0 - 2 \sum x_i y_i + 2a \sum x_i \\
&= 2b \sum x_i^2 - 2 \sum x_i y_i + 2a \sum x_i
\end{aligned}$$

Setting $\frac{\partial S}{\partial b} = 0$ and dividing by 2:

$$\boxed{a \sum x_i + b \sum x_i^2 = \sum x_i y_i} \quad \text{--- (Normal Equation 2)}$$

### Solving the $2 \times 2$ Linear System Using Cramer's Rule

Let:

$$A = n, \quad B = \sum x_i, \quad C = \sum y_i, \quad D = \sum x_i, \quad E = \sum x_i^2, \quad F = \sum x_i y_i$$

The determinant of the coefficient matrix:

$$\Delta = \begin{vmatrix} n & \sum x_i \\ \sum x_i & \sum x_i^2 \end{vmatrix} = n \sum x_i^2 - \left(\sum x_i\right)^2$$

Determinant for $a$ (replace first column with RHS):

$$\Delta_a = \begin{vmatrix} \sum y_i & \sum x_i \\ \sum x_i y_i & \sum x_i^2 \end{vmatrix} = \sum y_i \sum x_i^2 - \sum x_i \sum x_i y_i$$

Determinant for $b$ (replace second column with RHS):

$$\Delta_b = \begin{vmatrix} n & \sum y_i \\ \sum x_i & \sum x_i y_i \end{vmatrix} = n \sum x_i y_i - \sum x_i \sum y_i$$

### Final Closed-Form Expressions

$$a = \frac{\Delta_a}{\Delta} = \frac{\sum y_i \sum x_i^2 - \sum x_i \sum x_i y_i}{n \sum x_i^2 - \left(\sum x_i\right)^2}$$

$$b = \frac{\Delta_b}{\Delta} = \frac{n \sum x_i y_i - \sum x_i \sum y_i}{n \sum x_i^2 - \left(\sum x_i\right)^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification property:** The point $(\bar{x}, \bar{y})$ always lies on the fitted line $y = a + bx$, which provides a useful check.

## 3.2 Analytical Worked Example ŌĆö Worked Model for KTU

**Problem:** Fit a straight line $y = a + bx$ to the following data using the Method of Least Squares:

| $x$ | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
|:---:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| $y$ | 2 | 4 | 5 | 4 | 5 |

### Step 1 ŌĆö Compute the Required Sums ($n = 5$)

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum x_i &= 1 + 2 + 3 + 4 + 5 = 15 \\
\sum y_i &= 2 + 4 + 5 + 4 + 5 = 20 \\
\sum x_i^2 &= 1 + 4 + 9 + 16 + 25 = 55 \\
\sum x_i y_i &= (1)(2) + (2)(4) + (3)(5) + (4)(4) + (5)(5) = 2 + 8 + 15 + 16 + 25 = 66
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2 ŌĆö Set Up the Normal Equations

$$5a + 15b = 20$$

$$15a + 55b = 66$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Solve the System

From Equation 1: $a = \frac{20 - 15b}{5} = 4 - 3b$.

Substitute into Equation 2:

$$\begin{aligned}
15(4 - 3b) + 55b &= 66 \\
60 - 45b + 55b &= 66 \\
60 + 10b &= 66 \\
10b &= 6 \\
b &= 0.6
\end{aligned}$$

Then:

$$a = 4 - 3(0.6) = 4 - 1.8 = 2.2$$

### Step 4 ŌĆö Fitted Equation

$$\boxed{\hat{y} = 2.2 + 0.6x}$$

### Step 5 ŌĆö Verify Using the Closed-Form Formula

$$\begin{aligned}
b &= \frac{(5)(66) - (15)(20)}{(5)(55) - (15)^2} = \frac{330 - 300}{275 - 225} = \frac{30}{50} = 0.6 \quad \checkmark \\
a &= \frac{20 - (0.6)(15)}{5} = \frac{20 - 9}{5} = \frac{11}{5} = 2.2 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 6 ŌĆö Verify Mean Point Property

$$\bar{x} = \frac{15}{5} = 3, \qquad \bar{y} = \frac{20}{5} = 4$$

Check: $\hat{y}(3) = 2.2 + 0.6(3) = 2.2 + 1.8 = 4.0 = \bar{y}$ Ō£ō

## 3.3 Special Case ŌĆö Fitting $y = bx$ Through Origin

**Setup:** $a = 0$ is forced. Objective reduces to:

$$S = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - bx_i)^2$$

Differentiate w.r.t. $b$:

$$\frac{\partial S}{\partial b} = -2\sum x_i y_i + 2b \sum x_i^2 = 0$$

$$\boxed{b = \frac{\sum x_i y_i}{\sum x_i^2}}$$

**Quick numerical illustration using same data (if forced through origin):**

$$b = \frac{66}{55} = 1.2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \hat{y} = 1.2x$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix for Quick Problem Identification

| Problem Type | Unknowns | Normal Equation Form | Size of System |
|:-------------|:---------|:---------------------|:---------------|
| $y = a + bx$ | $a, b$ | 2 linear equations | $2 \times 2$ |
| $y = bx$ (origin) | $b$ | 1 linear equation | $1 \times 1$ |
| $y = a + bx + cx^2$ (parabola) | $a, b, c$ | 3 linear equations | $3 \times 3$ |
| Two lines $y = a_1 + b_1 x$, $y = a_2 + b_2 x$ | $a_1, b_1, a_2, b_2$ | 4 linear equations | $4 \times 4$ |
| Two parallel lines (common $b$) | $a_1, a_2, b$ | 3 linear equations | $3 \times 3$ |
| $y = ab^x$ (exponential, linearized as $\ln y = \ln a + x \ln b$) | $\ln a, \ln b$ | 2 linear equations | $2 \times 2$ |
| $y = ax^b$ (power, linearized as $\ln y = \ln a + b \ln x$) | $\ln a, b$ | 2 linear equations | $2 \times 2$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Concept Map ŌĆö Least Squares Straight-Line Fitting Pipeline

The following Mermaid block renders the complete procedural pipeline of fitting a straight line using the Method of Least Squares. This is the kind of structural understanding KTU examiners reward in 14-mark answers.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START: Given n data points (xi, yi)"] --> B["Step 1: Tabulate xi, yi, xi squared, xi yi"]
    B --> C["Step 2: Compute the four required sums"]
    C --> D["S1 = sum of xi"]
    C --> E["S2 = sum of yi"]
    C --> F["S3 = sum of xi squared"]
    C --> G["S4 = sum of xi yi"]
    D --> H["Step 3: Form the two normal equations"]
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I["Equation 1: n*a + S1*b = S2"]
    H --> J["Equation 2: S1*a + S3*b = S4"]
    I --> K["Step 4: Solve the 2x2 linear system"]
    J --> K
    K --> L["Use Cramer Rule or substitution"]
    L --> M["Step 5: Extract a and b"]
    M --> N["Step 6: Write the fitted line y = a + bx"]
    N --> O["Step 7: Verify using mean point property"]
    O --> P["Check: a + b*mean of x equals mean of y"]
    P --> Q["END: Fitted straight line ready"]
```

## 4.2 Residual Geometry Diagram (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following schematic illustrates the geometric meaning of the residual ŌĆö the *vertical* distance from the data point to the fitted line. This is a high-yield visualization frequently tested conceptually.

```mermaid
block-beta
    columns 3
    block:datapoints
        columns 1
        P1["Observed Point (xi, yi)"]
        P2["Observed Point (xj, yj)"]
        P3["Observed Point (xk, yk)"]
    end
    block:residuals
        columns 1
        R1["Residual ei = yi minus a minus b*xi"]
        R2["Residual ej = yj minus a minus b*xj"]
        R3["Residual ek = yk minus a minus b*xk"]
    end
    block:line
        columns 1
        L1["Fitted Line: y = a + bx"]
    end
    P1 --> R1
    P2 --> R2
    P3 --> R3
    R1 --> L1
    R2 --> L1
    R3 --> L1
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree ŌĆö Which Least Squares Form to Use

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1["Is the model a straight line?"] -->|Yes| Q2["Does the line pass through origin?"]
    Q1 -->|No, parabola| P1["Use 3 normal equations for y = a + bx + cx squared"]
    Q1 -->|No, exponential| P2["Linearize using ln and apply 2 normal equations"]
    Q2 -->|No, general line| Q3["Use y = a + bx with 2 normal equations"]
    Q2 -->|Yes, forced origin| Q4["Use y = bx with 1 normal equation"]
    Q3 --> Q5["Compute n, S1, S2, S3, S4 from data"]
    Q4 --> Q5
    Q5 --> Q6["Substitute sums into formulas and solve"]
    Q6 --> Q7["Obtain a and b: write the fitted equation"]
```

## 4.4 Functional Architecture ŌĆö Computational Flow Inside a Numerical Routine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["Input Layer"]
        A1["Raw data table of xi and yi"]
    end
    subgraph COMPUTE["Computation Layer"]
        B1["Sum reducer: S1 S2 S3 S4"]
        B2["Matrix constructor: 2x2 system"]
        B3["Linear solver: Cramer Rule"]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT["Output Layer"]
        C1["Slope b"]
        C2["Intercept a"]
        C3["Fitted line: y = a + bx"]
    end
    A1 --> B1 --> B2 --> B3 --> C1
    B3 --> C2
    C1 --> C3
    C2 --> C3
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Question, May 2024 Pattern]**
> **Define the Method of Least Squares for fitting a straight line. State the two normal equations obtained when fitting $y = a + bx$ to a set of $n$ data points.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Method of Least Squares** is a statistical procedure for determining the best-fitting straight line $y = a + bx$ to a given set of $n$ data points $(x_i, y_i)$ by minimizing the sum of the squares of the vertical deviations (residuals) $e_i = y_i - a - bx_i$ between the observed and predicted values.

$$\text{Minimize } S = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - a - bx_i)^2$$

Setting the partial derivatives $\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial a} = 0$ and $\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial b} = 0$ gives the **two normal equations**:

$$na + b\sum x_i = \sum y_i \quad \text{...(1)}$$

$$a\sum x_i + b\sum x_i^2 = \sum x_i y_i \quad \text{...(2)}$$

Solving these yields the unique least-squares estimates of $a$ and $b$. **(3 marks)**

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Question, Dec 2023 Pattern]**
> **How is the slope $b$ of the least-squares line $y = a + bx$ computed from $n$ data points? Write the final formula.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The slope $b$ of the least-squares line $y = a + bx$ is given by:

$$b = \frac{n \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i y_i - \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i \sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i}{n \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i^2 - \left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i\right)^2}$$

This is obtained by applying Cramer's Rule to the $2 \times 2$ system of normal equations. The intercept $a$ is then computed as $a = \bar{y} - b\bar{x}$, where $\bar{x}$ and $\bar{y}$ are the sample means. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Question ŌĆö Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2024, Model Pattern]**
>
> **(a)** Derive the normal equations for fitting a straight line $y = a + bx$ to a given set of $n$ data points using the Method of Least Squares. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Fit a straight line to the following data using the Method of Least Squares: **(7 marks)**
>
> | $x$ | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 |
> |:---:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
> | $y$ | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |

### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (a)

Let the data points be $(x_1, y_1), (x_2, y_2), \dots, (x_n, y_n)$ and the fitted line be $y = a + bx$.

**Step 1:** The residual at the $i$-th data point is $e_i = y_i - a - bx_i$.

**Step 2:** The sum of squared errors is:

$$S = \sum_{i=1}^{n} e_i^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - a - bx_i)^2$$

**Step 3:** Expanding:

$$S = \sum y_i^2 + na^2 + b^2 \sum x_i^2 - 2a\sum y_i - 2b\sum x_i y_i + 2ab\sum x_i$$

**Step 4:** Setting $\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial a} = 0$:

$$2na - 2\sum y_i + 2b \sum x_i = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad na + b\sum x_i = \sum y_i \quad \text{...(I)}$$

**Step 5:** Setting $\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial b} = 0$:

$$2b \sum x_i^2 - 2 \sum x_i y_i + 2a \sum x_i = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad a \sum x_i + b \sum x_i^2 = \sum x_i y_i \quad \text{...(II)}$$

Equations (I) and (II) are the required normal equations. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (b)

**Step 1:** Compute the required sums ($n = 5$):

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum x_i &= 0+1+2+3+4 = 10 \\
\sum y_i &= 1+2+3+4+5 = 15 \\
\sum x_i^2 &= 0+1+4+9+16 = 30 \\
\sum x_i y_i &= 0+2+6+12+20 = 40
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** Normal equations:

$$5a + 10b = 15 \quad \text{...(I)}$$

$$10a + 30b = 40 \quad \text{...(II)}$$

**Step 3:** From (I): $a = \dfrac{15 - 10b}{5} = 3 - 2b$.

**Step 4:** Substitute into (II):

$$10(3 - 2b) + 30b = 40 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 30 - 20b + 30b = 40 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 10b = 10 \quad \Rightarrow \quad b = 1$$

**Step 5:** $a = 3 - 2(1) = 1$.

**Step 6:** Fitted line: $\boxed{\hat{y} = 1 + x}$

**Step 7:** Verification using mean point: $\bar{x} = 2, \bar{y} = 3$; check $1 + 2 = 3$ Ō£ō. **(7 marks)**

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice**
>
> **(a)** State and explain the principle of the Method of Least Squares. Discuss why the errors are squared and not simply summed. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Fit a straight line of the form $y = a + bx$ to the following experimental data and estimate $y$ when $x = 6$: **(7 marks)**
>
> | $x$ | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
> |:---:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
> | $y$ | 2 | 5 | 4 | 7 | 8 |

### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (a)

**Principle:** The Method of Least Squares determines the parameters of a fitting curve such that the **sum of the squares of the deviations** of the observed data points from the curve is a **minimum**.

**Why errors are squared:**

1. **Cancellation avoidance:** If errors are simply added ($e_1 + e_2 + \dots + e_n$), positive and negative residuals cancel out, giving a misleading "zero" total even for a bad fit. Squaring eliminates this sign issue.

2. **Penalty on large errors:** The quadratic function grows rapidly, so the method automatically penalizes large deviations and prefers lines that are uniformly close to all points.

3. **Analytical tractability:** The squared-error function is differentiable and convex, leading to a system of **linear normal equations** with a unique, closed-form solution (via Cramer's Rule or matrix inversion).

4. **Statistical optimality:** Under the assumption of independent, normally distributed errors with equal variance, the Least Squares estimates coincide with the **Maximum Likelihood Estimates (MLE)**, which are statistically the most efficient unbiased estimates.

**(7 marks)**

### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (b)

**Step 1:** Compute sums ($n = 5$):

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum x_i &= 1+2+3+4+5 = 15 \\
\sum y_i &= 2+5+4+7+8 = 26 \\
\sum x_i^2 &= 1+4+9+16+25 = 55 \\
\sum x_i y_i &= (1)(2) + (2)(5) + (3)(4) + (4)(7) + (5)(8) = 2+10+12+28+40 = 92
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** Normal equations:

$$5a + 15b = 26 \quad \text{...(I)}$$

$$15a + 55b = 92 \quad \text{...(II)}$$

**Step 3:** From (I): $a = \dfrac{26 - 15b}{5}$.

**Step 4:** Substitute into (II):

$$15 \cdot \frac{26 - 15b}{5} + 55b = 92 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 3(26 - 15b) + 55b = 92$$

$$78 - 45b + 55b = 92 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 10b = 14 \quad \Rightarrow \quad b = 1.4$$

**Step 5:** $a = \dfrac{26 - 15(1.4)}{5} = \dfrac{26 - 21}{5} = \dfrac{5}{5} = 1$.

**Step 6:** Fitted line: $\hat{y} = 1 + 1.4x$.

**Step 7:** Estimate at $x = 6$: $\hat{y}(6) = 1 + 1.4(6) = 1 + 8.4 = \mathbf{9.4}$. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls (Read Carefully!)**
> 
> 1. **Arithmetic slip in $\sum x_i y_i$:** This is the most error-prone term. Students often forget the cross-multiplication. KTU examiners specifically scan for this in 14-mark answers ŌĆö a wrong sum here collapses the entire solution.
> 
> 2. **Mixing up $\sum x_i^2$ with $(\sum x_i)^2$:** These are *not* the same. The denominator of the slope formula is $n\sum x_i^2 - (\sum x_i)^2$, *not* $n\sum x_i^2 - \sum x_i^2$. This single error costs **2ŌĆō3 marks**.
> 
> 3. **Skipping the verification step:** Always plug in $\bar{x}$ into the fitted line and check it gives $\bar{y}$. Examiners reward this with **partial marks** even when the final answer is wrong.
> 
> 4. **Forgetting to set up a $2\times 2$ table for $x_i, y_i, x_i^2, x_i y_i$:** Disorganized working causes arithmetic mistakes. KTU strictly expects a *systematic tabular layout*.
> 
> 5. **Writing the wrong model:** If the data is exponential or power-type, you **must linearize first** (using $\ln y$ vs. $x$ or $\ln y$ vs. $\ln x$) before applying the straight-line formula. Fitting $y = a + bx$ directly to non-linear data is a common **conceptual blunder**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary ŌĆö Read 5 Minutes Before the Exam**

- **Curve Fitting** = finding a curve that best represents a set of scattered data points.
- **Method of Least Squares** minimizes $S = \sum e_i^2$ where $e_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i$ (vertical residual).
- **Errors are squared** to (i) prevent sign cancellation, (ii) penalize large deviations, (iii) yield a convex differentiable objective, (iv) coincide with MLE under normality.
- **Straight-line model:** $y = a + bx$ with unknowns $a$ (intercept) and $b$ (slope).
- **Two normal equations:**
  $$na + b\sum x_i = \sum y_i, \qquad a\sum x_i + b\sum x_i^2 = \sum x_i y_i$$
- **Closed-form solutions (memorize!):**
  $$b = \frac{n\sum x_i y_i - \sum x_i \sum y_i}{n\sum x_i^2 - (\sum x_i)^2}, \qquad a = \bar{y} - b\bar{x}$$
- **Line through origin** ($a = 0$): single equation $b\sum x_i^2 = \sum x_i y_i$, giving $b = \dfrac{\sum x_i y_i}{\sum x_i^2}$.
- **Tabulation is essential:** always construct a 4-column table for $x_i, y_i, x_i^2, x_i y_i$.
- **Verification trick:** the point $(\bar{x}, \bar{y})$ must lie on the fitted line $y = a + b\bar{x}$.
- **Sum of residuals check:** $\sum e_i = 0$ (guaranteed by the first normal equation).
- **For exponential data** $y = ab^x$: linearize as $\log y = \log a + x \log b$ and apply the $y = a + bx$ formulas to the transformed variables.
- **For power data** $y = ax^b$: linearize as $\log y = \log a + b \log x$.
- **Determinant check:** $n\sum x_i^2 - (\sum x_i)^2 \neq 0$, otherwise the system is singular (all $x_i$ equal).
- **Common pitfall:** $\sum x_i^2 \neq (\sum x_i)^2$. They differ by a factor involving the variance of $x$.
- **Sanity check the sign of $b$:** a positive slope for an increasing trend, negative for a decreasing trend.
- **KTU 14-mark standard structure:** (1) State the model, (2) Write the objective function, (3) Form normal equations, (4) Tabulate sums, (5) Solve, (6) Verify, (7) State the fitted equation clearly.
- **Internal choice in ESE:** Be ready to handle both Question A and Question B styles ŌĆö typically one emphasizes *derivation* and the other emphasizes *numerical fitting*.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Parabola
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GCMAT401 - MATHEMATICS FOR PHYSICAL SCIENCE AND LIFE SCIENCE ŌĆō 4
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Parabola

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of the Parabola

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Official Definition (Conic Section ŌĆō Parabola)**
> A **parabola** is the *locus* (set) of all points $P(x, y)$ in a plane that are **equidistant** from a fixed point called the **Focus** ($F$) and a fixed straight line called the **Directrix**. Mathematically, if $P(x, y)$ is any point on the parabola, $F(\alpha, \beta)$ is the focus, and $L: ax + by + c = 0$ is the directrix, then:
> $$PF = \text{Perpendicular distance from } P \text{ to the directrix } L$$

The constant ratio of the distance from a point to the focus versus the distance to the directrix is defined as the **Eccentricity** ($e$).

$$\boxed{e = \frac{PF}{PM} = 1 \quad \text{(specifically for the parabola)}}$$

where $PM$ is the perpendicular distance from $P$ to the directrix.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö "The Satellite Dish Intuition"

> [!NOTE]
> Imagine you are holding a torch and pointing it horizontally. The light beam travels in a straight line. Now imagine a satellite dish ŌĆö its curved surface is precisely a **paraboloid** (a 3-D parabola). Why? Because every incoming parallel signal (say, from a satellite in space) reflects off the curved surface and **converges exactly at a single point** ŌĆö the focus. The receiver is placed at that focus. This is the magic of $e=1$: the parabola takes a "spread-out" input (parallel rays) and focuses it to a point. Conversely, a light source at the focus shoots out a perfectly parallel beam (used in car headlights, searchlights, and telescope mirrors).

### 1.3 The Four Standard Orientations of a Parabola

The standard form of the parabola depends on which axis the parabola opens. In all cases, the vertex is taken as the origin and the axis of the parabola is taken as one of the coordinate axes.

| Form of Equation | Focus | Directrix | Axis of Symmetry | Opens Toward |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $y^2 = 4ax$ | $(a,\, 0)$ | $x = -a$ | $x$-axis | Right (Positive $x$) |
| $y^2 = -4ax$ | $(-a,\, 0)$ | $x = a$ | $x$-axis | Left (Negative $x$) |
| $x^2 = 4ay$ | $(0,\, a)$ | $y = -a$ | $y$-axis | Up (Positive $y$) |
| $x^2 = -4ay$ | $(0,\, -a)$ | $y = a$ | $y$-axis | Down (Negative $y$) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mnemonic Rule:** The square term tells you which axis the parabola opens *along* (perpendicular to it), and the coefficient $4a$ (positive by convention) controls the spread. The **focus always lies on the same side as the parabola opens**, and the **directrix always lies on the opposite side**.

### 1.4 Engineering & Real-World Significance

- **Parabolic Reflectors** in satellite dishes, radar antennas, and telescopes.
- **Headlights & Searchlights** use the *reverse* property ŌĆö a bulb at the focus creates a parallel beam.
- **Suspension Bridges** ŌĆö the main cable forms a parabola under uniform load.
- **Projectile Motion** ŌĆö neglecting air resistance, a thrown ball traces a parabola.
- **Parabolic Arches** in architecture (e.g., the Gateway of India arch, railway bridges).
- **McLaren / Formula-1 reflectors** ŌĆö parabolic surfaces concentrate light/heat.
- **Solar Concentrators** ŌĆö parabolic mirrors focus sunlight to generate heat/power.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Derivation of the Standard Equation $y^2 = 4ax$ (FocusŌĆōDirectrix Method)

Let us take the focus $F(a, 0)$ and the directrix $x = -a$. Let $P(x, y)$ be any arbitrary point on the parabola. Drop a perpendicular $PM$ from $P$ to the directrix, where $M$ is the foot of the perpendicular.

By definition of the parabola:

$$PF = PM$$

Step 1 ŌĆö Express $PF$ (distance formula):

$$PF = \sqrt{(x-a)^2 + (y-0)^2}$$

Step 2 ŌĆö Express $PM$ (perpendicular distance from a point to a vertical line $x = -a$):

$$PM = \vert x - (-a) \vert = \vert x + a \vert$$

Step 3 ŌĆö Square both sides to eliminate the radical:

$$(x-a)^2 + y^2 = (x+a)^2$$

Step 4 ŌĆö Expand and simplify:

$$x^2 - 2ax + a^2 + y^2 = x^2 + 2ax + a^2$$

$$y^2 = 4ax$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **derivation that KTU examiners expect to see in your answer scripts**. Practice it fluently ŌĆö it is the most frequently asked 7-mark derivation question.

### 2.2 Key Geometric Terminology of the Parabola $y^2 = 4ax$

- **Vertex ($V$)**: The midpoint of the perpendicular drawn from the focus to the directrix. For $y^2 = 4ax$, $V = (0, 0)$.
- **Axis**: The straight line passing through the focus and perpendicular to the directrix (the $x$-axis here).
- **Focal Chord**: Any chord passing through the focus.
- **Latus Rectum**: The focal chord *perpendicular* to the axis. Its length is $4a$, and its endpoints are $(a, 2a)$ and $(a, -2a)$.
- **Double Ordinate**: A chord perpendicular to the axis.
- **Eccentricity**: $e = 1$ (always, for any parabola).

### 2.3 Parametric Representation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **parametric form** of the parabola $y^2 = 4ax$ is the KTU-preferred way of representing a general point on the parabola. Every point $P$ on the parabola is written as $(at^2,\, 2at)$ for some real parameter $t$. This $t$ is called the *eccentric angle* of $P$.

| Standard Form | Parametric Form | Parameter $t$ corresponds to point |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $y^2 = 4ax$ | $x = at^2,\, y = 2at$ | $P(t) = (at^2,\, 2at)$ |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Notes |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Standard Form | $y^2 = 4ax$ | $a > 0$ |
| 2 | Focus | $F = (a,\, 0)$ | On the positive $x$-axis |
| 3 | Vertex | $V = (0,\, 0)$ | Origin |
| 4 | Directrix | $x = -a$ | Vertical line on the left |
| 5 | Axis | $x$-axis | Line $y = 0$ |
| 6 | Latus Rectum Length | $\vert 4a \vert$ | Endpoints: $(a, 2a)$ and $(a, -2a)$ |
| 7 | Eccentricity | $e = 1$ | Constant for every parabola |
| 8 | Parametric Form | $(at^2,\, 2at)$ | $t \in \mathbb{R}$ |
| 9 | Focal Distance | $\vert x + a \vert = at^2 + a$ | Distance from point to focus |
| 10 | Tangent at $P(t_1)$ | $ty = x + at_1^2$ | Use the rule $T = 0$ |
| 11 | Tangent in Slope Form | $y = mx + \dfrac{a}{m}$ | $m \ne 0$ is the slope |
| 12 | Point of Contact of $y = mx + c$ | $\left(\dfrac{a}{m^2},\,\dfrac{2a}{m}\right)$ | Where the tangent touches |
| 13 | Normal at $P(t_1)$ | $y + t_1 x = 2at_1 + at_1^3$ | Use the rule $T = 0$ with sign change |
| 14 | Normal in Slope Form | $y = mx - 2am - am^3$ | Solves a cubic in $m$ |
| 15 | Condition for Tangent | $c = \dfrac{a}{m}$ | $y = mx + c$ tangent to $y^2 = 4ax$ |
| 16 | Chord of Contact | $T = 0$ (from external point) | $ty_1 = x + at_1^2$ summed cyclically |
| 17 | Chord with Midpoint $(x_1, y_1)$ | $T = S_1$ | The "midpoint chord" formula |
| 18 | Equation of Directrix | $x + a = 0$ | For $y^2 = 4ax$ |
| 19 | Polar of $(x_1, y_1)$ | $ty_1 = x + a\,t_1^{\,2}$? ŌĆö use $T = 0$ form | $T = 0$ with $x \to x_1,\, y \to y_1$ |
| 20 | Focal Chord joining $t_1, t_2$ | $t_1 t_2 = -1$ | Product of parameters is $-1$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** When using the focal chord property, students often write $t_1 \cdot t_2 = 1$. The correct relation is $t_1 \cdot t_2 = -1$. This is a classic mark-loser.

### 2.5 The "T = 0" and "SŌéü" Trick (A KTU Favourite)

For the parabola $y^2 = 4ax$, we define two essential symbolic expressions:

$$S \equiv y^2 - 4ax$$

$$S_1 \equiv y_1^2 - 4ax_1 \quad \text{(value of } S \text{ at point } (x_1, y_1))$$

$$T \equiv yy_1 - 2a(x + x_1)$$

| Operation | Symbolic Form | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Tangent at $(x_1, y_1)$ | $T = 0$ | i.e., $yy_1 - 2a(x + x_1) = 0$ |
| Normal at $(x_1, y_1)$ | Replace $y_1 \to -y_1$ in $T = 0$ | $y + \dfrac{y_1}{2a}x = \ldots$ (use direct formula) |
| Chord of contact from $(x_1, y_1)$ | $T = 0$ | Same as tangent at $(x_1, y_1)$ |
| Chord with midpoint $(x_1, y_1)$ | $T = S_1$ | $yy_1 - 2a(x + x_1) = y_1^2 - 4ax_1$ |
| Pole of line $lx + my + n = 0$ | Use $T = 0$ | $T = 0$ should match given line |

### 2.6 Real-World Utility Recap

The "T = 0 / SŌéü" framework is used in:
- **Radar engineering** ŌĆö to find the chord of reflection when a beam hits a parabolic dish.
- **Solar concentrator design** ŌĆö chord of contact from a sun point.
- **Optics** ŌĆö tangent at a point gives the local angle of reflection (law: angle of incidence = angle of reflection off a parabolic mirror at the focus).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Tangent at $P(t_1) = (at_1^2, 2at_1)$ ŌĆö The "$T = 0$" Method

> **Goal:** Derive the equation of the tangent to $y^2 = 4ax$ at the point $P(at_1^2, 2at_1)$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Identify the symbolic $T$ expression.**

We have $S = y^2 - 4ax$. The $T$-form of the equation is obtained by:
- Replacing $y^2 \to y \cdot y_1$ (the geometric mean of $y$ terms).
- Replacing $x \to \dfrac{x + x_1}{2}$ (the arithmetic mean of $x$ terms).
- Constants are not replaced.

For $y^2 - 4ax = 0$:

$$T \equiv y \cdot y_1 - 4a \cdot \left(\dfrac{x + x_1}{2}\right) \equiv y y_1 - 2a(x + x_1) = 0$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Substitute the parametric point $P(t_1)$.**

Here $x_1 = at_1^2$ and $y_1 = 2at_1$. Substituting:

$$y \cdot (2at_1) - 2a \cdot (x + at_1^2) = 0$$

$$2at_1 \cdot y - 2ax - 2a^2 t_1^2 = 0$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Divide by $2a$ (assuming $a \ne 0$).**

$$t_1 y - x - a t_1^2 = 0$$

$$\boxed{ty = x + at_1^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **canonical tangent equation** for the parabola. It is THE most quoted result in the entire KTU Module 4 syllabus.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Tangent in Slope Form $y = mx + c$

> **Goal:** Find the equation of a tangent to $y^2 = 4ax$ that has slope $m$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö General form of a line with slope $m$.**

$$y = mx + c \quad (c \text{ is unknown})$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Condition for tangency.**

A line is tangent to a curve iff substituting $y$ into the curve gives a quadratic in $x$ with **discriminant equal to zero**.

Substitute $y = mx + c$ into $y^2 = 4ax$:

$$(mx + c)^2 = 4ax$$

$$m^2 x^2 + 2mcx + c^2 = 4ax$$

$$m^2 x^2 + (2mc - 4a) x + c^2 = 0$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö For tangency, discriminant $\Delta = 0$.**

$$\Delta = (2mc - 4a)^2 - 4 \cdot m^2 \cdot c^2 = 0$$

Expanding:

$$4m^2 c^2 - 16amc + 16a^2 - 4m^2 c^2 = 0$$

$$-16amc + 16a^2 = 0$$

$$16a(a - mc) = 0$$

Since $a \ne 0$:

$$mc = a \quad \Longrightarrow \quad c = \frac{a}{m}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö The tangent in slope form.**

Substituting $c = a/m$ back:

$$\boxed{y = mx + \frac{a}{m}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note the constraint:** $m \ne 0$ (a vertical line cannot be a tangent to the rightward-opening parabola $y^2 = 4ax$).

**Step 5 ŌĆö Point of contact of this tangent.**

Substitute $y = mx + a/m$ into $y^2 = 4ax$ and solve for $x$. Since the line is tangent, there is exactly one solution:

$$(mx + a/m)^2 = 4ax$$

$$m^2 x^2 + 2a x + \frac{a^2}{m^2} = 4ax$$

$$m^2 x^2 + (2a - 4a)x + \frac{a^2}{m^2} = 0$$

$$m^2 x^2 - 2a x + \frac{a^2}{m^2} = 0$$

This is a perfect square:

$$\left(m x - \frac{a}{m}\right)^2 = 0$$

$$m x = \frac{a}{m} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad x = \frac{a}{m^2}$$

And $y = mx + a/m = m \cdot \dfrac{a}{m^2} + \dfrac{a}{m} = \dfrac{a}{m} + \dfrac{a}{m} = \dfrac{2a}{m}$.

$$\boxed{\text{Point of contact: } \left(\frac{a}{m^2},\,\frac{2a}{m}\right)}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Normal at $P(t_1) = (at_1^2, 2at_1)$

> **Goal:** Derive the equation of the normal to $y^2 = 4ax$ at the point $P(t_1)$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Slope of the tangent at $P(t_1)$.**

Differentiate $y^2 = 4ax$ implicitly:

$$2y \frac{dy}{dx} = 4a \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{2a}{y}$$

At $P(t_1)$, $y = 2at_1$:

$$m_{\text{tangent}} = \frac{2a}{2at_1} = \frac{1}{t_1}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Slope of the normal at $P(t_1)$.**

The normal is perpendicular to the tangent:

$$m_{\text{normal}} = -t_1 \quad (\text{negative reciprocal of } 1/t_1)$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Equation of the normal using point-slope form.**

The normal passes through $P(at_1^2, 2at_1)$ with slope $-t_1$:

$$y - 2at_1 = -t_1 (x - at_1^2)$$

$$y - 2at_1 = -t_1 x + a t_1^3$$

Rearranging:

$$y + t_1 x = 2at_1 + a t_1^3$$

$$\boxed{y + tx = 2at + at^3 \quad \text{(dropping subscript } t_1 \text{)}}$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Normal in Slope Form $y = mx + c$ (and Co-normal Points)

> **Goal:** Find the condition for $y = mx + c$ to be a normal to $y^2 = 4ax$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö General line.**

$$y = mx + c$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Substitute into $y^2 = 4ax$.**

$$(mx + c)^2 = 4ax$$

$$m^2 x^2 + (2mc - 4a)x + c^2 = 0$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Roots of this quadratic are the $x$-coordinates of intersection.**

For a normal, the line meets the parabola in a way that one of the points of intersection has slope $m$ for the normal. The condition is derived as follows ŌĆö we equate the $x$-coordinate of the point of intersection to the parametric form:

Let one root be $at_1^2$. Then the quadratic must have $at_1^2$ as a root, and the slope condition gives $m = -t_1$, i.e., $t_1 = -m$.

Substituting $x = a t_1^2 = a m^2$ into the quadratic:

$$m^2 (a m^2)^2 + (2mc - 4a)(a m^2) + c^2 = 0$$

$$a^2 m^6 + 2am^3 c - 4a^2 m^2 + c^2 = 0$$

Divide by $a^2$ (using $a \ne 0$):

$$m^6 + \frac{2mc}{a} - 4m^2 + \frac{c^2}{a^2} = 0$$

Setting $c = -2am - am^3 + \ldots$? Let us use the cleaner direct method:

**Step 4 ŌĆö Alternative: Use pointŌĆōnormal coincidence.**

If $y = mx + c$ is the normal at $(at^2, 2at)$, then the slope is $m = -t$ (so $t = -m$), and the point satisfies:

$$2at = m \cdot at^2 + c$$

$$2at = m \cdot a t^2 + c$$

Substituting $t = -m$:

$$2a(-m) = m \cdot a (-m)^2 + c$$

$$-2am = a m^3 + c$$

$$\boxed{c = -2am - am^3}$$

So the **normal in slope form** is:

$$\boxed{y = mx - 2am - am^3}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Co-normal points.**

Three points $P(t_1), P(t_2), P(t_3)$ on the parabola are called **co-normal points** if the normals at these three points are concurrent. The condition is:

$$\boxed{t_1 + t_2 + t_3 = 0}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Favourite Question:** "Show that three co-normal points on the parabola $y^2 = 4ax$ satisfy $t_1 + t_2 + t_3 = 0$." This is a standard 7-mark problem.

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5: Focal Chord and the Property $t_1 t_2 = -1$

> **Goal:** Show that if a chord of the parabola $y^2 = 4ax$ passes through the focus $(a, 0)$ and joins $P(t_1)$ to $P(t_2)$, then $t_1 t_2 = -1$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Equation of chord joining $P(t_1)$ and $P(t_2)$.**

The chord $P_1 P_2$ on the parabola $y^2 = 4ax$ joining two parametric points has the equation:

$$y(t_1 + t_2) = 2x + 2a t_1 t_2$$

(Derived using the two-point form and substituting $y^2 = 4ax$ for both points.)

**Step 2 ŌĆö The focus $(a, 0)$ lies on this chord.**

Substitute $(x, y) = (a, 0)$:

$$0 \cdot (t_1 + t_2) = 2a + 2a t_1 t_2$$

$$0 = 2a(1 + t_1 t_2)$$

$$1 + t_1 t_2 = 0$$

$$\boxed{t_1 t_2 = -1}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **focal chord condition** ŌĆö one of the most tested results in KTU Module 4.

**Length of focal chord joining $t_1$ and $t_2$:**

$$L = a(t_1 - t_2)^2$$

(Can be derived using the distance formula between the two parametric points.)

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]**
*Define a parabola as a conic section. State the eccentricity and the focus-directrix condition. Also write the standard equation of a parabola with vertex at the origin and opening to the right.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **parabola** is the locus of all points in a plane that are equidistant from a fixed point (the **focus**) and a fixed straight line (the **directrix**).

**Eccentricity:** $e = 1$ (the ratio of distance from a point on the parabola to the focus versus the perpendicular distance to the directrix is always 1).

**Focus-Directrix Condition:** If $F$ is the focus and $L$ is the directrix, then for any point $P$ on the parabola, $PF = PM$ where $PM$ is the perpendicular distance from $P$ to $L$.

**Standard Equation** (vertex at origin, opening to the right):

$$\boxed{y^2 = 4ax, \quad a > 0}$$

with focus at $(a, 0)$ and directrix $x = -a$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]**
*Find the equation of the latus rectum of the parabola $y^2 = -16x$ and find its length.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Comparing $y^2 = -16x$ with $y^2 = 4ax$:

$$4a = -16 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad a = -4$$

(The parabola opens to the left, so $a$ is negative ŌĆö KTU allows this convention.)

**Focus:** $(-a, 0) = (4, 0)$.

**Latus Rectum:** The focal chord perpendicular to the axis, with endpoints at $(4, 2a)$ and $(4, -2a)$.

Substituting $a = -4$:

$$\text{Endpoints: } (4, -8) \text{ and } (4, 8)$$

**Length of Latus Rectum:** $\vert 4a \vert = \vert 4(-4) \vert = 16$ units.

**Equation of Latus Rectum:** $x = 4$ (a vertical line through the focus).

---

### 5.2 PART B: Long-Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Question Paper, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Derive the equation of the tangent to the parabola $y^2 = 4ax$ at the point $P(at_1^2, 2at_1)$ using the $T = 0$ method. Hence find the equation of the tangent in the slope form. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Find the equation of the normal to the parabola $y^2 = 4ax$ at the point where the slope of the tangent is $\dfrac{1}{2}$. Also find the point where the normal meets the curve again. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Solution to (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Form the $T = 0$ expression.**

The symbolic $T$ for $y^2 - 4ax = 0$ is:

$$T \equiv y y_1 - 2a(x + x_1) = 0$$

(Here $y^2$ is replaced by $y \cdot y_1$ and $x$ is replaced by $\dfrac{x + x_1}{2}$.)

**Step 2 ŌĆö Substitute the parametric point $P(t_1) = (at_1^2,\, 2at_1)$.**

Here $x_1 = at_1^2$ and $y_1 = 2at_1$:

$$y(2at_1) - 2a(x + at_1^2) = 0$$

$$2at_1 y - 2ax - 2a^2 t_1^2 = 0$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Simplify by dividing by $2a$.**

$$t_1 y - x - a t_1^2 = 0$$

$$\boxed{t y = x + a t^2 \quad \text{(tangent at } P(t) \text{)}}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Tangent in slope form.**

Let the tangent have slope $m$. Then the tangent equation is $y = mx + c$ for some $c$.

A line is tangent iff it meets the parabola at exactly one point, i.e., discriminant is zero.

Substitute $y = mx + c$ into $y^2 = 4ax$:

$$(mx + c)^2 = 4ax$$

$$m^2 x^2 + (2mc - 4a)x + c^2 = 0$$

Setting discriminant $\Delta = 0$:

$$(2mc - 4a)^2 - 4 m^2 c^2 = 0$$

$$4m^2 c^2 - 16amc + 16a^2 - 4m^2 c^2 = 0$$

$$-16amc + 16a^2 = 0$$

$$c = \frac{a}{m}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Tangent in slope form: } y = mx + \frac{a}{m}}$$

---

**Solution to (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Find the point where the tangent has slope $\dfrac{1}{2}$.**

Slope of tangent = $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = \dfrac{2a}{y} = \dfrac{1}{2}$

$$\Longrightarrow \quad y = 4a$$

Substituting into $y^2 = 4ax$:

$$(4a)^2 = 4ax \quad \Longrightarrow \quad 16a^2 = 4ax \quad \Longrightarrow \quad x = 4a$$

**Point of contact:** $P = (4a, 4a)$ ŌĆö note that in parametric form, $t_1 = 2$ (since $y = 2at_1 = 4a$).

**Step 2 ŌĆö Slope of the normal at this point.**

The normal is perpendicular to the tangent:

$$m_{\text{normal}} = -2$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Equation of the normal at $P(4a, 4a)$.**

Using point-slope form:

$$y - 4a = -2(x - 4a)$$

$$y - 4a = -2x + 8a$$

$$\boxed{2x + y = 12a}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Find the second intersection of this normal with the parabola.**

Substitute $y = 12a - 2x$ into $y^2 = 4ax$:

$$(12a - 2x)^2 = 4ax$$

$$144a^2 - 48ax + 4x^2 = 4ax$$

$$4x^2 - 52ax + 144a^2 = 0$$

Divide by 4:

$$x^2 - 13ax + 36a^2 = 0$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Use the fact that $x = 4a$ is one root** (verify by substitution: $16a^2 - 52a^2 + 36a^2 = 0$ Ō£ō).

By Vieta's formulas, the other root $x_2$ satisfies:

$$4a \cdot x_2 = 36a^2 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad x_2 = 9a$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Find the corresponding $y$ coordinate.**

$$y_2 = 12a - 2(9a) = 12a - 18a = -6a$$

$$\boxed{\text{Second intersection point: } (9a,\, -6a)}$$

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) ŌĆö INTERNAL CHOICE**

**(a)** Show that the locus of the point of intersection of perpendicular tangents to the parabola $y^2 = 4ax$ is the directrix $x = -a$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A chord of the parabola $y^2 = 4ax$ passes through the focus and has a length of $8a$. Find the parametric values of the endpoints of this chord, and show that the product $t_1 t_2 = -1$. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Solution to (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Write the two tangents with slopes $m_1$ and $m_2$.**

Tangent 1: $y = m_1 x + \dfrac{a}{m_1}$

Tangent 2: $y = m_2 x + \dfrac{a}{m_2}$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply the perpendicularity condition.**

For the tangents to be perpendicular:

$$m_1 \cdot m_2 = -1$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Find the intersection point $(h, k)$.**

Setting the two tangent equations equal:

$$m_1 h + \frac{a}{m_1} = m_2 h + \frac{a}{m_2}$$

$$h(m_1 - m_2) = \frac{a}{m_2} - \frac{a}{m_1}$$

$$h(m_1 - m_2) = \frac{a(m_1 - m_2)}{m_1 m_2}$$

Since $m_1 \ne m_2$ (they are not the same line), divide by $(m_1 - m_2)$:

$$h = \frac{a}{m_1 m_2} = \frac{a}{-1} = -a$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Identify the locus.**

The $x$-coordinate of the intersection is $h = -a$, *independent of $m_1$ and $m_2$*. The $y$-coordinate $k$ can take any real value. Therefore, the locus is the vertical line:

$$\boxed{x = -a \quad \text{(which is the directrix of the parabola)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a classic KTU result ŌĆö the **directrix of a parabola is the locus of perpendicular tangents**.

---

**Solution to (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Let the chord endpoints be $P(t_1)$ and $P(t_2)$ on the parabola.**

These are $(at_1^2, 2at_1)$ and $(at_2^2, 2at_2)$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply the focal chord condition.**

Since the chord passes through the focus $(a, 0)$:

$$t_1 t_2 = -1$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Apply the length condition.**

The length of the chord $P_1 P_2$ is given by:

$$L = a(t_1 - t_2)^2$$

(This formula is obtained by applying the distance formula and using $t_1 t_2 = -1$.)

Given $L = 8a$:

$$a(t_1 - t_2)^2 = 8a$$

$$(t_1 - t_2)^2 = 8$$

$$t_1 - t_2 = \pm 2\sqrt{2}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Solve the system.**

We have:
- $t_1 t_2 = -1$
- $t_1 - t_2 = 2\sqrt{2}$ (taking positive case)

Adding $t_1 t_2 = -1$ to $t_1 - t_2 = 2\sqrt{2}$:

Using $(t_1 + t_2)^2 = (t_1 - t_2)^2 + 4 t_1 t_2 = 8 - 4 = 4$

$$t_1 + t_2 = \pm 2$$

**Case 1:** $t_1 + t_2 = 2$ and $t_1 - t_2 = 2\sqrt{2}$:

$$t_1 = 1 + \sqrt{2}, \quad t_2 = 1 - \sqrt{2}$$

**Case 2:** $t_1 + t_2 = -2$ and $t_1 - t_2 = 2\sqrt{2}$:

$$t_1 = -1 + \sqrt{2}, \quad t_2 = -1 - \sqrt{2}$$

**Case 3:** $t_1 + t_2 = 2$ and $t_1 - t_2 = -2\sqrt{2}$:

$$t_1 = 1 - \sqrt{2}, \quad t_2 = 1 + \sqrt{2}$$

**Case 4:** $t_1 + t_2 = -2$ and $t_1 - t_2 = -2\sqrt{2}$:

$$t_1 = -1 - \sqrt{2}, \quad t_2 = -1 + \sqrt{2}$$

In all four cases, $t_1 t_2 = (1)^2 - (\sqrt{2})^2 = 1 - 2 = -1$ Ō£ō (verified).

**Step 5 ŌĆö Endpoints of the chord.**

Using the parametric form $(at^2, 2at)$:

**For Case 1** $(t_1 = 1 + \sqrt{2}, t_2 = 1 - \sqrt{2})$:

Endpoint 1: $\big(a(1 + \sqrt{2})^2,\, 2a(1 + \sqrt{2})\big) = (a(3 + 2\sqrt{2}),\, 2a(1 + \sqrt{2}))$

Endpoint 2: $\big(a(1 - \sqrt{2})^2,\, 2a(1 - \sqrt{2})\big) = (a(3 - 2\sqrt{2}),\, 2a(1 - \sqrt{2}))$

$$\boxed{t_1 t_2 = -1 \text{ is verified} \quad \text{and the chord endpoints are as derived above.}}$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Mark-Losing Mistakes in KTU Parabola Problems:**
> 1. **Sign of $a$:** For $y^2 = -4ax$, students often write the focus as $(a, 0)$ instead of $(-a, 0)$. *Always* compare carefully with $y^2 = \pm 4ax$ or $x^2 = \pm 4ay$.
> 2. **Focal chord condition:** Writing $t_1 t_2 = 1$ instead of $t_1 t_2 = -1$ ŌĆö a one-character error that costs full marks.
> 3. **Slope of normal:** Forgetting the sign change from tangent slope $\to$ normal slope. The normal at $P(t)$ has slope $-t$, not $+t$.
> 4. **T = 0 vs T = SŌéü confusion:** Using $T = 0$ where $T = S_1$ is required (or vice versa). Remember: **tangent** = $T = 0$, **chord with given midpoint** = $T = S_1$.
> 5. **Normal in slope form:** Forgetting that the equation $y = mx - 2am - am^3$ requires solving a cubic in $m$ for the co-normal points. KTU examiners expect you to show the cubic explicitly.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4 ŌĆö Parabola**

- [ ] **Standard form:** $y^2 = 4ax$; Focus $(a, 0)$; Directrix $x = -a$; Vertex $(0, 0)$; Latus Rectum $4a$.
- [ ] **Parametric form:** $(at^2, 2at)$ ŌĆö use this for *any* point on the parabola.
- [ ] **Eccentricity** is **always 1** for a parabola ŌĆö never changes.
- [ ] **Tangent at $P(t)$:** $ty = x + at^2$ (from $T = 0$).
- [ ] **Tangent in slope form:** $y = mx + a/m$, valid for $m \ne 0$.
- [ ] **Normal at $P(t)$:** $y + tx = 2at + at^3$, slope $= -t$.
- [ ] **Normal in slope form:** $y = mx - 2am - am^3$.
- [ ] **Point of contact of $y = mx + a/m$:** $(a/m^2, 2a/m)$.
- [ ] **Focal chord joining $t_1$ and $t_2$:** satisfies $t_1 t_2 = -1$.
- [ ] **Co-normal points** satisfy $t_1 + t_2 + t_3 = 0$.
- [ ] **Midpoint chord formula:** $T = S_1$ (use this for chords with given midpoint).
- [ ] **Chord of contact from external point** $(x_1, y_1)$: $T = 0$.
- [ ] **Polar of $(x_1, y_1)$:** $T = 0$ (same as chord of contact).
- [ ] **Locus of perpendicular tangents** = the directrix $x = -a$.
- [ ] **Latus Rectum endpoints** of $y^2 = 4ax$: $(a, 2a)$ and $(a, -2a)$.
- [ ] **For other orientations** ($x^2 = 4ay$, $x^2 = -4ay$, $y^2 = -4ax$): swap roles of $x$ and $y$ and adjust signs.
- [ ] **Practical memory aid:** The "square side" tells you the axis of opening, and $4a$ magnitude stays the same.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Tip:** Always re-check your final answer by substituting the point of contact into the tangent/normal equation. If both sides match, your answer is correct. In KTU valuation, this verification step often earns *partial* marks even if there is a small algebraic slip elsewhere.
